blocks:
Ayah

سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِۦ لَيْلًا مِّنَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ إِلَى ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْأَقْصَا ٱلَّذِى بَـٰرَكْنَا حَوْلَهُۥ لِنُرِيَهُۥ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (١)
Immaculate is He who carried His servant on a journey by night from the Sacred Mosque to the Farthest Mosque whose environs We have blessed, that We might show him some of Our signs. Indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (1)

(Commentary)

The Surah addresses the matter of His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and His transcendence above any partner whatsoever. Nevertheless, the aspect of glorification (التسبيح) predominates over the aspect of praise (التحميد), as indicated by its opening: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ "Immaculate is He who carried His servant by night" (17:1). This is repeated time and again, such as His saying: سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ "Immaculate is He and exalted is He above what they say" (17:43), and His saying: قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي "Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord!’" (17:93), and His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ سُبْحانَ رَبِّنا "and they say, ‘Immaculate is our Lord!’" (17:108). Even the concluding verse of the Surah: وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلِيٌّ مِنَ الذُّلِّ وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيراً "And say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has neither taken any son, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, nor has He any ally [out of a need] to [protect Him from] humiliation,’ and magnify Him with a [worthy] magnification" (17:111), praises Allah for His transcendence above having a partner, an ally, or taking a son.

The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as evidenced by the contents of its verses. Some have suggested, as mentioned in Ruh al-Ma'ani, the exception of two verses: وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ "Indeed they were about to beguile you..." (17:73) and وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَسْتَفِزُّونَكَ "Indeed they were about to unsettle you..." (17:76). Others excepted four verses: the two aforementioned, plus His saying: وَإِذْ قُلْنا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحاطَ بِالنَّاسِ "When We said to you, ‘Indeed your Lord encompasses all mankind’" (17:60) and وَقُلْ رَبِّ أَدْخِلْنِي مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ "And say, ‘My Lord! Admit me with a worthy entrance...’" (17:80).

It is narrated from al-Hasan that it is Meccan except for five verses: وَلا تَقْتُلُوا النَّفْسَ "Do not kill the soul..." (17:33), وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الزِّنى "Do not approach fornication..." (17:32), أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ "They [themselves] are the ones who supplicate..." (17:57), أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ "Maintain the prayer..." (17:78), and وَآتِ ذَا الْقُرْبى "Give the relative his due..." (17:26).

From Muqatil, it is Meccan except for five: وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ (17:73), وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَسْتَفِزُّونَكَ (17:76), وَإِذْ قُلْنا لَكَ (17:60), وَقُلْ رَبِّ أَدْخِلْنِي (17:80), and إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ "Indeed those who were given knowledge before it..." (17:107).

From Qatadah and al-Mu'addal from Ibn Abbas, it is Meccan except for eight verses, from His saying: وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ (17:73) until His saying: وَقُلْ رَبِّ أَدْخِلْنِي مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ (17:80).

However, there is no indication in the contents of these verses that they are Medinan (مدنية), nor are the rulings mentioned therein exclusive to the Medinan period; similar verses were revealed in Meccan Surahs like al-An'am and al-A'raf.

The Surah opens its intended theme of glorification by referring to the Ascension (المعراج) of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, mentioning his nocturnal journey (الإسراء) from the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) to the Farthest Mosque (المسجد الأقصى), which is Jerusalem (بيت المقدس) and the Temple built by David and Solomon عليه‌السلام, which Allah sanctified for the Children of Israel.

Then, the discourse transitions by association to what Allah decreed for the society of the Children of Israel regarding their rise and fall, honor and humiliation; whenever they obeyed, Allah raised them, and whenever they disobeyed, Allah lowered them. He had sent down the Book to them and commanded them toward monotheism and the negation of any partner.

The discourse then turns to the state of this Ummah and the Book revealed to them, in a manner resembling the state of the Children of Israel: if they obey, they are rewarded, and if they disobey, they are punished. For it is only deeds (الأعمال) that determine how a person is treated, and such has been the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the established way of Allah's action — regarding past nations.

The Surah then mentions numerous realities of the gnoses (المعارف) pertaining to the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد), as well as general laws (الشرائع العامة) consisting of commands, prohibitions, and other matters.

Among the most luminous verses (غرر الآيات) in it are His saying: قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى "Say, ‘Invoke ‘‘Allah’’ or invoke ‘‘the All-beneficent.’’ Whichever [of His names] you may invoke, to Him belong the Best Names’" (17:110); and His saying: كُلًّا نُمِدُّ هؤُلاءِ وَهَؤُلاءِ مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً "To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined" (17:20); and His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا نَحْنُ مُهْلِكُوها "There is no town but We shall destroy it..." (17:58), among others.

His saying, the Exalted: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلاً "Immaculate is He who carried His servant by night..." to the end of the verse. سبحان is a verbal noun (اسم مصدر) for glorification (التسبيح) meaning "transcendence" (التنزيه). It is used in the genitive construction and functions as an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) standing in place of its verb. Thus, the underlying meaning of "Subhan Allah" is "I glorify Allah with a glorification," meaning I declare Him transcendent above everything that does not befit the courtyard of His sanctity (ساحة قدسه). It is often used to express wonder (التعجب), but the context of the verses is more suited to transcendence, as it is the purpose of the exposition, even if some insist it is for wonder.

الإسراء and السري refer to traveling by night. It is said sara and asra, meaning he traveled by night, and sara bihi or asra bihi, meaning he made him travel by night. The term sayr (السير) is specific to the day or general to both day and night.

His saying لَيْلاً "by night" is an adverb of time (مفعول فيه). It provides the benefit that this journey was completed within the night, such that the departure and return occurred in a single night before the break of dawn.

His saying: إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى "to the Farthest Mosque" refers to Jerusalem (بيت المقدس) by the evidence of His saying: الَّذِي بارَكْنا حَوْلَهُ "whose environs We have blessed." القصا means distance (البعد). It was named the Farthest Mosque because it was the most distant mosque relative to the location of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and those with him among the addressees, which was Mecca, where the Sacred Mosque is located.

His saying: لِنُرِيَهُ مِنْ آياتِنا "that We might show him some of Our signs" explains the purpose of the nocturnal journey, which is to show some of the divine signs — indicated by the partitive "min" (من). The context indicates the greatness of these signs which Allah showed him, as He explicitly stated in another place in His speech regarding the account of the Ascension: لَقَدْ رَأى مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرى "He certainly saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord" (53:18).

His saying: إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ "Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing" is the justification for His carrying him by night to show him His signs. That is, He is the Hearer of His servants' words and the Seer of their actions; He heard from the speech of His servant and saw from his state that which necessitated honoring him with this nobility, by carrying him by night and showing him His greatest signs.

In the verse, there is a transition (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the first person plural (التكلم مع الغير) in His saying: بارَكْنا حَوْلَهُ لِنُرِيَهُ مِنْ آياتِنا "We have blessed... that We might show him," then a return to the previous third person. The reason for this is to indicate that the nocturnal journey and the resulting showing of signs issued from the Courtyard of Grandeur and Majesty (ساحة العظمة والكبرياء) and the Abode of Might and Omnipotence (موطن العزة والجبروت). Thus, the Supreme Sovereignty (السلطنة العظمى) acted therein, and Allah manifested Himself to him through His greatest signs. Had it been said "that He might show him of His signs" or otherwise, this subtle point (النكتة) would have been lost.

The meaning is: Let Him be glorified with a glorification—He who, by His grandeur, majesty, and perfect power and sovereignty, carried His servant Muhammad in the heart of a single night from the Sacred Mosque to the Farthest Mosque, which is Jerusalem, whose environs He blessed, to show him, by His grandeur and majesty, His greatest signs. He did this for him because He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing; He knew from what He heard of his speech and saw of his state that he was worthy of being honored with this nobility.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, narrated from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Hisham bin Salim, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Gabriel, Michael, and Israfil brought the Buraq (البراق) to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. One took the bridle, one the stirrup, and the other adjusted his clothes upon it. The Buraq became restive, so Gabriel struck it and said: 'Be still, O Buraq! No prophet before him has ridden you, nor shall anyone like him ride you after him.'"

He said: "It soared with him and raised him to a height that was not excessive, with Gabriel accompanying him, showing him the signs of the heavens and the earth. He said: 'While I was on my journey, a caller called out from my right: "O Muhammad!" I did not answer him nor turn toward him. Then a caller called out from my left: "O Muhammad!" I did not answer him nor turn toward him. Then a woman met me with her arms uncovered, adorned with every ornament of the world, saying: "O Muhammad, look at me so I may speak to you!" I did not turn toward her. Then I traveled on and heard a sound that terrified me, but I passed it. Gabriel descended with me and said: "Pray." So I prayed. He asked: "Do you know where you prayed?" I said: "No." He said: "You prayed at Mount Sinai (طور سيناء), where Allah spoke to Moses directly." Then I rode and we went as long as Allah willed. Then he said to me: "Descend and pray." I descended and prayed. He asked me: "Do you know where you prayed?" I said: "No." He said: "You prayed in Bethlehem (بيت لحم), and Bethlehem is in the region of Jerusalem, where Jesus son of Mary was born."'"

"'Then I rode and we went until we reached Jerusalem. I tied the Buraq to the ring to which the prophets used to tie [their mounts]. I entered the Mosque with Gabriel by my side. We found Abraham, Moses, and Jesus among those whom Allah willed of the prophets of Allah عليه‌السلام. They were gathered for me and the prayer was established. I had no doubt but that Gabriel would lead us. When they stood in rows, Gabriel took my arm and brought me forward, and I led them in prayer, and [I say this] without pride (ولا فخر).'"

"'Then the keeper brought me three vessels: a vessel containing milk, a vessel containing water, and a vessel containing wine. I heard a voice saying: "If he takes the water, he and his Ummah will drown; if he takes the wine, he and his Ummah will go astray; and if he takes the milk, he and his Ummah will be guided." He said: "So I took the milk and drank from it." Gabriel said to me: "You have been guided and your Ummah has been guided."'"

"'Then he said to me: "What did you see on your journey?" I said: "A caller called me from my right." He asked: "Did you answer him?" I said: "No, and I did not turn toward him." He said: "That was the caller of the Jews; had you answered him, your Ummah would have become Jews after you." Then he asked: "What [else] did you see?" I said: "A caller called me from my left." He asked: "Did you answer him?" I said: "No, and I did not turn toward him." He said: "That was the caller of the Christians; had you answered him, your Ummah would have become Christians after you." Then he asked: "What met you?" I said: "I met a woman with her arms uncovered, adorned with every ornament of the world, saying: 'O Muhammad, look at me so I may speak to you!'" He asked: "Did you speak to her?" I said: "I did not speak to her nor turn toward her." He said: "That was the world (الدنيا); had you spoken to her, your Ummah would have chosen the world over the Hereafter."'"

"'Then I heard a sound that terrified me. Gabriel said to me: "Do you hear, O Muhammad?" I said: "Yes." He said: "This is a rock that I cast from the edge of Hell seventy years ago; this is the moment it has settled [at the bottom]." They said that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not laugh until he passed away.'"

"He said: 'Then Gabriel ascended and I ascended with him to the lowest heaven (السماء الدنيا). Over it was an angel called Isma'il, the master of the "snatching" (صاحب الخطفة) of which Allah عز وجل said: إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ الْخَطْفَةَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ ثاقِبٌ "except him who snatches a fragment, whereat there pursues him a piercing flame" (37:10). Under him were seventy thousand angels, and under each angel were seventy thousand angels.'"

"'He said: "O Gabriel! Who is this with you?" He replied: "Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah." He asked: "Has he been sent?" He said: "Yes." So the gate was opened. I greeted him and he greeted me. I sought forgiveness for him and he sought forgiveness for me. He said: "Welcome to the righteous brother and the righteous prophet." The angels met me until I entered the lowest heaven. No angel met me except that he was laughing and joyful, until I met an angel among the angels—I have never seen a creature greater than him, grim of appearance, with manifest anger. He said to me the same prayer they had said, except that he did not laugh, nor did I see in him the joy I saw from the laughter of the [other] angels. I asked: "Who is this, O Gabriel? For I have been terrified by him." He said: "It is right to be terrified of him, for we all are terrified of him. This is Malik, the keeper of the Fire. He has never laughed. Since Allah put him in charge of Hell, he increases every day in anger and rage against the enemies of Allah and the people of disobedience to Him, so that Allah may take vengeance through him. Had he laughed for anyone before you, or were he to laugh for anyone after you, he would have laughed for you." So I greeted him, and he returned the greeting and gave me glad tidings of Paradise.'"

"'I said to Gabriel—and Gabriel is in the station described by Allah as مُطاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ "obeyed there and trustworthy" (81:21)—"Will you not command him to show me the Fire?" Gabriel said to him: "O Malik, show Muhammad the Fire." So he removed its cover and opened a gate of it. A flame burst forth from it, blazing into the sky. It boiled and rose until I thought it would reach me. I said: "O Gabriel! Tell him to return its cover." So he commanded it, and he said to it: "Return," and it returned to the place from which it had come.'"

"'Then I proceeded and saw a man of brown complexion (آدما) and large build. I asked: "Who is this, O Gabriel?" He said: "This is your father, Adam." His progeny were being presented to him, and he would say: "A good soul and a good scent from a good body." Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited Surah al-Mutaffifin at the seventeenth verse: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتابَ الْأَبْرارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ ـ وَما أَدْراكَ ما عِلِّيُّونَ كِتابٌ مَرْقُومٌ يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ "No indeed! The record of the pious is in Illiyyun. And what will show you what is Illiyyun? It is a transcribed record, witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]" (83:18-21). He said: "So I greeted my father Adam and he greeted me. I sought forgiveness for him and he sought forgiveness for me. He said: 'Welcome to the righteous son and the righteous prophet sent in a righteous time.'"'

"'He said: "Then I passed by an angel among the angels sitting on a seat, with the entire world between his knees. In his hand was a tablet of light which he was looking at—a book he was gazing into, not turning right or left, focused upon it like one who is sorrowful. I asked: 'Who is this, O Gabriel?' He said: 'This is the Angel of Death (ملك الموت), diligent in seizing souls.' I said: 'O Gabriel, bring me close to him so I may speak to him.' He brought me close to him and I greeted him. Gabriel said to him: 'This is Muhammad, the Prophet of Mercy, whom Allah has sent to the servants.' He welcomed me, greeted me with peace, and said: 'Rejoice, O Muhammad, for I see all good in your Ummah.' I said: 'Praise be to Allah, the All-Bounteous, the Possessor of favors upon His servants; that is from the grace of my Lord and His mercy upon me.' Gabriel said: 'He is the most intense of the angels in his work.' I asked: 'Do you seize the soul of everyone who has died or will die after this?' He said: 'Yes.' I asked: 'And you see them wherever they are and witness them yourself?' He said: 'Yes.' The Angel of Death said: 'The entire world is not to me, in what Allah has subjected for me and empowered me over, except like a dirham in the palm of a man's hand, which he turns however he wishes. There is no house but that I inspect it five times every day. I say, when the family of the deceased weep over their dead: "Do not weep over him, for I shall return to you again and again, until not one of you remains."' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Death is sufficient as a calamity (طامة), O Gabriel.' Gabriel said: 'Indeed, what is after death is more calamitous and more severe than death itself.'"'

"'He said: "Then I proceeded and saw a people before whom were tables of good meat and foul meat. They were eating the foul meat and leaving the good. I asked: 'Who are these, O Gabriel?' He said: 'These are those who eat what is forbidden (الحرام) and leave what is lawful (الحلال), and they are from your Ummah, O Muhammad.'"'

"The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Then I saw an angel among the angels whose affair Allah made wondrous: half of his body is fire and the other half is ice. Neither does the fire melt the ice, nor does the ice extinguish the fire. He calls out with a loud voice, saying: "Glory be to Him who restrained the heat of this fire so it does not melt the ice, and restrained the cold of this ice so it does not extinguish the heat of this fire. O Allah, O Uniter of ice and fire, unite the hearts of Your faithful servants!" I asked: "Who is this, O Gabriel?" He said: "This is an angel whom Allah has entrusted with the horizons of the sky and the ends of the earths. He is the most sincere of Allah's angels toward the inhabitants of the earth among His faithful servants; he has been praying for them with what you hear since he was created."'"

"'And I saw two angels calling out in the heaven. One of them was saying: "O Allah, give every spender a replacement," and the other was saying: "O Allah, give every withholder destruction."'"

"'Then I proceeded and saw people with lips like the lips of camels. Meat was being cut from their sides and cast into their mouths. I asked: 'Who are these, O Gabriel?' He said: 'These are the backbiters and the slanders (الهمازون اللمازون).'"'

"'Then I proceeded and saw people whose heads were being crushed with rocks. I asked: 'Who are these, O Gabriel?' He said: 'These are those who sleep through the night prayer (صلاة العشاء).'"'

"'Then I proceeded and saw people into whose mouths fire was being cast, and it was coming out of their posteriors. I asked: 'Who are these, O Gabriel?' He said: 'These are those who consume the property of orphans unjustly; they only consume fire into their bellies and shall enter a blazing fire.'"'

"'Then I proceeded and saw people, one of whom would try to stand but could not due to the greatness of his belly. I asked: 'Who are these, O Gabriel?' He said: 'These are those who consume usury (الربا); they do not stand except as one stands who is being beaten by Satan into insanity. They are on the path of the people of Pharaoh, being presented to the Fire morning and evening, saying: "Our Lord, when will the Hour be established?"'"'

"He said: 'Then I proceeded and saw women hanging by their breasts. I asked: 'Who are these, O Gabriel?' He said: 'These are those who bequeath the wealth of their husbands to the children of others.' Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'The wrath of Allah is intense against a woman who introduces into a people's lineage one who is not of them, so that he looks upon their private areas and consumes their treasures.'"'

"Then he said: 'We passed by angels among the angels of Allah عز وجل whom Allah created as He willed and placed their faces as He willed. Every part of their bodies was glorifying Allah and praising Him from every side with different voices. Their voices were raised in praise and weeping from the fear of Allah. I asked Gabriel about them, and he said: "As you see, they were created. An angel among them is beside his companion, yet he has never spoken a word to him, nor have they raised their heads to what is above them, nor lowered them to what is below them, out of fear of Allah and humility (خشوعا). I greeted them, and they returned the greeting by nodding their heads, not looking at me out of humility. Gabriel said to them: 'This is Muhammad, the Prophet of Mercy, whom Allah has sent to the servants as a messenger and a prophet. He is the Seal of the Prophets and their master; will you not speak to him?' He said: 'When they heard that from Gabriel, they turned to me with greetings, honored me, and gave me glad tidings of good for me and my Ummah.'"'

"He said: 'Then we ascended to the second heaven. In it were two men who resembled each other. I asked: "Who are these two, O Gabriel?" He said to me: "The two maternal cousins, John and Jesus عليه‌السلام." I greeted them and they greeted me. I sought forgiveness for them and they sought forgiveness for me. They said: "Welcome to the righteous brother and the righteous prophet." In it were angels upon whom was humility; Allah had placed their faces as He willed. There was no angel among them but that he was glorifying Allah with His praise in different voices.'"

"'Then we ascended to the third heaven. In it was a man whose beauty surpassed the rest of creation as the beauty of the moon on the night of the full moon surpasses the rest of the stars. I asked: "Who is this, O Gabriel?" He said: "This is your brother, Joseph." I greeted him and he greeted me. I sought forgiveness for him and he sought forgiveness for me. He said: "Welcome to the righteous prophet and the righteous brother sent in a righteous time." In it were angels upon whom was humility like what I described in the first and second heavens. Gabriel said to them regarding my affair what he had said to the others, and they did for me what the others had done.'"

"'Then we ascended to the fourth heaven. In it was a man. I asked: "Who is this, O Gabriel?" He said: "This is Idris; Allah raised him to a high station." I greeted him and he greeted me. I sought forgiveness for him and he sought forgiveness for me. In it were angels with humility like those in the heavens we had passed. They gave me glad tidings of good for me and my Ummah. Then I saw an angel sitting on a throne, under whose hand were seventy thousand angels, and under each angel were seventy thousand angels. It occurred to the soul of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that it was He [Allah], so Gabriel shouted at him, and he said: "Stand!" and he remains standing until the Day of Resurrection.'"

"'Then we ascended to the fifth heaven. In it was a middle-aged man (كهل) with large eyes; I have never seen a middle-aged man greater than him. Around him was a group of his Ummah. Their large number impressed me. I asked: "Who is this, O Gabriel?" He said: "This is the one beloved among his people, Aaron son of Amram." I greeted him and he greeted me. I sought forgiveness for him and he sought forgiveness for me. In it were angels with humility like those in the [other] heavens.'"

"'Then we ascended to the sixth heaven. In it was a man of brown complexion, tall, as if he were from the Shanu'ah (شنوة). If he had two shirts, his hair would still penetrate through them. I heard him saying: "The Children of Israel claim that I am the most honored of the children of Adam before Allah, yet this is a man more honored before Allah than I." I asked: "Who is this, O Gabriel?" He said: "This is your brother, Moses son of Amram." I greeted him and he greeted me. I sought forgiveness for him and he sought forgiveness for me. In it were angels with humility like those in the [other] heavens.'"

"He said: 'Then we ascended to the seventh heaven. I did not pass by an angel among the angels but that they said: "O Muhammad, perform cupping (احتجم) and command your Ummah to perform cupping." In it was a man with graying hair and beard, sitting on a chair. I asked: "O Gabriel, who is this in the seventh heaven at the gate of the Frequented House (البيت المعمور) in the proximity of Allah?" He said: "This, O Muhammad, is your father Abraham, and this is your place and the place of those who are God-conscious among your Ummah." Then the Messenger of Allah recited: إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْراهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ ـ وَهذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاللهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "Indeed the nearest of people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this prophet and those who have faith, and Allah is the guardian of the faithful" (3:68). I greeted him and he greeted me. He said: "Welcome to the righteous prophet and the righteous son sent in a righteous time." In it were angels with humility like those in the [other] heavens. They gave me glad tidings of good for me and my Ummah.'"

"The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'In the seventh heaven, I saw seas of light, their brilliance snatching away the sight. In it were seas of darkness and seas of ice that thundered. Whenever I was terrified and saw a horror, I asked Gabriel, and he said: "Rejoice, O Muhammad, and thank the nobility of your Lord, and thank Allah for what He has done for you." He said: "So Allah strengthened me with His power and help until my questioning of Gabriel and my wonder increased."'"

"Gabriel said: 'O Muhammad, do you find what you see to be great? This is but a creation among the creations of your Lord. What then of the Creator who created what you see and what you do not see? Greater than this among the creations of your Lord is that between Allah and His creation are seventy thousand veils (حجاب). The nearest of creation to Allah are I and Israfil, and between us and Him are four veils: a veil of light, a veil of darkness, a veil of cloud, and a veil of water.'"

"He said: 'And I saw among the wonders that Allah created and subjected according to His will a rooster whose feet were in the depths of the seventh earths and whose head was at the Throne (العرش). He is an angel among the angels of Allah the Exalted. Allah created him as He willed: his feet are in the depths of the seventh earths, then he rises up until he emerges into the air to the seventh heaven, and continues rising until his horn reaches near the Throne. He says: "Glory be to my Lord wherever I may be." You do not know where your Lord is due to the greatness of His state. He has two wings on his shoulders; when he spreads them, they surpass the East and the West. When it is the time of dawn (السحر), he spreads his wings, beats them, and cries out with glorification, saying: "Glory be to Allah, the King, the Holy; Glory be to Allah, the Great, the Most High; there is no god but Allah, the Living, the Sustainer." When he says that, all the roosters of the earth glorify [Allah], beat their wings, and begin to cry out. When that rooster in the heaven becomes silent, all the roosters of the earth become silent. That rooster has green down and white feathers, whiter than anything I have ever seen. He also has green down under his white feathers, greener than anything I have ever seen.'"

"He said: 'Then I proceeded with Gabriel and entered the Frequented House (البيت المعمور). I prayed two units (ركعتين) therein. With me were people from my companions wearing new clothes, and others wearing worn-out clothes. Those with new clothes entered, and those with worn-out clothes sat down.'"

"'Then I came out. Two rivers were led to me: a river called al-Kawthar and a river called Mercy (الرحمة). I drank from al-Kawthar and washed in the river of Mercy. Then they were both led to me until I entered Paradise. On its two banks were my houses and the houses of my family. Its soil was like musk. I saw a young maiden immersing herself in the rivers of Paradise. I asked: "To whom do you belong, O maiden?" She said: "To Zayd bin Harithah." I gave him the glad tidings of her when I reached the morning. Its birds were like camels (البخت), its pomegranates like large buckets. There was a tree such that if a bird were released at its base, it would not circle it for seven hundred years, and there is no station in Paradise but that it has a branch of it. I asked: "What is this, O Gabriel?" He said: "This is the Tuba tree (شجرة طوبى)." Allah said: طُوبى لَهُمْ وَحُسْنُ مَآبٍ "For them will be Tuba and a good destination" (13:29).'"

"The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'When I entered Paradise, I returned to myself. I asked Gabriel about those seas, their horror, and their wonders. He said: "They are the pavilions of the veils (سرادقات الحجب) with which Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has veiled Himself. Had it not been for those veils, the light of the Throne would have destroyed everything in it."'"

"'I reached the Lote Tree of the Boundary (سدرة المنتهى). A single leaf of it would shade a nation among the nations. I was from it as Allah the Exalted said: قابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنى "two bows’ length away or even closer" (53:9). He called out to me: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ رَبِّهِ "The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord" (2:285). I said, answering for myself and my Ummah: وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ كُلٌّ آمَنَ بِاللهِ ( وَمَلائِكته ) وَكُتُبِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ ـ لا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِنْ رُسُلِهِ ـ وَقالُوا سَمِعْنا وَأَطَعْنا غُفْرانَكَ رَبَّنا وَإِلَيْكَ الْمَصِيرُ "and the faithful—each [of them] has faith in Allah, His angels, His books and His apostles. [They say,] ‘We make no distinction between any of His apostles.’ And they say, ‘We hear and obey. Our Lord, [we crave] Your forgiveness, and toward You is the return’" (2:285). Allah said: لا يُكَلِّفُ اللهُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها ، لَها ما كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْها مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ "Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity. To its benefit is what it earns, and to its detriment is what it perpetrates" (2:286). I said: رَبَّنا لا تُؤاخِذْنا إِنْ نَسِينا أَوْ أَخْطَأْنا "Our Lord, do not take us to task if we forget or make a mistake!" (2:286). Allah said: "I shall not take you to task." I said: رَبَّنا وَلا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنا إِصْراً ـ كَما حَمَلْتَهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِنا "Our Lord, do not place upon us a burden as You placed on those who were before us!" (2:286). Allah said: "I shall not place it upon you." I said: رَبَّنا وَلا تُحَمِّلْنا ما لا طاقَةَ لَنا بِهِ ـ وَاعْفُ عَنَّا وَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا ـ أَنْتَ مَوْلانا فَانْصُرْنا عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكافِرِينَ "Our Lord, do not lay upon us what we have no strength to bear! Excuse us, forgive us, and be merciful to us! You are our Master, so help us against the faithless lot!" (2:286). Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said: "I have given you that, for you and for your Ummah."'"

"Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: 'No one ever went as a guest to Allah the Exalted more honored than the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله when he asked for these qualities for his Ummah.'"

"The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'O Lord, You gave Your prophets virtues, so give to me.' Allah said: 'I have given you, among what I have given you, two words from under My Throne: "There is no power nor strength except by Allah," and "There is no refuge from You except toward You."'"

"He said: 'And the angels taught me a saying to say when I reach the morning and the evening: "O Allah, my wrongdoing has sought refuge in Your pardon, my sin has sought refuge in Your forgiveness, my humiliation has sought refuge in Your might, my poverty has sought refuge in Your wealth, and my perishing face has sought refuge in Your abiding Face which does not perish." And I say that when I reach the evening.'"

"'Then I heard the call to prayer (الأذان). An angel gave the call who had not been seen in the heaven before that night. He said: "Allah is the Greatest, Allah is the Greatest (الله أكبر الله أكبر)." Allah said: "My servant has spoken the truth; I am greater than everything." He said: "I bear witness that there is no god but Allah (أشهد أن لا إله إلا الله)." Allah said: "My servant has spoken the truth; I am Allah, there is no god but I, and there is no god besides Me." He said: "I bear witness that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah (أشهد أن محمدا رسول الله)." Allah said: "My servant has spoken the truth; indeed Muhammad is My servant and My messenger; I sent him and chose him." He said: "Hasten to prayer (حي على الصلاة)." He said: "My servant has spoken the truth; he has called to My obligation. Whoever walks to it desiring it and seeking reward, it shall be an expiation for his past sins." He said: "Hasten to success (حي على الفلاح)." Allah said: "It is righteousness, triumph, and success." Then I led the angels in prayer in the heaven, just as I had led the prophets in Jerusalem.'"

"'Then a mist (ضبابة) enveloped me and I fell prostrate. My Lord called out to me: "I have enjoined fifty prayers upon every prophet before you, and I have enjoined them upon you and your Ummah; so fulfill them, you and your Ummah." The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "I descended until I passed by Abraham, and he did not ask me about anything, until I reached Moses. He asked: 'What have you done, O Muhammad?' I said: 'My Lord said: I have enjoined fifty prayers upon every prophet before you, and I have enjoined them upon you and your Ummah.' Moses said: 'O Muhammad, your Ummah is the last of the nations and the weakest of them. Your Lord is not increased by anything, and your Ummah will not be able to fulfill them. So return to your Lord and ask Him for a reduction for your Ummah.'"'

"'I returned to my Lord until I reached the Lote Tree of the Boundary and fell prostrate. Then I said: "You enjoined fifty prayers upon me and my Ummah, and I cannot bear that, nor can my Ummah; so reduce it for me." He reduced ten for me. I returned to Moses and informed him. He said: "Return, for you cannot bear it." I returned to my Lord and He reduced ten for me. I returned to Moses and informed him, and he said: "Return." In every return, I would fall prostrate, until it came down to ten prayers. I returned to Moses and informed him, and he said: "You cannot bear it." I returned to my Lord and He reduced five for me. I returned to Moses and informed him, and he said: "You cannot bear it." I said: "I have become shy before my Lord, but I shall be patient with them." Then a caller called out: "As you have been patient with them, these five are as fifty; every prayer is worth ten. Whoever of your Ummah intends a good deed and does it, ten shall be written for him; if he does not do it, one shall be written for him. Whoever of your Ummah intends an evil deed and does it, one shall be written against him; if he does not do it, I shall not write it against him."'"

"Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: 'May Allah reward Moses with good on behalf of this Ummah.' This is the interpretation of the saying of Allah: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلاً مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ ـ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى الَّذِي بارَكْنا حَوْلَهُ ـ لِنُرِيَهُ مِنْ آياتِنا إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ."

I say: Narrations very close to what this report recounts have been transmitted in very many narrations through both Shi'a and Sunni paths. His saying in the narration "a man of brown complexion (آدما)" — it is said a man is adam meaning brown-skinned. الطامة (the calamity) is the severe matter that overcomes everything else; for this reason, the Resurrection is called al-Tammah. الأكتاف (shoulders/sides) is the plural of katif, meaning the edges and regions. His saying: "It occurred to the soul of the Messenger of Allah that it was He" means that he was the angel who manages the affairs of the world and to whom every matter returns.

His saying: شنوة (Shanu'ah) with shin, nun, and waw, and sometimes with a hamzah, is a tribe known for their tall stature. His saying: "with graying hair and beard" — الشمط (graying) is the whiteness of hair mixed with blackness. الزغب (down) is the first hair or feathers to appear, or the small ones. البخت (Bukhth) are Khurasani camels. الدلي (buckets) with a dammah on the dal, kasrah on the lam, and shaddah on the ya is the plural of dalw (bucket) on the pattern of fu'ul. الصبابة (passion/mist) with a fathah on the sad and a ba means longing and tender love; with a dammah on the dad (ضبابة), it means a thin cloud.

In the Amali of al-Saduq, from his father, from Ali, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Aban bin Uthman, from Abu Abdillah Ja'far bin Muhammad al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was carried by night to Jerusalem, Gabriel carried him on the Buraq. They came to Jerusalem, and the prayer niches (محاريب) of the prophets were shown to him. He prayed in them and returned. On his return, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله passed by a caravan of the Quraysh. They had water in a vessel and had lost a camel of theirs and were searching for it. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله drank from that water and poured out the rest."

"When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله reached the morning, he said to the Quraysh: 'Indeed Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, carried me by night to Jerusalem and showed me the traces of the prophets and their abodes. I passed by a caravan of the Quraysh at such-and-such a place; they had lost a camel of theirs, so I drank from their water and poured out the rest of it.' Abu Jahl said: 'The opportunity against him has come to you! Ask him how many pillars are in it and how many lamps?' They said: 'O Muhammad, there are those here who have entered Jerusalem; describe for us how many are its pillars, lamps, and prayer niches?' Gabriel came and suspended an image of Jerusalem before his face, so he began informing them of what they asked him. When he informed them, they said: 'Wait until the caravan arrives and we ask them about what you said.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to them: 'The confirmation of that is that the caravan will appear to you with the rising of the sun, led by a dusky camel (جمل أورق).'"

"The next day, they went out looking toward the mountain pass (العقبة), saying: 'This is the sun rising now.' While they were thus, the caravan appeared to them as the solar disk rose, led by a dusky camel. They asked them about what the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had said, and they replied: 'This indeed happened: a camel of ours was lost at such-and-such a place, and we had set out water, but in the morning we found the water had been poured out.' This only increased them in rebellion (عتوا)."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning from the paths of both groups.

In the same source, with its chain to Abdullah bin Abbas, who said: "When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was carried by night to the heaven, Gabriel brought him to a river called the Light (النور), which is [referred to in] His saying عز وجل: جَعَلَ الظُّلُماتِ وَالنُّورَ 'and made the darkness and the light' (6:1). When he reached it, Gabriel said to him: 'O Muhammad, cross with the blessing of Allah, for Allah has enlightened your sight for you and cleared your path before you. This is a river that no one has ever crossed—neither an angel brought near nor a prophet sent—except that I immerse myself in it every day, then I come out and shake my wings, and not a drop falls from my wings but that Allah, Blessed and Exalted, creates from it an angel brought near, who has twenty thousand faces and forty thousand tongues, each tongue speaking a language that the other tongue does not understand.'"

"The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله crossed until he reached the veils (الحجب). The veils were five hundred veils; from one veil to the next is a journey of five hundred years. Then he said: 'Proceed, O Muhammad.' He said to him: 'O Gabriel, why will you not be with me?' He said: 'It is not for me to pass this place.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله proceeded as long as Allah willed him to proceed, until he heard what the Lord, Blessed and Exalted, said: 'I am the Praised (المحمود) and you are Muhammad; I derived your name from My name. Whoever joins you, I join him, and whoever cuts you off, I sever him. Descend to My servants and inform them of My honoring of you, and that I have not sent a prophet but that I made for him a vizier (وزيرا), and that you are My messenger and Ali is your vizier.'"

In al-Manaqib, from Ibn Abbas in a report: "He—meaning the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—heard a voice: 'We believe in the Lord of the worlds.' He—meaning Gabriel—said: 'These are the magicians of Pharaoh.' He heard: 'At Your service, O Allah, at Your service (لبيك اللهم لبيك).' He said: 'These are the pilgrims.' He heard the Takbir (التكبير). He said: 'These are the warriors.' He heard the glorification (التسبيح). He said: 'These are the prophets.'"

"When he reached the Lote Tree of the Boundary and reached the veils, Gabriel said: 'Proceed, O Messenger of Allah; it is not for me to pass this place. Had I drawn near by a fingertip, I would have been burnt.'"

In al-Ihtijaj, from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said, in what he argued against the Jews: 'I was carried on the wing of Gabriel until I reached the seventh heaven. I passed the Lote Tree of the Boundary, near which is the Garden of the Abode, until I clung to the leg of the Throne. I was called from the leg of the Throne: "Indeed I am Allah, there is no god but I—the Peace, the Faithful, the All-preserver, the All-mighty, the All-compeller, the All-magnanimous, the All-kind, the All-merciful." I saw Him with my heart and did not see Him with my eye.'" (The report continues).

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu al-Rabi', who said: "We performed the pilgrimage with Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in the year Hisham bin Abd al-Malik performed the pilgrimage. With him was Nafi', the client of Umar bin al-Khattab. Nafi' looked at Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) at the corner of the House (ركن البيت) while people had gathered around him. Nafi' said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, who is this around whom the people have crowded?' He said: 'This is the prophet of the people of Kufa; this is Muhammad bin Ali.'"

"He said: 'I bear witness that I shall go to him and ask him questions that no one can answer except a prophet, a successor (وصي), or the son of a prophet.' He said: 'Go to him and ask him; perhaps you will embarrass him.'"

"Nafi' came and leaned against the people, then looked over at Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and said: 'O Muhammad bin Ali, I have read the Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, and the Criterion (الفرقان), and I have known their lawful and forbidden. I have come to ask you about questions that no one answers except a prophet, a successor of a prophet, or the son of a prophet.' He said: Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) raised his head and said: 'Ask whatever occurs to you.'"

"He said: 'Inform me, how many years were between Jesus and Muhammad?' He said: 'Shall I inform you according to my word or your word?' He said: 'Inform me by both words.' He said: 'As for my word, it is five hundred years; as for your word, it is six hundred years.' He said: 'Then inform me about the saying of Allah عز وجل: وَسْئَلْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رُسُلِنا ـ أَجَعَلْنا مِنْ دُونِ الرَّحْمنِ آلِهَةً يُعْبَدُونَ "Ask those of Our apostles whom We sent before you: ‘Did We appoint any gods to be worshipped besides the All-beneficent?’" (43:45). Whom did Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ask, when there were five hundred years between him and Jesus عليه‌السلام?'"

"He said: Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) recited this verse: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلاً مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ ـ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى الَّذِي بارَكْنا حَوْلَهُ ـ لِنُرِيَهُ مِنْ آياتِنا. Among the signs that Allah, Blessed and Exalted, showed Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله when he was carried by night to Jerusalem was that Allah gathered the first and the last of the prophets and messengers. Then He commanded Gabriel, who gave the call to prayer (أذن) in pairs and the iqamah (أقام) in pairs. In his call, he said: 'Hasten to the best of deeds (حي على خير العمل).' Then Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله came forward and led the people in prayer."

"When he finished, he said to them: 'To what do you bear witness? What were you worshipping?' They said: 'We bear witness that there is no god but Allah alone, without partner, and that you are the Messenger of Allah; our covenants and pacts were taken upon that.' Nafi' said: 'You have spoken the truth, O Abu Ja'far.'"

In al-Ilal, with a chain to Thabit bin Dinar, who said: "I asked Zayn al-Abidin Ali bin al-Husayn (peace be upon him) about Allah, Majestic is His Majesty: can He be described with a place? He said: 'Allah is exalted above that.' I said: 'Then why did He carry His Prophet Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by night to the heaven?' He said: 'To show him the kingdom of the heavens (ملكوت السماوات) and what is in them of the wonders of His handiwork and the innovations of His creation.'"

"I said: 'Then what of the saying of Allah عز وجل: ثُمَّ دَنا فَتَدَلَّى فَكانَ قابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنى "Then he drew near and hovered, until he was within two bows’ length or even closer" (53:8-9)?' He said: 'That was the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; he drew near to the veils of light and saw the kingdom of the heavens, then he hovered and looked from beneath him at the kingdom of the earth, until he thought he was in proximity to the earth like two bows' length or even closer.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, with its chain to Isma'il al-Ju'fi, who said: "I was sitting in the Sacred Mosque while Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) was in a corner. He raised his head and looked at the sky once and at the Kaaba once, then said: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلاً ـ مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى. He repeated that three times, then turned to me and said: 'What do the people of Iraq say about this verse, O Iraqi?' I said: 'They say he was carried by night from the Sacred Mosque to Jerusalem.' He said: 'It is not as they say; rather, he was carried from this to this'—and he pointed with his hand to the sky—'and what is between them is a sanctuary (حرم).'"

"He said: 'When he reached the Lote Tree of the Boundary, Gabriel stayed behind. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "O Gabriel, in such a place do you abandon me?" He said: "Proceed before you; by Allah, you have reached a place that no creature of Allah's creation before you has ever reached." I saw my Lord, and the Subhah (السبحة) intervened between me and Him.' I asked: 'What is the Subhah, may I be your ransom?' He gestured with his face to the ground and with his hand to the sky, saying: 'The Majesty of my Lord, the Majesty of my Lord,' three times. He said: 'O Muhammad!' I said: 'At Your service, O Lord.' He said: 'About what does the High Assembly (الملأ الأعلى) dispute?' I said: 'Glory be to You! I have no knowledge except what You have taught me.'"

"He said: 'So He placed His hand between my breasts, and I felt its coolness between my shoulders. He said: He did not ask me about what has passed nor what remains but that I knew it. He said: "O Muhammad, about what does the High Assembly dispute?" He said: I said: "Regarding the degrees (الدرجات), the expiations (الكفارات), and the good deeds (الحسنات)." He said: "O Muhammad, your prophethood has concluded and your sustenance is cut off; so who is your successor?" I said: "O Lord, I have tested Your creation, and I have not seen anyone among Your creation more obedient to me than Ali." He said: "And to Me, O Muhammad." I said: "O Lord, I have tested Your creation, and I have not seen anyone among Your creation with more intense love for me than Ali bin Abi Talib." He said: "And to Me, O Muhammad. So give him glad tidings that he is the sign of guidance (آية الهدى), the Imam of My friends, the light for whoever obeys Me, and the abiding word (الكلمة الباقية) which I have made binding upon the God-conscious. Whoever loves him loves Me, and whoever hates him hates Me. Furthermore, I shall distinguish him with that which I have not distinguished anyone else." I said: "O Lord, my brother, my companion, my vizier, and my heir." He said: "It is a matter that has already passed: that he is tested and others are tested through him. Furthermore, I have bestowed upon him, and bestowed upon him, and bestowed upon him, and bestowed upon him four things"—he closed his hand and did not disclose what he had closed it upon.'"

I say: His saying (peace be upon him): "rather, he was carried from this to this"—meaning from the Kaaba to the Frequented House (البيت المعمور). The intent is not to negate the nocturnal journey to Jerusalem, nor to interpret the "Farthest Mosque" in the verse as the Frequented House. Rather, the intent is to negate that the journey ended at Jerusalem and did not go beyond it, for the narrations are exhaustive (مستفيضة) in interpreting the Farthest Mosque as Jerusalem.

His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "I saw my Lord" means I witnessed Him with the eye of my heart, as mentioned in some previous narrations, and this is supported by the interpretation of "vision" (الرؤية) as such in other narrations.

His saying: "and the Subhah intervened between me and Him" means I reached a level of proximity and closeness (الزلفى) such that nothing remained between me and Him except His Majesty. His saying: "So He placed His hand between my breasts," etc., is a metaphor for divine mercy (الرحمة الإلهية), and its sum is the descent of knowledge from His presence (لدنه تعالى) upon his heart such that it removes every doubt and uncertainty.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Muslim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated through the path of Thabit from Anas that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "I was brought the Buraq, which is a white beast, taller than a donkey and smaller than a mule, which places its hoof at the end of its sight. I rode it until I reached Jerusalem. I tied it to the ring to which the prophets tie [their mounts]. Then I entered the Mosque and prayed two units in it."

"Then I came out, and Gabriel brought me a vessel of wine and a vessel of milk. I chose the milk. Gabriel said: 'You have chosen the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being.' Then we were ascended to the lowest heaven. Gabriel asked for it to be opened. It was said: 'Who are you?' He said: 'Gabriel.' It was said: 'And who is with you?' He said: 'Muhammad.' It was said: 'Has he been sent for?' He said: 'He has been sent for.' It was opened for us, and there I was with Adam. He welcomed me and prayed for good for me."

"Then we were ascended to the second heaven. Gabriel asked for it to be opened. It was said: 'Who are you?' He said: 'Gabriel.' It was said: 'And who is with you?' He said: 'Muhammad.' It was said: 'Has he been sent for?' He said: 'He has been sent for.' It was opened for us, and there I was with the two maternal cousins, Jesus son of Mary and John son of Zechariah. They welcomed me and prayed for good for me."

"Then we were ascended to the third heaven. Gabriel asked for it to be opened. It was said: 'Who are you?' He said: 'Gabriel.' It was said: 'And who is with you?' He said: 'Muhammad.' It was said: 'Has he been sent for?' He said: 'He has been sent for.' It was opened for us, and there I was with Joseph, and he had been given half of [all] beauty. He welcomed me and prayed for good for me."

"Then we were ascended to the fourth heaven. Gabriel asked for it to be opened. It was said: 'Who is this?' He said: 'Gabriel.' It was said: 'And who is with you?' He said: 'Muhammad.' It was said: 'Has he been sent for?' He said: 'He has been sent for.' It was opened for us, and there I was with Idris. He welcomed me and prayed for good for me."

"Then we were ascended to the fifth heaven. Gabriel asked for it to be opened. It was said: 'Who is this?' He said: 'Gabriel.' It was said: 'And who is with you?' He said: 'Muhammad.' It was said: 'Has he been sent for?' He said: 'He has been sent for.' It was opened for us, and there I was with Aaron. He welcomed me and prayed for good for me."

"Then we were ascended to the sixth heaven. Gabriel asked for it to be opened. It was said: 'Who is this?' He said: 'Gabriel.' It was said: 'And who is with you?' He said: 'Muhammad.' It was said: 'Has he been sent for?' He said: 'He has been sent for.' It was opened for us, and there I was with Moses. He welcomed me and prayed for good for me."

"Then we were ascended to the seventh heaven. Gabriel asked for it to be opened. It was said: 'Who is this?' He said: 'Gabriel.' It was said: 'And who is with you?' He said: 'Muhammad.' It was said: 'Has he been sent for?' He said: 'He has been sent for.' It was opened for us, and there I was with Abraham, leaning his back against the Frequented House (البيت المعمور). Seventy thousand angels enter it every day and do not return to it."

"Then he took me to the Lote Tree of the Boundary. Its leaves were like the ears of elephants and its fruits like large jars. When it was enveloped by the command of Allah, it changed; no one of Allah's creation can describe it due to its beauty. He revealed to me what He revealed, and He enjoined upon me fifty prayers every day and night. I descended until I reached Moses. He asked: 'What did your Lord enjoin upon your Ummah?' I said: 'Fifty prayers.' He said: 'Return to your Lord and ask Him for a reduction, for your Ummah cannot bear that; I have tested the Children of Israel and experienced them.'"

"I returned to my Lord and said: 'O Lord, reduce it for my Ummah.' He removed five for me. I returned to Moses and said: 'He removed five for me.' He said: 'Your Ummah cannot bear that, so return to your Lord and ask Him for a reduction.' He said: I did not cease returning between my Lord and Moses until He said: 'O Muhammad, they are five prayers for every day and night; for every prayer there is ten, so that is fifty prayers. Whoever intends a good deed and does not do it, a good deed is written for him; if he does it, ten are written for him. Whoever intends an evil deed and does not do it, nothing is written; if he does it, one evil deed is written.' I descended until I reached Moses and informed him. He said: 'Return to your Lord and ask Him for a reduction.' I said: 'I have returned to my Lord until I have become shy before Him.'"

I say: The report has been narrated from Anas through various chains. Among them is what has been recorded by al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Mardawayh, via Sharik ibn ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Namir from Anas, who said: "On the night the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was taken on the nocturnal journey (الإسراء) from the Mosque of the Kaaba—three individuals came to him before revelation (الوحي) had been sent down to him—while he was sleeping in the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام). The first of them asked, 'Which one is he?' The middle one replied, 'He is the best of them.' Then one of them said, 'Take the best of them.' That was all that happened that night, and he did not see them again until they came to him on another night—in what his heart perceived while his eyes slept but his heart did not—and likewise, the eyes of the Prophets sleep but their hearts do not. They did not speak to him until they carried him and placed him near the Well of Zamzam. Jibril took charge of him among them; Jibril split open what was between his throat and the pit of his chest until he finished with his chest and his interior. He washed it with Zamzam water with his own hand until he had cleansed his interior. Then a golden basin was brought, filled with faith (الإيمان) and wisdom (الحكمة), and he filled his chest and his throat veins (لغاديده) with it. Then he closed it up and ascended with him to the lowest heaven..." then he continued the narration similar to what has preceded.

That which occurred regarding the splitting of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) abdomen, its washing, cleansing, and then filling it with faith and wisdom, was an imaginal state (حال مثالية) that he witnessed; it was not a material matter (أمر مادي) as some might imagine. This is evidenced by its being filled with "faith and wisdom," and the reports of the Ascension (المعراج) are filled with such imaginal sightings (مشاهدات مثالية) and spiritual representations (تمثلات روحية). This meaning has been mentioned in several reports of the Ascension narrated through the chains of the Sunnis (القوام), and there is no harm in it, as is not hidden.

The apparent sense of the narration suggests that his Ascension (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took place before the Prophetic mission (البعثة) and that it occurred in a dream. As for it being before the mission, this is refuted by the vast majority of narrations regarding the Nocturnal Journey (الإسراء), which are too numerous to count, and the scholars of this field are in agreement upon this.

Furthermore, the hadith itself refutes the idea of the Nocturnal Journey occurring before the mission, as it includes the prescription of the prayers (الصلوات)—that they were initially fifty and then the request for reduction was made at the suggestion of Musa (عليه‌السلام). There is no meaning to a prescription (فرض) before Prophethood (النبوة). Thus, it is appropriate to interpret the beginning of the hadith as meaning that the angels came to him first before he received revelation, then they left him, and then they came to him on another night after his mission. It has been mentioned in some of our narrations that those who were sleeping with him in the Mosque on the night he was taken on the journey were Hamzah ibn ‘Abd al-Muttalib, and Ja‘far and ‘Ali, the two sons of Abu Talib.

As for what occurred in the report regarding this being in a dream, it is possible—though unlikely—that it refers specifically to the account of the splitting and washing. However, it is more apparent that it intends the occurrence of the Nocturnal Journey in its entirety as a dream, as indicated by the narrations that follow.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Jarir also extracted from Mu‘awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan: that whenever he was asked about the nocturnal journey of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he would say: "It was a true vision (رؤيا صادقة) from Allah."

I say: The apparent meaning of the noble verse: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ — "Immaculate is He who carried His servant by night" (17:1) until His saying: لِنُرِيَهُ مِنْ آياتِنا — "that We might show him some of Our signs" (17:1) refutes this. Likewise, the verses at the beginning of Surah al-Najm, which include His saying: ما زاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَما طَغى لَقَدْ رَأى مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرى — "The eye did not swerve, nor did it overstep the bounds. Certainly he saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord" (53:17-18). Moreover, the verses are in the context of conferring a favor (الامتنان) and contain praise for Allah, the Glorified, by mentioning His wondrous mercy and marvelous power. It is necessary to note that this is not achieved through a dream seen by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); for dreams are seen by both the righteous and the wicked, and perhaps a profligate sinner might see something more wondrous than what a God-conscious believer (المؤمن المتقي) sees. Among the general public, a dream is considered nothing more than a type of imagination (تخيل) and is not used as evidence for power or authority; rather, the most that can be said of it is that one might take a good omen from it, hoping for its good, or a bad omen, fearing its evil.

In the same source, Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Jarir extracted from ‘Aishah that she said: "The body of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was never missing, but Allah took his soul (روحه) on the journey."

I say: This is refuted by the same arguments as the previous one. Furthermore, it is sufficient to invalidate the narration that there is a consensus among the narrators and masters of biography (أرباب السير) that the Nocturnal Journey took place a considerable time before the Migration (الهجرة), and that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) consummated his marriage with ‘Aishah in Medina a considerable time after the Migration; no two people disagree on this. The verse is also explicit regarding his journey (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام).

In the same source, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it as fair), al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I met Ibrahim on the night I was taken on the journey, and he said: 'O Muhammad, convey my greetings of peace (السلام) to your community, and inform them that Paradise (الجنة) has fertile soil and sweet water, and that it consists of vast plains (قيعان), and that its seedlings are: Glory be to Allah (سبحان الله), and praise be to Allah (والحمد لله), and there is no god but Allah (ولا إله إلا الله), and Allah is the Greatest (والله أكبر), and there is no power or strength except by Allah (ولا حول ولا قوة إلا بالله).'"

In the same source, al-Tabarani extracted from ‘Aishah that she said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When I was taken up to heaven, I was entered into Paradise. I came upon one of the trees of Paradise; I have never seen a tree in Paradise more beautiful than it, nor one with whiter leaves or finer fruit. I took one of its fruits and ate it, and it became a drop of semen (نطفة) in my loins. When I descended to the earth, I had intercourse with Khadijah and she became pregnant with Fatimah. Thus, whenever I long for the fragrance of Paradise, I smell the fragrance of Fatimah."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from his father, from Ibn Mahbub, from Ibn Ri’ab, from Abu ‘Ubaydah, from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to kiss Fatimah frequently, and ‘Aishah disapproved of that. So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'O ‘Aishah, when I was taken up to heaven, I entered Paradise. Jibril brought me near the Tuba tree (شجرة طوبى) and gave me some of its fruit. I ate it, and Allah transformed that into fluid in my back. When I descended to the earth, I had intercourse with Khadijah and she became pregnant with Fatimah. I have never kissed her without finding the scent of the Tuba tree in her.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tabarani extracted in al-Awsat from Ibn ‘Umar: that when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was taken up to heaven, the Call to Prayer (الأذان) was revealed to him. He descended with it and Jibril taught it to him.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from ‘Ali: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was taught the Call to Prayer on the night he was taken on the journey, and the prayer (الصلاة) was made obligatory upon him.

In al-‘Ilal, with its chain to Ishaq ibn ‘Ammar, who said: I asked Abu al-Hasan Musa ibn Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام): "How did the prayer become one bowing (ركعة) and two prostrations (سجدتين)? And why, when it became two prostrations, did it not become two bowings?" He replied: "When you ask about something, empty your heart so that you may understand. The first prayer that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed, he prayed it in heaven, before Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, in front of His Throne (العرش), majestic is His Majesty (جل جلاله)."

"That was because when he was taken on the journey and reached His Throne, the Blessed and Exalted, He said: 'O Muhammad, draw near to Sad (ص) and wash the members of your body used for prostration (مساجدك), purify them, and pray to your Lord.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) drew near to where Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, commanded him. He performed the ritual ablution (الوضوء) thoroughly, then he faced the Compeller (الجبار), the Blessed and Exalted, standing. He commanded him to open the prayer, and he did so."

"He said: 'O Muhammad, recite: In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds...' to the end of it. He did that. Then He commanded him to recite the lineage of his Lord, the Blessed and Exalted: In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. Say, "He is Allah, the One. Allah is the All-embracing..." Then He withheld the speech from him. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Say, "He is Allah, the One. Allah is the All-embracing."' He said: 'Say: "He neither begat, nor was begotten, nor has He any equal."' Then He withheld the speech from him. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Thus is Allah, my Lord; thus is Allah, my Lord.'"

"When he said that, He said: 'Bow, O Muhammad, to your Lord.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) bowed. He said to him while he was bowing: 'Say: "Glory be to my Lord, the Great, and by His praise."' He did that three times. Then He said: 'Raise your head, O Muhammad.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did that and stood upright before Allah. Then He said: 'Prostrate, O Muhammad, to your Lord.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) fell down prostrating. He said: 'Say: "Glory be to my Lord, the Most High, and by His praise."' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did that three times. Then He said: 'Sit upright, O Muhammad.' He did so. When he sat upright, he remembered the majesty of his Lord, majestic is His Majesty (جل جلاله), and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) fell down prostrating of his own accord, not because of a command his Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, gave him. He glorified Him again three times. Then He said: 'Stand upright.' He did so, but he did not see what he had seen of the greatness of his Lord, majestic is His Majesty."

"He said to him: 'Recite, O Muhammad, and do as you did in the first bowing.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did that. Then he prostrated one prostration. When he raised his head, he remembered the majesty of his Lord, the Blessed and Exalted, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) fell down prostrating of his own accord, not because of a command his Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, gave him. He glorified Him again, then He said to him:"

"'Raise your head, may Allah keep you firm, and bear witness that there is no god but Allah, and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, and that the Hour is coming, there is no doubt about it, and that Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves. O Allah, bless Muhammad and the Progeny of Muhammad, as You blessed, bestowed grace, and had mercy upon Ibrahim and the Progeny of Ibrahim; indeed, You are All-Praiseworthy, All-Glorious. O Allah, accept his intercession for his community and raise his rank.' He did so."

"He said: 'O Muhammad.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) faced his Lord, the Blessed and Exalted, with his head bowed. He said: 'Peace be upon you (السلام عليك).' The Compeller (الجبار), majestic is His Majesty, answered him, saying: 'And upon you be peace (وعليك السلام), O Muhammad. By My favor, I have strengthened you for My obedience, and by My protection (عصمتي), I have taken you as a Prophet and a Beloved.'"

"Then Abu al-Hasan (عليه‌السلام) said: 'The prayer that was commanded was two bowings and two prostrations. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only prostrated twice in every bowing, as I informed you, because of his remembrance of the greatness of his Lord, the Blessed and Exalted. So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made it an obligatory duty (فرضا).'"

"I said: 'May I be your ransom, what is Sad (ص) from which he was commanded to wash?' He said: 'A spring that gushes forth from one of the pillars of the Throne, called the Water of Life (ماء الحياة). It is what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, referred to: ص وَالْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ — "Sad. By the Quran, containing the Reminder" (38:1). He only commanded him to perform ablution, recite, and pray.'"

I say: There are other narrations with the same meaning.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to ‘Ali ibn Abi Hamzah, who said: Abu Basir asked Abu ‘Abdullah (عليه‌السلام) while I was present: "May I be your ransom, how many times was the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) taken up (عرج)?" He replied: "Twice. Jibril stopped him at a certain place and said to him: 'Stay in your place, O Muhammad, for you have stood in a place where no angel or prophet has ever stood. Your Lord is praying (يصلي).' He asked: 'O Jibril, and how does He pray?' He replied: 'He says: "All-Glorious, All-Holy (سبوح قدوس), I am the Lord of the angels and the Spirit (الروح). My mercy has preceded My wrath." He said: "O Allah, Your pardon, Your pardon (عفوك عفوك)."'"

"He said: 'And he was as Allah said: قابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنى — "two bows’ length away or even closer" (53:9).' Abu Basir said to him: 'May I be your ransom, what is "two bows’ length away or even closer"?' He said: 'The distance between its curved end (سيتها) and its head.' He said: 'Between them was a shimmering veil (حجاب), and I believe he said: "of emerald." He looked through something like the eye of a needle at whatever Allah willed of the light of Greatness...'" the hadith continues.

I say: The verses at the beginning of Surah al-Najm support what is in the narration regarding the Ascension occurring twice. Furthermore, rational consideration (الاعتبار) supports what is in the narration regarding His prayer, the Exalted; for the root meaning of prayer (الصلاة) is inclination and turning toward (الميل والانعطاف). From Allah, the Glorified, it is mercy (الرحمة), and from the servant, it is supplication (الدعاء), as has been said. The fact that what Jibril reported of His prayer, the Exalted, included the statement: "My mercy has preceded My wrath" supports what we have mentioned. For this reason also, Jibril stopped him at the position he did, mentioning that it was a ground no one had trodden before him. This is because the implication of describing it in this way is that the position is the dividing boundary between the creation and the Creator, and the ultimate point to which a human can reach in perfection (الكمال). It is the boundary where Divine mercy appears and is poured forth upon what is below it; hence he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was stopped there to witness it.

In al-Majma‘—which is a summary of the narrations—it is stated that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Jibril came to me while I was in Mecca and said: 'Rise, O Muhammad.' I rose with him and went out to the door. There were Jibril, Mika’il, and Israfil. Jibril brought the Buraq (البراق)—it was larger than a donkey and smaller than a mule. Its face was like a human face, its tail like a cow's tail, its mane like a horse's mane, and its legs like a camel's legs. Upon it was a saddle from Paradise. It had two wings from its thighs, and its stride reached as far as its eye could see. He said: 'Mount.' So I mounted and proceeded until I reached the Holy House (بيت المقدس)..." then he continued the narration until he said: "When I reached the Holy House, angels descended from heaven with glad tidings and honor from the Lord of Might. I prayed in the Holy House." In some reports: "Ibrahim was given glad tidings of me among a group of Prophets." Then he described Musa and ‘Isa. "Then Jibril took my hand to the Rock (الصخرة) and seated me upon it. Suddenly, there was a ladder of ascent (معراج) to heaven; I have never seen anything like it in beauty and splendor."

"I ascended to the lowest heaven and saw its wonders, its kingdom (ملكوتها), and its angels, who greeted me with peace. Then Jibril ascended with me to the second heaven, where I saw ‘Isa ibn Maryam and Yahya ibn Zakariyya. Then he ascended with me to the third heaven, where I saw Yusuf. Then he ascended with me to the fourth heaven, where I saw Idris. Then he ascended with me to the fifth heaven, where I saw Harun. Then he ascended with me to the sixth heaven, where there was a vast creation surging one into another, and in it were the Cherubim (الكروبيون). Then he ascended with me to the seventh heaven, where I beheld a creation and angels." In the hadith of Abu Hurayrah: "I saw Musa in the sixth heaven, and I saw Ibrahim in the seventh heaven."

He said: "Then we passed beyond them, ascending to the Highest of the High (أعلى عليين)..." and he described that until he said: "Then my Lord spoke to me and I spoke to Him. I saw Paradise and the Fire, and I saw the Throne and the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary (سدرة المنتهى). Then I returned to Mecca. When morning came, I narrated it to the people. Abu Jahl and the polytheists called me a liar. Mut‘im ibn ‘Adi said: 'Do you claim that you traveled a journey of two months in one hour? I bear witness that you are a liar.'"

They said: "Then the Quraysh said: 'Tell us about what you saw.' He said: 'I passed by the caravan of the tribe of so-and-so; they had lost a camel of theirs and were searching for it. In their baggage was a large bowl (قعب) filled with water. I drank the water and then covered it. Ask them: did they find the water in the cup?' They said: 'This is one sign.'"

He said: "And I passed by the caravan of the tribe of so-and-so; the young she-camel of so-and-so bolted and its leg was broken. Ask them about that.' They said: 'This is another sign.' They said: 'Then tell us about our caravan.' He said: 'I passed by it at al-Tan‘im,' and he described to them its loads and its appearance, saying: 'It is led by a grey camel upon which are two black sacks, and it will appear to you at sunrise.' They said: 'This is another sign.'"

Then they went out rushing toward the high ground, saying: "Muhammad has made a clear judgment between us and him." They sat waiting for when the sun would rise so they could call him a liar. Someone said: "By Allah, the sun has risen!" Another said: "By Allah, here is the caravan appearing, led by a grey camel!" They were dumbfounded but did not believe.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, from Abu ‘Abdullah (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed the late evening prayer (العشاء الآخرة) and prayed the dawn prayer (الفجر) in Mecca on the night he was taken on the journey."

I say: In some reports, it is mentioned that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed the sunset prayer (المغرب) at the Sacred Mosque and then was taken on the journey. There is no contradiction between the two reports. Likewise, there is no contradiction between his praying the sunset, late evening, or dawn prayers in Mecca and the fact that the five prayers were made obligatory upon him in heaven on the night of the Nocturnal Journey. This is because the obligation of the essence of prayer (أصل الصلاة) existed before that. As for how many bowings (ركعة) it consisted of, that is not known, except that the historical reports indicate that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to perform prayer since Allah commissioned him as a Prophet. In Surah al-‘Alaq: أَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يَنْهى عَبْداً إِذا صَلَّى — "Have you seen him who forbids a servant when he prays?" (96:9-10). It has been narrated that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to pray with ‘Ali and Khadijah (عليه‌السلام) at the Sacred Mosque for a period before he announced his call publicly.

In al-Kafi, from al-‘Amiri, from Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام), who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was taken up, he descended with the prayer being ten bowings—two bowings, two bowings. When al-Hasan and al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام) were born, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) added seven bowings out of gratitude to Allah, and Allah permitted that for him. He left the dawn prayer as it was, not adding to it, because the angels of the night and the angels of the day attend it. When Allah commanded him to shorten the prayer during travel (التقصير في السفر), He removed six bowings from his community, but left the sunset prayer without reducing anything from it. Forgetfulness (السهو) only applies to what the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) added; so whoever doubts the original obligation (أصل الفرض) in the first two bowings must restart his prayer."

Al-Saduq narrated in al-Faqih, with his chain to Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib: that he asked ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام): "When was the prayer made obligatory upon the Muslims as it is today?" He replied: "In Medina, when the call became manifest and Islam grew strong, and Allah prescribed Jihad (الجهاد) upon the Muslims. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) added seven bowings to the prayer: two bowings in the noon prayer (الظهر), two in the afternoon prayer (العصر), one in the sunset prayer (المغرب), and two in the late evening prayer (العشاء الآخرة). He kept the dawn prayer (الفجر) as it was made obligatory in Mecca."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, al-Nasa’i, al-Bazzaz, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il extracted with a sound chain from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When I was taken on the journey, a pleasant fragrance passed by me. I said: 'O Jibril, what is this pleasant fragrance?' He said: 'The hairdresser of Pharaoh’s household and her children. She was combing her hair when the comb fell from her hand and she said: "In the Name of Allah (بسم الله)." Pharaoh’s daughter asked: "My father?" She replied: "No, my Lord and your Lord and the Lord of your father." She asked: "Do you have a lord other than my father?" She replied: "Yes." She said: "Shall I inform my father of that?" She replied: "Yes."'"

"So she informed him. He summoned her and asked: 'Do you have a lord other than me?' She replied: 'Yes, my Lord and your Lord is Allah who is in heaven.' He ordered a copper cow to be heated, then he ordered her and her children to be thrown into it. She said: 'I have a request of you.' He asked: 'What is it?' She said: 'That you gather my bones and the bones of my children and bury them together.' He said: 'That is yours, due to the right you have over us.'
