They were thrown in one by one, until it reached a nursing infant among them. He said: 'Go forth, O mother, and do not hesitate, for you are upon the truth.' So she and her child were thrown in."

Ibn ‘Abbas said: "Four spoke while they were small: this one, the witness of Yusuf, the companion of Jurayj, and ‘Isa ibn Maryam."

I say: It has been narrated through another way from Ibn ‘Abbas from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Anas that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "On the night I was taken on the journey, I passed by people whose lips were being clipped with shears of fire. Every time they were clipped, they returned to how they were. I said: 'Who are these, O Jibril?' He said: 'These are the orators of your community who say what they do not do.'"

I say: This type of barzakh-level representation (التمثلات البرزخية)—which depicts actions through their consequences and the punishments prepared for them—is frequently mentioned in the reports of the Nocturnal Journey, and a portion of them has already been presented within the narrations.

Know that what we have cited of the reports of the Nocturnal Journey is but a small portion of them. They are numerous to the point of being widely transmitted (التواتر), narrated by a large group of the Companions such as Anas ibn Malik, Shaddad ibn al-Aws, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه‌السلام), Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, Abu Hurayrah, ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud, ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abbas, Ubayy ibn Ka‘b, Samurah ibn Jundub, Buraydah, Suhayb ibn Sinan, Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, Sahl ibn Sa‘d, Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah, Abu al-Hamra’, Abu al-Darda’, ‘Urwah, Umm Hani, Umm Salamah, ‘Aishah, and Asma’ bint Abi Bakr—all of them from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They have also been narrated by a large group of Shi‘i narrators from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام).

The views of those whose opinions are considered among the scholars of Islam have agreed that the Nocturnal Journey took place in Mecca before the Migration, as is understood from His saying, the Exalted: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلاً مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ — "Immaculate is He who carried His servant by night from the Sacred Mosque" (17:1). This is also indicated by what many narrations contain regarding his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) informing the Quraysh of that on the morning of that night, their denial of it, and his informing them of the pillars of the Furthest Mosque (المسجد الأقصى) and the caravans and other things he encountered on the way.

Then they differed regarding the year in which he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was taken on the journey. It was said: in the second year of the mission, as reported from Ibn ‘Abbas. It was said: in the third year, as in al-Khara’ij from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام). It was said: in the fifth or sixth year. It was said: ten years and three months after the mission. It was said: in the twelfth year. It was said: a year and five months before the Migration. It was said: a year and three months before it. It was said: six months before it.

It does not concern us to delve deeply into that, nor into the month or day in which the Nocturnal Journey occurred, as there is no reliable basis to depend upon. However, one should take note that among the narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) are those that explicitly state that the Nocturnal Journey occurred twice. This is what is understood from the verses of Surah al-Najm where the Glorified says: وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى — "Certainly he saw him yet another time" (53:13), as will come in its commentary, if Allah wills.

Based on this, it is permissible that what he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) described in some narrations of the wondrous things he witnessed refers to what he saw in the first journey, and some of what he described in others refers to the second journey, and some of it was what he witnessed in both journeys together.

Then they differed regarding the place from which he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was taken on the journey. It was said: he was taken from the Valley of Abu Talib (شعب أبي طالب). It was said: he was taken from the house of Umm Hani, and some narrations indicate this. They interpreted His saying, the Exalted: أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلاً مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ — "carried His servant by night from the Sacred Mosque" (17:1) to mean the entire Sanctuary (الحرم) metaphorically, thus including all of Mecca. It was also said: he was taken from the Sacred Mosque itself, due to the apparent meaning of the noble verse, and there is no evidence for reinterpretation (التأويل).

It is possible, in light of what we noted regarding the journey occurring twice, that one of the two journeys was from the Sacred Mosque and the other was from the house of Umm Hani. As for it being from the Valley (الشعب), what was mentioned in the narrations regarding that—that Abu Talib was searching for him all night, and that he and the Banu Hashim gathered in the Sacred Mosque, then he drew his sword and threatened the Quraysh if he did not find the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), then his descent from heaven and coming to them and informing the Quraysh of what he saw—all of this does not align with the state of hardship and affliction that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and all of the Banu Hashim were in during the days they were in the Valley.

In any case, the Nocturnal Journey indicated by the verse: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلاً مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى — "Immaculate is He who carried His servant by night from the Sacred Mosque to the Furthest Mosque" (17:1), which was the journey to the Holy House (بيت المقدس), had its starting point at the Sacred Mosque due to the complete clarity of the verse, and there is no reason for reinterpretation.

Then they differed regarding the modality of the Nocturnal Journey. It was said: his journey (عليه‌السلام) was with his soul and body from the Sacred Mosque to the Holy House, then from there to the heavens; this is the view of the majority. It was said: it was with his soul and body from Mecca to the Holy House, then with his soul from the Holy House to the heavens; a group held this. It was said: it was with his soul (عليه‌السلام) and was a true vision that Allah showed His Prophet; this is attributed to some of them.

It is stated in al-Manaqib: "People differed regarding the Ascension (المعراج). The Kharijites deny it. The Jahmiyyah said: he ascended with his soul without his body by way of a vision. The Imamiyyah, Zaydiyyah, and Mu‘tazilah said: rather, he ascended with his soul and body to the Holy House—due to His saying, the Exalted: إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى — 'to the Furthest Mosque' (17:1). Others said: rather, he ascended with his soul and body to the heavens; this is narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, Ibn Mas‘ud, Jabir, Hudhayfah, Anas, ‘Aishah, and Umm Hani."

"We do not deny that if the evidence is established. Allah made the ascension of Musa to the Mount: وَما كُنْتَ بِجانِبِ الطُّورِ — 'You were not on the side of the Mount' (28:44); and for Ibrahim to the lowest heaven: وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ — 'Thus did We show Ibrahim' (6:75); and for ‘Isa to the fourth: بَلْ رَفَعَهُ اللهُ إِلَيْهِ — 'Rather Allah raised him up toward Himself' (4:158); and for Idris to Paradise: وَرَفَعْناهُ مَكاناً عَلِيًّا — 'And We raised him to a station sublime' (19:57); and for Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): فَكانَ قابَ قَوْسَيْنِ — 'so he was two bows’ length away' (53:9), and that was due to the loftiness of his aspiration." End quote.

What ought to be said is that the essence of the Nocturnal Journey is something that cannot be denied, as the Quran has explicitly stated it and the reports from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams from his Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) are widely transmitted (تواترت).

As for the modality of the Nocturnal Journey, the apparent sense of the verse and the narrations, along with the surrounding contextual clues, is so manifest that it cannot be refuted: he was taken on the journey from the Sacred Mosque to the Furthest Mosque with both his soul and body. As for the ascent to the heavens, the apparent sense of the verses of Surah al-Najm—as will come, if Allah wills, in their commentary—and the explicit statements of the narrations in their vast number, confirm its occurrence. There is no way to deny it in principle. However, it is possible to say it was with his soul, but not in the manner intended by those who claim it was like dreams or the visions seen by a sleeper. If it were so, the verses in their context of demonstrating power and honor would have no meaning, nor would there be any basis for the intense denial shown by the Quraysh when he (عليه‌السلام) recounted the story to them, nor would his informing them of the events on the road have any intelligible concept.

Rather, it was—if it was—by the ascent of his noble soul (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to what lies beyond this material world (العالم المادي), where the honored angels dwell, where deeds end, and from where decrees originate. There, he saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord, the realities of things and the consequences of deeds were represented (تمثلت) to him, he witnessed the souls of the great Prophets and conversed with them, met the honored angels and spoke with them, and saw such Divine signs as can only be described through parables, like the Throne (العرش), the Veils (الحجب), and the Pavilions (السرادقات).

Because some people believe in the primacy of material existence and restrict non-material existence to Allah alone, when they found the Book and the Sunnah describing non-sensory matters by representing them through the properties of sensory bodies—such as the honored angels, the Throne, the Chair (الكرسي), the Tablet (اللوح), the Pen (القلم), the Veils, and the Pavilions—they interpreted them as being material bodies that the senses do not perceive and to which the laws of matter do not apply. They also interpreted the representations mentioned regarding the stations of the righteous, the ascents of proximity, the inner forms of sins, the consequences of deeds, and similar things as a type of simile or metaphor. Thus, they fell into the pit of sophistry by deeming the senses erroneous and affirming arbitrary links between deeds and their consequences, among other problems.

For this reason also, when those among them who denied his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ascent to the heavens with his material body were forced to speak, they said it was in a dream—which to them is a material property of the material soul—and they were thus forced to reinterpret the verses and narrations in a way that none of them could accommodate.

Another Discussion:

It is stated in al-Majma‘ al-Bayan: "As for the place to which he was taken on the journey, it was to the Holy House (بيت المقدس); the Quran has explicitly stated this and no Muslim denies it. What some have said—that it was in sleep—is clearly false, as there would be no miracle (معجز) in it nor any proof (برهان)."

"Many narrations have been reported regarding the story of the Ascension concerning our Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ascent to heaven, narrated by many Companions such as Ibn ‘Abbas, Ibn Mas‘ud, Anas, Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah, Hudhayfah, ‘Aishah, Umm Hani, and others from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Some added details and others omitted them, and they can be divided into four categories:"

"First: That which is certain to be authentic due to the wide transmission (تواتر) of reports and the encompassing knowledge of its correctness."

"Second: That which has been reported and is permitted by reason and not rejected by fundamental principles (الأصول); so we permit it and then conclude that it was in his wakefulness, not his sleep."

"Third: That whose apparent meaning contradicts some principles, but can be reinterpreted in a way that agrees with reason; so it is better to interpret it in a way that agrees with truth and evidence."

"Fourth: That whose apparent meaning is not correct and cannot be interpreted except through far-fetched straining; so it is better not to accept it."

"As for the first, which is certain, it is that he was taken on the journey in general. As for the second, it includes what is narrated that he circumambulated the heavens and saw the Prophets, the Throne, the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary, Paradise, the Fire, and the like. As for the third, it is like what is narrated that he saw people in Paradise enjoying its bliss and people in the Fire being punished; this is interpreted as him seeing their attributes or their names. As for the fourth, it is like what is narrated that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) spoke to Allah openly, saw Him, and sat with Him on His couch, and similar things whose apparent meaning necessitates anthropomorphism (التشبيه), while Allah, the Glorified, is transcendent above that. Likewise, what is narrated regarding the splitting of his abdomen and its washing, because he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was pure and purified from every evil and defect; and how can the heart and the beliefs within it be purified with water?" End quote.

The division he mentioned is appropriate, but most of the examples he provided for the categories are subject to scrutiny. What he mentioned regarding the circumambulation and seeing the Prophets and the like are barzakh-level or spiritual representations (تمثلات برزخية أو روحية). Likewise, what he mentioned regarding the hadith of the splitting of the abdomen and the washing is a barzakh-level representation that carries no harm. The hadiths of the Nocturnal Journey are filled with this type of representation, such as the representation of the world in the form of a woman wearing every adornment of the world, the representation of the call of Judaism and Christianity, and what he witnessed of the types of bliss and punishment for the people of Paradise and the Fire, and other such things.

What supports what we have mentioned is the variation found in these reports when describing a single reality. For instance, in some, his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ascent to heaven is via the Buraq; in another, on the wing of Jibril; in another, by a ladder of ascent (معراج) fixed upon the Rock of the Holy House to heaven; and other such variations that a contemplative researcher encounters within these narrations.

These and their like indicate that these descriptions are based on parable (التمثيل) or spiritual representation (التمثل الروحي). The occurrence of these representations in the outward sense of the Book and the Sunnah is something that cannot be denied at all.

Ayahs

وَءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هُدًى لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَلَّا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن دُونِى وَكِيلًا (٢)
We gave Moses the Book, and made it a guide for the Children of Israel—[saying,] ‘Do not take any trustee besides Me’— (2)
ذُرِّيَّةَ مَنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَبْدًا شَكُورًا (٣)
descendants of those whom We carried [in the ark] with Noah. Indeed, he was a grateful servant. (3)
وَقَضَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَتُفْسِدُنَّ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَّتَيْنِ وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا (٤)
We revealed to the Children of Israel in the Book: ‘Twice you will cause corruption on the earth, and you will perpetrate great tyranny.’ (4)
فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ أُولَىٰهُمَا بَعَثْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ عِبَادًا لَّنَآ أُو۟لِى بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ فَجَاسُوا۟ خِلَـٰلَ ٱلدِّيَارِ ۚ وَكَانَ وَعْدًا مَّفْعُولًا (٥)
So when the first occasion of the two [prophecies] came, We aroused against you Our servants possessing great might, and they ransacked [your] habitations, and the promise was bound to be fulfilled. (5)
ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَا لَكُمُ ٱلْكَرَّةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَمْدَدْنَـٰكُم بِأَمْوَٰلٍ وَبَنِينَ وَجَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ أَكْثَرَ نَفِيرًا (٦)
Then We gave you back the turn [to prevail] over them, and We aided you with children and wealth, and made you greater in number, (6)
إِنْ أَحْسَنتُمْ أَحْسَنتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ ۖ وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَهَا ۚ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لِيَسُـۥٓـُٔوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ وَلِيَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ كَمَا دَخَلُوهُ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَلِيُتَبِّرُوا۟ مَا عَلَوْا۟ تَتْبِيرًا (٧)
[saying,] ‘If you do good, you will do good to your [own] souls, and if you do evil, it will be [evil] for them.’ So when the occasion for the other [prophecy] comes, they will make your faces wretched, and enter the Temple just as they entered it the first time, and destroy utterly whatever they come upon. (7)
عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يَرْحَمَكُمْ ۚ وَإِنْ عُدتُّمْ عُدْنَا ۘ وَجَعَلْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ حَصِيرًا (٨)
Maybe your Lord will have mercy on you, but if you revert, We [too] will revert, and We have made hell a prison for the faithless. (8)

(Commentary)

It is evident from the context of the opening verses of this surah that they are aimed at explaining that the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the established way of Allah — regarding human nations has proceeded by guiding them toward the path of servitude (العبودية) and the way of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. He enabled them to reach this by their own choice, granting them the blessings of this world and the Hereafter, and providing them with the means for both obedience and disobedience. Thus, if they obeyed and did good, He rewarded them with the felicity of this world and the Hereafter; and if they did evil and disobeyed, He recompensed them with the exemplary punishment (نكال) of this world and the torment of the Hereafter.

Accordingly, these seven verses serve as a paradigm illustrating how this general sunnah was applied to the Children of Israel. Allah sent down the Book to their Prophet and made it a guidance (هدى) for them by which they might be guided. He decreed for them therein that they would surely rise high, transgress, and act wickedly; consequently, Allah would take vengeance upon them by allowing their enemies to overpower them through humiliation, slaughter, and captivity. Then, they would return to obedience, and the Exalted would return to them with blessing and mercy. Subsequently, they would rise high and transgress a second time, and Allah would take vengeance upon them a second time with a punishment more severe than the first. Finally, it is hoped that their Lord will have mercy on them, but if they return [to transgression], He will return [to punishment].

From this, it is concluded that these seven verses serve as a preamble (توطئة) for what will be mentioned afterward regarding the application of this general sunnah to this [Muslim] nation. These seven verses are like a parenthetical statement (المعترضة) between the first and the ninth verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلْنَاهُ هُدًى لِّبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ أَلَّا تَتَّخِذُوا مِن دُونِي وَكِيلًا — We gave Moses the Book, and made it a guidance for the Children of Israel, [saying,] ‘Do not take any guardian besides Me’ (17:2). The "Book" (الكتاب) is frequently applied in the speech of the Exalted to the totality of the legislated laws (الشرائع) prescribed for people to judge between them in their differences regarding belief and action. Thus, it indicates that it contains the doctrinal and practical duties they must adopt and embody. Perhaps for this reason, He said, "We gave Moses the Book," and did not say "the Torah," to indicate its inclusion of the laws made obligatory upon them.

By this, it becomes clear that His saying, "and made it a guidance for the Children of Israel," is in the position of an explanation for His giving of the Book. Its being a guidance (هدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — means it was a clarifier for them of the laws of their Lord, which, had they adopted and acted upon them, would have guided them to the truth and secured for them the felicity of both abodes.

Regarding His saying, أَلَّا تَتَّخِذُوا مِن دُونِي وَكِيلًا — ‘Do not take any guardian besides Me’, the particle an (أن) [implied or explicit in some readings] is for explanation (تفسيرية), and what follows it is the sum total of what the Book contains, which was made a guidance for them. Thus, the meaning resolves to: the essence of what the Book clarified for them and guided them toward was His prohibition against them associating anything with Allah or taking any guardian (وكيلاً) — one who manages affairs and is relied upon — besides Him. Therefore, His saying, "Do not take any guardian besides Me," is an explanation of His saying, "and made it a guidance for the Children of Israel," if the pronoun in "do not take" refers to them, as is apparent. It would be an explanation for all that preceded it if the pronoun is understood to refer to both Moses and the Children of Israel together.

In this sentence, there is a shift (التفات) from the first-person plural ("We") to the first-person singular ("Me"). The reason for this is to clarify that while the first-person plural is used for the purpose of glorification (التعظيم), maintaining that context—such as saying "do not take any guardians besides Us"—would not suit the meaning of monotheism (التوحيد) for which the sentence was intended. Therefore, the phrasing shifted to the first-person singular. Then, when the need for this shift passed, the speech returned to its previous context, as in: ذُرِّيَّةَ مَنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ — [O] descendants of those whom We carried [in the ark] with Noah.

The reason taking a guardian (الوكيل) besides Allah amounts to polytheism (الشرك) is that a guardian is the one who ensures the rectification of the essential affairs of the client and undertakes the fulfillment of his needs. This is Allah, the Glorified; thus, taking another as a lord is taking a guardian besides Him.

His saying, the Exalted: ذُرِّيَّةَ مَنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ إِنَّهُ كَانَ عَبْدًا شَكُورًا — [O] descendants of those whom We carried [in the ark] with Noah. Indeed he was a grateful servant (17:3). The term descendants (الذرية) is applied to offspring with the consideration of them being young and attached to their fathers. According to what the context suggests, it is in the accusative case for the purpose of specialization (الاختصاص). This specialization implies a particular concern from the Speaker in His judgment, serving as a justification (التعليل), similar to His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنكُمُ الرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ — Indeed Allah desires to repel all impurity from you, O People of the Household (33:33), meaning: He does this for you because you are the People of the Prophetic Household.

Thus, His saying, "descendants of those whom We carried with Noah," provides the benefit of justification for what preceded it, just as His saying, "Indeed he was a grateful servant," provides the benefit of justification for it.

As for the first, it is because the apparent concern for them stems from the beautiful promise Allah previously made to the people of Noah's ark when He saved them from the Flood and commanded Noah to descend, saying: يَا نُوحُ اهْبِطْ بِسَلَامٍ مِّنَّا وَبَرَكَاتٍ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰ أُمَمٍ مِّمَّن مَّعَكَ ۚ وَأُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ ثُمَّ يَمَسُّهُم مِّنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ — ‘O Noah, descend with peace from Us, and blessings upon you and upon nations [to issue] from those who are with you. But [there will be other] nations whom We shall provide for, then a painful punishment from Us will visit them’ (11:48). Thus, His sending down the Book to Moses and making it a guidance for the Children of Israel was a fulfillment of the good promise previously made to their ancestors among the people of the ark, proceeding according to the divine sunnah applied to nations. It is as if it were said: We sent down the Book to Moses and made it a guidance for the Children of Israel because they are the descendants of those We carried with Noah, and We had promised them peace, blessings, and provision.

As for the second, it is because this sunnah—namely, the sunnah of guidance and direction and the method of calling to monotheism—is the very same sunnah that Noah (peace be upon him) was the first to establish in the human world. By doing so, he showed gratitude for Allah's blessing and was sincere to Him in servitude (العبودية). It has been mentioned repeatedly that the reality of gratitude (الشكر) necessitates sincerity in servitude. Thus, Allah thanked him, made his sunnah endure as long as the world remains, sent peace upon him among all the worlds, and rewarded him for every good word and righteous deed until the Day of Resurrection, as He, the Exalted, said: وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْبَاقِينَ * وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ * سَلَامٌ عَلَىٰ نُوحٍ فِي الْعَالَمِينَ * إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ — And We made his descendants the survivors, and left for him [a good name] among posterity. ‘Peace be to Noah throughout the worlds!’ Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous (37:77-80).

The meaning of the two verses can be summarized as follows: We rewarded Noah for being a grateful servant to Us by preserving his call and applying his sunnah and way among the descendants of those We carried with him in the ark; and part of that was Our sending down the Book to Moses and making it a guidance for the Children of Israel.

It appears from His saying in the verse, "descendants of those whom We carried with Noah," and His saying, "And We made his descendants the survivors," that all people are the descendants of Noah (peace be upon him) through both sons and daughters. If the lineage ended with his sons only, and the meaning of "those whom We carried with Noah" were his sons only, it would have been better—indeed, necessary—to say "the descendants of Noah," which is obvious.

The commentators have other views regarding the grammatical analysis (إعراب) of the verse, such as the view that "descendants" (ذرية) is in the vocative case (منصوب على النداء) with the particle omitted, meaning "O descendants of those We carried." Others say it is the first object of "do not take" and "guardian" is the second object, meaning "do not take the descendants of those We carried with Noah as guardians besides Me." Others say it is a substitute (بدل) for Moses in the previous verse. These views are clearly weak.

Following these in weakness is the view that the pronoun in "Indeed he" (إنه) refers to Moses rather than Noah, and that the sentence is a justification for giving him the Book or for making him (peace be upon him) a guidance for the Children of Israel, based on the pronoun in "and We made it" (وجعلناه) referring to Moses rather than the Book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَضَيْنَا إِلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ فِي الْكِتَابِ لَتُفْسِدُنَّ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَّتَيْنِ وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا — We revealed to the Children of Israel in the Book: ‘You will surely cause corruption on the earth twice, and you will surely rise to [a state of] great arrogance’ (17:4). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Decreeing (القضاء) is the finalization of a matter, whether by word or by deed. Each of these is of two types: divine and human. Among the divine word is His saying: وَقَضَىٰ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ — Your Lord has decided that you shall not worship anyone but Him (17:23), meaning He commanded it. And He said: وَقَضَيْنَا إِلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ فِي الْكِتَابِ — We revealed to the Children of Israel in the Book; this is a decree by informing and finalization in judgment, meaning We informed them and revealed to them a decisive revelation. Based on this is: وَقَضَيْنَا إِلَيْهِ ذَٰلِكَ الْأَمْرَ أَنَّ دَابِرَ هَٰؤُلَاءِ مَقْطُوعٌ — We revealed to him this matter: that the last remnant of these [people] will be cut off (15:66)."

"Among the divine deed is His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَقْضِي بِالْحَقِّ ۖ وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ لَا يَقْضُونَ بِشَيْءٍ — Allah judges with justice, while those they invoke besides Him do not judge with anything (40:20), and His saying: فَقَضَاهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَاوَاتٍ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ — Then He finished them as seven heavens in two days (41:12), referring to His creative origination (إيجاده الإبداعي) and its completion, similar to: بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — Originator of the heavens and the earth (2:117)."

He continued: "Among the human word is when a judge decrees such-and-such, for the judge's ruling is by word. Among the human deed is: فَإِذَا قَضَيْتُم مَّنَاسِكَكُمْ — And when you have finished your rites (2:200), and ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا تَفَثَهُمْ وَلْيُوفُوا نُذُورَهُمْ — Then let them finish with their self-neglect and fulfill their vows (22:29)." End of the relevant quote.

Rising high (العلو) means elevation, and in the verse, it is a metaphor (كناية) for transgression (الطغيان) through injustice and aggression. This is evidenced by its being coupled with "causing corruption" (الإفساد) as an explanatory conjunction. In this sense is His saying: إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلَا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلَ أَهْلَهَا شِيَعًا — Indeed Pharaoh tyrannized in the land and reduced its people to factions (28:4).

The meaning of the verse is: We informed and made known to the Children of Israel with a decisive report in the Book—which is the Torah—swearing and confirming this statement: You, the people of Israel, will surely cause corruption on the earth—which is the land of Palestine and its surroundings—twice, one after the other, and you will surely rise to a state of great arrogance and transgress with immense tyranny.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا جَاءَ وَعْدُ أُولَاهُمَا بَعَثْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ عِبَادًا لَّنَا — So when the first of the two promises came to pass, We roused against you some of Our servants... (17:5) etc. Al-Raghib said: al-Bu’s (البؤس), al-Ba’s (البأس), and al-Ba’sa’ (البأساء) all mean severity and hardship, except that al-bu’s is more common in poverty and war, while al-ba’s and al-ba’sa’ are used for inflicting harm, as in: وَاللَّهُ أَشَدُّ بَأْسًا وَأَشَدُّ تَنكِيلًا — Allah is more severe in might and more severe in exemplary punishment (4:84). End of the relevant quote.

In al-Majma‘: "Probing (الجوس) means penetrating through the houses. It is said: I left so-and-so probing (يجوس) the children of so-and-so, meaning treading upon them. Abu Ubayd said: Every place you have mixed with and trodden upon, you have husta (حسته) and justa (جسته) it. It is also said: Probing (الجوس) is seeking something with thoroughness." End quote.

His saying, "So when the first of the two promises came to pass," is a derivation (تفريع) from His saying, "You will surely cause corruption," etc. The dual pronoun refers to the two times, which are the two instances of corruption. Thus, it refers to the first instance of corruption. The "promise of the first of them" refers to what Allah promised them of exemplary punishment and vengeance for their corruption; thus, "promise" (وعد) means the "promised event" (موعود). The "coming of the promise" is a metaphor for the time of its fulfillment. This indicates that He promised them two punishments for their two instances of corruption, though they were not explicitly mentioned as fulfillments. It is as if it were said: You will surely cause corruption on the earth twice, and We promise you vengeance for each of them; so when the promise of the first time came... all this is supported by the context.

His saying, بَعَثْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ عِبَادًا لَّنَا أُولِي بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ — We roused against you some of Our servants of great might, means We stirred them up and sent them against you to humiliate you and take vengeance upon you. The evidence that this "rousing" (البعث) was for vengeance and humiliation is His saying, "of great might," etc.

There is no harm in considering their coming to the Children of Israel—despite the massive slaughter, captivity, enslavement, looting, and destruction—as a divine rousing (بعثاً إلهياً), because it was by way of recompense (المجازاة) for their corruption on the earth and their arrogance and wrongful rebellion. Thus, Allah did not wrong them by rousing their enemies and supporting them against them; rather, they were the ones who wronged themselves.

By this, it becomes clear that there is no evidence in the text for the view of those who say that the meaning of "We roused against you" refers to Our commanding a group of believers to fight and wage jihad against you, based on the apparent meaning of "We roused" and "servants." This is because, as you have seen, there is no obstacle to calling this a divine rousing since it was by way of recompense. Similarly, there is no obstacle to calling the disbelievers "servants" (عباداً) of Allah, especially when followed by His saying, "of great might."

Similar to this is the view of those who say: It is possible that these roused ones were believers whom Allah commanded to wage jihad against these [Israelites], or it is possible they were disbelievers whom a prophet from among the prophets rallied to fight them, setting them upon their like among the disbelievers and the wicked. The same rebuttal applies to this as to the previous view.

His saying, وَكَانَ وَعْدًا مَّفْعُولًا — and it was a promise bound to be fulfilled, is an emphasis that the decree was a necessary certainty. The meaning is: So when the time of the promise came, which We promised for the first of your two instances of corruption, We roused and stirred up against you some people, servants of Ours, of great might and severity. They entered your land by force and conquest, penetrated through your dwellings, humiliated you, and took away your independence, arrogance, and sovereignty; and it was a promise bound to be fulfilled, from which there was no escape.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَا لَكُمُ الْكَرَّةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَمْدَدْنَاكُم بِأَمْوَالٍ وَبَنِينَ وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ أَكْثَرَ نَفِيرًا — Then We gave you back the turn [to prevail] over them, and We aided you with wealth and children, and made you greater in numbers (17:6). It is said in al-Majma‘: "The turn (الكرة) means the return and the state (الدولة). Numbers (النفير) refers to the number of men. Al-Zajjaj said: It may be the plural of nafar (نفر), just as ‘abid (العبيد) is used. A person's nafar, nafrah, and nafir—and those he competes with (nafarahu)—are his kinsmen who support him and go forth (yanfirun) with him." End quote.

The meaning of the verse is clear. It suggests that the Children of Israel would have the state return to them over their enemies after the promise of the first time; they would overcome them, conquer them, and free themselves from their enslavement and bondage. This state would return to them gradually over a significant period of time, as necessitated by the aiding with wealth and children and making them greater in numbers.

In the following verse, His saying: إِنْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَها — If you do good, you will do good to your own souls, and if you do evil, it will be [to their detriment], there is an indication—indeed, a proof supported by the context—that this event, namely the return of the turn to the Children of Israel over their enemies, only occurred because of their return to doing good (الإحسان) after they had tasted the evil consequence of their previous wrongdoing. Similarly, the fulfillment of the "latter promise" (وعد الآخرة) only occurred because of their return to doing evil a second time after this return to doing good.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَهَا — If you do good, you will do good to your own souls, and if you do evil, it will be [to their detriment] (17:7). The lam (لـ) in "to your own souls" (لأنفسكم) and "to them" (فلها) is for specialization (الاختصاص), meaning that each of your good deeds and evil deeds is specialized to your own souls without affecting others. This is the established sunnah of Allah: that the effect and consequence of an action return to its doer, whether good or evil. It is like His saying: تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ ۖ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَلَكُم مَّا كَسَبْتُمْ — That was a nation that has passed: for it is what it earned, and for you is what you earn (2:141).

The context is one of explaining that the effect of an action belongs to its doer, whether good or evil. It is not a context for explaining that doing good benefits the doer while doing evil harms him—in which case it would have been said "and if you do evil, it is against them" (فعليها), as in: لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ — For it is what it has earned, and against it is what it has incurred (2:286).

Thus, there is no need for the strained interpretations of some who say the lam in "and if you do evil, it will be to them" (فلها) means "against" (على), or others who say it means "toward" (إلى) because doing evil is transitive with it (one says "he did evil toward so-and-so"), or others who say it is for entitlement (الاستحقاق), as in: وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ — and for them is a painful punishment (2:10).

An objection might be raised against the lam being for specialization, arguing that reality is contrary to this: often the effect of a good deed reaches someone other than the doer, and the effect of an evil deed reaches someone other than the perpetrator.

The answer is that this stems from an oversight regarding how the Quran views the effects of actions. As for the effects of actions in the Hereafter, they absolutely do not transcend the doer; the Exalted said: مَن كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَلِأَنفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ — Whoever disbelieves, his disbelief will be to his own detriment, and those who act righteously, they prepare for their own souls (30:44). As for worldly effects, actions do not produce an effect in anyone other than the doer unless Allah wills something of that by way of blessing upon another, or vengeance, or trial and examination. It is not within the power of the doer to always make the effect of his action reach another, except occasionally when Allah wills it; however, the doer is always reached by the effect of his good or evil action without fail.

Thus, the doer of good has a share of his good deed, and the doer of evil has a share of his evil deed. The Exalted said: فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُ * وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ — So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it (99:7-8). Thus, the effect of the action does not leave its doer for another. This is the meaning of what is narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) that he said: "I have never done good to anyone nor have I done evil to anyone," and then he recited this verse.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا جَاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ لِيَسُوءُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ وَلِيَدْخُلُوا الْمَسْجِدَ كَمَا دَخَلُوهُ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَلِيُتَبِّرُوا مَا عَلَوْا تَتْبِيرًا — So when the last of the two promises came to pass, [We roused them] to sadden your faces, and to enter the Temple just as they had entered it the first time, and to destroy utterly whatever they overran (17:7). Utter destruction (التتبير) means annihilation, from tabar (تبار) meaning ruin and destruction.

His saying, لِيَسُوءُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ — to sadden your faces, is from al-masa’ah (المساءة). It is said: Zayd sa’a (ساء) so-and-so when he grieved him. It is, as has been said, connected to an omitted verb for the sake of brevity, and the lam is for purpose (للغاية). The implied meaning is: We roused them to sadden your faces through the appearance of grief and gloom and the manifestation of the marks of humiliation, wretchedness, and the smallness of enslavement upon them, due to the massive slaughter, captivity, and looting they would commit against you.

His saying, وَلِيَدْخُلُوا الْمَسْجِدَ كَمَا دَخَلُوهُ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ — and to enter the Temple just as they had entered it the first time, the "Temple" (المسجد) refers to al-Masjid al-Aqsa—the House of the Sanctuary (بيت المقدس). No weight should be given to what some have mentioned that it refers to the entire Holy Land metaphorically. The speech indicates, first, that in the promise of the first time they also entered the Temple by force, though it was not mentioned before for the sake of brevity; second, that their entry into the Temple was for the purpose of desecration and destruction; and third, the speech suggests that these roused attackers, sent to recompense the Children of Israel and take vengeance upon them, were the same ones who were roused against them the first time.

His saying, وَلِيُتَبِّرُوا مَا عَلَوْا تَتْبِيرًا — and to destroy utterly whatever they overran, means to annihilate what they overcame—killing people, burning property, demolishing buildings, and ruining the land. It is also possible that ma (ما) is infinitive (مصدرية) with an omitted possessive, meaning: "and to destroy utterly during the period of their dominance." The first meaning is closer to understanding and more in accord with the context.

A comparison between the two promises—namely, His saying, "We roused against you some of Our servants," etc., and His saying, "to sadden your faces," etc.—shows that the second was more severe and bitter for the Children of Israel; they were nearly wiped out and annihilated therein. Sufficient for this is His saying, the Exalted: "and to destroy utterly whatever they overran."

The meaning is: So when the time of the latter promise came—which is the second of the two instances of corruption—We roused them to sadden your faces with the appearance of grief and gloom and the manifestation of humiliation and wretchedness, and to enter al-Masjid al-Aqsa just as they had entered it the first time, and to annihilate what they overcame and destroy what they passed over with utter destruction and annihilation.

His saying, the Exalted: عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يَرْحَمَكُمْ ۚ وَإِنْ عُدتُّمْ عُدْنَا ۘ وَجَعَلْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَافِرِينَ حَصِيرًا — Maybe your Lord will have mercy on you, but if you return, We will [also] return, and We have made hell a prison for the faithless (17:8). Prison (الحصير) is from al-hasr (الحصر), which—according to what they have mentioned—means restriction and confinement. The Exalted said: وَاحْصُرُوهُمْ — and besiege them (9:5), meaning restrict them.

His saying, "Maybe your Lord will have mercy on you," means after the second rousing, as the context suggests. It is a hope for mercy on the condition that they repent and return to obedience and doing good, as evidenced by His saying: "but if you return, We will [also] return," meaning: if you return to corruption and arrogance after you had desisted and your Lord had mercy on you, We will return to punishment and exemplary penalty; and We have made Hell a prison and a place of confinement for the disbelievers from which they cannot escape.

In His saying, "Maybe your Lord will have mercy on you," there is a shift from the first-person plural to the third-person. The reason for this seems to be an indication that the principle necessitated by His Lordship (ربوبيته), the Exalted, is to have mercy on His servants if they proceed according to what their creation necessitates and their primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides them to, unless they deviate from the line of creation and exit the path of the primordial nature. Alluding to this point requires mentioning the attribute of "Lord" (الرب), so the context needed to change from the first-person plural to the third-person. Then, when the point was fully made with His saying, "Maybe your Lord will have mercy on you," the speech returned to its previous context.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babawayh with his chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "Noah was only named a grateful servant because he used to say when he reached evening and morning: 'O Allah, I call You to witness that whatever blessing or well-being in religion or world has come to me in the evening or morning, it is from You alone, You have no partner; to You belongs praise and to You belongs thanks for it until You are pleased and after pleasure.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated with slight variations through several chains in al-Kafi and the tafsirs of al-Qummi and al-Ayyashi.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Abu Fatima that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "Noah (peace be upon him) would not carry anything small or large except that he said: 'In the Name of Allah' and 'Praise be to Allah'; so Allah named him a grateful servant."

I say: These narrations do not contradict the previous interpretation of gratitude as sincerity. It is well known that his supplication only stemmed from his realization of the reality of what he supplicated, and that is inseparable from sincerity in servitude.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Qulawayh with his chain of narration from Salih ibn Sahl from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَقَضَيْنَا إِلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ فِي الْكِتَابِ لَتُفْسِدُنَّ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَّتَيْنِ — "He said: 'The killing of the Commander of the Faithful and the stabbing of al-Hasan ibn Ali (peace be upon them).' وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا — 'He said: The killing of al-Husayn (peace be upon him).' فَإِذَا جَاءَ وَعْدُ أُولَاهُمَا — 'He said: When the victory for al-Husayn comes.' بَعَثْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ عِبَادًا لَّنَا أُولِي بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ فَجَاسُوا خِلَالَ الدِّيَارِ — 'A people whom Allah will rouse before the rising of the Qā’im; they will not leave any blood-debt (وتر) for the family of Muhammad except that they take it.' وَكَانَ وَعْدًا مَّفْعُولًا."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, and they are aimed at applying the events that occur in this nation to what occurred in the Children of Israel, confirming what has been widely transmitted from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) that this nation will surely follow the path of the Children of Israel, step by step and arrow by arrow, such that if they entered a lizard's hole, these people would enter it too. These narrations are not intended as the literal interpretation (تفسير) of the verses; one evidence for this is the variation in the applications found within them.

As for the original story contained in the noble verses, the narrations regarding it have differed in an extraordinary manner that strips them of reliability. For this reason, we have omitted them here; whoever desires them should refer to the compendiums of hadith from both the Sunnis and the Shi'a.

Since the Children of Israel became independent in sovereignty and leadership, many significant calamities have befallen them—more than two, according to what their history records. It is possible to apply what these verses contain to two of them. However, what is almost accepted among them is that one of these two punishments referred to by the verses is what occurred at the hands of Nebuchadnezzar (Bakht Nasr), one of the kings of Babylon, approximately six centuries before the Common Era.

He was a king of power and might among the tyrants of his time. He used to protect the Children of Israel, but they disobeyed and rebelled against him. So he marched against them with armies they could not withstand and besieged their cities, then conquered them by force. He ruined the land, destroyed al-Masjid al-Aqsa, burned the Torah and the books of the prophets, and annihilated the people in a general slaughter. Only a small remnant of women, children, and weak men remained; he took them captive and marched them with him to Babylon. They remained there, with no protector to protect them nor any defender to repel harm from them, throughout the life of Nebuchadnezzar and for a long time after him, until Cyrus (Koresh), one of the great kings of the Persians, targeted Babylon and conquered it. He showed kindness to the captives of the Children of Israel and permitted them to return to the Holy Land. He aided them in rebuilding the Temple—al-Masjid al-Aqsa—and renewing the buildings, and he authorized Ezra, one of their priests, to write the Torah for them. This was in the year 450-plus before the Common Era.

What appears from the history of the Jews is that the one roused first to destroy the House of the Sanctuary was Nebuchadnezzar, and it remained in ruins for seventy years. The one roused second was the Roman Emperor Vespasian, who sent his general Titus against them; he destroyed the House and humiliated the people approximately a century before the Common Era [sic - Titus destroyed the Temple in 70 CE].

It is not far-fetched that these two incidents are what are intended in the verses, for other events did not annihilate their gathering nor take away their kingdom and independence entirely. However, the calamity of Nebuchadnezzar took away all of them and their sovereignty until the time of Cyrus; then their ranks were gathered for a period after him, then the Romans overcame them and took away their power and might, and they remained in that state until the time of the appearance of Islam.

The only thing that makes this far-fetched is what was previously alluded to in the commentary of the verses: that there is an indication therein that the ones roused against the Children of Israel in the first and second times were the same people, and that His saying, "Then We gave you back the turn [to prevail] over them," suggests that the turn was for the Children of Israel over the very people who were roused against them first, and that His saying, "So when the latter promise came to pass, to sadden your faces," suggests the return of the plural pronoun to what preceded in His saying, "some of Our servants."

However, this is an indication without a clear proof, for it is possible that the "turn" refers to a turn for someone other than the Children of Israel over their enemies from which they benefited, and that the plural pronoun refers to what the speech indicates by its context, without the context necessitating that the ones roused second be the same as those roused first.

Ayahs

إِنَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ يَهْدِى لِلَّتِى هِىَ أَقْوَمُ وَيُبَشِّرُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا كَبِيرًا (٩)
Indeed this Quran guides to what is most upright, and gives the good news to the faithful who do righteous deeds that there is a great reward for them. (9)
وَأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٠)
As for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have prepared a painful punishment for them. (10)
وَيَدْعُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ بِٱلشَّرِّ دُعَآءَهُۥ بِٱلْخَيْرِ ۖ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَجُولًا (١١)
Man prays for ill as [avidly as] he prays for good, and man is overhasty. (11)
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ءَايَتَيْنِ ۖ فَمَحَوْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَجَعَلْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلنَّهَارِ مُبْصِرَةً لِّتَبْتَغُوا۟ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَلِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ ۚ وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ فَصَّلْنَـٰهُ تَفْصِيلًا (١٢)
We made the night and the day two signs. Then We faded out the sign of the night, and made the sign of the day lightsome, so that you may seek from your Lord’s bounty and that you may know the number of years and calculation [of time], and We have elaborated everything in detail. (12)
وَكُلَّ إِنسَـٰنٍ أَلْزَمْنَـٰهُ طَـٰٓئِرَهُۥ فِى عُنُقِهِۦ ۖ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ كِتَـٰبًا يَلْقَىٰهُ مَنشُورًا (١٣)
We have attached every person’s omen to his neck, and We shall bring it out for him on the Day of Resurrection as a book that he will find wide open. (13)
ٱقْرَأْ كِتَـٰبَكَ كَفَىٰ بِنَفْسِكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيبًا (١٤)
‘Read your book! Today your soul suffices as your own reckoner.’ (14)
مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا (١٥)
Whoever is guided is guided only for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, goes astray only to its detriment. No bearer shall bear another’s burden. We do not punish [any community] until We have sent [it] an apostle. (15)
وَإِذَآ أَرَدْنَآ أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا۟ فِيهَا فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا ٱلْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَـٰهَا تَدْمِيرًا (١٦)
And when We desire to destroy a town We command its affluent ones [to obey Allah]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly. (16)
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ نُوحٍ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ بِذُنُوبِ عِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا (١٧)
How many generations We have destroyed since Noah! Your Lord is sufficient as [a witness who is] well aware and percipient of His servants’ sins. (17)
مَّن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنَا لَهُۥ فِيهَا مَا نَشَآءُ لِمَن نُّرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا لَهُۥ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَىٰهَا مَذْمُومًا مَّدْحُورًا (١٨)
Whoever desires this transitory life, We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire. Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blameful and spurned. (18)
وَمَنْ أَرَادَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَسَعَىٰ لَهَا سَعْيَهَا وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَانَ سَعْيُهُم مَّشْكُورًا (١٩)
Whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with an endeavour worthy of it, should he be faithful,—the endeavour of such will be well-appreciated. (19)
كُلًّا نُّمِدُّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مِنْ عَطَآءِ رَبِّكَ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ عَطَآءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا (٢٠)
To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined. (20)
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَلَلْـَٔاخِرَةُ أَكْبَرُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ وَأَكْبَرُ تَفْضِيلًا (٢١)
Observe how We have given some of them an advantage over some others; yet the Hereafter is surely greater in respect of ranks and greater in respect of relative merit. (21)
لَّا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتَقْعُدَ مَذْمُومًا مَّخْذُولًا (٢٢)
Do not set up another god besides Allah, or you will sit blameworthy, forsaken. (22)

(Commentary)

The preceding group of verses described the unfolding of the Divine Sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the established way of Allah — in guiding humanity toward the path of truth and the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. It then detailed the felicity of those who respond to the true call in this world and the Hereafter, and the punishment of those who disbelieve in the truth and transgress against the command in their worldly life and their final end. The mention of the revelation of the Torah and what subsequently befell the Children of Israel served as an example cited within the discourse to apply the universal rule (الحكم الكلي) to its individual instances and manifestations (مصاديقه). This current group of verses mentions the unfolding of the aforementioned Sunnah in this Ummah (الأمة) — the community of believers — just as it unfolded in the community of Moses. A conclusion has been drawn from the verses regarding the necessity of avoiding polytheism (الشرك) and the obligation of adhering to the path of monotheism, where it was said: لا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ فَتَقْعُدَ مَذْمُوماً مَخْذُولاً — "Do not set up another god besides Allah, or you will sit blamed and forsaken." (17:22)

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَهْدِي لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ — "Indeed this Quran guides to that which is most upright" (17:9); meaning to the creed (الملة) that is most upright, as the Exalted said: قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدانِي رَبِّي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ دِيناً قِيَماً مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً — "Say, ‘Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path, the upright religion, the creed of Abraham, a Hanif...’" (6:161).

The term most upright (الأقوم) is a superlative form (أفعل تفضيل). The root of the word is "standing" (القيام), the opposite of "sitting" (القعود), which is one of the states and postures of a human being. It is the most balanced of his states, through which he gains mastery over the actions he intends to perform, unlike sitting, lying on one's back, or lying prostrate. It was then used metaphorically to denote one's excellent management of affairs when one is capable of them without incapacity or fatigue, and one's optimal administration of them toward their goal. It is said: "He stood by such an affair" (قام بأمر كذا) when he took charge of it, and "He stood over such an affair" (قام على أمر كذا) meaning he monitored it, preserved it, and looked after its condition in a manner that suits it.

Allah, the Glorified, has described this primordial creed (الملة الحنيفية) with "standing" (القيام), as He said: فأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ — "So set your heart on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature of Allah which He has created in mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion..." (30:30), and He said: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ الْقَيِّمِ — "So set your heart on the upright religion..." (30:43).

This is because this religion is a guardian (مهيمناً) over that which contains the good of their worldly life and their Hereafter, standing (قيماً) over the rectification of their state in their livelihood and their return to Allah. This is only because it is in accordance with what is necessitated by the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and the creation upon which Allah, the Glorified, fashioned him and equipped him accordingly with that which guides him to the goal intended for him and the felicity prepared for his sake.

Accordingly, the description of this creed in His saying: لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ — "to that which is most upright" — as being "most upright," if compared to all other creeds, is from the perspective that each of those creeds is a life-system (سنة حيوية) adopted by people to benefit from in some of their life affairs. However, if it benefits them in some aspects, it harms them in others; and if it secures for them a portion of what accords with their whims, it causes them to miss a great portion of what constitutes their true good. It is only Islam that stands over their lives and all that concerns them in this world and the Hereafter without anything being lost. Thus, the primordial creed is more upright (أقوم) than any other regarding human life.

If the comparison is made to the other previous Divine legislations (الشرائع الإلهية), such as the legislation of Noah, Moses, and Jesus (peace be upon them) — as is apparent from making it that which the Quran guides to, in contrast to the previous mention of the Torah and its being made a guidance for the Children of Israel — then it is from the perspective that this primordial creed is more perfect than the previous creeds contained in the books of the previous prophets. It encompasses of Divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) the utmost that the human constitution can bear, and of legislations that which leaves no individual or social human action unaddressed. The Exalted has said: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَمُهَيْمِناً عَلَيْهِ — "We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian over it." (5:48). Thus, that which the Quran guides to is more upright than that which other books guide to.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُبَشِّرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ الصَّالِحاتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْراً كَبِيراً — "and gives the good news to the faithful who do righteous deeds that there is a great reward for them" (17:9). "Righteous" (الصالحات) is an adjective whose noun is omitted for brevity; the implied meaning is "and they performed righteous deeds" (الأعمال الصالحات).

In the verse, a right (حق) is established for the believers who have attained faith and performed righteous deeds (الصالحات) — actions that are inherently good and performed in accordance with Divine command — upon Allah, the Glorified, as supported by naming it a "reward" (أجراً). This is also supported by His saying in another place: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there is for them an undiminished reward." (41:8). There is no problem in them having a right upon Allah if Allah, the Glorified, is the one who established it. A similar instance is His saying: ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كَذلِكَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نُنْجِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "Then We shall deliver Our apostles and those who have faith. Thus it is a duty incumbent upon Us to deliver the faithful." (10:103).

The focus in the verse is on clarifying the fulfilled promise (الوعد المنجز), just as the following verse is concerned with clarifying the fulfilled threat (الوعيد المنجز), which is the punishment for those who disbelieve in the Hereafter. As for the one who believes but does not perform righteous deeds, he is not among those who have a fulfilled reward and a fixed right upon Allah; rather, his matter is deferred (مراعى) pending repentance or intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — until he joins the company of the righteous among the believers. The Exalted said: وَآخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ خَلَطُوا عَمَلاً صالِحاً وَآخَرَ سَيِّئاً عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ — "And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins: they have mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil. Maybe Allah will accept their repentance." (9:102), and He said: وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ — "And [there are] others waiting for Allah’s decree: either He shall punish them, or He shall accept their repentance." (9:106).

Indeed, they possess a firmness upon the truth by virtue of their faith, as the Exalted said: وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ — "and give good news to those who have faith that they have a standing of sincerity with their Lord." (10:2), and He said: يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ — "Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter." (14:27).

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ أَعْتَدْنا لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً — "and that for those who do not believe in the Hereafter We have prepared a painful punishment." (17:10). "Preparation" (الاعتاد) means to make ready and set aside, derived from "equipment" (العتاد). According to what al-Raghib mentioned, it is the storing of something before the need for it arises, similar to "preparation" (الإعداد).

The apparent context (السياق) is that it is conjoined to His saying in the previous verse: أَنَّ لَهُمْ — "that for them..." — etc. Thus, the implied meaning is: "and He gives the good news to the believers who do righteous deeds that those who do not believe..." etc. This being "good news" for the believers is from the perspective that it is a Divine retribution against their enemies in religion.

The mention was specifically restricted to their lack of belief in the Hereafter, despite the possibility of their disbelieving in other things such as monotheism and prophethood, because the discourse is directed toward clarifying the effect that follows the upright religion. There is no place for religion, nor any benefit in it, alongside the denial of the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and final judgment — even if one were to acknowledge the oneness of the Lord, the Exalted, and other matters of gnosis. For this reason, the Glorified counted the forgetting of the Day of Reckoning as the root of all misguidance in His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ — "Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because of their forgetting the Day of Reckoning." (38:26).

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَدْعُ الْإِنْسانُ بِالشَّرِّ دُعاءَهُ بِالْخَيْرِ وَكانَ الْإِنْسانُ عَجُولاً — "Man prays for evil as [vehemently as] he prays for good, and man is ever hasty." (17:11). What is meant by "prayer" (الدعاء), as inferred from the context, is absolute seeking (مطلق الطلب), whether it be through the verbal form of prayer, such as saying: "O Allah, provide me with wealth and children," and so on, or without a verbal prayer, but rather through seeking and striving. All of that is a prayer and a request from Allah, whether the human being believes in it and is conscious of it or not, since there is no giver or withholder in reality except Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted said: يَسْئَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him." (55:29), and He said: وَآتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ — "And He gave you all that you had asked Him." (14:34). Thus, prayer is absolute seeking. The preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying بِالشَّرِّ (for evil) and بِالْخَيْرِ (for good) is for connection (للصلة), and the meaning is that man calls for evil and requests it just as he calls for good, requests it, and seeks it.

Accordingly, the meaning of man being "hasty" (عجولاً) is that he does not adopt deliberation (الأناة) when he desires something so that he might reflect and think upon the aspects of its benefit and harm until the face of good in the matter he desires becomes clear to him, and he then seeks it and strives toward it. Rather, he hastens in seeking it as soon as he remembers it and his whim attaches to it; thus, it may be evil and he is harmed by it, or it may be good and he benefits from it.

The verse and the verses following it are in the context of reproach and blame, branching out from what preceded regarding the Divine favor of guidance to that which is most upright. It is as if it were said: We have sent down a Book that guides to a creed that is most upright, leading those who take it to felicity and Paradise and bringing them to a great reward, and directing them to all good. However, the human species is hasty; due to his haste, he does not distinguish between good and evil, but rather seeks everything that appears to him and asks for everything that occurs to him and to which his whim attaches, without distinguishing between good and evil, or truth and falsehood. Thus, he pursues evil as he pursues good and rushes toward falsehood as he rushes toward truth.

It is not appropriate for him to be hasty and seek everything he desires and craves, nor is it right for him to commit everything he has the capacity to commit and perpetrate everything Allah has empowered him to do and enabled him for, based on the argument that it is from Divine facilitation and that had He willed, He would have prevented him. For these are the night and the day, two Divine signs (آيتان إلهيتان), and they are not of the same pattern. Rather, the sign of the night is "effaced" (ممحوة), wherein movements cease and eyes rest, and the sign of the day is "luminous" (مبصرة), wherein faculties awaken and people seek of the bounty of their Lord and through it know the number of years and the reckoning.

Similarly, the deeds of evil and good all exist in being by the permission of Allah, within the power of man through the empowerment of the Glorified. However, that is not a proof for the permissibility of man committing both evil and good, nor for him to be hasty and seek everything that appears to him, pursuing evil as he pursues good and committing disobedience as he commits obedience. Rather, it is obligatory upon him to take the deed of evil as "effaced" and thus not commit it, and the deed of good as "luminous" and thus perform it, seeking thereby the bounty of his Lord in the felicity of the Hereafter and the noble provision. For the deed of man is his "bird" (طائره) — his omen or destiny — which points him toward his felicity or wretchedness, and it is inseparable from him. Whatever he performs of good or evil does not leave him for another, nor is anyone else guided by it.

This is what the context provides for the meaning of the verse, and through it, several points become clear:

First: That the verse and the verses following it are directed toward the purpose of reproach and blame. This is the aspect of their connection, and the connection of what follows them, to what preceded in His saying: إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَهْدِي لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ — "Indeed this Quran guides to that which is most upright" — as we indicated just now. Thus, the Glorified blames man because, due to his innate disposition toward haste, he does not appreciate the blessing of Divine guidance as it deserves, nor does he distinguish between the creed that is most upright and others; thus, he prays for evil as he prays for good and intends wretchedness as he intends felicity.

Second: That what is meant by "man" (الإنسان) is the species, not specific individuals such as the disbelievers and polytheists, as has been suggested. By "prayer" (الدعاء) is meant absolute seeking, not the technical prayer, as has been suggested. By "good" and "evil" is meant that which contains the felicity of his life or its wretchedness in reality, not absolutely everything that harms or benefits, such as a person praying for the success and prosperity of one with whom he is pleased, and for the failure and loss of one with whom he is angry, and so on.

By "haste" (العجلة) is meant the human desire for what he loves to be realized quickly, not persistence and perseverance in seeking punishment and the disliked.

The commentators have a strange disagreement regarding the individual words of the verse, and confused statements in explaining the aspect of the connection of the verse, as well as the following verses, to what preceded. We have omitted mentioning them or delving into them due to the lack of benefit in addressing them; whoever desires them may refer to their books.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ آيَتَيْنِ فَمَحَوْنا آيَةَ اللَّيْلِ وَجَعَلْنا آيَةَ النَّهارِ مُبْصِرَةً — "We made the night and the day two signs. Then We effaced the sign of the night and made the sign of the day luminous..." (17:12) to the end of the verse. It is said in al-Majma': "Luminous (مبصرة) means radiant, illuminating, and bright. Abu Amr said: He intended that one sees by means of it, just as it is said: 'a sleeping night' (ليل نائم) and 'a hiding secret' (سر كاتم). Al-Kisa'i said: The Arabs say: 'The day became luminous' (أبصر النهار) when it shone." This is the end of the relevant portion.

The night and the day are the light and darkness that alternate upon the earth due to the sun being faced at its rising and its disappearance at its setting. Like everything else in the universe of the essences of things and their states, they are two signs (آيتان) of Allah, the Glorified, pointing by their very essence to His uniqueness in Lordship (الربوبية).

From here it becomes clear that what is meant by making them two signs is creating them as such, not that they were created and were not signs, then He made them signs and clothed them in the garment of indication. For all things are signs of Him, the Exalted, from the perspective of the origin of their existence and their being, which points to their Originator, not due to an accidental attribute that occurs to them.

From here it also becomes clear that what is meant by the "sign of the night" is the night itself, just as the "sign of the day" is the day itself — on the basis that the genitive construction (الإضافة) is explanatory (بيانية), not possessive (لامية). What is meant by the "effacing" of the night is its darkening and its being hidden from sight, in contrast to the day.

Thus, what some have mentioned — that the "sign of the night" refers to the moon and its "effacing" refers to the spots seen on its face, just as the "sign of the day" refers to the sun and its being made "luminous" refers to its disk being free from effacement and blackness — is not sound. For the discourse is about the "two signs," not the "two signs of the two signs." Furthermore, what was branched from that in His saying: لِتَبْتَغُوا فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ — "that you may seek the bounty of your Lord" — etc., is branched from the light of the day and the darkness of the night, not from what is seen of the spots on the face of the moon and the sun's disk being free from that.

Similar in its failure is the statement of some: that the "sign of the night" is its darkness and the "sign of the day" is its light, and the meaning of "effacing the sign of the night" is the effacing of its darkness by the light of the day, and similarly the effacing of the light of the day by the darkness of the night, and He merely sufficed with mentioning one of them because it points to the other.

The reason for its failure will not be hidden from you if you recall what we indicated previously: that the purpose is to clarify the existence of a difference between the two signs despite their sharing in being signs and indications. The meaning he mentioned nullifies this difference.

His saying: لِتَبْتَغُوا فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ — "that you may seek the bounty of your Lord" — is branched from His saying: وَجَعَلْنا آيَةَ النَّهارِ مُبْصِرَةً — "and We made the sign of the day luminous" — meaning We made it radiant so that you may seek therein provision from your Lord, for provision is His bounty and His gift, the Exalted.

Some mentioned that the implied meaning is: "that you may rest in the night and that you may seek the bounty of your Lord in the day," but "that you may rest in the night" was omitted because He mentioned it in other places. In this is the fact that such an implication contradicts the discourse being directed toward clarifying the consequence of effects upon one of the two signs and not the other, despite both being signs.

His saying: وَلِتَعْلَمُوا عَدَدَ السِّنِينَ وَالْحِسابَ — "and that you may know the number of years and the reckoning" — meaning that you may know, through the effacing of the night and the luminosity of the day, the number of years by making a number of days into a single unit upon which a contract is made, and that you may know thereby the reckoning of times and terms. The apparent context is that the knowledge of years and reckoning is branched from making the day luminous, similar to the branching of the seeking of provision upon it. This is because we only become conscious of non-existences (الأعدام) and absences from the direction of existences (الوجودات), not the other way around. Darkness is the absence of light; were it not for light, we would not transition to either light or darkness. Although we utilize both night and day in reckoning and distinguish each by the other outwardly, that which is existential (الوجودي) among them — namely the day — is that to which our sensation first attaches, then we become conscious of that which is non-existential (العدمي) among them — namely the night — by a kind of comparison. Such is the case with every existential thing and the non-existential thing compared to it.

Al-Razi mentioned in his Tafsir that it is better for the meaning of "effacing the sign of the night" — according to the view that it is the moon — to be the variation of light that occurs to the moon from one conjunction (المحاق) to the next, by increase and decrease night after night, because of the great effects it has on the seas, the deserts, and the temperaments of people.

The necessary consequence of this — as he indicated — is that His saying: لِتَبْتَغُوا فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ and His saying: وَلِتَعْلَمُوا عَدَدَ السِّنِينَ وَالْحِسابَ are both branched from the effacing of the sign of the night and the making of the sign of the day luminous together. The meaning would be: We made it so that you may seek your provisions through the illumination of the sun and the variation of the moon's light, and that you may also know thereby the years and the reckoning; for the sun is what distinguishes the day from the night, and the moon, through its varying phases, delineates the lunar months, and the months delineate the years. Thus, the "L" (لام) in the two phrases — namely "that you may seek" and "that you may know" — is connected to both verbs "We effaced" and "We made" together.

In this is the fact that the verse is in a context that does not suit making what was mentioned therein of the purpose a purpose resulting from both signs together — namely the effaced sign and the established sign. For you have known that the verses are in the context of reproach and blame, and the verse — namely His saying: وَجَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ آيَتَيْنِ — is like a response to what was assumed that man might argue with in his prayer for evil as he prays for good.

Its summary is: that man, because of his haste, does not pay attention to what Allah has sent down of the Book and guided him to of the creed that is most upright. Instead, he plunges into evil and seeks it as he seeks good, without reflecting upon his action or contemplating the aspect of benefit and corruption therein. Rather, he plunges into it as soon as his whim attaches to it and his power facilitates it. He relies in that upon his natural freedom in action, as if he were arguing that Allah empowered him for that and did not prevent him, as Allah narrated from the saying of the polytheists: لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ نَحْنُ وَلا آباؤُنا — "Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him—neither we nor our fathers..." (16:35).

So it was answered, after being presented in the context of reproach and blame, that the mere attachment of power and the validity of the act do not necessitate the permissibility of the action, nor does His empowering him for both good and evil together indicate the permissibility of plunging into evil like good. For the night and the day are two signs from the signs of Allah in which man lives, yet Allah, the Glorified, effaced the sign of the night and decreed therein stillness and dormancy, and made the sign of the day luminous and perceptive, wherein provision is sought and the number of years and the reckoning are known.

Just as the night and the day sharing in being signs does not necessitate their sharing in movements and fluctuations — for these are for the day specifically — similarly, the sharing of the deeds of good and evil in that they both are realized by the permission of Allah, the Glorified, and are among what Allah has empowered man for equally, does not necessitate the permissibility of his committing both and performing them on an equal footing. Rather, the permissibility of performance and commission is among the properties of the deed of good, not the deed of evil. Thus, it is not for man to follow every path that appears to him, nor to perform everything he craves and to which his whim attaches, relying in that upon what he has been given of natural freedom and Divine empowerment.

From what has preceded, the invalidity of what some mentioned — that the verse is directed toward arguing for monotheism (التوحيد) — becomes clear. For the night and the day, and the variation that occurs to them, and the blessings that result from that, are among the clearest signs of monotheism.
