Do not kill a soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause, and whoever is killed wrongfully, We have certainly given his heir an authority. But let him not commit any excess in killing [the murderer], for he has been assisted [by law]. (33)
وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ مَالَ ٱلْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِٱلْعَهْدِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْعَهْدَ كَانَ مَسْـُٔولًا (٣٤)
Do not approach the orphan’s property except in the best manner, until he comes of age. Fulfill the covenants; indeed all covenants are accountable. (34)
وَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ إِذَا كِلْتُمْ وَزِنُوا۟ بِٱلْقِسْطَاسِ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا (٣٥)
When you measure, observe fully the measure, [and] weigh with an even balance. That is better and more favourable with respect to the outcome [in the Hereafter]. (35)
وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْبَصَرَ وَٱلْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْـُٔولًا (٣٦)
Do not pursue that of which you have no knowledge. Indeed hearing, eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable. (36)
وَلَا تَمْشِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّكَ لَن تَخْرِقَ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَن تَبْلُغَ ٱلْجِبَالَ طُولًا (٣٧)
Do not walk exultantly on the earth. Indeed, you will neither pierce the earth, nor reach the mountains in height. (37)
كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ كَانَ سَيِّئُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّكَ مَكْرُوهًا (٣٨)
The evil of all these is detestable to your Lord. (38)
ذَٰلِكَ مِمَّآ أَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ رَبُّكَ مِنَ ٱلْحِكْمَةِ ۗ وَلَا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتُلْقَىٰ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَلُومًا مَّدْحُورًا (٣٩)
These are among [precepts] that your Lord has revealed to you of wisdom. Do not set up another god besides Allah, or you will be cast into hell, being blameworthy and banished [from His mercy]. (39)

(Commentary)

Allah, the Glorified, mentions a number of the universals of religion (كليات الدين), which follow His previous saying a few verses prior: إِنَّ هَذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَهْدِي لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ "Indeed this Quran guides to that which is most upright" (17:9).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَضَىٰ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ — "Your Lord has decided that you shall not worship anyone but Him" (17:23). The phrase أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا (that you shall not worship) etc., consists of a negation and an exception. The particle an (أن) is infinitival (مصدرية). It has also been suggested that it could be a prohibition and an exception, with an being either infinitival or explanatory (مفسرة). In any case, the combination of the excepted and the exception-from resolves into two sentences, such as our saying: "You worship Him and you do not worship other than Him." These two sentences return, in another sense, to a single ruling, which is the decree to worship Him with sincerity (إخلاص).

The "saying"—whether it is resolved into two sentences or returns to one—is the object of the "decision" (القضاء), which is the legislative decision (القضاء التشريعي) pertaining to rulings and legislative matters. It conveys the meaning of a decisive separation and a definitive, masterly decree (الحكم القاطع المولوي). Just as it pertains to commands, it pertains to prohibitions; and just as it firms up affirmative rulings, it firms up negative rulings. Had the word "command" (الأمر) been used—such as saying: "And your Lord commanded that you worship none but Him"—it would not be correct except through a type of interpretation (تأويل) or metaphorical usage (تجوز).

The command to be sincere in worship (إخلاص العبادة) to Allah, the Glorified, is the greatest of religious commands, and sincerity in worship is the most obligatory of obligations. Conversely, disobedience to it—which is polytheism (الشرك) with Allah, the Glorified—is the greatest of the destructive major sins. The Exalted says: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ — "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:48).

All acts of disobedience return to it upon analysis; for were it not for the obedience to other than Allah—from the devils among jinn and men, the caprice of the soul (هوى النفس), and ignorance—man would not venture into disobeying his Lord in what He commanded or forbade. Obedience is worship (العبادة). The Exalted says: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَا بَنِي آدَمَ أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطَانَ — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan’?" (36:60), and He says: أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلَٰهَهُ هَوَاهُ — "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god?" (45:23). Even the disbeliever who denies the Creator is a polytheist by handing over the reins of the world's governance to matter, nature, time, or otherwise, while he acknowledges the Creator, the Exalted, in the simplicity of his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being.

Due to the gravity of this ruling, He placed it before all other mentioned rulings of significant status, such as undutifulness to parents, withholding financial rights, squandering, killing children, adultery, killing a protected soul, consuming the orphan's wealth, breaking covenants, cheating in weights, and following that which is not based on knowledge. Then He concluded them by prohibiting polytheism a second time.

His saying, the Exalted: وَبِالْوَالِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا — "and [He has enjoined] kindness to parents" (17:23). This is coordinated with the preceding; meaning: "And your Lord has decided that you should show kindness to parents," or "that you be kind to parents." Kindness (الإحسان) in action is the opposite of maltreatment (الإساءة).

This, after monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — is among the most obligatory of obligations, just as undutifulness to them (عقوقهما) is the greatest of major sins after polytheism with Allah. Therefore, He mentioned it after the ruling of monotheism and prioritized it over the other mentioned and enumerated rulings, as He has done in several places in His speech.

It has been previously mentioned in the parallel verse of Surah al-An'am (verse 151) that the emotional bond mediating between the father and mother on one side and the child on the other is among the greatest pillars upon which human society stands. It is the natural means that keeps the spouses in a state of social union. Thus, it is mandatory, in view of the primordial social sunnah (السنة الاجتماعية الفطرية), that man respects his parents by honoring them and being kind to them. If this ruling were not practiced and human society abandoned it, the affection and bond of children to parents would vanish, and the contract of society would dissolve.

His saying, the Exalted: إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِندَكَ الْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهُمَا أَوْ كِلَاهُمَا فَلَا تَقُل لَّهُمَا أُفٍّ وَلَا تَنْهَرْهُمَا وَقُل لَّهُمَا قَوْلًا كَرِيمًا — "Should any of them or both of them reach old age with you, do not say to them, ‘Fie!’ and do not chide them, but speak to them noble words" (17:23). The word إِمَّا is composed of the conditional in (إن) and the extra ma (ما), which justifies the entry of the emphatic nun (نون التأكيد) upon the conditional verb. Old age (الكبر) refers to advanced years. أف (Fie) is a word expressing annoyance and irritation. Rebuking (النهر) is to chide with shouting, raising the voice, and harshness in speech.

The specification of the state of old age is because it is the most difficult state parents pass through, wherein they feel the need for the children's assistance and their fulfillment of life's duties which the parents are unable to perform. This is among the hopes parents have for their children while they are nursing and raising them in childhood, at a time when the children have no power over any of life's necessities and duties.

The verse indicates the obligation of honoring them and observing perfect etiquette in socializing and conversing with them at all times, especially at a time when their need for that intensifies—which is the time of reaching old age for one or both of them while with the child. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاخْفِضْ لَهُمَا جَنَاحَ الذُّلِّ مِنَ الرَّحْمَةِ وَقُل رَّبِّ ارْحَمْهُمَا كَمَا رَبَّيَانِي صَغِيرًا — "Lower the wing of humility to them out of mercy, and say, ‘My Lord! Have mercy on them, just as they reared me when I was [a] small [child]’" (17:24). Lowering the wing is a metaphor for exaggeration in humility and submissiveness in word and deed, taken from a bird lowering its wing so its mother will be compassionate and feed it. Therefore, He qualified it with "humility" (الذُّلِّ), for it is the habit of young birds when they want food from their mothers. Thus, the meaning is: face them in your socializing and conversing in a manner from which your humility, submissiveness, and self-abasement before them out of mercy for them is apparent.

This is if "humility" (الذل) is taken in the sense of lowliness (المسكنة). If it is in the sense of compliance (المطاوعة), then it is taken from the bird lowering its wing to gather its chicks under it out of mercy for them and to protect them.

His saying: وَقُل رَّبِّ ارْحَمْهُمَا كَمَا رَبَّيَانِي صَغِيرًا means: remember their rearing of you when you were small, and supplicate to Allah, the Glorified, to have mercy on them just as they had mercy on you and reared you when you were small.

It is said in al-Majma': "In this is an indication that the child's prayer for his deceased parent is heard; otherwise, there would be no meaning to the command for it." (End quote). What it indicates is that this prayer is in a position of likely acceptance, and it is a religious etiquette from which the child benefits even if it were assumed that his parents do not benefit from it. Furthermore, the reason for restricting the response of the prayer to the deceased parent is not apparent, as the verse is general (مطلقة).

His saying, the Exalted: رَّبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا فِي نُفُوسِكُمْ ۚ إِن تَكُونُوا صَالِحِينَ فَإِنَّهُ كَانَ لِلْأَوَّابِينَ غَفُورًا — "Your Lord knows best what is in your souls. If you are righteous, He is indeed all-forgiving to those who turn to Him often" (17:25). The context suggests that the verse is connected to what preceded it regarding the obligation of kindness to parents and the prohibition of undutifulness. Accordingly, it addresses cases where a lapse (بادرة) might occur from the child toward the parents in word or deed that causes them pain. It was not stated explicitly to indicate that such a thing should not even be mentioned, just as it should not occur.

So His saying: رَّبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا فِي نُفُوسِكُمْ means: He knows it better than you do. This is a preamble to what follows: إِن تَكُونُوا صَالِحِينَ (If you are righteous), thus confirming the meaning of righteousness; i.e., if you are righteous and Allah knows that from your souls, then He is... etc. His saying: فَإِنَّهُ كَانَ لِلْأَوَّابِينَ غَفُورًا means: to those who return to Him after every sin. This is an instance of placing a general statement in place of a specific one.

The meaning is: if you are righteous and Allah knows that from your souls, and you return and repent to Him regarding a lapse that appeared from you toward your parents, Allah will forgive you for that, for He is all-forgiving to those who turn to Him often.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتِ ذَا الْقُرْبَىٰ حَقَّهُ وَالْمِسْكِينَ وَابْنَ السَّبِيلِ — "Give the relative his right, and the needy and the traveller" (17:26). Discussion on this has preceded in its parallels. From the verse, it appears that giving to the relative, the needy, and the traveler was among what was legislated before the Hijrah, because it is a Meccan verse from a Meccan Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تُبَذِّرْ تَبْذِيرًا إِنَّ الْمُبَذِّرِينَ كَانُوا إِخْوَانَ الشَّيَاطِينِ ۖ وَكَانَ الشَّيْطَانُ لِرَبِّهِ كَفُورًا — "and do not squander wastefully. Indeed the wasteful are brothers of the devils, and Satan is ungrateful to his Lord" (17:26-27). It is said in al-Majma': Squandering (التبذير) is dispersal through extravagance (الإسراف). Its origin is to disperse as one disperses seeds (بذر), except that it is specific to what is done by way of corruption (إفساد). That which is done by way of reform (إصلاح) is not called squandering, even if it is much. (End quote).

His saying: إِنَّ الْمُبَذِّرِينَ كَانُوا إِخْوَانَ الشَّيَاطِينِ is the justification for the prohibition of squandering. The meaning is: do not squander, for if you squander, you will be among the wasteful, and the wasteful are brothers of the devils. It is as if the reason for brotherhood between them is that each one of them becomes inseparable from his devil and vice versa, like two brothers who are siblings inseparable in their single origin, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted: وَقَيَّضْنَا لَهُمْ قُرَنَاءَ — "We have assigned for them companions" (41:25), and His saying: احْشُرُوا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَأَزْوَاجَهُمْ — "Muster the wrongdoers and their mates" (37:22), meaning their companions; and His saying: وَإِخْوَانُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِي الْغَيِّ ثُمَّ لَا يُقْصِرُونَ — "And their brothers, they draw them into error, and then they do not stint" (7:202).

From here it appears that the interpretation of those who explained the verse as meaning they are companions (قرناء) of the devils is better than the saying of those who said: the meaning is that they are followers of the devils, walking in their path.

As for His saying: وَكَانَ الشَّيْطَانُ لِرَبِّهِ كَفُورًا, the "Satan" intended here is Iblis, who is the father of the devils, and they are his progeny and tribe; the definite article al (ال) is thus for mental reference (العهد الذهني). It is also possible that the article is for the genus (الجنس), meaning the genus of Satan. In any case, his being ungrateful (كفور) to his Lord is from the perspective of his ingratitude for Allah's blessings, as he spends what He gave him of power, ability, and capacity in the way of seducing people, inciting them to disobedience, calling them to sin, and ingratitude for blessings.

The subtle point in pluralizing "devils" first and singularizing "Satan" second has become clear from what preceded: the first consideration is that every wasteful person is a brother to his specific devil, so all are brothers to the devils; the second consideration is Iblis, who is the father of the devils, or the genus of Satan.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِمَّا تُعْرِضَنَّ عَنْهُمُ ابْتِغَاءَ رَحْمَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ تَرْجُوهَا فَقُل لَّهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّيْسُورًا — "And if you ever have to turn away from them, seeking a mercy from your Lord which you expect, then speak to them a gentle word" (17:28). Its origin is "if you turn away from them," with the extra ma for emphasis and the nun for emphasis.

The context testifies that the discourse is about spending wealth. So the meaning of His saying: وَإِمَّا تُعْرِضَنَّ عَنْهُمُ is turning away from one who asks him for some wealth to spend on him and close his need. It does not mean every turning away however it happens, but rather turning away when he has no wealth to give him, yet he is not despairing of finding it, as evidenced by His saying: ابْتِغَاءَ رَحْمَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ تَرْجُوهَا — i.e., you were turning away from them not because you were wealthy and stingy with it, nor because you lacked it and despaired of obtaining it, but because you lacked it while seeking and requesting a mercy from your Lord which you expect—meaning provision (الرزق).

His saying: فَقُل لَّهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّيْسُورًا means: an easy and soft word; i.e., do not be harsh in speech nor dry in rejection, as the Exalted said: وَأَمَّا السَّائِلَ فَلَا تَنْهَرْ — "and as for the beggar, do not chide him" (93:10), but rather turn him away with an easy, soft word.

It is said in al-Kashshaf: "His saying: ابْتِغَاءَ رَحْمَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ either relates to the response of the condition, placed before it—meaning: speak to them an easy, soft word and promise them a beautiful promise out of mercy for them and to comfort their hearts, seeking a mercy from your Lord; i.e., seek the mercy of Allah which you expect through your mercy toward them. Or it relates to the condition—meaning: if you turn away from them due to the lack of provision from your Lord which you hope He will open for you—so He called provision 'mercy'—then turn them away with a beautiful rejection. Thus, He placed 'seeking' (الابتغاء) in the place of 'lacking' (الفقد) because the one who lacks provision seeks it; so the lack was the cause of the seeking, and the seeking was the effect of it, so the effect was placed in the place of the cause." (End quote).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَجْعَلْ يَدَكَ مَغْلُولَةً إِلَىٰ عُنُقِكَ وَلَا تَبْسُطْهَا كُلَّ الْبَسْطِ فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُومًا مَّحْسُورًا — "Do not keep your hand fastened to your neck, nor open it altogether, lest you should sit blamed and regretful" (17:29). Making the hand fastened to the neck is a metaphor for withholding, like one who does not give or bestow anything due to his stinginess and the greed of his soul. Opening the hand altogether is a metaphor for a person spending everything in his possession such that nothing remains, like one who opens his hand so completely that nothing can settle upon it. Thus, in the speech is a profound prohibition against both negligence (التفريط) and excess (الإفراط) in spending.

His saying: فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُومًا مَّحْسُورًا is branched from His saying: وَلَا تَبْسُطْهَا etc. Regret (الحسر) is exhaustion or nakedness; i.e., do not open your hand altogether such that it results in you sitting blamed by yourself and others, cut off from the necessities of living, or naked, unable to appear before people, socialize with them, or frequent them.

It has been said that His saying: فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُومًا مَّحْسُورًا is branched from both sentences, not just the last one. The meaning would be: if you withhold, you sit blamed and censured; and if you are extravagant, you remain regretful and grieved.

However, it is not certain that His saying: وَلَا تَبْسُطْهَا كُلَّ الْبَسْطِ is explicitly a prohibition against squandering (التبذير) and extravagance (الإسراف). Likewise, it is not apparent that spending all one's wealth in the way of Allah is extravagance and squandering, even if it is prohibited by this verse. For included in the concept of squandering is that it be by way of corruption (إفساد), and placing wealth—even if it is much or all of it—in the way of Allah and spending it on those who deserve it is not a corruption of it. There is no reason for regret and grief over what was not corrupted nor caused corruption.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّهُ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِ خَبِيرًا بَصِيرًا — "Indeed your Lord expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it. Indeed He is all-aware, all-seeing of His servants" (17:30). The appearance of the context is that the verse is in the position of justifying what preceded in the previous verse regarding the prohibition of excess and negligence in spending and bestowing wealth.

The meaning is: this is the habit of your Lord and His ongoing sunnah; He expands provision for whomever He wishes and tightens it for whomever He wishes. He does not expand it altogether nor withhold it altogether, observing the welfare of the servants. Indeed, He is all-aware, all-seeing of His servants. It is appropriate for you to take on the character of Allah, adopt the path of moderation, and avoid excess and negligence.

It has been said that it is a justification in the sense that your Lord expands and contracts, and that is among the divine affairs specific to Him, the Exalted, and it is not for you to be characterized by it. What is upon you is to be economical without deviating from it toward excess or negligence. Other views have been mentioned regarding the meaning of the justification, but they are far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا أَوْلَادَكُمْ خَشْيَةَ إِمْلَاقٍ ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُهُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ قَتْلَهُمْ كَانَ خِطْئًا كَبِيرًا — "Do not kill your children for fear of penury: We will provide for them and for you. Killing them is indeed a great iniquity" (17:31). Penury (الإملاق) is destitution and poverty. It is said in al-Mufradat: Error (الخطأ) is deviating from the right direction, and that is of several types: One is to intend other than what is good to intend and do; this is the complete error for which man is held accountable. It is said: khati'a yakhta'u and khit'atan. The Exalted said: إِنَّ قَتْلَهُمْ كَانَ خِطْئًا كَبِيرًا, and He said: وَإِن كُنَّا لَخَاطِئِينَ — "and we were indeed erring" (12:91). The second is to intend what is good to do, but what occurs from him is contrary to what he intended; so it is said: akhta'a ikhta'an, so he is mukhti'. This person was correct in intention but erred in action. This is the meaning in His saying: وَمَن قَتَلَ مُؤْمِنًا خَطَأً فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ — "He who kills a believer by mistake, there is the freeing of a... slave" (4:92). The third is to intend what is not good to do, and what happens from him is contrary to it; so he is an erring person in intention, correct in action; he is censured for his purpose, not praised for his action.

The summary of the matter is that whoever intends something and something else happens from him, it is said: akhta'a (he erred). If it occurs as he intended, it is said: asaba (he was correct). Sometimes it is said of one who does an act that is not good or intends an intention that is not beautiful: that he erred (akhta'a). Therefore, it is said: "he hit the error," "he missed the right," "he hit the right," and "he missed the error." This word is homonymous (مشتركة), as you see, fluctuating between meanings that one who seeks the truth must contemplate. (End quote, summarized).

In the verse is a severe prohibition against killing children out of fear of poverty and need. His saying: نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُهُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ is the justification for the prohibition and a preamble to His saying after it: إِنَّ قَتْلَهُمْ كَانَ خِطْئًا كَبِيرًا.

The meaning is: do not kill your children for fear that you will be afflicted with poverty and need, leading them to the humiliation of begging, or the marriage of your daughters to those who are not their peers, or other things that would take away your dignity. For you are not the ones providing for them such that you would lose the provision upon your poverty and hardship; rather, We provide for them and for you. Indeed, killing them was a great iniquity.

The prohibition against killing children for fear of poverty and penury has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted. Despite it being the killing of a protected soul—which His speech, the Exalted, exaggerates in prohibiting—it was singled out for mention and specified with a particular prohibition because it is among the ugliest of wretchedness and the most intense of cruelty. Also, because they—as has been said—lived in lands where years of hardship were frequent and drought was quick to arrive; so when the signs of destitution and hardship appeared through drought or otherwise, they hastened to kill the children for fear of the loss of dignity and honor.

In al-Kashshaf: "Their killing of their children was their burying of their daughters alive (الوأد). They used to bury them alive for fear of destitution, which is penury (imlaq), so Allah forbade them and guaranteed their provisions." (End quote). The apparent meaning is contrary to what he mentioned; the verses addressing the burying of daughters alive are specific verses that state it and its prohibition explicitly, such as the saying of the Exalted: وَإِذَا الْمَوْؤُودَةُ سُئِلَتْ بِأَيِّ ذَنْبٍ قُتِلَتْ — "when the girl-child-buried-alive is asked for what sin she was killed" (81:8-9), and His saying: وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِالْأُنثَىٰ ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ يَتَوَارَىٰ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ مِن سُوءِ مَا بُشِّرَ بِهِ ۚ أَيُمْسِكُهُ عَلَىٰ هُونٍ أَمْ يَدُسُّهُ فِي التُّرَابِ ۗ أَلَا سَاءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ — "When one of them is brought the news of a female [newborn], his face turns black and he chokes with suppressed agony. He hides from the people out of the distress of what he has been given news of [and debates]: shall he keep it in humiliation, or bury it in the ground? Look! Evil is the judgment they make" (16:58-59).

As for the verse we are in and its likes, it prohibits the killing of children for fear of penury. There is no reason to restrict "children" (الأولاد) to daughters when it is more general, nor to restrict "humiliation" (الهون) to the fear of poverty when they are distinct. The truth is that the verse reveals another evil sunnah other than burying daughters alive to avoid humiliation, which is the killing of children—male and female—for fear of poverty and destitution, and the verses prohibit it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا الزِّنَىٰ ۖ إِنَّهُ كَانَ فَاحِشَةً وَسَاءَ سَبِيلًا — "Do not approach fornication. It is indeed an indecency and an evil way" (17:32). A prohibition against adultery (الزنا). He exaggerated in its prohibition by forbidding them from even approaching it. He justified it by His saying: إِنَّهُ كَانَ فَاحِشَةً, thus conveying that indecency (الفحش) is an inseparable attribute of it. His saying: وَسَاءَ سَبِيلًا conveys that it is an evil way leading to the corruption of society in all its affairs until its bond dissolves and its order is disrupted, and in it is the destruction of humanity. The Glorified has exaggerated in the threat against one who commits it, saying regarding the attributes of the believers: وَلَا يَزْنُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثَامًا يُضَاعَفْ لَهُ الْعَذَابُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِ مُهَانًا إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلًا صَالِحًا — "and do not commit fornication. Whoever does that shall encounter its retribution, the punishment shall be doubled for him on the Day of Resurrection, and he shall abide in it in humiliation, except those who repent, attain faith, and act righteously" (25:68-70).

(A Discourse on the Prohibition of Adultery)

This is a Quranic and sociological discussion.

It is observed that in each of the two human spouses—meaning the male and the female—when they reach maturity and their constitution is healthy, there is an instinctive inclination toward the other. This is not something specific to humans; rather, we find it in all animals as well, based on this natural instinct.

They have been equipped, in terms of organs and faculties, with that which calls them to this proximity and inclination. Contemplation of the type of equipment of the two genders leaves no doubt that this natural desire is a creative means (وسيلة تكوينية) toward procreation and reproduction, which is the vehicle for the survival of the species. They have been equipped with other matters completing this natural aim, such as the love of the child and the equipping of the female among mammals with milk to feed her infant until it is able to take coarse food, chew it, and digest it. All of this is a divine subjugation (تسخير إلهي) by which the survival of the species is sought.

Therefore, we see that the animal—despite not needing society and civilization due to the simplicity of its life and the fewness of its needs—is guided from time to time, according to its instinct, to the conjugal union (السفاد). Then the two mates, or the female among them, commit to the infant or chick and undertake—or the female undertakes—its feeding and rearing until it matures and becomes independent in managing the mill of its life.

For this reason also, people—since history began recording their courses and sunnahs—have continued to practice the sunnah of marriage (الازدواج), which involves a type of exclusivity and companionship between the man and the woman to answer the call of instinct and to seek the procreation of progeny. It is a natural foundation for the formation of human society; for it is necessary that the various human peoples, despite their breadth and numbers, terminate in small domestic societies formed in past ages.

What has passed—that in the sunnah of marriage there is something of the meaning of exclusivity—is the origin of why men used to consider their families as honors (أعراض) for themselves and saw the defense of the family and its protection from the interference of others as an obligation upon themselves, like the defense of themselves or even more intense. The instinct raging then is what is called protective jealousy (الغيرة), and it is not envy or stinginess.

For this reason also, they have continued through the centuries and generations to praise marriage and consider it a good, praised sunnah, while they find adultery (الزنا) — which is intercourse without the bond of marriage — to be ugly. They loathe it in general and consider it a social sin and an indecency (فاحشة); i.e., a shameful act that is not made public, even if it was sometimes found among some barbaric peoples under specific conditions between free women and youths or between slave girls, as mentioned in the histories of nations and peoples.

They only found it indecent and denounced it because of the corruption of lineages, the cutting off of progeny, the appearance of venereal diseases, and its invitation to many social crimes such as murder, wounding, theft, betrayal, and otherwise, and the loss of chastity, modesty, protective jealousy, affection, and mercy.

However, modern Western civilization—because it is built upon the complete enjoyment of the advantages of material life and the freedom of individuals in matters not attended to by civil laws, whether they be national sunnahs, religious laws, or human ethics—has permitted it if it occurs without coercion, however it may be. Perhaps some other partial conditions are added in specific cases. They did not care about the types of corruption it entails, out of concern for the freedom of individuals in what they desire and approve, and social laws observe the opinion of the majority.

Thus, indecency spread among men and women until it encompassed the married men and women and the unmarriageable kin (المحارم), until it was almost the case that no one could be found who was not afflicted by it. Its offspring increased so much that they almost tipped the scales. Noble ethics—which natural humanity used to be characterized by and approve for itself by legislating the sunnah of marriage, such as chastity, protective jealousy, and modesty—began to weaken day by day until some of these virtues became a laughingstock and a mockery. Were it not that mentioning shameful things involves some shame, especially within Quranic discussions, we would have cited some of the statistics published by periodicals in this regard.

The heavenly religions, according to what the Quran mentions—and reference to this has passed in the commentary of verses 151-153 of Surah al-An'am—prohibit adultery most severely. It was prohibited in the religion of the Jews, and its prohibition is understood from the Gospels.

It has been prohibited in Islam and counted among the major sins. The prohibition was made even more severe regarding the unmarriageable kin (المحارم) such as the mother, daughter, sister, paternal aunt, and maternal aunt; and in the prohibition of adultery with إحصان (fortification) — which is the adultery of a man who has a wife and a woman who has a husband. The legal punishment (الحد) legislated for it was made severe: one hundred lashes, and execution in the third or fourth instance if the punishment was carried out twice or thrice, and stoning (الرجم) in adultery with fortification.

The Glorified has pointed to the wisdom of the prohibition in what He forbade by His saying: وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا الزِّنَىٰ ۖ إِنَّهُ كَانَ فَاحِشَةً وَسَاءَ سَبِيلًا, where He first counted it an indecency and then described it second by His saying: وَسَاءَ سَبِيلًا. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is the way of survival, as understood from His saying: أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَال... وَتَقْطَعُونَ السَّبِيلَ — "Do you indeed come to men... and cut off the way?" (29:29); i.e., you abandon coming to women, which is the way, so it is cut off thereby. It is nothing but a way for survival from the perspective of its causing the birth of offspring and the survival of progeny thereby, and from the perspective that marriage and the formation of domestic society is the strongest means ensuring the survival of civil society after its formation.

With the opening of the door of adultery, desires continue to be cut off from marriage and union, as nothing remains for it but the trial of spending, the hardship of carrying children and rearing them, and enduring difficulties in protecting them and fulfilling the duties of their lives, while the instinct is satisfied through another way without toil or fatigue. This is observed in the state of youths and girls in these lands. It was said to one of them: "Why do you not marry?" He replied: "What would I do with marriage when all the women of the city are my women?" At that point, nothing remains for marriage and union except the partnership of the spouses in the partial efforts of life other than procreation, such as partnership in a trade or work, and separation hastens to them for the slightest excuse. All of this is observed today in Western societies.

From here, they consider marriage a partnership in life formed between the two spouses, the man and the woman, and they made it the sought-after goal in itself from marriage, rather than procreation and the preparation of children, nor answering the instinct of natural inclination. Rather, they counted that among the effects resulting from it if they agree upon it. This is a deviation from the path of the primordial nature (الفطرة). Contemplation of the state of animals in their various types leads to the fact that the sought-after goal for them is the satisfaction of the raging instinct and the procreation of progeny. Likewise, deep consideration of the state of man when he first inclines to that shows that the immediate goal calling him to it is the satisfaction of the instinct, followed by the seeking of the child.

If the human instinct that drives him to this natural sunnah only sought partnership in life and cooperation in the duties of food, drink, clothing, and housing—and such a thing can be realized between two men or between two women—its effect would have appeared in human society and been legislated, or at least in some societies throughout human history, and a man would have married a man occasionally or a woman a woman. The sunnah of marriage would not have proceeded on a single pattern always, and this bond would not have been established between two parties, one of whom is from the men and the other from the women, forever.

From another perspective, the increase of the offspring of adultery day by day cuts off the sources of affection and mercy and the attachment of the children's hearts to the fathers. This necessitates the cutting off of affection and mercy from the side of the fathers toward the children. The abandonment of affection and mercy between the two generations—fathers and children—leads to the abandonment of the sunnah of marriage for society, and in that is their extinction. All of this is also what is looming from Western societies.

It is a false conception to imagine that humanity will one day succeed in managing the mill of its society by technical principles and scientific methods without needing to seek help from natural instincts. At that time, a generation of offspring would be prepared while dispensing with the instinct of the love of children by placing prizes that drive them to procreation and reproduction, or otherwise, as is practiced in some countries today. For national sunnahs and civil laws derive their life from what man has been equipped with of natural powers and instincts. If they vanish or are abolished, the bond of his society would thereby be severed. The form of society is maintained by its individuals, and its sunnahs are built upon their answering them and their satisfaction with them. How can a sunnah run in a society that their temperaments do not approve and their souls do not answer, and then the matter continues?

So, the abandonment of natural instincts and the heedlessness of human society regarding its original goals threatens humanity with a destruction that will envelop it, and it cries out that before them is a day in which the breach will widen beyond the patcher's ability, even if today it is not fully felt because its growth is not yet complete.

Furthermore, this indecency has another evil effect in the view of Islamic legislation, which is its corruption of lineages, upon which marriages and inheritances in Islam have been built.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا النَّفْسَ الَّتِي حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ — "Do not kill a soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause" (17:33) to the end of the verse. A prohibition against killing a protected soul except with due cause; i.e., unless it be a killing by right, such that it deserves that for retaliation (قود), apostasy, or other legal reasons. Perhaps in describing the soul as that which حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ (Allah has made inviolable) without qualification is an indication of the prohibition of killing the soul in all heavenly religions, so it would be among the general laws as previously indicated at the end of verses 151-153 of Surah al-An'am.

His saying: وَمَن قُتِلَ مَظْلُومًا فَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا لِوَلِيِّهِ سُلْطَانًا فَلَا يُسْرِف فِّي الْقَتْلِ ۖ إِنَّهُ كَانَ مَنصُورًا — "and whoever is killed wrongfully, We have certainly given his heir an authority. But let him not commit any excess in killing—he is indeed helped [by the Law]" (17:33). The meaning of giving authority (سلطان) to his heir (ولي) is giving him legal power over killing the killer of his relative as retaliation (قصاص). The two pronouns in فَلَا يُسْرِف (let him not commit excess) and إِنَّهُ (he is) refer to the heir, and the meaning of his being "helped" (منصور) is the aforementioned legal empowerment.

The meaning is: whoever is killed wrongfully, We have established, according to legislation, for his heir—who is the guardian of his blood—authority over retaliation, taking blood money, or pardoning. So let the heir not commit excess in killing by killing other than the killer or killing more than one. Indeed, he was helped; i.e., let him not commit excess in it because he was helped, so the killer will not escape him because We helped him; or, let him not commit excess relying on the fact that We helped him.

Some have considered the possibility of the pronoun in His saying: فَلَا يُسْرِف returning to the killer, as indicated by the context, and in His saying: إِنَّهُ to the "whoever" (مَن). The meaning would be: We have given the heir of the one killed wrongfully authority, so let the first killer not commit excess by venturing into killing wrongfully, for the one killed wrongfully is helped from Our side because of the authority We gave to his heir. This is a meaning far from the context, and less likely than returning the pronoun إِنَّهُ only to the one killed.

Discussion on the meaning of retaliation (القصاص) has preceded at the end of the saying of the Exalted: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْقِصَاصِ حَيَاةٌ — "There is life for you in retribution" (2:179) in the first volume of the book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا مَالَ الْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُ — "Do not approach the orphan’s property except in the best [manner], until he comes of age" (17:34). A prohibition against consuming the orphan's wealth, which is among the major sins for which Allah has promised the Fire. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَالَ الْيَتَامَىٰ ظُلْمًا إِنَّمَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ نَارًا ۖ وَسَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيرًا — "Indeed those who consume the property of orphans wrongfully, only consume fire into their bellies, and they shall enter the Blaze" (4:10).

In the prohibition of "approaching" (الاقتراب) is an exaggeration to convey the intensity of the prohibition.

His saying: إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ means: by the way that is best, in which there is the benefit of growing his wealth. His saying: حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُ is the time of maturity and discernment (الرشد), and at that point, orphanhood is lifted from him. The limitation by this phrase is because the prohibition of approaching is in the sense of the command for protection and preservation; it is as if it were said: preserve his wealth until he reaches maturity, then return it to him. In other words, the speech is in the sense of our saying: do not approach the orphan's wealth as long as he is an orphan. Some of what suits the context has preceded in Surah al-An'am, verse 152.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْفُوا بِالْعَهْدِ ۖ إِنَّ الْعَهْدَ كَانَ مَسْئُولًا — "and fulfill the covenants; indeed all covenants are accountable" (17:34); i.e., asked about. This is from the deletion and connection (الحذف والإيصال) permissible in speech. It has been said: the meaning is the questioning of the covenant itself, for it is possible that deeds will be personified on the Day of Resurrection and testify for or against man, intercede for him, or dispute with him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ إِذَا كِلْتُمْ وَزِنُوا بِالْقِسْطَاسِ الْمُسْتَقِيمِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا — "Fill up the measure when you measure, and weigh with an even balance. That is better and fairer in outcome" (17:35). The balance (القسطاس) — with the qaf having either a kasrah or dammah — is the scale. It is said to be a Romanized Arabized word, and it is said to be Arabic. It is also said to be originally composed of القسط (justice) and طاس (the pan of the scale). The even balance (القسطاس المستقيم) is the just scale that does not lose in its weighing.

His saying: ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا — Good (الخير) is that which man must choose if the matter fluctuates between it and something else. Outcome (التأويل) is the reality to which the matter terminates. Fulfilling the measure and weighing with an even balance being "better" is due to what it contains of avoiding the stealing and misappropriation of people's wealth from where they do not perceive, and attracting their trust.

Their being "fairer in outcome" is due to what they contain of observing discernment and uprightness in people's estimation of their livelihood. For their livelihoods stand, in the enjoyment of life's goods, upon two principles: acquiring goods suitable for enjoyment, and exchanging the surplus beyond their need. Thus, they estimate their livelihood according to what they can spend of wealth in kind or value, and according to what they need of purchased goods. If they lose through cheating (التطفيف) and deficiency in measure and weight, then life has become disordered for them from both sides together, and general security has been lifted from among them.

But if the weight is established with justice, then discernment has dawned upon them, and their economic conditions have become upright by hitting the mark in what they estimated for their livelihood, and the trust of the people of the market has been attracted, and general security has settled among them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ ۚ إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَالْبَصَرَ وَالْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُولَٰئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْئُولًا — "Do not follow that of which you have no knowledge. Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable" (17:36). The famous reading is لَا تَقْفُ with a sukun on the qaf and a dammah on the fa, from qafa yaqfu qafwan when he follows it. From it is the rhyme (قافية) of poetry because it is at the end of the hemistich, following what preceded it. It is also read لَا تَقْفُ with a dammah on the qaf and a sukun on the fa, from qafa in the sense of qafa. Therefore, it is narrated from some linguists that qafa is the inversion of qafa, like jabadha is the inversion of jadhabha. From it is physiognomy (القيافة) in the sense of following the trace of footsteps.

The verse prohibits following that of which there is no knowledge. Due to its generality, it includes following in belief and action. it resolves into something like our saying: do not believe what you have no knowledge of, do not say what you have no knowledge of, and do not do what you have no knowledge of, because in all of that is "following."

In this is an endorsement of what the human primordial nature (الفطرة) decrees, which is the obligation of following knowledge and the prohibition of following other than it. For man, by his gifted nature, does not desire in the course of his life—in his belief or action—except hitting the reality and obtaining what is in the core of external reality. The known is that which it is correct for him to say: "it is it." As for the conjectured, the doubted, and the imagined, it is not correct to apply the statement "it is it" to them; so understand that.

Man, by his sound nature, follows in his belief what he sees as truth and finds as reality in the external world, and follows in his action what he sees himself as correct in identifying. This is in what it is possible for him to obtain knowledge of. As for what it is not possible for him to have knowledge of—like the branches of belief for some people and most actions for most people—the sound nature drives him to follow the knowledge of one who has knowledge of that and expertise, considering his knowledge and expertise as knowledge for himself. Thus, his following in that returns in reality to following his knowledge that the other has knowledge and expertise, just as the traveler who does not know the road returns to the guide, but with his knowledge of the guide's expertise and acquaintance; and the sick person returns to the physician, and similarly the owners of needs return to the various industries related to their needs, to the masters of those industries.

It results from this that he does not step beyond knowledge in the course of his life according to what his nature guides him to, except that he counts what his soul trusts and his heart is tranquil with as knowledge, even if that is not the certainty (اليقين) called knowledge in the art of demonstration in logic.

So he has, in every matter that comes upon him, either knowledge of the matter itself or a scientific proof (دليل علمي) for the obligation of acting upon what it leads to and indicates. Upon this, His saying, the Glorified: وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ should be understood. Thus, following conjecture based on a scientific proof for the obligation of following it is following knowledge, just like following knowledge in the case of knowledge.

The meaning resolves to the fact that it is prohibited to venture into a belief or action for which knowledge can be obtained except after obtaining knowledge; and to venture into what is not possible therein except after relying on a scientific proof that permits venturing and entering. This is like taking rulings from the Prophet and following him and obeying him in what he commands and forbids from his Lord, and the sick person taking what the physician commands, and returning to the masters of industries in what pertains to their industry. For the scientific proof for the infallibility of the Prophet is a scientific proof for the correspondence of what he informs of or what he commands and forbids with reality, and the hitting of the mark by one who follows him. The scientific proof for the expertise of the physician in his medicine and the masters of industries in their industries is a scientific proof for the correctness of one who returns to them in what he acts upon.

Were it not for the fact that venturing into action based on a scientific proof for the obligation of venturing is a scientific venture, the verse would be incapable of indicating its meaning from the start. For the way to understand the meaning of the verse is its linguistic appearance (ظهورها اللفظي), and linguistic appearance is among the conjectural proofs, except that it is a proof based on a scientific proof, which is the practice of the rational (بناء العقلاء) upon its authority. If other than what knowledge specifically pertained to were among what is absolutely not known, then following the appearance—and among it the appearance of the verse itself—would be prohibited by the verse, and the verse would be prohibiting the following of itself, thus contradicting itself.

From here, the objection raised by some in this regard is refuted—as narrated from al-Razi in his Tafsir—that acting upon conjecture is frequent in the branches (الفروع), so clinging to the verse is clinging to a specific general (عام مخصوص), which only yields conjecture. If it indicated that clinging to conjecture is not permissible, it would indicate that clinging to it is not permissible; so the statement of its authority leads to its negation, which is not permissible.

The refutation is that the verse indicates the impermissibility of following other than knowledge without doubt, but the cases of acting upon conjecture legally are cases upon which a scientific proof has been established. So acting in them is in reality only acting upon those scientific proofs, and the verse remains in its generality without specification. Even if it were conceded, acting upon a specified general regarding the remaining individuals safe from specification is acting upon a rational proof (حجة عقلائية) similar to acting upon a non-specified general, with no difference between them whatsoever.

Similar to it is the problem raised that the way to understand the intent of the verse is its appearance, and appearance is a conjectural path; so if the verse indicated the prohibition of following other than knowledge, it would indicate the prohibition of taking the appearance of itself, and its necessity would be the prohibition of acting upon itself.

This is refuted by what was previously indicated: that following the appearance is following a rational scientific proof, which is the practice of the rational upon its authority; so following it is not from following other than knowledge at all.

His saying: إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَالْبَصَرَ وَالْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُولَٰئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْئُولًا is the justification for the previous prohibition in His saying: وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ.

The apparent meaning that comes to mind is that the two pronouns in كَانَ عَنْهُ return to كُلُّ, so عَنْهُ would be the pro-agent (نائب فاعل) for His saying: مَسْئُولًا, placed before it as mentioned by al-Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf, or sufficing for the pro-agent. His saying: أُولَٰئِكَ is an indication to the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart. He expressed them with أُولَٰئِكَ (those), which is specific to the rational, because each of them being "accountable" treats them like the rational, which has many parallels in His speech, the Exalted.

Some have denied that أُولَٰئِكَ is specific to the rational, relying on the saying of Jarir:

Censure the dwellings after the dwelling of al-Liwa And life after those (أولئك) days

Accordingly, the "accountable" is each of the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart; it is asked about itself, so it testifies for or against man, as the Exalted said: وَتُكَلِّمُنَا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — "their hands speak to Us, and their feet bear witness to what they used to earn" (36:65).

Some chose the return of the pronoun عَنْهُ to كُلُّ, and the return of the remaining pronouns to the "follower" (القافي) indicated in the speech. Thus, the accountable is the follower; he is asked about his hearing, his eyesight, and his heart: how did he use them? And in what did he use them? Accordingly, in the speech is a transition (التفات) from address to the third person; the original would have been: "you were accountable for it." This is far-fetched.

The meaning is: do not follow what you have no knowledge of, because Allah, the Glorified, will ask about the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart, which are the means man uses to obtain knowledge. The result of the justification, according to its application to the case, is that the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart are only blessings Allah gave to man so that he may distinguish the truth thereby and obtain reality, thus believing in it and building his action upon it. He will be asked about each of them: did it perceive what it was used for with a scientific perception? And did man follow what that means obtained of knowledge?

So the hearing is asked: was what it heard known and certain? And the eyesight: was what it saw apparent and clear? And the heart: was what it thought and judged certain, with no doubt in it? They will inevitably answer with the truth and testify to what is the reality. So it is mandatory for man to guard against following what he has no knowledge of, for the organs and means of knowledge that are with him will be asked, and they will testify against him regarding what he followed of what they obtained while he had no knowledge of it, and no excuse will be accepted from him then.

Its outcome is similar to our saying: do not follow what you have no knowledge of, for it is preserved for you in your hearing, your eyesight, and your heart, and Allah will inevitably ask them about your action. Thus, the verse is in the meaning of the saying of the Exalted: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا مَا جَاءُوهَا شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَأَبْصَارُهُمْ وَجُلُودُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — "When they come to it, their hearing, their eyes and their skins will bear witness against them as to what they used to do" — until He said — وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَتِرُونَ أَن يَشْهَدَ عَلَيْكُمْ سَمْعُكُمْ وَلَا أَبْصَارُكُمْ وَلَا جُلُودُكُمْ وَلَٰكِن ظَنَنتُمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَعْلَمُ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ وَذَٰلِكُمْ ظَنُّكُمُ الَّذِي ظَنَنتُم بِرَبِّكُمْ أَرْدَاكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُم مِّنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ — "You did not use to hide yourselves lest your hearing, your eyes, or your skins should bear witness against you, but you supposed that Allah does not know much of what you do. That was the supposition you entertained about your Lord, and it has ruined you. So you have become among the losers" (41:20-23), and other verses of the testimony of the organs.

However, the verse adds to them the heart (الفؤاد) among the witnesses against man, and it is that by which man feels what he feels and perceives what he perceives. It is among the most amazing things understood from the verses of the Gathering (الحشر) that Allah will stop the human soul and ask it about what it perceived, so it testifies against the man himself.

It has become clear that the verse prohibits venturing into a matter while being ignorant of it, whether it be a belief with ignorance or an action with ignorance of its permissibility and the correct way in it, or arranging an effect for a matter while being ignorant of it. Its conclusion justifies that by His questioning of the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart. There is no harm in the cause being more general than what it justifies, for the organs are accountable even for when man ventures while knowing the impermissibility of venturing. The Exalted said: الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلَىٰ أَفْوَاهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنَا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُم — "Today We shall seal their mouths, and their hands shall speak to Us, and their feet shall bear witness..." (36:65).

It is said in al-Majma' regarding the meaning of His saying: وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ: "Its meaning is: do not say, 'I heard' when you did not hear, nor 'I saw' when you did not see, nor 'I knew' when you did not know—from Ibn Abbas and Qatadah. It is said: its meaning is: do not speak behind someone's back (قفا); i.e., when he passes by you, do not backbite him—from al-Hasan. It is said: it is false testimony—from Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyyah.

The principle is that it is general for every word, deed, or intention that is without knowledge. It is as if the Glorified said: do not say except what you know is permissible to be said, do not do except what you know is permissible to be done, and do not believe except what you know is among what is permissible to be believed." (End quote).

In this is that what he mentioned is more general than what the verse conveys; for it prohibits following what there is no knowledge of, not following except with knowledge. The second is more general than the first, for it includes the prohibition of following with knowledge of impermissibility. But the first only includes the prohibition of following with ignorance of the aspect of belief or action. As for the views he narrated at the beginning of his speech, it is more appropriate that they be mentioned in the commentary of the justification as an indication of some instances (مصاديق), rather than the justified itself.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَمْشِ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَخْرِقَ الْأَرْضَ وَلَنْ تَبْلُغَ الْجِبَالَ طُولًا "Do not walk exultantly on the earth. Indeed you will neither pierce the earth, nor reach the mountains in height" (17:37). Exultance (المرح) is an excess of joy in falsehood—as has been said. Perhaps the qualification "in falsehood" is to indicate its departure from the bounds of moderation; for true joy is that which constitutes rejoicing in a blessing from the blessings of Allah as a form of gratitude to Him, and such joy does not exceed the limit of moderation. However, when one rejoices and this intensifies until his intellect becomes light and its effects appear in his actions, speech, standing, and sitting—and especially in his gait—then it is of falsehood.

His saying: وَلَا تَمْشِ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا "Do not walk exultantly on the earth" is a prohibition against man magnifying himself beyond what he truly is, due to factors such as insolence (البطر), vanity (الأشر), pride (الكبر), and conceit (الخيلاء). Walking exultantly on the earth was specifically mentioned because these traits are most manifest in it.

His saying: إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَخْرِقَ الْأَرْضَ وَلَنْ تَبْلُغَ الْجِبَالَ طُولًا "Indeed you will neither pierce the earth, nor reach the mountains in height" is a metaphorical expression (كناية) conveying that these actions of yours, through which you intend to display power, strength, and greatness, are merely an illusion you have conceived. For there are things stronger than you that cannot be pierced by your feet—namely the earth—and things taller than you—namely the mountains. Therefore, acknowledge that you are lowly and insignificant. Nothing that man seeks and competes for in this worldly life (النشأة)—such as kingdom, might, authority, power, nobility, wealth, and the like—consists of anything but illusory matters that have no reality beyond human perception. Allah has harnessed souls to believe in them and rely upon them in action for the sake of flourishing in this life and the fulfillment of His word. Were it not for these illusions, man would not live in this world, nor would His saying, the Exalted, be fulfilled: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ "There is for you a dwelling place on the earth and an enjoyment for a while" (2:36).

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ كَانَ سَيِّئُهُ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ مَكْرُوهًا "The evil of all these is detestable to your Lord" (17:38). The demonstrative "all these" (ذلك) refers to the previously mentioned obligations and prohibitions—as has been said—and the pronoun in "its evil" (سيئه) refers back to it. The meaning is: all that has preceded, the evil part of it—which refers to what was prohibited and constituted disobedience among the mentioned matters—is detestable to your Lord; Allah the Exalted does not will it.

In a reading other than the well-known one, it is recited as "a sin" (سيئة) with a fatha on the hamza and a ta’ at the end. According to this reading, it is the predicate (خبر) of "was" (كان), and the meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: ذَٰلِكَ مِمَّا أَوْحَىٰ إِلَيْكَ رَبُّكَ مِنَ الْحِكْمَةِ "That is from the wisdom your Lord has revealed to you" (17:39). "That" (ذلك) is an allusion to the detailed religious duties (التكاليف) mentioned previously. In this verse, wisdom (الحكمة) — the precise knowledge that leads to right action and prevents falsehood — is applied to subsidiary rulings (الأحكام الفرعية). This may be due to the benefits (المصالح) they contain, which are understood generally from the preceding discourse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلٰهًا آخَرَ فَتُلْقَىٰ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَلُومًا مَدْحُورًا "And do not set up another god besides Allah, or you will be cast into hell, blamed and banished" (17:39). The Glorified has repeated the prohibition of polytheism (الشرك), having prohibited it previously, out of concern for the matter of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and to emphasize its importance. It serves as a link connecting the subsequent discourse with the preceding, and the meaning of the verse is apparent.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ihtijaj, narrated from Burayd ibn Umayr ibn Mu'awiyah al-Shami, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him): In a tradition in which he mentions predestination (الجبر), delegation (التفويض), and the matter between the two matters (الأمر بين أمرين)—he said: I said to him: "Does Allah have a volition (مشية) and a will (إرادة) in that?"—meaning the act of the servant. He replied: أما الطاعات فإرادة الله ومشيته فيها الأمر بها والرضا لها والمعاونة لها، ومشيته في المعاصي النهي عنها والسخط بها والخذلان عليها — "As for acts of obedience, Allah's will and volition regarding them consist of commanding them, being pleased with them, and providing assistance for them. His volition regarding acts of disobedience consists of prohibiting them, being displeased with them, and withdrawing His support (الخذلان) from them." (The tradition continues).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Abu Wallad al-Hannat, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: وَبِالْوَالِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا "and [enjoining] kindness to parents" (17:23). He said: "Kindness (الإحسان) is that you accompany them well and do not burden them with having to ask you for anything they need, even if they are self-sufficient. Does Allah not say: لَنْ تَنَالُوا الْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنْفِقُوا مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ 'You will never attain piety until you spend out of what you love' (3:92)?"

Then Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "As for His saying: إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِنْدَكَ الْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهُمَا أَوْ كِلَاهُمَا فَلَا تَقُلْ لَهُمَا أُفٍّ 'Should any of them or both of them reach old age with you, do not say to them, "Fie!"' (17:23), he said: 'If they annoy you, do not say "Fie" to them, and do not chide them if they strike you.' And He said: وَقُلْ لَهُمَا قَوْلًا كَرِيمًا 'and speak to them noble words' (17:23), he said: 'You should say to them: "May Allah forgive you both," for that is a noble word from you.' And He said: وَاخْفِضْ لَهُمَا جَنَاحَ الذُّلِّ مِنَ الرَّحْمَةِ 'Lower the wing of humility to them out of mercy' (17:24), he said: 'Do not fill your eyes with looking at them except with mercy and tenderness; do not raise your voice above theirs, nor your hands above theirs, and do not walk in front of them.'"

I say: It was also narrated by al-Kulayni in al-Kafi with his chain of transmission from Abu Wallad al-Hannat from him (peace be upon him).

In al-Kafi, with his chain of transmission from Hadid ibn Hakim, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: أدنى العقوق أف، ولو علم الله عز وجل شيئا أهون منه لنهى عنه — "The lowest form of undutifulness (العقوق) is saying 'Fie' (أف), and had Allah, Mighty and Majestic, known anything lesser than that, He would have prohibited it."

I say: It was also narrated from him through another chain. This meaning was also narrated with his chain from Abu al-Bilad from him (peace be upon him). It was narrated by al-Ayyashi in his Tafsir from Hariz from him (peace be upon him), and by al-Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan from al-Rida from his father from him (peace be upon him). The narrations regarding the obligation of being dutiful to parents and the prohibition of being undutiful to them—during their lives and after their deaths—from both the Sunni and Shi'a paths from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) and the Imams of his household (peace be upon them) are more than can be counted.

In al-Majma', from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "The oft-returning (الأواب) is the one who is repentant (التواب), devoted in worship (المتعبد), and who turns away from his sin."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "O Abu Muhammad, you must adhere to godwariness (الورع), diligence (الاجتهاد), the fulfillment of trusts (أداء الأمانة), truthfulness in speech (صدق الحديث), good companionship to those who accompany you, and lengthening the prostration (السجود); for that was among the practices (سنن) of the repentant and the oft-returning (التوابين الأوابين)." Abu Basir said: "The oft-returning are the repentant."

I say: It was also narrated from Abu Basir from him (peace be upon him) regarding the meaning of the verse: "They are the repentant and the devoted in worship."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah and Hannad narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: "When the shadows incline and the spirits find rest, seek your needs from Allah, for it is the hour of the oft-returning (الأوابين)," and he recited: فَإِنَّهُ كَانَ لِلْأَوَّابِينَ غَفُورًا "for He is indeed all-forgiving to those who are penitent" (17:25).

In the same source, Ibn Hariz narrated from Ali ibn al-Husayn (may Allah be pleased with him) that he said to a man from the people of Sham: "Have you read the Quran?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Have you not read in [Surah] Bani Isra'il: وَآتِ ذَا الْقُرْبَىٰ حَقَّهُ 'Give the relative his due' (17:26)?" He said: "Are you indeed the relatives whom Allah commanded should be given their due?" He replied: "Yes."

I say: It was narrated in al-Burhan from al-Saduq with his chain from him (peace be upon him), and from al-Tha'labi in his Tafsir from al-Suddi from Ibn al-Daylami from him (peace be upon him).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abd al-Rahman ibn al-Hajjaj, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about His saying: وَلَا تُبَذِّرْ تَبْذِيرًا "and do not squander wastefully" (17:26). He said: "Whoever spends anything in other than the obedience of Allah is a squanderer (مبذر), and whoever spends in the way of good is moderate (مقتصد)."

In the same source, from Abu Basir from him (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "It is a man giving away his wealth and then sitting down without any wealth." I asked: "Can there be squandering (تبذير) in what is permissible (حلال)?" He said: "Yes."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, he said: Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "The destitute (المحسور) is the naked one."

In al-Kafi, with his chain from Ajlan, who said: I was with Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) when a beggar came. He went to a basket containing dates, filled his hand, and gave it to him. Then another came and asked, so he stood up, took some with his hand, and gave it to him. Then another came, and he said: "Allah is our provider and yours."

Then he said: "No one ever asked the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) for anything of this world except that he gave it to him. A woman sent her son to him and said: 'Ask him; if he says "We have nothing," then say: "Give me your shirt."' He said: So he took his shirt and threw it to him—and in another version: and gave it to him. Thus Allah, Blessed and Exalted, disciplined him toward moderation (القصد), saying: وَلَا تَجْعَلْ يَدَكَ مَغْلُولَةً إِلَىٰ عُنُقِكَ وَلَا تَبْسُطْهَا كُلَّ الْبَسْطِ فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُومًا مَحْسُورًا 'Do not keep your hand fastened to your neck, nor open it altogether, or you will sit blamed and destitute' (17:29)." He said: "Destitution (الإحسار) is poverty (الفاقة)."

I say: It was narrated by al-Ayyashi in his Tafsir from Ajlan from him (peace be upon him). The story of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) was narrated by al-Qummi in his Tafsir, and in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abi Hatim from al-Minhal ibn Amr, and from Ibn Jarir al-Tabari from Ibn Mas'ud.

In al-Kafi, with his chain from Mas'adah ibn Sadaqah, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah, exalted is His Name, taught His Prophet how to spend. This was because he had an ounce of gold and disliked that it should stay with him overnight, so he gave it in charity. In the morning, he had nothing left. Someone came to ask him, but he had nothing to give, so the asker blamed him, and he became grieved because he had nothing, for he was merciful and tender-hearted. Thus Allah, Mighty and Majestic, disciplined His Prophet by His command, saying: وَلَا تَجْعَلْ يَدَكَ مَغْلُولَةً إِلَىٰ عُنُقِكَ وَلَا تَبْسُطْهَا كُلَّ الْبَسْطِ فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُومًا مَحْسُورًا 'Do not keep your hand fastened to your neck, nor open it altogether, or you will sit blamed and destitute' (17:29). He says: 'People may ask you and not excuse you; if you give away all the wealth you have, you will have become destitute (حسرت) of wealth.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Ibn Sinan from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَلَا تَجْعَلْ يَدَكَ مَغْلُولَةً إِلَىٰ عُنُقِكَ "Do not keep your hand fastened to your neck", he said: He clenched his hand and said: "Like this." Then regarding: وَلَا تَبْسُطْهَا كُلَّ الْبَسْطِ "nor open it altogether", he opened his palm and said: "Like this."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from Ishaq ibn Ammar from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: I asked: "What is penury (الإملاق)?" He said: "Insolvency (الإفلاس)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Qatadah regarding His saying: وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا الزِّنَىٰ ۖ إِنَّهُ كَانَ فَاحِشَةً "Do not approach fornication. It is indeed an indecency" (17:32). Qatadah narrated from al-Hasan that the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) used to say: "A servant does not commit fornication while he is a believer, nor does he slander while he is a believer, nor does he steal while he is a believer, nor does he drink wine while he is a believer, nor does he embezzle while he is a believer." It was said: "O Messenger of Allah, by Allah, we used to think that he does these things while he is a believer." The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "When he does any of that, faith (الإيمان) is stripped from his heart; if he repents, Allah accepts his repentance."

I say: The tradition is narrated through other paths from Aisha and Abu Hurayrah. It has also been narrated through the paths of the People of the Household (peace be upon them) that the spirit of faith (روح الإيمان) departs from him at that moment.

In al-Kafi, with his chain from Ishaq ibn Ammar, who said: I said to Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him): Allah, Mighty and Majestic, says in His Book: وَمَنْ قُتِلَ مَظْلُومًا فَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا لِوَلِيِّهِ سُلْطَانًا فَلَا يُسْرِفْ فِي الْقَتْلِ ۖ إِنَّهُ كَانَ مَنْصُورًا "and whoever is killed wrongfully, We have certainly given his heir an authority. But let him not commit any excess in killing. He is indeed helped [by the Law]" (17:33). What is this excess (الإسراف) that Allah has prohibited? He said: "He prohibited that anyone other than the killer be killed, or that the killer be mutilated (يمثل)." I asked: "What is the meaning of إِنَّهُ كَانَ مَنْصُورًا 'He is indeed helped'?" He said: "What help is greater than the killer being handed over to the heirs of the slain so that they may kill him, without any liability (تبعة) falling upon them for his killing, neither in religion nor in this world?"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu al-Abbas, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about two men who killed a man. He said: "His heir is given the choice to kill whichever of the two he wishes, and the other must pay half of the blood money (الدية)—meaning the blood money of the slain—which is returned to his progeny. Likewise, if a man kills a woman: if they accept the blood money of the woman, then so be it; but if her heirs refuse anything but the killing of her killer, they must pay half of the man's blood money and then kill him. This is the saying of Allah: فَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا لِوَلِيِّهِ سُلْطَانًا فَلَا يُسْرِفْ فِي الْقَتْلِ 'We have certainly given his heir an authority. But let him not commit any excess in killing'."

I say: There are other narrations with the same meaning as these two. It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan from Zayd ibn Aslam: "In the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), when a man from a tribe killed another, they would not be satisfied until they killed a noble man in his place. If their killer was not noble, they would not kill him but would kill someone else. So they were exhorted regarding that by the saying of Allah: وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا up to His saying فَلَا يُسْرِفْ فِي الْقَتْلِ."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَزِنُوا بِالْقِسْطَاسِ الْمُسْتَقِيمِ "and weigh with an even balance" (17:35). In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "The even balance (القسطاس المستقيم) is the scale that has a tongue (indicator)."

I say: The mention of the "tongue" is to indicate uprightness (الاستقامة), for a two-pan scale is thus.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Amr al-Zubayri, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has enjoined faith (الإيمان) upon the limbs of the children of Adam and distributed it among them. There is no limb among his limbs except that it has been entrusted with a part of faith different from what its sister limb has been entrusted with. Among them are his two eyes with which he sees, and his two feet with which he walks.

So He enjoined upon the eye that it should not look at what Allah has forbidden, and that it should lower its gaze from what Allah has prohibited that is not lawful. This is its action, and it is part of faith. Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said: وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ ۚ إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَالْبَصَرَ وَالْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُولٰئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْئُولًا 'Do not pursue that of which you have no knowledge. Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable' (17:36). This is what was enjoined regarding lowering the gaze from what Allah has forbidden, and it is its action and part of faith.

And Allah enjoined upon the two feet that they should not walk toward any of the acts of disobedience to Allah. Allah enjoined upon them walking in what He has made obligatory, saying: وَلَا تَمْشِ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَخْرِقَ الْأَرْضَ وَلَنْ تَبْلُغَ الْجِبَالَ طُولًا 'Do not walk exultantly on the earth. Indeed you will neither pierce the earth, nor reach the mountains in height' (17:37). And He said: وَاقْصِدْ فِي مَشْيِكَ وَاغْضُضْ مِنْ صَوْتِكَ ۚ إِنَّ أَنْكَرَ الْأَصْوَاتِ لَصَوْتُ الْحَمِيرِ 'Be modest in your stride, and lower your voice. Indeed the most ungainly of voices is the donkey’s voice' (31:19)."

I say: It was narrated in al-Kafi with his chain from Abu Amr al-Zubayri from him (peace be upon him) in a detailed tradition.

In the same source, from Abu Ja'far, who said: I was with Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) when a man said to him: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you! I enter a latrine of mine and I have neighbors who have singing girls who sing and play the lute. Sometimes I prolong my sitting to listen to them." He said: "Do not do that." The man said: "By Allah, I do not go to them; it is only a sound I hear with my ears."

He said to him: "Have you not heard Allah saying: إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَالْبَصَرَ وَالْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُولٰئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْئُولًا 'Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable'?" He said: "Yes, by Allah, it is as if I had never heard this verse from the Book of Allah, neither from a non-Arab nor an Arab. Certainly, I shall not return, if Allah wills, and I seek forgiveness from Allah."

He said: "Stand up, perform a ritual bath (الغسل), and pray as much as you wish, for you were persisting in a grave matter. How wretched would your state have been had you died upon that! Praise Allah and ask Him for repentance from everything He dislikes, for He dislikes nothing but what is ugly. Leave the ugly to its people, for everything has its people."

I say: It was narrated by al-Shaykh in al-Tahdhib from him (peace be upon him), and by al-Kulayni in al-Kafi from Mas'adah ibn Ziyad from him (peace be upon him).

In the same source, from al-Husayn ibn Harun from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَالْبَصَرَ وَالْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُولٰئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْئُولًا "Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable": "The hearing will be questioned about what it heard, the eyesight about what it glanced at, and the heart about what it resolved upon (يعقد عليه)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ "Do not pursue that of which you have no knowledge", he said: "Do not accuse (لا تؤم) anyone of that which you have no knowledge." He said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) said: "Whoever slanders a believing man or woman will be made to stand in the mud of corruption (طينة خبال) until he comes out of what he said."

I say: "The mud of corruption" (طينة خبال) was explained in a narration by Ibn Abi Ya'fur from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him)—as found in al-Kafi—as being the pus that comes out of the private parts of prostitutes. Similar reports have been narrated through Sunni paths from Abu Dharr and Anas from him (peace be upon him and his progeny).

The narrations—as you can see—are based partly on the specific context of the verse and partly on the generality of the underlying reason (التعليل), as was pointed out in the preceding commentary.

Ayahs

أَفَأَصْفَىٰكُمْ رَبُّكُم بِٱلْبَنِينَ وَٱتَّخَذَ مِنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنَـٰثًا ۚ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَقُولُونَ قَوْلًا عَظِيمًا (٤٠)
Did your Lord prefer you for sons, and [Himself] adopt females from among the angels? Indeed, you say a monstrous word! (40)
وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا۟ وَمَا يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُورًا (٤١)
Certainly We have variously paraphrased [the principles of guidance] in this Quran so that they may take admonition, but it increases them only in aversion. (41)
قُل لَّوْ كَانَ مَعَهُۥٓ ءَالِهَةٌ كَمَا يَقُولُونَ إِذًا لَّٱبْتَغَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ ذِى ٱلْعَرْشِ سَبِيلًا (٤٢)
Say, ‘Were there [other] gods besides Him, as they say, they would surely have encroached on the Lord of the Throne. (42)
سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا (٤٣)
Immaculate is He, and greatly exalted above what they say!’ (43)
تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ ٱلسَّبْعُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهِنَّ ۚ وَإِن مِّن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ حَلِيمًا غَفُورًا (٤٤)
