The seven heavens glorify Him, and the earth [too], and whoever is in them. There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification. Indeed, He is all-forbearing, all-forgiving. (44)
وَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ جَعَلْنَا بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حِجَابًا مَّسْتُورًا (٤٥)
When you recite the Quran, We draw a hidden curtain between you and those who do not believe in the Hereafter, (45)
وَجَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا ۚ وَإِذَا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِى ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَحْدَهُۥ وَلَّوْا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَـٰرِهِمْ نُفُورًا (٤٦)
and We cast veils on their hearts, lest they should understand it, and a deafness into their ears. When you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn their backs in aversion. (46)
نَّحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَسْتَمِعُونَ بِهِۦٓ إِذْ يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَإِذْ هُمْ نَجْوَىٰٓ إِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَّسْحُورًا (٤٧)
We know best what they listen for, when they listen to you, and when they hold their secret talks, when the wrongdoers say, ‘[If you follow him] You will be following just a bewitched man.’ (47)
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ فَضَلُّوا۟ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا (٤٨)
Look, how they coin epithets for you; so they go astray, and cannot find a way. (48)
وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا كُنَّا عِظَـٰمًا وَرُفَـٰتًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقًا جَدِيدًا (٤٩)
They say, ‘What, when we have become bones and dust, shall we really be raised in a new creation?’ (49)
۞ قُلْ كُونُوا۟ حِجَارَةً أَوْ حَدِيدًا (٥٠)
Say, ‘[That is bound to happen] even if you should become stones, or iron, (50)
أَوْ خَلْقًا مِّمَّا يَكْبُرُ فِى صُدُورِكُمْ ۚ فَسَيَقُولُونَ مَن يُعِيدُنَا ۖ قُلِ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ۚ فَسَيُنْغِضُونَ إِلَيْكَ رُءُوسَهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هُوَ ۖ قُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ قَرِيبًا (٥١)
or a creature more fantastic to your minds!’ They will say, ‘Who will bring us back?’ Say, ‘He who originated you the first time.’ They will nod their heads at you, and say, ‘When will that be?’ Say, ‘Maybe it is near! (51)
يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِۦ وَتَظُنُّونَ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٥٢)
The day He calls you, you will respond to Him, praising Him, and you will think you remained [in the world] only for a little while.’ (52)
وَقُل لِّعِبَادِى يَقُولُوا۟ ٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ يَنزَغُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ كَانَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ عَدُوًّا مُّبِينًا (٥٣)
Tell My servants to speak in a manner which is the best. Indeed Satan incites ill feeling between them, and Satan is indeed man’s manifest enemy. (53)
رَّبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ ۖ إِن يَشَأْ يَرْحَمْكُمْ أَوْ إِن يَشَأْ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ ۚ وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا (٥٤)
Your Lord knows you best. He will have mercy on you if He wishes, or punish you, if He wishes, and We did not send you to watch over them. (54)
وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَلَقَدْ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ زَبُورًا (٥٥)
Your Lord knows best whoever is in the heavens and the earth. Certainly, We gave some prophets an advantage over the others, and We gave David the Psalms. (55)

(Commentary)

The verses continue the discussion on monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and rebuke the polytheists (المشركين) for taking gods besides Him and for attributing femininity to the noble angels. They also address their failure to take heed of the arguments for divine unity presented in the Quran, their lack of understanding of the signs (الآيات), and their mockery of the Messenger and the matter of the Resurrection (البعث), as well as their evil speech regarding Allah’s command and other matters.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَأَصْفاكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ بِالْبَنِينَ وَاتَّخَذَ مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ إِناثاً إِنَّكُمْ لَتَقُولُونَ قَوْلاً عَظِيماً — "What! Has your Lord distinguished you with sons, and taken for Himself daughters from among the angels? Indeed, you say a monstrous thing!" (17:40). Distinguishing (الإصفاء) means making something pure or exclusive. It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: "You say: 'I distinguished (أصفيت) so-and-so with something' when you favor them with it." End quote.

This is an address to those among them who say that the angels are the daughters of Allah, or that some of them are His daughters. The interrogative form is for denunciation (الإنكار). Perhaps He substituted "daughters" with "females" (إناثاً) because they considered femininity to be a trait of baseness.

The meaning is: If He, the Glorified, is your Lord and there is no lord besides Him, and He is the one who governs the affairs of all things, do you then claim that He favored you with a dignity He did not grant Himself? That is, that He reserved sons for you while taking for Himself only females as children—namely the noble angels whom you claim are female? Indeed, you are uttering a monstrous saying in terms of the evil consequences it entails.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنا فِي هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا وَما يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُوراً — "Certainly We have variously dispensed [the truths] in this Quran so that they may take admonition, but it only increases them in aversion." (17:41). It is stated in al-Mufradat: Dispensing (الصرف) is the turning of a thing from one state to another or replacing it with something else. He said: al-Tasrif (التصريف) is like al-Sarf except it implies intensification (التكثير). It is most often used for turning a thing from one state to another or from one matter to another. The "dispensing of the winds" (تصريف الرياح) is their turning from one state to another. The Exalted said: وَصَرَّفْنَا الآيات ("and We have variously dispensed the signs") and وَصَرَّفْنا فِيهِ مِنَ الْوَعِيدِ ("and variously dispensed in it the threats"). From this comes the "inflection of speech" (تصريف الكلام) and the "exchange of coins" (تصريف الدراهم). End quote.

He also said: Aversion (النفر) is the agitation away from a thing or toward a thing, like fleeing toward or away from something. It is said: "He turned away from the thing in aversion (نفوراً)." The Exalted said: ما زادَهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُوراً ("it only increased them in aversion") and وَما يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُوراً ("and it only increases them in aversion"). End quote.

Thus, His saying: وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنا فِي هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا means, as evidenced by the context: "I swear, We have repeated the discourse with them regarding the matter of monotheism and the negation of partners from one perspective to another, and We have shifted it from one tone to another in this Quran. We have presented it in various expressions and explained it through different modes of exposition so that they might take heed and the truth might become clear to them."

And His saying: وَما يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُوراً means that this varied exposition only increases their agitation; whenever a new explanation is initiated, it causes them a new sense of aversion.

In this verse, there is a transition (التفات) from direct address to the third person (الغيبة), serving as a notification that they are no longer fit for direct address and speech given that this is their state.

It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: "If it is asked: 'If it was known that they would increase in aversion upon the revelation of the Quran, what was the meaning of its revelation? And what was the wisdom (الحكمة) in it?'"

"It is replied: The wisdom in it is the establishment of the proof (إلزام الحجة) and the removal of any excuse by manifesting the evidences that justify religious obligation (التكليف). Furthermore, upon its revelation, a group of people becomes righteous who would not have been so had it not been revealed. Had it not been revealed, those who turn away from faith would have fallen into a corruption even greater than this aversion. Thus, wisdom necessitated its revelation for these reasons. They only increased in aversion upon witnessing the signs and evidences because of their belief that these were mere fallacies and tricks, and due to their lack of reflection upon them." End quote.

His statement that "had it not been revealed, they would have fallen into a corruption greater than aversion" is not without its issues. For the increase in aversion leads them to denial (الجحود), stubborn opposition to the truth, and barring others from it—and there is no corruption greater than this in the context of the divine call (الدعوة).

However, it should be known that while disbelief (الكفر), denial, aversion to the truth, and stubbornness harm those who possess them and lead them to destruction, they also benefit the people of faith, those who are pleased with the truth and submit to it. For if these beautiful traits and noble qualities did not have their opposites, their own reality would not be realized. Understand this.

Thus, it is necessary in divine wisdom that the proof be completed, and then increased in its completion, until the wretched one (الشقي) manifests all the wretchedness within his capacity, and the felicitous (السعداء) attain, through their various efforts, the degrees that correspond to the depths of the wretched. The Exalted has said: كُلًّا نُمِدُّ هؤُلاءِ وَهَؤُلاءِ مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً — "To these and those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined." (17:20).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَوْ كانَ مَعَهُ آلِهَةٌ كَما يَقُولُونَ إِذاً لَابْتَغَوْا إِلى ذِي الْعَرْشِ سَبِيلاً — "Say, ‘Were there [other] gods with Him, as they say, they would have surely sought a way to the Lord of the Throne.’" (17:42). He turned away from addressing them directly and directed the speech to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), commanding him to speak to them regarding monotheism and the negation of partners. What they claim is that there are gods besides Allah who govern various aspects of the world according to their ranks, and each of them is a lord (رب) over what he governs, such as the god of the heavens, the god of the earth, the god of war, or the god of the Quraysh.

Since they would be partners in governance, each would possess sovereignty (الملك) according to his lordship. Sovereignty is a consequence of creation (الخلق), which is exclusive to Him, the Glorified—even according to their own belief (1). Thus, sovereignty would be something that could potentially be held by someone other than the Exalted. Since the love for sovereignty and dominion is inherent to every existent, they would necessarily seek to contest His kingdom and wrest it from His hand so that one of them might be unique in sovereignty and dominion, distinguished by might and hegemony. Exalted is Allah far above that.

The summary of the argument is: If there were gods with Him as they say, and it were possible for someone other than the Exalted to attain any part of His kingdom—which is a necessary concomitant of His essence that overflows (الفياضة) upon all things—and since the love for sovereignty and dominion is ingrained in every existent by necessity, those gods would have sought to attain His kingdom, depose Him from His Throne, and add kingdom to their own kingdom out of their necessary love for that. However, there is no way for anyone to reach Him; Exalted is He above that.

Thus, His saying: إِذاً لَابْتَغَوْا إِلى ذِي الْعَرْشِ سَبِيلاً means they would have sought a way to Him to overcome Him in His sovereignty. Describing Him, the Exalted, as the "Lord of the Throne" (ذِي الْعَرْشِ)—which is an attribute specific to a king—indicates that their seeking a way to Him would only be because He is the Lord of the Throne, seeking a way to His Throne to establish themselves upon it.

From this, it becomes clear that the opinion of some—that the argument in this verse is identical in meaning to the argument in His saying: لَوْ كانَ فِيهِما آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللهُ لَفَسَدَتا ("Had there been any gods in them other than Allah, they would surely have fallen apart" [21:22])—is misplaced.

This is because the two arguments differ in their premises. The argument in the current verse proceeds toward the negation of a partner from the perspective of the gods seeking a way to the Lord of the Throne and their desire for dominance by seizing sovereignty from Him. The argument in the verse of al-Anbiya’ proceeds from the perspective that the difference in the essences of the gods would lead to a difference in their governance, which would lead to the corruption of the cosmic order. In truth, the argument here is distinct from the one in al-Anbiya’. The one that is close to the argument in al-Anbiya’ is His saying: إِذاً لَذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلهٍ بِما خَلَقَ وَلَعَلا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ ("...otherwise each god would have taken away what he had created, and some of them would have surely risen up against others" [23:91]).

Similarly, what is narrated from some early exegetes—that "seeking a way to the Lord of the Throne" means seeking proximity and nearness to Him due to His loftiness over them—is also misplaced. Their explanation of the argument was: "If there were gods with Him as they say, they would have sought proximity to Him and nearness before Him due to their knowledge of His loftiness and greatness; and one who is in such a state cannot be a god, therefore they are not gods."

This is misplaced because the context testifies against it, such as His describing Himself as the "Lord of the Throne" and His subsequent saying: سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ ("Immaculate is He and exalted is He..."). This clearly indicates that the existence of the gods they assume entails a grave danger that the presence of Greatness and Majesty cannot tolerate—such as His kingdom being subject to the seeking of a way against it, the encroachment of others upon it, and it not being inherently resistant to being seized or transferred to those below Him.

His saying, the Exalted: سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ عُلُوًّا كَبِيراً — "Immaculate is He and exalted is He high above what they say!" (17:43). Exaltation (التعالي) is reaching the utmost height. For this reason, the absolute object (المفعول المطلق), namely "height" (عُلُوًّا), is described as "great" (كَبِيراً). The sentence is in the sense of "He exalted Himself with a great exaltation." The verse is a glorification of Him, the Exalted, far above what they say regarding the existence of gods and the possibility of His kingdom and lordship being attained by others.

His saying, the Exalted: تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ السَّماواتُ السَّبْعُ وَالْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيهِنَّ وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلكِنْ لا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ — "The seven heavens glorify Him, and the earth [too], and whoever is in them. There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification..." (17:44). This verse and the one before it, though they occur in a context of magnification—like His saying: وَقالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللهُ وَلَداً سُبْحانَهُ ("They say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!")—they also contribute to the preceding argument. They serve as a completing premise for His saying: لَوْ كانَ مَعَهُ آلِهَةٌ كَما يَقُولُونَ ("Were there [other] gods with Him..."). The argument is essentially a disjunctive syllogism (قياس استثنائي), and the part that serves as the exception (الاستثناء) is the glorification of all things for Him, the Glorified, in this verse. It is as if it were said: "If there were gods with Him, His kingdom would be subject to contest and encroachment, but the kingdom—consisting of the heavens, the earth, and whoever is in them—sanctifies Him from that and bears witness that He has no partner in sovereignty. For they do not originate except from Him, do not end except in Him, do not subsist except through Him, and do not bow in prostration except to Him. Thus, none possesses sovereignty or is fit for it except Him; there is no lord besides Him."

It is possible that both verses—namely His saying: سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ عُلُوًّا كَبِيراً and تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ السَّماواتُ—together constitute the exception. The meaning would be: "If there were gods with Him, they would have sought to overcome Him and depose Him from His kingdom, but He, the Glorified, sanctifies His own essence from that through His overflowing essence (الذات الفياضة) by which everything subsists and which lordship necessarily accompanies without ever departing from Him or transferring to another. Likewise, His kingdom—the world of the heavens, the earth, and whoever is in them—sanctifies Him through their very essences which glorify Him, as they are essentially subsistent through Him; were they to be severed or veiled from Him for the blink of an eye, they would perish and cease to exist. Therefore, there are no gods with Him, nor is His kingdom and lordship something that another could seek." Reflect upon this.

In any case, His saying: تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ السَّماواتُ السَّبْعُ وَالْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيهِنَّ establishes glorification (التسبيح) for the parts of the manifest world, and that they glorify Allah and sanctify Him from the partners they claim and attribute to Him.

Glorification (التسبيح) is a verbal, linguistic sanctification. The reality of speech (الكلام) is the disclosure of what is in the mind through some form of indication and pointing. However, since man could not find a way to indicate everything he intends through the medium of physical creation (التكوين), he resorted to using words (الألفاظ)—which are sounds conventionalized for meanings—and indicated what was in his mind through them. This became the custom of communication and understanding. Sometimes he seeks help for some of his objectives by gesturing with his hand, his head, or otherwise; and sometimes he seeks help through writing or setting up a sign.

In short, that by which an intended meaning is disclosed is "saying" (قول) and "speech" (كلام), and the undertaking of this disclosure by a thing is a "saying" and "speaking" from it, even if it is not through an audible sound or a conventionalized word. Evidence for this includes what the Quran attributes to Him, the Exalted, of speech, saying, command, revelation (الوحي), and the like, which contain the meaning of disclosing intentions but are not of the same nature as the speech and words familiar to us who speak various languages; yet Allah, the Glorified, has named it "saying" and "speech."

These manifest existents—the heavens, the earth, and whoever is in them—possess that which discloses with explicit clarity the oneness of their Lord in His lordship and sanctifies Him, the Exalted, from every deficiency and blemish. Thus, they glorify Allah, the Glorified.

This is because they possess nothing in themselves but pure need and absolute poverty (الفاقة) toward Him in their essence, attributes, and states. Need is the strongest discloser of that toward which the need is directed; the needy cannot be independent of it nor detached from it. Thus, each of these existents discloses, through its need in its existence and its deficiency in its essence, its Originator who is Self-Sufficient (الغني) in His existence and Perfect in His essence. Through its connection to other existents—which it uses to complete its existence and remove its essential deficiencies—it discloses that its Originator is its Lord, the Disposer of all things, the Governor of its affairs.

Furthermore, the general order (النظام العام) prevailing in all things, which gathers their fragments and links them together, discloses the oneness of their Originator. It shows that He is the One to whom, in His oneness, all things return, and through whom, in His oneness, all needs and deficiencies are removed. Nothing besides Him is free from need, and nothing other than Him is devoid of deficiency. He is the Lord, there is no lord besides Him; the Self-Sufficient with whom there is no poverty; and the Perfection in which there is no flaw.

Thus, every one of these existents discloses, through its need and deficiency, the sanctification of its Lord from need and His innocence from deficiency. Even the ignorant one who affirms partners for his Lord besides Him, or attributes to Him some deficiency or blemish—exalted and sanctified is He—thereby affirms His sanctification from partners and attributes to Him innocence from deficiency. For the meaning conceived in the mind of this person, the word uttered by his tongue, and everything he employed to convey this intention—all of these are existing things that disclose, through their existential need, a single Lord who has no partner and no deficiency.

The case of such a denier, in that his denial is actually an admission, is like a person who claims that there is no speaking human being in the world and testifies to that through speech. His testimony is the strongest proof against what he claimed and testified to. Whenever the testimony is repeated in this manner and the witnesses increase, the proof is strengthened by way of the testimony against itself.

If you say: "Mere disclosure of sanctification is not called glorification (تسبيح) unless it is accompanied by intention (القصد), and intention is something that depends on life (الحياة). Most of these existents lack life, such as the earth, the heavens, and various types of inanimate matter. Therefore, there is no escape from taking 'glorification' as a metaphor (المجاز), meaning their existence points to the sanctification of their Lord."

I say: His speech, the Exalted, suggests that knowledge (العلم) permeates all existents along with the permeation of creation. Thus, each of them has a share of knowledge according to its share of existence. This does not necessitate that all are equal in terms of knowledge, or identical in terms of genus and species, or that each possesses what man possesses, or that man understands the knowledge they have. The Exalted said, narrating from the limbs of man: قالُوا أَنْطَقَنَا اللهُ الَّذِي أَنْطَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ ("They will say, ‘Allah gave us speech, [He] who gives speech to all things’" [41:21]). And He said: فَقالَ لَها وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً قالَتا أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ ("...and said to it and to the earth: ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come willingly’" [41:11]). The verses in this sense are many, and an independent discussion on this will reach you, Allah willing.

If this is the case, then there is no created existent but that it perceives itself with some perception, and it intends by its existence to manifest its needy and deficient self, which is encompassed by the self-sufficiency and perfection of its Lord—there is no lord besides Him. Thus, it glorifies its Lord and sanctifies Him from partners and from every deficiency attributed to Him.

By this, it becomes clear that there is no reason to interpret "glorification" in the verse as mere metaphorical pointing. Metaphor is not resorted to except when literal interpretation is impossible. Similar to this is the claim of some that the glorification of some of these existents is literal and verbal (قالي حقيقي)—such as the glorification of the angels and the believers among men—while the glorification of others is metaphorical and situational (حالي مجازي)—such as the pointing of inanimate objects through their existence to Him, the Exalted—and that the word "glorification" is used in the verse as a general metaphor. You have just seen the weakness of this view.

The truth is that glorification in all of them is literal and verbal (حقيقي قالي), except that its being verbal does not necessitate it being through conventionalized words and audible sounds, as previously indicated. A discussion on "speech" (الكلام) that is beneficial for this context was presented at the end of the second volume of this book.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ السَّماواتُ السَّبْعُ وَالْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيهِنَّ establishes for them a literal glorification, which is their speaking through their existence and its connection to all other existing things, and their explanation of the sanctification of their Lord from the partners and aspects of deficiency that the polytheists attribute to Him.

And His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ generalizes the glorification to every single thing. The previous sentence had enumerated the seven heavens, the earth, and whoever is in them; this adds to it by mentioning "praise" (الحمد) along with "glorification." It signifies that everything, just as it glorifies Him, also praises Him by extolling His beautiful attributes and acts.

This is because just as each of these things has an element of need and deficiency returning to itself, it also possesses an element of His beautiful handiwork and His blessing returning to Him, the Exalted, bestowed from His presence. Just as the manifestation of these things for themselves in existence is a manifestation of their need and deficiency and a disclosure of the sanctification of their Lord from need and deficiency—which is their glorification—so too their bringing themselves forth is a bringing forth of the beautiful act of their Lord that lies behind His beautiful attributes. This is their praise. For praise is nothing but the extolling of a beautiful voluntary act. Thus, they praise their Lord just as they glorify Him, and that is His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ.

In other words: if things are considered from the perspective of their disclosing what is with their Lord by manifesting their own need and deficiency, while possessing perception of that, it is "glorification" from them. If they are considered from the perspective of their disclosing what belongs to their Lord by manifesting the blessing of existence and other aspects of perfection they possess, it is "praise" from them for their Lord. If their disclosure of Allah’s attributes of beauty (جمال) or majesty (جلال) is considered regardless of their knowledge and perception of what they disclose, it is "pointing" (دلالة) from them to Him, the Exalted, and they are His "signs" (آياته).

This is an excellent witness that the meaning of "glorification" in the verse is not mere pointing to Him, the Exalted, by negating partners and aspects of deficiency. For the address in His saying: وَلكِنْ لا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ ("...but you do not understand their glorification") is either to the polytheists or to people in general, including both believers and polytheists. In either case, they do understand the pointing of things to their Creator, yet the verse negates their understanding (الفقه).

One should not listen to the claim of those who say: "The address is to the polytheists, and because of their lack of reflection and benefit from it, their understanding was treated as non-existent." Nor to the claim of those who assert: "Because they do not understand some of what is meant by glorification, they were treated as those who do not understand any of it, by way of predominance (تغليباً)."

This is because treating understanding as non-existent or treating the part as the whole does not suit the context of argumentation (الاحتجاج), and He, the Glorified, is addressing them in the preceding verse with the proof for sanctification. Furthermore, this type of laxity through "predominance" and the like is not tolerated by His speech, the Exalted.

As for what occurs in His saying after this verse: وَإِذا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ ("And when you recite the Quran...") until the end of the verses regarding the negation of understanding from the polytheists, it does not support what they mentioned. For those verses negate their understanding of the Quran, which is different from negating the understanding of the pointing of things to His sanctification, as the proof is established against them through the latter.

The truth is that the glorification established by the verse for everything is glorification in its literal sense. Its attribution to the heavens, the earth, and whoever and whatever is in them is repeated in His speech, the Exalted, in contexts that can only be taken literally, such as His saying: وَسَخَّرْنا مَعَ داوُدَ الْجِبالَ يُسَبِّحْنَ وَالطَّيْرَ ("...and We disposed the mountains to celebrate Our glory with David, and the birds [as well]" [21:79]), and His saying: إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا الْجِبالَ مَعَهُ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِشْراقِ ("Indeed We disposed the mountains to celebrate Our glory with him at nightfall and sunrise" [38:18]). Close to this is His saying: يا جِبالُ أَوِّبِي مَعَهُ وَالطَّيْرَ ("O mountains, chime in with him, and you [too] O birds!" [34:10]). There is no meaning in interpreting these as "glorification through the language of the state" (لسان الحال).

Narrations have reached the level of profusion (استفاضت) through both Shi‘i and Sunni chains that things possess glorification. Among them are the narrations of the glorification of the pebbles in the palm of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and some of them will reach you in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

And His saying: إِنَّهُ كانَ حَلِيماً غَفُوراً — "Indeed He is All-forbearing, All-forgiving." (17:44). That is, He grants respite and does not hasten with punishment, and He forgives whoever repents and returns to Him. In these two attributes is a proof of His sanctification from every deficiency. For the necessity of forbearance (الحلم) is that He does not fear losing the opportunity [to punish], and the necessity of forgiveness (المغفرة) is that He is not harmed by forgiveness nor by the overflowing of mercy. Thus, His kingdom and lordship accept neither deficiency nor cessation.

It has been said regarding the reason for this concluding phrase that it is an indication that man—in his failure to understand this glorification which every thing continues to be occupied with, even his own self through all the pillars of its existence, in the most eloquent manner—is in error and deserves to be taken to task for it. However, Allah, the Glorified, through His forbearance and forgiveness, does not hasten him and pardons that if He wills.

This is a good explanation, and its implication is that it is within man's capacity to understand this glorification from himself and from others. Perhaps we will be successful in explaining this, Allah willing, in a suitable place.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ جَعَلْنا بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ حِجاباً مَسْتُوراً — "When you recite the Quran, We draw between you and those who do not believe in the Hereafter a hidden curtain." (17:45). The apparent meaning of describing the curtain (الحجاب) as "hidden" (مَسْتُوراً) is that it is a curtain hidden from the senses, unlike the common curtains used among people to hide one thing from another. It is a spiritual curtain drawn between the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—insofar as he is the reciter and bearer of the Quran—and the polytheists who do not believe in the Hereafter. it veils him from them so they cannot understand the reality of the Quranic knowledge he possesses and believe in it, nor can they acknowledge that he is a Messenger from Allah who came to them with the truth. Therefore, they turn away from him when Allah alone is mentioned, go to extremes in denying the Resurrection, and accuse him of being a bewitched man. The following verses support this meaning.

He described the polytheists as الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ ("those who do not believe in the Hereafter") because the denial of the Hereafter renders faith in Allah alone and in the Message (الرسالة) futile. Thus, disbelief in the Resurrection necessitates disbelief in all the principles of religion (أصول الدين), and also to serve as a prelude to what will be mentioned of their denial of the Resurrection.

The meaning is: When you recite the Quran and intone it to them, We place between you and the polytheists who do not believe in the Hereafter—and in describing them as such is a condemnation of them—a spiritual curtain hidden from their understanding. Thus, they are unable to hear His mention alone, nor to recognize you through the true Message, nor to believe in the Resurrection and understand its reality.

The scholars have other opinions regarding His saying: حِجاباً مَسْتُوراً. Some say that the passive participle (مفعول) here is for attribution, meaning "a curtain possessing a covering" (ذا ستر), similar to their saying: "a moist man" (رجل مرطوب), "a terrifying place" (مكان مهول), or "a coquettish girl" (جارية مغنوجة), meaning one possessing moisture, terror, or coquetry. From this is His saying, the Exalted: وَعْدُهُ مَأْتِيًّا ("His promise is bound to be fulfilled [literally: to be come to]"), meaning "possessing fulfillment." However, the most common form for this is the active participle (فاعل).

It is narrated from al-Akhfash that the passive participle (مفعول) sometimes occurs with the meaning of the active participle (فاعل), like maymun (ميمون) and mash’um (مشئوم) meaning "auspicious" and "unlucky" [literally: "bringing good/bad luck"]. Just as the active participle sometimes occurs with the meaning of the passive participle, like "gushing water" (ماء دافق) meaning "poured out" (مدفوق). Thus, "hidden" (مستور) means "hiding" (ساتر).

Some say it is a case of metaphorical attribution (الإسناد المجازي), and that which is hidden in reality is what is behind the curtain, not the curtain itself.

Some say it is of the type of "omission and connection" (الحذف والإيصال), and its origin is "a curtain by which the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is hidden from them."

It is also said: The meaning is a curtain hidden by another curtain, i.e., by multiple curtains. And it is said: The meaning is a curtain whose being a curtain is hidden, meaning they do not know that they do not know. These last three are the weakest of views.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَنْ يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفي آذانِهِمْ وَقْراً وَإِذا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ وَحْدَهُ وَلَّوْا عَلى أَدْبارِهِمْ نُفُوراً — "And We cast veils on their hearts lest they should understand it, and a deafness into their ears. And when you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn their backs in aversion." (17:46). Veils (الأكنة) is the plural of kinn with a kasrah, which according to al-Raghib is that in which a thing is preserved and by which it is hidden from others. Deafness (الوقر) is heaviness in hearing. It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: Aversion (النفور) is the plural of "one who turns away" (نافر), and this plural is the standard for every active participle derived from a verb whose verbal noun (مصدر) is on the pattern of fu‘ul, like ruku‘, sujud, and shuhud. End quote.

His saying: وَجَعَلْنا عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً etc., is like an explanation for the curtain mentioned previously. That is: We covered their hearts with coverings and veils lest they should understand the Quran, and We placed in their ears a deafness and heaviness lest they should hear it. Thus, they do not hear the Quran with the hearing of acceptance, nor do they understand it with the understanding of faith and affirmation. All of this is a recompense for them for their disbelief and transgression.

And His saying: وَإِذا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ وَحْدَهُ means: on the description of monotheism and the negation of partners, they turned their backs in aversion and turned away from it, retreating.

His saying, the Exalted: نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَسْتَمِعُونَ بِهِ إِذْ يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ — "We know best what they listen for when they listen to you, and when they conspire privately, when the wrongdoers say, ‘You are just following a bewitched man.’" (17:47). Private conspiracy (النجوى) is a verbal noun, and therefore it can describe the singular, dual, plural, masculine, and feminine without changing its form.

The verse is like a proof for what was mentioned in the previous verse—that He placed veils on their hearts lest they understand it and deafness in their ears. Thus, His saying: نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَسْتَمِعُونَ بِهِ refers to the placing of deafness, and His saying: وَإِذْ هُمْ نَجْوى refers to the placing of veils.

The Exalted says: We know best their ears with which they listen to you and their hearts with which they look into your affair—and how could it be otherwise? For He, the Exalted, is their Creator and the Governor of their affairs. Thus, His informing that He placed veils on their hearts and deafness in their ears is most truthful and most worthy of acceptance. So, We know best that with which they listen, which is their ears, at the time they listen to you. And We know best—meaning their hearts—when they are in private conspiracy, as they whisper to one another, avoiding speaking out loud or raising their voices, while they are forming an opinion, when the wrongdoers say—meaning those among them who speak while being unjust in their speech—"You are just following a bewitched man." This is a confirmation that they did not understand the truth.

In the verse, there is an indication, or rather a proof, that they used to not come to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to listen to the Quran openly for fear of blame. Rather, they would come undercover and in secret, until when they saw one another in this state, they would blame each other in private conspiracy, fearing that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers would sense their presence. Then some would say to others: "You are just following a bewitched man." This supports what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) in this sense, and we will present it in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: انْظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلاً — "Look, how they coin similitudes for you! So they have gone astray, and cannot find a way." (17:48). Similitude (المثل) here means description, and "coining similitudes" means attributing qualities. The meaning of the verse is clear, and it signifies that there is no hope for their faith, as the Exalted said: وَسَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ ("It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not believe" [36:10]).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا أَإِذا كُنَّا عِظاماً وَرُفاتاً أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقاً جَدِيداً — "They say, ‘What, when we have become bones and decayed [matter], shall we really be raised amongst a new creation?’" (17:49). It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: Decayed matter (الرفات) is whatever is broken and worn out of anything. The pattern fu‘al is frequent for everything that is crushed or pounded, such as hutam (debris), duqaq (fragments), and turab (dust). Al-Mubarrad said: "Everything pounded to the extreme until it becomes powder is rufat." End quote.

The verse continues the explanation of their lack of understanding of Quranic knowledge, as they found the Resurrection (البعث) improbable, though it is among the most important things the Quran establishes and the clearest of what has been proven through revelation and reason—to the point that Allah described it in places in His speech as that in which لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ("there is no doubt"). They have no proof for its negation other than their mere deeming it improbable.

Among the greatest things that make this improbability seem fair in their hearts is their assumption that death is the annihilation of man, and it is improbable for a thing to be formed from sheer non-existence. As they said: "When we have become bones and decayed matter through the corruption of our bodies due to death, until nothing remains of them but bones, and then the bones decay and become dust—shall we then be in a new creation, returning as humans as we were? That is a far-off return." Therefore, the Glorified refuted them by reminding them of Absolute Power and the first creation, as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ كُونُوا حِجارَةً أَوْ حَدِيداً أَوْ خَلْقاً مِمَّا يَكْبُرُ فِي صُدُورِكُمْ — "Say, ‘Be you stones or iron, or a creature more improbable [to be raised] in your minds!’" (17:50-51). This is a response to their deeming it improbable. They had expressed themselves by saying: "When we have become [bones]...", so the Glorified commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to command them with a command of subjugation (أمر تسخير) to be stones or iron, etc.—things whose transformation into a human is, in their view, even further and more difficult than the transformation of decayed bones into one.

This is an indication that the Absolute Divine Power is not challenged by anything it intends to recreate, whether it be bones and dust, or stones, or iron, or anything else.

The meaning is: Say to them: "Be something even harder than bones and dust—stones or iron, or another creature from among the things that seem great in your minds and whose transformation into a human you find extremely improbable—be whatever you wish, for Allah will surely restore your first creation to you and raise you."

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَيَقُولُونَ مَنْ يُعِيدُنا قُلِ الَّذِي فَطَرَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ — "Then they will say, ‘Who will bring us back?’ Say, ‘He who created you the first time.’" (17:51). That is: when you answer their improbability by stating they will be raised regardless of what they become or what state they transform into, they will ask you and say: "Who will bring us back to what we were of human creation?" Then mention Allah, the Glorified, to them, and remind them of His attribute that leaves no room for their improbability—namely His creating them the first time when they were nothing. Say: "He who created you the first time will bring you back."

Thus, in substituting the Name of Majesty with the phrase الَّذِي فَطَرَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ, there is an affirmation of possibility and a removal of improbability by showing the precedent.

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَيُنْغِضُونَ إِلَيْكَ رُؤُسَهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هُوَ قُلْ عَسى أَنْ يَكُونَ قَرِيباً — "Then they will shake their heads at you and say, ‘When will it be?’ Say, ‘Maybe it is near.’" (17:51). Al-Raghib said: Shaking (الإنغاض) is moving the head toward another like one who is amazed by something. End quote.

The meaning is: When you confront them with the proof and remind them of Allah’s power over all things and His creating them the first time, you will find them moving their heads toward you with the movement of one who mocks, belittles you, and holds you in contempt, saying: "When will it be?" Say: "Maybe it is near," for there is no way to know its [exact time], as it is from the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — which none knows but Allah. However, the description of the Day is known through the information of the Exalted; therefore, He described it for them, placing the description in the place of the time, saying: يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِ ("The day He will call you, and you will respond with His praise...").

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِ وَتَظُنُّونَ إِنْ لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — "The day He will call you, and you will respond with His praise, and you will think you have stayed only a little while." (17:52). "The day" (يَوْمَ) is in the accusative case due to a hidden verb, i.e., "you will be raised on such-and-such a day." The "call" (الدعوة) is His command to them to rise for the Day of Recompense, and their "response" (استجابتهم) is their acceptance of the divine call. His saying: بِحَمْدِهِ ("with His praise") is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the subject of "you respond." The meaning is: you will respond while being clothed in His praise, i.e., praising Him, considering the Resurrection and restoration from Him to be a beautiful act for which the Doer is praised and extolled. This is because the realities will be disclosed to you on that day, and it will become clear to you that it was necessary in divine wisdom that people be raised for recompense and that there be a second life after the first.

And His saying: وَتَظُنُّونَ إِنْ لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً means: you will assume on the Day of Resurrection that you did not stay in the graves after death except for a short time, and you will see that the Day was very near to you.

Allah has confirmed them in this assumption and that their error was only in the duration they estimated. The Exalted said: قالَ إِنْ لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً لَوْ أَنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ ("He will say, ‘You have stayed only a little, if you only knew’" [23:114]). And He said: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ما لَبِثُوا غَيْرَ ساعَةٍ كَذلِكَ كانُوا يُؤْفَكُونَ وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَالْإِيمانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتابِ اللهِ إِلى يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ فَهذا يَوْمُ الْبَعْثِ ("And on the day when the Hour sets in, the guilty will swear that they had stayed no more than an hour. Thus they were used to being perverted. But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you have stayed in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection. So this is the Day of Resurrection...’" [30:55-56]), among other verses.

In mentioning وَتَظُنُّونَ إِنْ لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً, there is an allusion to them regarding their deeming the Day slow to arrive and their mockery of it, and a support for what preceded regarding the hope of its nearness in His saying: قُلْ عَسى أَنْ يَكُونَ قَرِيباً, i.e., that you will soon consider it near. Likewise, in His saying: فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِ, there is an allusion to them regarding their mockery of it and their amazement at it, i.e., that you will praise Him on the Day of Resurrection while today you deem it improbable and mock its matter.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِعِبادِي يَقُولُوا الَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ يَنْزَغُ بَيْنَهُمْ — "Tell My servants to speak that which is best. Indeed Satan incites ill feeling between them..." (17:53). It appears from the context that "My servants" refers to the believers; thus, the possessive construction is for honor. His saying: قُلْ لِعِبادِي يَقُولُوا means "command them to speak," so it is a command and the response to a command in the jussive mood. And His saying: الَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ means "the word that is best," which includes beautiful etiquette and is free from harshness, insult, and evil speech.

This verse and the two following it share a single context. The summary of their content is the command to speak well and to adhere to beautiful etiquette in speech to guard against the incitement of Satan. They should also know that the matter belongs to the will of Allah, not to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), such that the pen would be lifted from everyone who believes in him and associates with him, making them automatically entitled to felicity, saying whatever they wish and depriving others of all good while speaking ill of them. Man has nothing but the goodness of his inner self and the perfection of his etiquette. Allah has favored some prophets over others and specifically gave David the Psalms (الزبور), which contain the best of speech and beautiful praise for Allah, the Glorified.

From this, it appears that the believers before the Hijrah (الهجرة) — the migration of the Prophet from Mecca to Medina — would sometimes debate with the polytheists and use harsh language, speaking roughly to them and perhaps confronting them with the claim that they were the people of the Fire while the believers were the people of Paradise by the blessing of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). This would provoke the polytheists against them, increase their enmity, and drive them to go to extremes in their persecution, torture, and harm of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and their stubbornness against the truth.

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to command them to speak that which is best. The context is suitable for this, as the preceding verses narrated the ill-etiquette of the polytheists toward the Prophet, calling him a bewitched man and mocking the Quran and its knowledge of the Origin and the Return. This is the reason for the connection of these three verses to what preceded them and the connection of the three to one another. Understand this.

So His saying: وَقُلْ لِعِبادِي يَقُولُوا الَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ is a command to give a command, and the commanded act is to speak the word that is best. It is similar to His saying: وَجادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ ("...and dispute with them in a manner that is best" [16:125]). His saying: إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ يَنْزَغُ بَيْنَهُمْ is the justification for the command, and His saying: إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ كانَ لِلْإِنْسانِ عَدُوًّا مُبِيناً ("Indeed Satan is a manifest enemy to man") is the justification for Satan’s incitement between them.

It has been said that the meaning of "speaking that which is best" is to refrain from fighting the polytheists and to treat them with peace, and the address is to the believers in Mecca before the Hijrah. Thus, the verse is similar to His saying: وَقُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْناً ("...and speak kindly to people" [2:83]), according to what is narrated regarding the occasions of revelation. However, you are aware that the context of the justification in the verse does not suit this.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِنْ يَشَأْ يَرْحَمْكُمْ أَوْ إِنْ يَشَأْ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلاً — "Your Lord knows you best: if He wishes, He will have mercy on you, or if He wishes, He will punish you. We did not send you to be their guardian." (17:54). It was mentioned that this verse and the one after it are a continuation of the previous context. Accordingly, the beginning of the verse is part of the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) which he was commanded to deliver to the believers by His saying: قُلْ لِعِبادِي يَقُولُوا, while the end of the verse is an address specifically to the Prophet; thus, there is no transition (التفات) in the speech.

It is also possible that the address at the beginning of the verse is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers together, by giving predominance to his address over their absence. This is more suitable to the context of the previous verse and the continuity of the discourse, and all of it is the speech of Allah.

In any case, His saying: رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِنْ يَشَأْ يَرْحَمْكُمْ أَوْ إِنْ يَشَأْ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ serves as a second justification for the previous command. it signifies that the believers must refrain from harsh speech toward others and from passing judgment on what Allah knows best—namely felicity or wretchedness—such as saying: "So-and-so is felicitous because he follows the Prophet, and so-and-so is wretched, and so-and-so is of the people of Paradise, and so-and-so is of the people of the Fire." They must return the matter and delegate it to their Lord. For your Lord—and the address is to the Prophet and others—knows you best, and He judges among you according to what He knows of the entitlement to mercy or punishment. If He wishes, He will have mercy on you—and He does not wish that except with faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), as He has explained in His speech—or if He wishes, He will punish you—and He does not wish that except with disbelief and transgression. And We have not made you, O Prophet, a guardian (وكيلاً) over them, to whom their affair is delegated so that you may choose for whom you wish what you wish, giving to this one and depriving that one.

From this, it becomes clear that the alternative in His saying: إِنْ يَشَأْ يَرْحَمْكُمْ أَوْ إِنْ يَشَأْ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ is based on the will (المشية) which varies according to the cases of faith, disbelief, and righteous or evil deeds. And His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلاً is to deter the believers from relying for their salvation solely on the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and their association with accepting his religion, similar to His saying: لَيْسَ بِأَمانِيِّكُمْ وَلا أَمانِيِّ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً يُجْزَ بِهِ ("It will be neither according to your fancies nor the fancies of the People of the Book: whoever commits evil shall be requited for it..." [4:123]) and His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالنَّصارى وَالصَّابِئِينَ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ ("Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—they shall have their reward from their Lord..." [2:62]), and other verses in this sense.

There are other opinions regarding the verse which we have omitted as they are not useful.

His saying, the Exalted: وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلَقَدْ فَضَّلْنا بَعْضَ النَّبِيِّينَ عَلى بَعْضٍ وَآتَيْنا داوُدَ زَبُوراً — "Your Lord knows best whoever is in the heavens and the earth. Certainly We have preferred some of the prophets over others, and We gave David the Psalms." (17:55). The beginning of the verse is an expansion of the previous justification, as if it were said: "And how could He not know you best when He knows best whoever is in the heavens and the earth, and you are among them?"

And His saying: وَلَقَدْ فَضَّلْنا بَعْضَ النَّبِيِّينَ عَلى بَعْضٍ is like a prelude to His saying: وَآتَيْنا داوُدَ زَبُوراً. The sentence mentions the merit of David (peace be upon him) through his book, which is the Psalms (الزبور), containing the best of words in glorifying and praising Him, the Exalted. In it is an exhortation for the believers to desire the best of speech and to be disciplined with beautiful etiquette in conversation and speech.

There are other opinions regarding the interpretation of the verse which we have omitted; whoever desires them may refer to the more extensive commentaries.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَوْ كانَ مَعَهُ آلِهَةٌ كَما يَقُولُونَ ـ إِذاً لَابْتَغَوْا إِلى ذِي الْعَرْشِ سَبِيلاً, he said: "He (peace be upon him) said: 'If the idols were gods as you claim, they would have ascended to the Throne.'"

I say: Meaning they would have seized the kingdom of the Exalted and taken the reins of affairs. As for the "Throne" (العرش) meaning the celestial sphere that defines directions, or a great luminous body above the physical world as some have mentioned, there is no evidence for it from the Book. Even assuming its existence, there is no necessary connection between lordship and ascending upon this body.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ahmad and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Umar that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "When death approached Noah, he said to his two sons: 'I command you with [saying] "Glory be to Allah and with His praise" (سبحان الله وبحمده), for it is the prayer of everything, and by it everything is provided for.'"

I say: It has become clear from what we presented regarding the meaning of the glorification of things that there is a connection, as indicated in the narration, between the glorification of everything and its provision (الرزق). For provision is measured by need and request, and everything glorifies Allah, the Exalted, by indicating—through the manifestation of its need and deficiency—His sanctification from that.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Abi al-Sabbah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'The saying of Allah: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ.' He said: 'Everything glorifies with His praise, and we consider the cracking of walls to be their glorification.'"

I say: It was also narrated from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id from him (peace be upon him).

In the same source, from al-Nawfali from al-Sakuni from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his father (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) forbade branding animals or striking their faces, for they glorify with the praise of their Lord."

I say: al-Kulayni narrated the prohibition of striking them on their faces in al-Kafi, with his chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). In another hadith: "Do not brand them on their faces."

In the same source, from Ishaq ibn ‘Ammar from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "No bird is caught in the land or sea, nor any wild animal is caught, except by its neglecting glorification."

I say: This meaning was narrated by the Sunnis through many chains from Ibn Mas‘ud, Abu al-Darda’, Abu Hurayrah, and others from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In the same source, from Mas‘adah ibn Sadaqah from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his father (peace be upon him): "A man entered upon him and said: 'May my father and mother be your ransom! I find Allah saying in His Book: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلكِنْ لا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ.' He said to him: 'It is as Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said.' The man asked: 'Does the dry tree glorify?' He replied: 'Yes, have you not heard the wood of the house how it creaks? That is its glorification. So glory be to Allah in every state.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Indeed, the ants glorify."

In the same source: al-Nasa’i, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Umar who said: "The Messenger of Allah forbade the killing of frogs and said: 'Their croaking is glorification.'"

In the same source: al-Khatib narrated from Abu Hamzah who said: "We were with ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn when some sparrows passed by us chirping. He said: 'Do you know what these sparrows are saying?' We said: 'No.' He said: 'I do not say that we know the Unseen, but I heard my father saying: I heard ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, the Commander of the Faithful, saying: When the birds wake up, they glorify their Lord and ask Him for their day's sustenance—and these are glorifying their Lord and asking for their day's sustenance.'"

I say: The like of it was also narrated from Abu al-Shaykh and Abu Nu‘aym in al-Hilyah from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali from Muhammad ibn ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him), and its wording is: "Muhammad ibn ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn heard sparrows chirping and said: 'Do you know what they say?' I said: 'No.' He said: 'They glorify their Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, and ask for their day's sustenance.'"

In the same source: al-Khatib narrated in his Tarikh from ‘A’ishah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) entered upon me and said: 'O ‘A’ishah, wash these two cloaks.' I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I washed them yesterday.' He said to me: 'Do you not know that the garment glorifies, and when it becomes dirty, its glorification is cut off?'"

In the same source: al-‘Uqayli in al-Du‘afa’, Abu al-Shaykh, and al-Daylami narrated from Anas who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The lifespans of all animals, the insects of the earth, the ants, the fleas, the locusts, the horses, the mules, and all beasts—their lifespans are in glorification. When their glorification is finished, Allah takes their souls, and the Angel of Death has nothing to do with them.'"

I say: Perhaps the meaning of his saying "the Angel of Death has nothing to do with them" is that he does not personally undertake the taking of their souls; rather, some of the angels and assistants carry it out, and the angels are intermediate causes in any case.

In the same source: Ahmad narrated from Mu‘adh ibn Anas from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "He passed by a people who were standing on their beasts and mounts, and he said to them: 'Ride them safely and leave them safely, and do not take them as chairs for your conversations in the roads and markets—for perhaps a mount is better than its rider and more frequent in the remembrance of Allah than him.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from al-Sakuni, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The beast of burden has six rights over its owner: he should not load it beyond its capacity; he should not take its back as a seat to converse upon; he should begin by feeding it when he halts; he should not brand it on its face; he should not strike it, for it glorifies Allah; and he should offer it water when he passes by it."

In the Manaqib of Ibn Shahr Ashub, narrated from ‘Alqamah and Ibn Mas‘ud: "We used to sit with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and hear the food glorifying Allah while the Messenger of Allah was eating. Mikraz al-‘Amiri came to him and asked him for a sign, so he called for nine pebbles, and they glorified Allah in his hand." In the hadith of Abu Dharr: "He placed them on the ground and they did not glorify and remained silent; then he returned and took them, and they glorified." Ibn ‘Abbas said: "The kings of Hadramawt came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'How do we know that you are the Messenger of Allah?' He took a handful of pebbles and said: 'This bears witness that I am the Messenger of Allah.' The pebbles glorified Allah in his hand and bore witness that he is the Messenger of Allah."

In the same source, from Abu Hurayrah, Jabir al-Ansari, Ibn ‘Abbas, Ubayy ibn Ka‘b, and Zayn al-‘Abidin (peace be upon him): "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to deliver sermons in Medina leaning against one of the palm trunks. When the people increased in number and they made a pulpit for him, and he moved to it, the trunk yearned (حن) just as a she-camel yearns. When he came to it and honored it, it began to moan with the moaning of a child being quieted."

I say: The narrations regarding the glorification (التسبيح) of things across their various kinds are very numerous. Sometimes the matter becomes confused for some, leading them to imagine that this general glorification is of the nature of sounds, and that all things possess a language or languages with words coined for meanings—similar to what humans possess—used to disclose what is in the mind, except that our senses are diverted from them; but this is as you see (i.e., an untenable view).

What has been concluded from the preceding discussion in the commentary on the noble verse is that things possess a glorification that is speech (الكلام) in the true sense of the word. It is their manifestation of their Lord’s transcendence by manifesting the deficiency of their own essences, attributes, and acts, arising from a knowledge they possess of that. This is speech. Thus, what is narrated regarding their hearing the glorification of the pebbles in the palm of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), or the hearing of the glorification of the mountains and birds when Dawud (peace be upon him) glorified, or similar instances, was only through their perception of the actual glorification (تسبيحها الواقعي) in its true sense via the inner path (طريق الباطن). Subsequently, the senses simulated that with corresponding and appropriate phonemes and words coined to convey the meaning they had perceived.

This is similar to what was mentioned previously regarding the appearance of meanings—abstracted from form—in a dream (الرؤيا) through appropriate familiar forms, such as the appearance of the reality of Ya‘qub, his wife, and his sons to Yusuf (peace be upon him) in his dream in the form of the sun, the moon, and the stars, and similar to the other dreams narrated by Allah, the Glorified, in Surah Yusuf, the discussion of which has already preceded.

Therefore, that which is attained by one to whom the glorification of things, their praise, their testimony, or the like is unveiled, is the reality of the meaning (حقيقة المعنى) first; then the inner sense simulates it in the form of audible words that convey the meaning he attained. And Allah knows best.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Abu Ya‘la and Ibn Abi Hatim—who authenticated it—Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym, and al-Bayhaqi together in al-Dala’il extracted from Asma’ bint Abi Bakr, who said: "When تَبَّتْ يَدَا أَبِي لَهَبٍ [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 111, Ayah 1] was revealed, the one-eyed Umm Jamil approached, wailing, with a stone in her hand, saying:

'The dispraised one (مذمماً) we have rejected—his religion we have loathed—and his command we have disobeyed.'

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was sitting with Abu Bakr beside him. Abu Bakr said: 'This woman has approached and I fear she will see you.' He replied: 'She shall never see me,' and he recited a portion of the Quran by which he sought protection, as the Exalted said: وَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ جَعَلْنَا بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ حِجَابًا مَسْتُورًا 'When you recite the Quran, We draw between you and those who do not believe in the Hereafter a hidden curtain' (17:45). She came until she stood over Abu Bakr but did not see the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). She said: 'O Abu Bakr, I have been informed that your companion has lampooned me.' Abu Bakr replied: 'No, by the Lord of this House, he has not lampooned you.' So she turned away saying: 'The Quraysh know that I am the daughter of their master.'"

I say: It is also narrated through another chain from Asma’, and from Abu Bakr and Ibn ‘Abbas in brief. It was also narrated in al-Bihar from Qurb al-Isnad from al-Hasan ibn Zarif, from Mu‘ammar, from al-Rida, from his father, from his grandfather (peace be upon them) in a hadith mentioning the compendium of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) miracles.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Zurarah, from one of the two [Imams al-Baqir or al-Sadiq] (peace be upon them) regarding بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful). He said: "It is the most deserving thing to be recited aloud, and it is the verse about which Allah said: وَإِذَا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ وَحْدَهُ وَلَّوْا عَلَىٰ أَدْبَارِهِمْ نُفُورًا '...and when you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn their backs in aversion' (17:46)—[meaning] 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful.' The polytheists used to listen to the recitation of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and when he recited 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful,' they would flee and depart; when he finished it, they would return and listen."

I say: This meaning is also narrated from Mansur ibn Hazim from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), and al-Qummi narrated it in his Tafsir as an unattributed (مضمراً) report.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Bukhari extracted in his Tarikh from Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali that he said: "Why did you conceal 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful'? What a fine name, by Allah, they concealed! For the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), whenever he entered his home, the Quraysh would gather around him, and he would recite 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful' aloud, raising his voice with it. The Quraysh would then turn away in flight, so Allah revealed: وَإِذَا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ وَحْدَهُ وَلَّوْا عَلَىٰ أَدْبَارِهِمْ نُفُورًا '...and when you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn their backs in aversion' (17:46)."

In the same source: Ibn Ishaq and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il extracted from al-Zuhri, who said: "I was told that Abu Jahl, Abu Sufyan, and al-Akhnas ibn Shariq went out one night to listen to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while he was praying at night in his house. Each man took a place to listen, none of them knowing the location of his companion. They spent the night listening to him until dawn broke and they dispersed. They met on the road and blamed one another. Some said to others: 'Do not return, for if some of your foolish ones saw you, you would cause doubt in their hearts.'

Then they departed until the second night came, and each man returned to his place. They spent the night listening to him until dawn broke and they dispersed. They met on the road and said to one another the same as they said the first time. Then they departed until the third night came, and each man took his place and spent the night listening to him until dawn broke and they dispersed. They met on the road and said to one another: 'We shall not leave until we make a covenant not to return.' They made a covenant on that and then dispersed.

When morning came, al-Akhnas went to Abu Sufyan in his house and said: 'Tell me your opinion of what you heard from Muhammad.' He replied: 'By Allah, I heard things I recognize and know what is intended by them, and I heard things whose meaning I did not recognize nor what is intended by them.' al-Akhnas said: 'And I, by Him by whom I swore [the same].'

Then he left him and went to Abu Jahl and said: 'What is your opinion of what you heard from Muhammad?' He replied: 'What did I hear? We and the Banu ‘Abd Manaf competed for honor: they fed people, so we fed people; they bore burdens, so we bore burdens; they gave, so we gave; until we were neck and neck, like two racing horses, they said: "From us is a prophet to whom revelation comes from heaven." When will we ever attain the like of this? No, by Allah, we will never believe in him nor ever deem him truthful.' So al-Akhnas stood up and left him."

In al-Majma‘: "The polytheists used to harm the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in Mecca, and they would say: 'O Messenger of Allah, permit us to fight them.' He would say to them: 'I have not been commanded regarding them with anything.' So Allah, the Glorified, revealed: قُلْ لِعِبَادِي 'Say to My servants...' (17:53) the verse." Narrated from al-Kalbi.

I say: We have already indicated in the commentary (تفسير) of the verse that this does not harmonize with its context. And Allah knows best.

Ayahs

قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ فَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ كَشْفَ ٱلضُّرِّ عَنكُمْ وَلَا تَحْوِيلًا (٥٦)
Say, ‘Invoke those whom you claim [to be gods] besides Him. They have no power to remove your distress, nor to bring about any change [in your state]. (56)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ يَبْتَغُونَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمُ ٱلْوَسِيلَةَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ وَيَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَهُۥ وَيَخَافُونَ عَذَابَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّكَ كَانَ مَحْذُورًا (٥٧)
They [themselves] are the ones who supplicate, seeking a recourse to their Lord, whoever is nearer [to Him], expecting His mercy and fearing His punishment.’ Indeed your Lord’s punishment is a thing to beware of. (57)
وَإِن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا نَحْنُ مُهْلِكُوهَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُوهَا عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۚ كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَسْطُورًا (٥٨)
There is not a town but We will destroy it before the Day of Resurrection, or punish it with a severe punishment. That has been written in the Book. (58)
وَمَا مَنَعَنَآ أَن نُّرْسِلَ بِٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ إِلَّآ أَن كَذَّبَ بِهَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا ثَمُودَ ٱلنَّاقَةَ مُبْصِرَةً فَظَلَمُوا۟ بِهَا ۚ وَمَا نُرْسِلُ بِٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ إِلَّا تَخْوِيفًا (٥٩)
Nothing keeps Us from sending signs except that the former peoples denied them. We gave Thamud the she-camel as an eye-opener, but they wronged her. We do not send the signs except as warning. (59)
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحَاطَ بِٱلنَّاسِ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا ٱلرُّءْيَا ٱلَّتِىٓ أَرَيْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَٱلشَّجَرَةَ ٱلْمَلْعُونَةَ فِى ٱلْقُرْءَانِ ۚ وَنُخَوِّفُهُمْ فَمَا يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا طُغْيَـٰنًا كَبِيرًا (٦٠)
When We said to you, ‘Indeed your Lord encircles those people,’ We did not appoint the vision that We showed you except as a tribulation for the people and the tree cursed in the Quran. We warn them, but it only increases them in their outrageous rebellion. (60)
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ قَالَ ءَأَسْجُدُ لِمَنْ خَلَقْتَ طِينًا (٦١)
When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they [all] prostrated, but not Iblis: he said, ‘Shall I prostrate before someone whom You have created from clay?’ (61)
قَالَ أَرَءَيْتَكَ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كَرَّمْتَ عَلَىَّ لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٦٢)
Said he, ‘Do You see this one whom You have honoured above me? If You respite me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely lay my yoke on his progeny, [all] except a few.’ (62)
قَالَ ٱذْهَبْ فَمَن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزَآؤُكُمْ جَزَآءً مَّوْفُورًا (٦٣)
Said He, ‘Begone! Whoever of them follows you, indeed the hell shall be your requital, an ample reward. (63)
وَٱسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُم بِصَوْتِكَ وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِم بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَوْلَـٰدِ وَعِدْهُمْ ۚ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا (٦٤)
Instigate whomever of them you can with your voice; and rally against them your cavalry and your infantry, and share with them in wealth and children, and make promises to them!’ But Satan promises them nothing but delusion. (64)
إِنَّ عِبَادِى لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنٌ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا (٦٥)
‘As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them.’ And your Lord suffices as trustee. (65)

(Commentary)

This is an argument from another perspective for monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the negation of the lordship of those deities invoked besides Allah. It demonstrates that they possess no power to remove harm or divert it from those who worship them; rather, they are like their worshippers in their need for Allah, the Glorified, seeking a means of recourse (الوسيلة) to Him, hoping for His mercy, and fearing His punishment.

Furthermore, harm, destruction, and punishment are in the hand of Allah alone. He has decreed in the Book (الكتاب) for every town that He will destroy it before the Day of Resurrection or punish it with a severe punishment. The former peoples were sent divine signs (الآيات الإلهية), but when they disbelieved and denied them, and the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) followed, Allah did not send them to the later generations. This is because He willed not to hasten their destruction, though the root of corruption would grow among them and Satan would mislead them, so the word would be justified against them and Allah would seize them—and that was a matter decreed.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ ادْعُوا الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ فَلا يَمْلِكُونَ كَشْفَ الضُّرِّ عَنْكُمْ وَلا تَحْوِيلاً — Say, ‘Invoke those whom you claim besides Him: they have no power to remove your adversity nor to change [your state].’ (17:56). The word "claim" (الزعم) — with any of the three vowelings of the letter zay — refers to belief in general, but its usage became predominant for false belief. For this reason, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that whenever "claim" (الزعم) appears in the Quran, it refers to a lie.

Supplication (الدعاء) and calling (النداء) are essentially the same, except that calling (النداء) is only used when accompanied by a voice, whereas supplication (الدعاء) is sometimes applied to what is done by gesture or otherwise. Some have mentioned a distinction between them: that calling (النداء) may be said when one says "O" (يا) or "O you" (أيا) or similar particles without attaching the name, while supplication (الدعاء) is hardly said unless the name is included, such as "O so-and-so." End quote.

The verse argues against the divinity of their deities besides Allah on the basis that the Lord (الرب) worthy of worship (العبادة) must be capable of bringing benefit and averting harm, as this is an inherent requirement of the lordship (ربوبية) of the Lord. Moreover, the polytheists (المشركون) concede this, for they only took these deities and worshipped them out of desire for their benefit and fear of their harm. However, those they invoke besides Allah are incapable of this, so they are not deities. The proof of this is that if these worshippers were to invoke them to remove a harm that touched them or to divert it from them to someone else, they would possess neither the power of removal nor diversion.

And how could they possess from themselves the power to remove harm or divert it, or independently fulfill a need or alleviate poverty, when they themselves are created by Allah, seeking a means of recourse (الوسيلة) to Him, hoping for His mercy and fearing His punishment—by the very admission of the polytheists?

Thus, it has become clear, firstly, that the intended meaning of His saying: الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ (those whom you claim besides Him) refers to those they used to worship among the angels, the jinn, and humans. Indeed, they only intended by the worship of idols to seek proximity to them, just as by the worship of the sun, the moon, and the stars, they intended to seek proximity to their spiritual realities (روحانيتهم) among the angels.

Furthermore, idols, insofar as they are idols, are not real things, as He, the Exalted, said: إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْماءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ — They are but names which you have named—you and your fathers... (53:23).

As for what they are made of—wood or metal—these are merely inanimate matter (جماد), and their state is that of any inanimate object in terms of seeking proximity to it, prostrating to it, or its glorification (تسبيح); they are not idols from that perspective.

Secondly, the negation of their power refers to the negation of their independent power (استقلالهم بالقدرة) without the assistance of Allah and the support of His permission. The evidence for this is His saying, the Glorified, in the following verse: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ يَبْتَغُونَ إِلى رَبِّهِمُ الْوَسِيلَةَ (They [themselves] are the ones who supplicate, seeking a means of recourse to their Lord...) etc.

Some commentators have said: It seems the negation of their "ownership" (ملكهم) of that refers to the negation of their complete and perfect power over it. The fact that the power of false deities is bestowed (مفاضة) by Him, the Exalted, is accepted by the disbelievers, for they do not deny that they are created by Allah, the Exalted, with all their attributes, and that Allah, the Glorified, is stronger and more perfect in attribute than they are.

With this, the proof is completed and the silencing of the opponent (الإفحام) is achieved. Otherwise, the absolute negation of the power of the likes of the jinn and angels—who were worshipped besides Allah—to remove harm is something for which the evidence is not apparent. For if it were said that the evidence is that the disbelievers entreat them and receive no response, this would be countered by the fact that we also see Muslims entreating Allah, the Exalted, and not receiving a response.

It might also be said: The intention is to negate their power over that entirely, and this is argued for using the Ash'arite proof regarding the attribution of all contingent beings (الممكنات) to Him, the Mighty and Majestic, directly (ابتداء). End quote.

I say: He, the Glorified, affirms in His speech various types of power (القدرة) for the angels, the jinn, and humans in many verses that absolutely do not accept metaphorical interpretation (التأويل). However, He reserves the reality of power (حقيقة القدرة) for Himself in verses such as His saying: أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً — ...that power belongs entirely to Allah... (2:165).
