It becomes clear through this that others only have power over what He enables them to have power over, and they only own what He grants them ownership of. Thus, no one is independent in power or ownership except Him. Whatever power or ownership others possess is borrowed (مستعار), and its effect is contingent upon His permission (الإذن) and will (المشية).

Accordingly, there is no way to interpret the argument in the verse as a negation of the power of their deities—among the angels, jinn, and humans—from its very root. Rather, the argument is built on the fact that those invoked are not independent in ownership or power, and that in what they do possess, they are like those who invoke them: in need of Allah, seeking a means of recourse (الوسيلة) to Him. Supplication (الدعاء) only pertains to power that is independent in its effect (المستقلة بالتأثير). Supplicating and asking from one who is powerful through the power of another, and an owner through the granting of another—while the power and ownership remain with their original possessor—is, in reality, a supplication and request from the one in whom they reside truly and independently, not from the one who is made an owner by His granting.

As for his remark that the absolute negation of their power lacks apparent evidence—because if it is said that the disbelievers entreat them without response, it is countered by the fact that Muslims also entreat Allah without response—Allah, the Glorified, has answered such a counter-argument in His speech.

To clarify: He, the Exalted, said—and His word is the truth: أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ — ...I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me... (2:186), and He said: ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ — ‘Call Me, I will answer you!’ (40:60). He left the statement absolute, indicating that when a servant is sincere in supplication, does not treat it as a game, and his heart in his serious supplication is attached to none but Him, the Exalted—by cutting himself off from all others and taking refuge in Him—he will certainly be answered. Then He mentioned this detachment (الانقطاع) in supplication and asking at the end of these verses, which serves as a completion to what is in this argument, by His saying: وَإِذا مَسَّكُمُ الضُّرُّ فِي الْبَحْرِ ضَلَّ مَنْ تَدْعُونَ إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا نَجَّاكُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ أَعْرَضْتُمْ — When distress befalls you at sea, those whom you invoke leave [you] in the lurch, except Him. But when He delivers you to land, you disregard [Him]... (17:67). This indicates that when adversity touches you at sea, you detach yourselves from everything else toward Him, invoking Him by the guidance of your primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — so He answers you and delivers you to land.

The conclusion of all this is that when a servant detaches himself from everything and invokes Allah with an empty, sound heart, He answers him; whereas if someone else is invoked and the supplicant detaches himself from Allah to ask them sincerely, they do not possess the power of response.

On this basis, there is no room for a counter-argument from the polytheists, for they are not answered when they invoke their deities, yet they themselves see that when adversity touches them at sea and they detach themselves toward Allah and ask Him for salvation, He delivers them to land—and they admit this. If the Muslims were to invoke Him in this manner, with seriousness in supplication and detachment toward Him, their state on land would be like the state of others at sea; they would neither be disappointed nor turned away.

He, the Glorified, did not contrast in His speech between their supplication to their deities and the Muslims' supplication to their God, such that it could be countered by the fact that both supplications share in being rejected or not answered. Rather, He contrasted between the polytheists' supplication to their deities and their own supplication to Him, the Glorified, at sea when all means are cut off and every invoked being besides Allah is lost.

Among the subtle points (النكتة) in the discourse is His presenting the argument to them through His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as He said: قُلِ ادْعُوا الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ (Say, ‘Invoke those whom you claim besides Him’). Had the polytheists disputed with him using such a counter-argument, he would have invoked his Lord with detachment and sincerity, and he would have been answered.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ يَبْتَغُونَ إِلى رَبِّهِمُ الْوَسِيلَةَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ (They [themselves] are the ones who supplicate, seeking a means of recourse to their Lord, [to see] which of them is nearest...) to the end of the verse. أُولئِكَ (Those) is the subject (مبتدأ), الَّذِينَ (who) is its attribute (صفة), and يَدْعُونَ (they invoke) is its relative clause (صلة), with its pronoun referring back to the polytheists. يَبْتَغُونَ (they seek) is the predicate (خبر) of أُولئِكَ, and its pronoun and all subsequent plural pronouns to the end of the verse refer back to أُولئِكَ. His saying: أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ (which of them is nearest) is an explanation (بيان) of seeking the means of recourse, because "seeking" (الابتغاء) implies searching and questioning in meaning; this is what the context (السياق) provides.

The means of recourse (الوسيلة), according to what they have explained, is the act of attaining and seeking proximity. It is also sometimes used to mean that by which one attains and seeks proximity, and perhaps this is more suitable to the context given that it is followed by His saying: أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Those whom the polytheists invoke among the angels, jinn, and humans themselves seek that by which they may draw near to their Lord, inquiring as to which of them is nearest, so that they may follow his path and emulate his deeds to draw near to Him, the Exalted, as he draws near. They hope for His mercy in everything they are supported by in their existence, and they fear His punishment, so they obey Him and do not disobey Him. Indeed, the punishment of your Lord is something to be wary of (محذوراً), which must be avoided.

Seeking recourse (التوسل) to Allah through some of those near to Him—as in this noble verse, and similar to it is His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوَسِيلَةَ — O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and seek the means of recourse to Him... (5:35)—is different from what the polytheistic idolaters (الوثنيين) intend. They seek recourse to Allah and draw near through the noble angels, the jinn, and the saints (الأولياء) among humans, yet they abandon His worship, neither hoping in Him nor fearing Him. Rather, they worship the "means" (الوسيلة), hoping for its mercy and fearing its wrath. Then they seek recourse to these lords and deities through idols and statues, abandoning the former and worshipping the idols, drawing near to them with offerings and sacrifices.

In short, they claim to draw near to Allah through some of His servants or the idols of His creation, but then they worship nothing but the "means" independently, hoping in it and fearing it independently of Allah. Thus, they commit polytheism (الشرك) by granting it independence in lordship (الربوبية) and worship (العبادة).

If the intended meaning of "those whom they invoke" is the noble angels, the righteous among the jinn, and the prophets and saints among humans, then the meaning of their seeking the means, hoping for mercy, and fearing punishment is its apparent, primary sense. If the intended meaning is broader, including those they used to worship among the rebellious devils and the transgressors among humans like Pharaoh, Nimrod, and others, then the meaning of their seeking the means to Him, the Exalted, refers to their existential (تكويني) submission, prostration, and glorification, and similarly, their hope and fear refer to what is inherent to their essences.

Some have mentioned that all the plural pronouns in the verse refer back to "those" (أولئك), and the meaning is: Those prophets whom they worship besides Allah call people to the truth, or they call upon Allah and entreat Him, seeking proximity to their Lord. This is as you see [weak].

In al-Kashshaf, regarding the meaning of the verse, it says: It means that those deities of theirs seek the means of recourse, which is proximity to Allah, the Exalted. أَيُّهُمْ (which of them) is a substitute (بدل) for the "w" (واو) in يَبْتَغُونَ (they seek), and ay is a relative pronoun; meaning, he who is nearest and most favored among them seeks the means to Allah, so how much more so those who are not the nearest?

Or, يَبْتَغُونَ إِلى رَبِّهِمُ الْوَسِيلَةَ (they seek the means of recourse to their Lord) is imbued with the meaning of "they are eager," as if it were said: they are eager to see which of them will be nearest to Allah, and that is through obedience and increasing in goodness and righteousness. They hope and fear just like other servants of Allah, so how can they claim that they are deities? End quote.

There is no harm in these two meanings, were it not for the fact that the context does not entirely suit them, though the second is closer to it than the first.

It has been said: The meaning of the verse is that those whom they invoke and worship, believing them to be deities, seek the means and proximity to Allah, the Exalted, through their worship, and each of them strives to be nearest to His mercy. End quote. This is a meaning that does not correspond to the wording of the verse at all.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا نَحْنُ مُهْلِكُوها قَبْلَ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُوها عَذاباً شَدِيداً كانَ ذلِكَ فِي الْكِتابِ مَسْطُوراً — There is no town but We shall destroy it before the Day of Resurrection, or punish it with a severe punishment. That has been written in the Book. (17:58). They have mentioned that the intended meaning of "severe punishment" is the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال), which leaves "destruction" (الإهلاك) as its counterpart to mean dying a natural death (حتف الأنف). Thus, the meaning is: there is no town but that We shall cause its people to die before the Day of Resurrection, or We shall punish them with the punishment of extirpation before the Day of Resurrection, for no town remains after the carpet of the world is rolled up by the arrival of the Hour. He, the Exalted, has said: وَإِنَّا لَجاعِلُونَ ما عَلَيْها صَعِيداً جُرُزاً — And indeed We will turn whatever is on it into a barren plain. (18:8). Therefore, some have said: destruction is for the righteous towns and punishment is for the wicked towns.

Regarding the connection of the verse, they have mentioned it is an admonition. Some said: It is as if He, the Exalted, after mentioning what He did regarding the Resurrection and Monotheism, mentioned some of what occurs before the Day of Resurrection to indicate His greatness, the Glorified, and to support what was mentioned before it.

It appears that the verse is linked to what preceded it several verses ago: وَإِذا أَرَدْنا أَنْ نُهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنا مُتْرَفِيها فَفَسَقُوا فِيها فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا الْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْناها تَدْمِيراً — When We desire to destroy a town, We command its luxury-loving elite, and they commit transgression in it. So the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly. (17:16). Indeed, the verses of this Surah continually link back to one another. The general purpose is to explain the established way (سنة) of Allah, the Exalted, in calling them to the truth, then the felicity of those who were felicitous among them through listening and obedience, and the punishment of those who opposed and rebelled through arrogance.

On this basis, the intended meaning of "destruction" (الإهلاك) is annihilation (التدمير) by the punishment of extirpation, as narrated from the commentator Abu Muslim. The "severe punishment" refers to punishment less than that, such as famine or high prices leading to the displacement of its people and the ruin of its buildings, or other such calamities and trials.

Thus, the verse contains an indication that each of these towns will be ruined due to the corruption of its people and the transgression of its luxury-loving elite, and that this is by the decree (قضاء) of Allah, the Glorified, as indicated by the end of the verse. Through this, the connection of the following verse وَما مَنَعَنا (And nothing prevented Us...) etc. to this verse becomes clear. The meaning is that they are predisposed to corruption and prepared to deny the divine signs, which are followed by destruction and annihilation for whoever rejects and denies them. We sent them to the former peoples, and they denied them and were extirpated. Had We sent to these people anything of the nature of those terrifying signs, destruction and annihilation would have befallen them and the carpet of the world would have been rolled up. So We granted them respite until a time, but it will catch up to them and not bypass them—as indicated in His saying: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ (For every nation there is a messenger...) (10:47).

Some have mentioned that the intended meaning of "towns" in the verse is the disbelieving towns, and that the generalization of "towns" is not supported by the context. End quote. This is a claim for which there is no evidence.

His saying: كانَ ذلِكَ فِي الْكِتابِ مَسْطُوراً (That has been written in the Book) means the destruction of the towns or their severe punishment was written in the Book and was a finalized decree. Through this, it becomes clear that the intended meaning of "the Book" is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), which the Quran mentions that Allah has written everything in, such as His saying: وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ — ...and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam. (36:12), and His saying: وَما يَعْزُبُ عَنْ رَبِّكَ مِنْ مِثْقالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي السَّماءِ وَلا أَصْغَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ وَلا أَكْبَرَ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ — ...Not an atom’s weight escapes your Lord in the earth or in the sky, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book. (10:61).

Among the strange remarks is what some have mentioned: "More than one has stated that there is nothing but that it is explained therein—meaning in the Preserved Tablet and the Written Book—with its qualities, its necessitating causes, and its appointed time." The universality (العموم) was challenged on the grounds that it necessitates the non-finitude of dimensions, while traditional and rational proofs have established the opposite. Thus, it must be said that it is specified (تخصيص) by taking "thing" to mean what pertains to this world (هذه النشأة) or the like.

Some argued for universality but maintained that the explanation is in a manner that is consistent with finitude. Thus, the Preserved Tablet, in its explanation of all worldly and otherworldly things, what was and what will be, is like the Jafr al-Jami' in its explanation of what it explains. End quote.

This discourse is built on it being a physical tablet (لوحاً جسمانياً) placed in some region of the world, upon which are written the names of things, their descriptions, their states, and what occurs to them in the systems specific to each and the general system governing them from all aspects. If it were, as they say, a material, physical tablet, it would not suffice to write the names of the parts that compose its own body and the detail of their attributes and states, let alone other existents whose attributes and states and the events that occur to them and the relations between them none can count or encompass in detail except Allah, the Glorified. Specification to what is in this world or less than that does not help in this regard, and this is obvious.

As for what some maintained—that it is of the nature of the non-finite being enfolded within the finite, similar to how the disjointed letters (الحروف المقطعة) encompass all speech despite the non-finitude of verbal compositions—this is an admission of the existence of the forms of events in it by way of potentiality and possibility or summarization. Yet His speech, the Glorified, in describing this Tablet is like an explicit statement—or is indeed explicit—that it encompasses things and events, whether they were, will be, or are currently existing, in a detailed manner and with the mark of necessity (الوجوب) that allows no way for change. If it were as they say, it would have sufficed to write the letters of the alphabet in a circle on a tablet.

Furthermore, combining the physicality and materiality of the Tablet—the characteristic of which is the acceptance of change—with its being preserved (محفوظاً) from any assumed change or transformation is something that requires a clearer proof than these depictions. There are other points in the discourse open to critique.

The truth is that the Manifest Book (الكتاب المبين) is the very essence (متن) of the concrete entities (الأعيان) including the events therein, from the perspective of the necessity of the sequence of effects upon their causes. It is the Decree (القضاء) that is neither turned back nor changed, not from the perspective of the possibility and potentiality of matter. Expressing it as a "Book" and a "Tablet" is to bring the reality of the meaning closer to understanding through representation (التمثيل). We shall complete the discussion on this research, Allah willing, in a suitable place.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما مَنَعَنا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآياتِ إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ (And nothing prevented Us from sending the signs except that the former peoples denied them...) to the end of the verse. The connection of the verse to what preceded it has already been mentioned, and its summary is: the previous verse indicated that people—the later ones like the earlier ones—deserve, by virtue of their innate disposition toward corruption and transgression, the descent of destruction and all types of severe punishment. Allah has decreed for the towns that they be destroyed or punished with a severe punishment. This is what prevented Us from sending the signs they propose; for their predecessors proposed them, so We sent them to them, but they denied them and We destroyed them. These later ones are of the same character as their predecessors; if We were to send the signs according to their proposal, they would certainly deny them, and destruction would inevitably befall them as it befell their predecessors. And Allah, the Glorified, does not wish to hasten their punishment.

Through this, it becomes clear that the two verses are linked to what He will narrate of their proposal of signs in His saying: وَقالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى تَفْجُرَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ يَنْبُوعاً — They say, ‘We will not believe you until you make a spring gush forth for us from the ground...’ (17:90) to the end of the verses. The apparent sense of the verses of the Surah is that they were revealed all at once.

So His saying: وَما مَنَعَنا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآياتِ (And nothing prevented Us from sending the signs)—"prevention" (المنع) is the forcing of another away from what he intends to do and restraining him from it. Allah, the Glorified, judges and there is none to reverse His judgment; He is the Victorious, the Subduer, who when He intends a thing, says to it "Be" and it is. Thus, the fact that the former peoples' denial of His signs prevented Him from sending the proposed signs thereafter means that the action—in view of the character of denial rooted in them—was devoid of benefit (المصلحة) for a nation whom Allah willed not to hasten with punishment and destruction, or devoid of benefit absolutely due to the knowledge that the generality of them would not believe in the proposed signs.

If you wish, you could say: the incompatibility between sending the proposed signs—given the denial of the former peoples and the later ones following their path which entails extirpation—and the attachment of the Will (المشية) to granting this nation respite, was expressed in the verse as "prevention" (المنع) metaphorically.

It is as if, to signal this, He expressed the giving of signs as "sending" (الإرسال), as if they were supporting one another and calling for descent, but denial and the deep-rooting of corruption in the nature of people prevent that.

His saying: إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ (except that the former peoples denied them)—expressing the destroyed nations as "the former ones" (الأولين), which is the counterpart to "the later ones," contains an allusion that these are the end of those former ones. They are, in reality, one nation; the later part has the character and instinct of the earlier part, and the judgment for the tail is the same as for the head. For this reason, they used to say: ما سَمِعْنا بِهذا فِي آبائِنَا الْأَوَّلِينَ — ...We never heard of this among our forefathers. (23:24), repeating this phrase.

In any case, the meaning of the verse is: We did not send the signs they propose—and the proposers are the Quraysh—because if We sent them, they would not believe and would deny them, thus deserving the punishment of extirpation. Just as We sent them to the former peoples after they proposed them, and they denied them, so We destroyed them. However, We have decreed for this nation that We shall not punish them except after a delay and respite, as appears from various places in His speech, the Exalted.

They have mentioned two other views regarding the meaning of the noble verse:

First: We do not send the signs because of Our knowledge that they will not believe in them, so their revelation would be in vain (عبثاً) with no benefit. Just as those before them did not believe when the signs were revealed. This only applies to the proposed signs; as for the signs upon which the proof of prophethood depends, Allah certainly gives them to His Messenger. Likewise, the signs whose revelation constitutes a grace (لطف) from Him, the Glorified, Allah also manifests them as a grace from Him. As for types other than these two, there is no benefit in revealing them.

Second: The meaning is: We do not send the signs because your fathers and ancestors asked for the like of them and did not believe in them when they were revealed, and you are following in the footsteps of your ancestors; so just as they did not believe, you will not believe.

The second meaning is narrated from Abu Muslim, and distinguishing it from the two previous meanings without it coinciding with one of them is not without difficulty.

His saying: وَآتَيْنا ثَمُودَ النَّاقَةَ مُبْصِرَةً فَظَلَمُوا بِها — ...We gave the She-camel to Thamud as a visible sign, but they wronged her... (17:59). Thamud were the people of Salih, and He gave them the She-camel as a sign (آية). "Visible" (المبصرة) means manifest and clear, similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا آيَةَ النَّهارِ مُبْصِرَةً — ...and We made the sign of the day visible... (17:12). It is either an attribute of the She-camel or an attribute of an omitted noun, the estimation being "a visible sign." The meaning is: We gave the people of Thamud the She-camel while she was manifest and clear, or while she was a manifest and clear sign, but they wronged themselves because of her, or they wronged by denying her.

His saying: وَما نُرْسِلُ بِالْآياتِ إِلَّا تَخْوِيفاً — ...We do not send the signs except as a deterrent. (17:59). That is, the wisdom in sending the signs is to deter and warn. If they are among the signs that entail the punishment of extirpation, then they contain a deterrence through destruction in this world and the punishment of the Fire in the Hereafter. If they are of another kind, they contain deterrence and warning of the punishment of the afterlife.

It is not far-fetched that the intended meaning of "deterrence" (التخويف) is the creation of fear and apprehension by sending what is less than the punishment of extirpation, similar to His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ عَلى تَخَوُّفٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ — ...or that He should not seize them by a gradual wasting? Indeed your Lord is Most Kind, Most Merciful. (16:47). Thus, the summary of the verse's meaning would be: We do not send the proposed signs because We do not wish to punish them with the punishment of extirpation; rather, We only send the signs We send as a deterrence, so that by witnessing them they may beware of what is more severe and more horrific. This view has been attributed to some.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قُلْنا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحاطَ بِالنَّاسِ وَما جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْناكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ وَالشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ وَنُخَوِّفُهُمْ فَما يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا طُغْياناً كَبِيراً — When We said to you, ‘Indeed your Lord encompasses all mankind,’ and We did not appoint the vision which We showed you except as a test for the people, and the tree cursed in the Quran. We deter them, but it only increases them in great rebellion. (17:60). The segments of the verse—which are four—are clear in their individual meanings, but in terms of their connection and the link between them, they are not without ambiguity (إجمال). The primary cause for this is the ambiguity of the two middle segments, the second and the third.

For He, the Glorified, did not clarify what this vision (الرؤيا) was that He showed His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), nor does there occur in the rest of His speech anything suitable to interpret this vision. What He mentioned of his visions in verses like His saying: إِذ يُرِيكَهُمُ اللهُ فِي مَنامِكَ قَلِيلاً وَلَوْ أَراكَهُمْ كَثِيراً لَفَشِلْتُمْ — When Allah showed them to you in your dream as few; and had He shown them to you as many, you would have surely lost heart... (8:43) and His saying: لَقَدْ صَدَقَ اللهُ رَسُولَهُ الرُّؤْيا بِالْحقِّ لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ — Certainly Allah has fulfilled His Apostle’s vision in all truth: You will surely enter the Holy Mosque... (48:27) are among the events that occurred after the Hijrah, while this verse is Meccan, revealed before the Hijrah.

Nor is it known what this "cursed tree in the Quran" is, which He made a test for the people. There is no tree in the Quran that Allah mentions and then curses. Yes, He, the Glorified, mentioned the tree of Zaqqum and described it as a test (فتنة), as in His saying: أَمْ شَجَرَةُ الزَّقُّومِ إِنَّا جَعَلْناها فِتْنَةً لِلظَّالِمِينَ — ...or the tree of Zaqqum? Indeed We have made it a punishment for the wrongdoers. (37:62-63). However, He, the Glorified, did not curse it in any of the places where He mentioned it. If the mere fact of it being a tree that comes forth from the bottom of Hell and a cause of punishment for the wrongdoers necessitated its being cursed, then the Fire and everything Allah prepared in it for punishment would be cursed. The angels of punishment—those of whom He, the Exalted, said: وَما جَعَلْنا أَصْحابَ النَّارِ إِلَّا مَلائِكَةً وَما جَعَلْنا عِدَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا — We have appointed only angels as keepers of the Fire, and We have made their number only a test for the faithless... (74:31)—would also be cursed, yet Allah praised them with that profound praise in His saying: عَلَيْها مَلائِكَةٌ غِلاظٌ شِدادٌ لا يَعْصُونَ اللهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ — ...over it are angels, stern and severe, who do not disobey Allah in what He commands them, and they carry out what they are commanded. (66:6). He, the Glorified, even counted the hands of the believers as among the causes of the disbelievers' punishment when He said: قاتِلُوهُمْ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ اللهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ — Fight them! Allah will punish them by your hands... (9:14), yet they are not cursed.

This supports the idea that the verse was not intended to lift the veil from the two segments and clarify the story of the vision and the cursed tree in the Quran—which were made a test for the people—but rather it was intended to allude to their ambiguity and to remind of what they necessitate by the requirement of the context.

Indeed, the context may hint at some aspect of the two matters: the vision and the cursed tree. For the previous verses were describing the people—the later ones like the earlier ones—in their lack of regard for the signs of Allah, the Glorified, and their denial of them, and that human societies are tasters of Allah's punishment, town by town and generation after generation, through destruction or a terrifying punishment less than that. The subsequent verses وَإِذْ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ (When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam...’) etc., which contain the story of Iblis and his wondrous power to mislead the children of Adam, follow the context of the previous verses.

Through this, it appears that the vision and the tree referred to in the verse are two matters that will manifest among the people, or they are manifest things by which people are tested, through which corruption spreads among them and rebellion and arrogance take root. The end of the verse وَنُخَوِّفُهُمْ فَما يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا طُغْياناً كَبِيراً (We deter them, but it only increases them in great rebellion) points to this and supports it, as does the beginning of the verse وَإِذْ قُلْنا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحاطَ بِالنَّاسِ (When We said to you, ‘Indeed your Lord encompasses all mankind’).

Add to this that He, the Exalted, described this tree which He mentioned as being "cursed in the Quran." Through this, it appears that the Quran contains its curse, and that its curse is prominent among the curses found in the Quran, as is the apparent sense of His saying: وَالشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ. In the Quran, Iblis has been cursed, the Jews have been cursed, the polytheists have been cursed, the hypocrites have been cursed, and people have been cursed under other titles, such as those who die while they are disbelievers, those who conceal what Allah has revealed, and those who annoy Allah and His Messenger, among others.

He has made the subject of this curse a "tree" (شجرة). Just as "tree" is applied to a plant with a trunk, it is also used for the root (الأصل) from which branches emerge and upon which they grow, whether by lineage or by following a fundamental belief. It is said in Lisan al-Arab: "It is said: so-and-so is from a blessed tree, meaning from a blessed root." End quote. This has appeared frequently in his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) speech, such as his saying: "I and Ali are from one tree." From this category is his saying in the hadith of al-Abbas: "A man's paternal uncle is the twin (صنو) of his father." (1)

By reflecting on this, it becomes clear to the meditative researcher that this cursed tree is a group of those cursed in His speech who possess the quality of a tree in terms of emergence, growth, and the branching out of offshoots from a root that has a share of survival and fruit-bearing, and they are a test (فتنة) by which this nation is tried. Nothing is suitable for this description except three groups among those counted: the People of the Book, the polytheists, and the hypocrites. Their remaining among the people and their continuity in succession is either through lineage and procreation—like a household from the aforementioned groups living among the people and corrupting their religion and worldly life, and the people being tested by them—or by the emergence of a false belief followed in succession by one generation after another.

There did not appear from the polytheists or the People of the Book in the time of the Messenger—before the Hijrah or after it—a group with this description. Allah had already secured the people from their evil independently through verses like His saying revealed at the end of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) era: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ — ...Today the faithless have despaired of your religion. So do not fear them, but fear Me... (5:3). We have already completed the research on the meaning of this verse previously.

Thus, what profound investigation leads to is that the intended meaning of the "cursed tree" is a group of hypocrites (المنافقين) who outwardly profess Islam, taking root among the Muslims either by lineage or by belief and path, and they are a test for the people. One should not doubt that in the context of the verse there is an allusion to the link between the two segments, namely His saying: وَما جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْناكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ وَالشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ (and We did not appoint the vision which We showed you except as a test for the people, and the tree cursed...), especially after reflecting on the preceding His saying: وَإِذْ قُلْنا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحاطَ بِالنَّاسِ (When We said to you, ‘Indeed your Lord encompasses all mankind’) and the concluding of all segments with His saying: وَنُخَوِّفُهُمْ فَما يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا طُغْياناً كَبِيراً (We deter them, but it only increases them in great rebellion). For the connection of the segments to one another is clear in that the verse is in the process of pointing to a single matter which He, the Glorified, encompasses, and for which neither admonition nor deterrence avails except as an increase in rebellion.

It is inferred from this that the matter is that Allah, the Glorified, showed His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the vision this cursed tree and some of their deeds in Islam, then He clarified to His Messenger that this is a test.

So His saying: وَإِذْ قُلْنا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحاطَ بِالنَّاسِ (When We said to you, ‘Indeed your Lord encompasses all mankind’)—the requirement of the context is that the intended meaning of "encompassing" (الإحاطة) is encompassing in knowledge (الإحاطة العلمية). The adverbial phrase is linked to an omitted verb, the estimation being: "And remember when We said to you such and such." The meaning is: And remember, for the sake of steadfastness in what We have mentioned to you in these verses—that the character of people is to persist in corruption and transgression, and for their successors to follow their predecessors in turning away from the remembrance of Allah and not regarding the signs of Allah—a time when We said to you that your Lord has encompassed the people in knowledge and knew that this established way (السنة) would run among them as it used to run.

His saying: وَما جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْناكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ وَالشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ (and We did not appoint the vision which We showed you except as a test for the people, and the tree cursed in the Quran)—the summary of its meaning based on what preceded is: We did not make the cursed tree in the Quran—which you know by Our defining it—and what We showed you in the dream regarding their affair, except as a test (فتنة) for the people and a trial and tribulation by which We try them and test them, and We have encompassed them.

His saying: وَنُخَوِّفُهُمْ فَما يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا طُغْياناً كَبِيراً (We deter them, but it only increases them in great rebellion)—the plural pronouns refer to the people apparently. The intended meaning of "deterrence" (التخويف) is either deterrence through admonition and explanation, or through terrifying signs that are less than the signs of destruction and annihilation. The meaning is: We deter the people, but the deterrence only increases them in rebellion—and not just any rebellion, but a "great rebellion" (طغياناً كبيراً); meaning they do not fear Our deterrence such that they would cease what they are upon, but rather they answer Us with great rebellion. Thus, they exaggerate in their rebellion and exceed all bounds in their enmity toward the truth.

The context of the verse is one of consolation (التسلية); for Allah, the Glorified, consoles His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) therein regarding the matter He showed him and the trials He made known to him. His established way, the Exalted, has run toward testing His servants with tribulations and trials. More than one commentator has admitted this.

All that has preceded is supported by what has been narrated through the paths of the Sunnis and what the traditions of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) have agreed upon: that the intended meaning of the "vision" in the verse is a vision the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw regarding the Umayyads, and the "tree" is their tree. The narrations will reach you in the upcoming traditional research, Allah willing.

A group of commentators, relying on what was narrated from Ibn Abbas, have mentioned that the intended meaning of the "vision" which Allah showed His Prophet is the Night Journey (الإسراء), and the "cursed tree in the Quran" is the tree of Zaqqum. They mentioned that when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) returned from the Night Journey and morning came, he informed the polytheists of that, so they denied him and mocked him. Likewise, when the polytheists heard verses in which Allah mentioned Zaqqum, they denied him and ridiculed him. So Allah revealed in this verse that the vision which We showed you—which is the Night Journey—and the tree of Zaqqum, We only made them as a test for the people.

Then, when it was pointed out to them that "vision" (الرؤيا), as clarified by the linguists, is what a sleeper sees in his dream, while the Night Journey was in a state of wakefulness, they excused it sometimes by saying that ru'ya (الرؤيا), like ru'yah (الرؤية), is a verbal noun of "to see" (رأى) and is not exclusive to sleep. Sometimes they said that ru'ya is what a person sees at night, whether in sleep or wakefulness. Sometimes they said it was in conformity (مشاكلة) with the polytheists' naming it a vision. Sometimes they said it followed their claim, just as they called their idols "gods"; for it was narrated that some of them said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when he recounted his Night Journey to them: "Perhaps it is something you saw in your dream," so Allah called it a vision according to their claim, just as He said regarding the idols: آلِهَتُهُمُ (their gods). Sometimes they said it was called a vision to liken it to a dream because of the wonders it contained, or because of its occurrence at night, or because of its speed.

Some answered this by saying that the Night Journey was in a dream, as narrated from Aisha and Mu'awiyah.

When it was also pointed out to them that there is no meaning in calling Zaqqum a "cursed tree" and that the tree has no sin, they excused it sometimes by saying that the intended meaning of its being cursed is the cursing of those who eat from it, by way of metaphor in attribution (المجاز في الإسناد) to indicate exaggeration in their being cursed, as has been said. Sometimes they said that "curse" (اللعنة) means distance (البعد), and it is in the furthest place from mercy because it grows at the bottom of Hell. Sometimes they said it was made cursed because its fruit resembles the heads of devils, and devils are cursed. Sometimes they said that the Arabs call every disliked, harmful food "cursed."

As for what they mentioned regarding the meaning of "vision" (الرؤيا), the claim that it is a verbal noun synonymous with "seeing" (الرؤية) or that it means seeing at night is refuted by its lack of linguistic establishment. They did not rely in that on anything from their speech, whether poetry or prose, except mere claim.

As for their saying that it was in conformity with the polytheists' naming the Night Journey a vision, or that it followed their claim that it was a vision, His speech, the Glorified, must be absolutely purified from that. What is the context (القرينة) indicating this artifice and that there is no admission of it being a vision in reality? He, the Exalted, did not call their idols "gods" (آلهة) or "partners" (شركاء) [absolutely], but rather He called them "their gods" (آلِهَتُهُمُ) and "for their partners" (لِشُرَكائِهِمْ), adding them to them. The addition (الإضافة) is an excellent context for the lack of concession. A similar argument applies to their excuse that it is from likening the Night Journey to a vision; for metaphor (الاستعارة), like other tropes, is not valid except with a context. If there were a context there, everyone who said the Night Journey was dream-like would not have used the occurrence of the word "vision" in the verse as evidence, based on the verse referring to the Night Journey.

As for the saying of the one who says that the Night Journey was in a dream, its falsehood has become clear at the beginning of the Surah in the commentary on the verse of the Night Journey.

As for the excuses they mentioned to escape from making the tree cursed in the Quran: their saying that the reality of its being cursed is the cursing of those who eat from it by way of metaphor in attribution for exaggeration in their being cursed—this, although it has many parallels in common parlance, is something from which the threshold of His speech, the Exalted, must be purified. Rather, it is the habit of ignorant and base people; you see them, when they want to insult someone, cursing him by cursing his father, his mother, and his tribe, as an exaggeration in insulting him. When they revile a man, they speak ill of his wife and daughter, and they revile the sky that shades him, the earth that carries him, the house he inhabits, and the people he associates with. The etiquette of the Quran prevents Him from exaggerating the curse of the companions of the Fire by cursing the tree with whose fruits Allah punishes them.

Their saying that "curse" (اللعن) is absolute distancing (الإبعاد) is something not established linguistically. What they mentioned, and what is testified to by its usage in the Quran, is that its meaning is distancing from mercy and honor. As for what was said—that since it is as Allah said: شَجَرَةٌ تَخْرُجُ فِي أَصْلِ الْجَحِيمِ (a tree that comes forth from the bottom of Hell), it is in the furthest place from mercy—if by "mercy" Paradise is intended, then it is a saying without evidence. If by it the mercy that is the opposite of punishment is intended, its necessity would be that the tree is cursed in the sense of being distanced from mercy and honor, and its implication would be that Hell and what Allah prepared in it of punishment, and the angels of the Fire and its keepers, are cursed, wrath-stricken, and distanced from mercy. Yet none of these are cursed; rather, the curse, wrath, and distance are for those punished therein among humans and jinn.

Their saying that it was made cursed because its fruit resembles the heads of devils and devils are cursed—this is a metaphor in attribution far from understanding, and what we raised against the first view applies to it.

Their saying that the Arabs call every disliked, harmful food "cursed"—this involves using "tree" and intending "fruit" metaphorically, then making it cursed because it is disliked and harmful, or attributing the curse—which is a description of the fruit—to the tree metaphorically. In any case, its being one of the meanings of "curse" is not established; rather, it appears they describe it with "curse" in its known meaning, and the common folk curse everything they do not approve of, whether food, drink, or otherwise.

As for the attribution of the view to Ibn Abbas, even assuming its authenticity, there is no authority (حجية) in it, especially with its contradiction to what is in the hadith of Aisha mentioned later and others, which contains the interpretation of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the view of anyone else does not contradict it.

In al-Kashshaf, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قُلْنا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحاطَ بِالنَّاسِ (When We said to you, ‘Indeed your Lord encompasses all mankind’): "And remember when We revealed to you that your Lord has encompassed the Quraysh, meaning We gave you glad tidings of the Battle of Badr and of victory over them, and that is His saying: سَيُهْزَمُ الْجَمْعُ وَيُوَلُّونَ الدُّبُرَ — The host will be routed and they will turn their backs [to flee]. (54:45), and قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَتُغْلَبُونَ وَتُحْشَرُونَ — Say to the faithless, ‘You will be overcome and mustered...’ (3:12), and other than that. So He made it as if it had already happened and existed, saying: أَحاطَ بِالنَّاسِ (encompassed the people) according to His custom in His reporting.

When the two groups advanced toward each other on the day of Badr, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was in the arbor with Abu Bakr, he was supplicating and saying: 'O Allah, I ask You for Your covenant and Your promise.' Then he went out wearing the coat of mail, inciting the people and saying: سَيُهْزَمُ الْجَمْعُ وَيُوَلُّونَ الدُّبُرَ.

Perhaps Allah, the Exalted, showed him their places of death in his vision; for he used to say when he arrived at the water of Badr: 'By Allah, it is as if I am looking at the places of death of the people,' and he would point to the ground and say: 'This is the place of death of so-and-so, this is the place of death of so-and-so.' The Quraysh heard of what was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding the matter of the day of Badr and what he saw in his vision of their places of death, so they laughed, ridiculed, and sought to hasten it in mockery.

When they heard His saying: إِنَّ شَجَرَةَ الزَّقُّومِ طَعامُ الْأَثِيمِ — Indeed the tree of Zaqqum is the food of the sinful. (44:43-44), they made it a subject of ridicule and said: 'Muhammad claims that Hell burns stones, then he says: trees grow in it'—until he said—and the meaning is that the signs are only sent as a deterrence for the servants, and these have been deterred by the punishment of this world, which is the killing on the day of Badr." End quote. Then he mentioned the interpretation of the vision in the verse as the Night Journey, attributing it to a "it was said."

It is apparent that he did not approve of the interpretation of the vision in the verse as the Night Journey, even if it was attributed to a narration. So he turned away from it to interpreting it as the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) vision of the Battle of Badr before its occurrence, and the Quraysh hearing of that and mocking him, thus making it a test for them. He escaped the محذور (problematic implication) that follows their interpretation of the vision as the Night Journey, but he fell into something no less problematic—if not more so—which is interpreting the vision as what he hoped the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) might have seen in his dream of the Battle of Badr and the places of death of the people before its occurrence, and the Quraysh mocking him, thus making it a test for them. He has no proof for what he interpreted except his saying: "Perhaps Allah showed him their places of death in his vision." How can one dare to interpret His speech, the Exalted, by imagining a matter for which there is no support or proof from a reliable tradition or evidence from within the verses to which one can refer?

Some have mentioned that the intended meaning of the vision is the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) vision that he would enter Mecca and the Holy Mosque, which is what Allah, the Glorified, mentioned in His saying: لَقَدْ صَدَقَ اللهُ رَسُولَهُ الرُّؤْيا (Certainly Allah has fulfilled His Apostle’s vision...) the verse.

The problem with this is that this vision was only seen by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) after the Hijrah, before the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, while the verse is Meccan. We shall complete the research on this vision, Allah willing.

Some have mentioned that the intended meaning of the "cursed tree in the Quran" is the Jews, and this was attributed to the commentator Abu Muslim.

What can be used to justify this view has already been mentioned, along with what refutes it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ قالَ أَأَسْجُدُ لِمَنْ خَلَقْتَ طِيناً — When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis. He said, ‘Shall I prostrate before one whom You have created of clay?’ (17:61). It says in al-Majma': "Az-Zajjaj said: 'clay' (طيناً) is in the accusative case as a circumstantial qualifier (حال), meaning that You created him in the state of being from clay. It is also possible that its estimation is 'from clay' (من طين), so 'from' was omitted and the verb reached the noun directly; similar to it is His saying: أَنْ تَسْتَرْضِعُوا أَوْلادَكُمْ (that you should have your children suckled), meaning 'for your children.' It was also said that it is in the accusative as a specification (تمييز)." End quote.

In al-Kashshaf, it was permitted to be a circumstantial qualifier for the relative pronoun (الموصول), not for the object of "You created" (خَلَقْتَ) as Az-Zajjaj said. It was said that being a circumstantial qualifier is, in any case, contrary to the apparent sense because "clay" (طِيناً) is a concrete noun (جامد).

In the verse is another reminder for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of the story of Iblis and what transpired between him and Allah, the Glorified, of dialogue when he disobeyed the command of prostration. This is so he may be steadfast in what Allah informed him of the state of the people: that they have never ceased to disregard the command of Allah, to be arrogant toward the truth, and to not regard the signs of Allah, and they will never cease to be so. So let him remember the story of Iblis and what he resolved upon—to bring the progeny of Adam under his sway—and that Allah gave him power that day over those of the children of Adam who obeyed him and followed his call and the call of his horsemen and footmen, and He did not except in his resolution anyone but His sincere servants.

The meaning is: And remember when your Lord said to the angels, "Prostrate before Adam," and they prostrated except Iblis—so it is as if it were said: what did he do? Or what did he say when he did not prostrate? It was said: he denied the command of prostration and said, "Shall I prostrate"—the interrogation being for denial—"to one whom You created from clay, while You created me from fire, and it is more noble than clay?"

In the story there is a summarization by the omission of some of its segments. The reason for this is that the context necessitated it; for the purpose is to explain the causes and factors necessitating the persistence of the children of Adam in injustice and transgression. He mentioned first that the former ones among them did not believe in the proposed signs, and the later ones are built upon emulating them. Then He reminded him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that there are trials by which they will be tested. Then He reminded him of what He recounted to him of the story of Adam and Iblis, which contains Iblis's resolution to mislead the progeny of Adam and his asking that Allah give him power over them, and His, the Exalted, answering him in that regarding the perverse. Thus, it is not far-fetched that most people should incline toward the path of misguidance and fall into injustice, rebellion, and turning away from the signs of Allah, while the divine test has encompassed them from one side and Satan with his horsemen and footmen from another.

(1) al-Sinwan: two palm trees that emerge from a single root. (2) With its fixity in the rank of its causes, not in the rank of itself. "Author's note."

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ أَرَأَيْتَكَ هَذَا الَّذِي كَرَّمْتَ عَلَيَّ لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا "He said, ‘Tell me, this one whom You have honoured above me, if You reprieve me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his progeny, all but a few.’" (17:62). The kāf in the word ara’aytaka (أَرَأَيْتَكَ) is augmentative (زائدة) and has no grammatical position; rather, it serves to denote address (الخطاب), as is the case with demonstrative pronouns. What is meant by His saying: "this one whom You have honoured above me" (هذَا الَّذِي كَرَّمْتَ عَلَيَ) is Adam (peace be upon him). His being honored over Iblis refers to his being preferred over him by the command to prostrate to him, and the subsequent banishment of Iblis when he refused.

From this, it becomes apparent that Iblis understood this preference from the command of prostration, just as he ventured to intend the seduction of Adam's progeny based on what transpired in the dialogue between the Exalted and the angels, specifically their saying: أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَنْ يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ "Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood?" (2:30). What was previously mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of that verse is beneficial here.

The term iḥtināk (الاحتناك), according to al-Majma‘, means uprooting or severing from the source (الاقتطاع من الأصل). It is said: "So-and-so iḥtanaka what another possessed of wealth or knowledge" when he exhausts it and takes it all. Similarly, "the locusts iḥtanaka the crop" when they consume it entirely. It is also said to be derived from their saying: "He ḥanaka the beast with its rope" when he places a rope in its lower jaw (حنكها الأسفل) to lead it. It appears that this latter meaning is the root of the matter; thus, iḥtināk means bridling (الإلجام).

The meaning is: Iblis said, after he had disobeyed and the Divine wrath had seized him: "O Lord, tell me about this one whom You have preferred over me by commanding me to prostrate to him and banishing me for my disobedience; I swear that if You reprieve me until the Day of Resurrection—which is the duration of the stay of the children of Adam on earth—I will surely bridle his progeny (لألجمن ذريته) except for a few of them," and these are the sincere ones (المخلصون).

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ اذْهَبْ فَمَنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزَاؤُكُمْ جَزَاءً مَوْفُورًا "He said, ‘Go! Whoever of them follows you, then hell shall be the recompense of you [all], a full recompense." (17:63). It has been said that the command to "Go" (اذْهَبْ) is not intended literally, but is rather a metaphor (كناية) for leaving him to his own devices, just as you might say to someone who opposes you: "Do as you wish." Others say the command is literal, being another expression of His saying in another place: فَاخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ "Begone from here, for you are indeed an outcast." (15:34). The term mawfür (الموفور) means that which is completed; thus, a "full recompense" (الجزاء الموفور) is the reward that is paid in full without anything being withheld. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُمْ بِصَوْتِكَ وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِمْ بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ "And incite whomever of them you can with your voice, and rally against them your cavalry and your infantry..." (17:64) until the end of the verse. Istifzāz (الاستفزاز) means to unsettle and rouse with lightness and haste. Ijlāb (الإجلاب), as stated in al-Majma‘, means to drive with a clamor (جلبة) from the driver, and jalbah is the intensity of sound. In al-Mufradāt, it is said: "The root of jalb is the driving of a thing... and ajlabtu ‘alayhi means I shouted at him with subjugation."

The term khayl (الخيل)—as has been said—literally refers to horses and has no singular form from its own root, and it is used metaphorically for horsemen (الفرسان). The term rajil (الرجل) refers to the foot-soldier (الراجل), which is the opposite of the rider. Its being contrasted with "cavalry" (الخيل) suggests that it refers to the infantry (الرجالة), who are the members of the army other than the horsemen.

Thus, His saying: "And incite whomever of them you can with your voice" (وَاسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُمْ بِصَوْتِكَ) means: rouse toward disobedience whomever you are able to rouse from the progeny of Adam—those who take him as a guardian and follow him, as mentioned in Surah al-Hijr—with your voice. It is as if inciting with the voice is a metaphor for belittling them through false whispering (الوسوسة) that lacks reality, and a representation (تمثيل) of how sheep and other animals are driven by shouting and scolding, which is a sound devoid of [rational] meaning.

His saying: "and rally against them your cavalry and your infantry" (وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِمْ بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ) means: shout at them to drive them toward the disobedience of Allah with your assistants and your armies, both their horsemen and their foot-soldiers. This seems to be an indication that among his tribe and assistants are those who perform their actions with speed, as is the nature of cavalry in the heat of battle, and those who are employed in situations other than rapid charges, like the infantry. Thus, "cavalry and infantry" is a metaphor for those who are swift in action and those who are slow, and it contains a representation of the nature of their work.

His saying: وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْوَالِ وَالْأَوْلَادِ "and share with them in wealth and children" (17:64). Partnership (الشركة) can only be conceived in ownership (الملك) and exclusive right (الاختصاص), the consequence of which is that the partner becomes a co-sharer with his associate in the benefit, which is the purpose of acquiring wealth and children. For wealth is an external entity separate from the human being, and likewise, a child is an independent human person separate from his parents. Were it not for the purpose of benefit, man would not consider wealth as property nor a child as an exclusive relation.

Therefore, the Devil's partnership with man in his wealth or child is his participation in the exclusive right and benefit. For instance, wealth—which Allah made to fulfill man's natural needs—might be acquired through unlawful means, so the Devil benefits from it for his purpose and man for his natural purpose. Or he might acquire it through lawful means but use it in other than the obedience of Allah, so they both benefit from it while he remains empty-handed of the mercy of Allah. Similarly, a child might be born through unlawful means, or born lawfully but then raised with an unrighteous upbringing and disciplined with other than the discipline of Allah, thereby giving the Devil a share and himself a share, and so on.

This is a sound perspective on the meaning of the verse and encompasses the various views mentioned by the commentators (المفسرون). For example, some said that the wealth and children in which the Devil shares are every piece of wealth acquired from the forbidden (حرام) or taken unjustly, and every child of adultery, as narrated from Ibn Abbas and others.

Another view is that his partnership in wealth consists of his commanding them to designate animals as sā’ibah and baḥīrah and the like, and in children by their making them Jews, Christians, or Magians, as narrated from Qatadah.

Another view is that every forbidden wealth and forbidden intimacy involves his partnership, as narrated from al-Kalbi. Another view is that what is meant by children is naming them ‘Abd al-Shams, ‘Abd al-Harith, and the like. Another view is that it refers to the killing of the infant girls (الموءودة) among their children, as also narrated from Ibn Abbas. Another view is that partnership in wealth refers to sacrificing for idols, as narrated from al-Dahhak, among other reports from the early commentators.

His saying: وَعِدْهُمْ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا "and make promises to them.’ But Satan makes them no promises except to delude [them]." (17:64). That is, he promises them nothing but a deceptive promise by presenting error in the form of correctness and falsehood in the guise of truth. Thus, "delusion" (الغرور) is a verbal noun (مصدر) used in the sense of an active participle for emphasis.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ وَكَفَى بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them,’ and your Lord suffices as a guardian." (17:65). What is meant by "My servants" (عبادي) is broader than the sincere ones (المخلصون) whom Iblis excluded by his saying: "all but a few" (إِلَّا قَلِيلًا). Rather, it refers to those other than the perverse (الغاوين) among the followers of Iblis, as He said in another place: إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you." (15:42). The possessive construction (الإضافة) here is for honor (التشريف).

His saying: "and your Lord suffices as a guardian" (وَكَفَى بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا) means: as One who watches over their souls and their deeds, preserving their interests and taking charge of their affairs. For the guardian (الوكيل) is the one who sponsors the affairs of another and stands in his place in managing and directing them. By this, it becomes clear that what is meant is His specific guardianship (وكالته الخاصة) for those of His servants who are not perverse, as previously mentioned in Surah al-Hijr.

Various discussions regarding the story of Adam's prostration, which are useful in this context, have already been presented in scattered places of His speech, such as Surah al-Baqarah, Surah al-A‘raf, and Surah al-Hijr.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Ibn Sinan from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا نَحْنُ مُهْلِكُوهَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ "There is no town but We shall destroy it before the Day of Resurrection" (17:58), he said: "It is annihilation through death or otherwise." In another narration from him (peace be upon him) regarding the same verse, he said: "By killing and death or otherwise."

I say: This is perhaps an explanation of the entire verse.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا مَنَعَنَا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآيَاتِ "Nothing kept Us from sending the signs..." (17:59), he said: "It was revealed concerning the Quraysh." He said: In a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "That was because Muhammad asked his people to bring them [signs]—so Gabriel descended and said: 'Indeed Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: وَمَا مَنَعَنَا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآيَاتِ إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ (Nothing kept Us from sending the signs except that the former peoples denied them). Whenever We sent a sign to the Quraysh and they did not believe in it, We would have destroyed them—therefore We delayed the signs from your people.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, al-Nasa’i, al-Bazzar, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il, and al-Diya’ in al-Mukhtarah narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The people of Mecca asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to turn al-Safa into gold for them and to remove the mountains from them so they could farm. It was said to him: 'If you wish, We shall be patient with them, or if you wish, We shall give them what they asked—but if they then disbelieve, they shall be destroyed as the nations before them were destroyed.' He said: 'No, rather I will be patient with them.' So Allah revealed: وَمَا مَنَعَنَا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآيَاتِ إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ."

I say: Something similar has been narrated through numerous chains.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from Sahl ibn Sa‘d who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw the children of so-and-so leaping upon his pulpit like the leaping of monkeys, and that distressed him. He was never seen laughing fully until he died. So Allah revealed: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ 'We did not appoint the vision that We showed you except as a tribulation for the people' (17:60)."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Umar that the Prophet said: "I saw the children of al-Hakam ibn Abi al-‘As on the pulpits as if they were monkeys, and Allah revealed concerning that: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ وَالشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ 'We did not appoint the vision that We showed you except as a tribulation for the people and the cursed tree' (17:60), meaning al-Hakam and his children."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ya‘la ibn Murrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'I was shown the Umayyads on the pulpits of the earth, and they will soon rule over you, and you will find them to be evil masters.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was grieved by that, so Allah revealed: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Husayn ibn ‘Ali: "The Messenger of Allah woke up one morning feeling distressed. It was said: 'What is the matter with you, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'I was shown in a dream as if the Umayyads were taking turns on this pulpit of mine.' It was said: 'O Messenger of Allah, do not be distressed, for it is only the world (دنيا) that they shall attain.' So Allah revealed: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il, and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw the Umayyads on the pulpits and that distressed him. Then Allah revealed to him that it is only the world (دنيا) they have been given, so his eyes were comforted. This is His saying: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ, meaning a trial (بلاء) for the people."

I say: It is also narrated in Tafsir al-Burhan from al-Tha‘labi in his Tafsir, tracing it back to Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from the book Fadilat al-Husayn, tracing it back to Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'I saw in a dream the children of al-Hakam or the children of al-‘As leaping upon my pulpit as monkeys leap.' He woke up as if enraged, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was never seen laughing fully after that until he died."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘A’ishah that she said to Marwan ibn al-Hakam: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying to your father and your grandfather: 'Indeed, you are the cursed tree in the Qur'an.'"

In Majma‘ al-Bayan: "A vision the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw that monkeys were ascending his pulpit and descending, and that distressed and grieved him." Narrated by Sahl ibn Sa‘d from his father. Then he said: "And it is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them), and they said: 'According to this interpretation (تأويل), the cursed tree in the Qur'an is the Umayyads.'"

I say: This is not a matter of interpretation (تأويل) at all, but rather it is the actual revelation (تنزيل) as previously explained, except that the term ta’wīl was sometimes used in their speech for any explanation of the intended meaning.

Al-‘Ayyashi narrated this meaning in his Tafsir from several trustworthy narrators such as Zurarah, Humran, Muhammad ibn Muslim, Ma‘ruf ibn Kharbudh, Salam al-Ju‘fi, al-Qasim ibn Sulayman, Yunus ibn ‘Abd al-Rahman al-Ashal, and ‘Abd al-Rahim al-Qasir from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them). It was also narrated by al-Qummi in his Tafsir as a suspended (مضمر) report, and by al-‘Ayyashi from Abu al-Tufayl from ‘Ali (peace be upon him).

In some of these narrations, others are mentioned alongside the Umayyads, and what was previously discussed regarding the meaning of the verse guides toward this. Narrations have also passed under His saying: وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ "And the parable of an evil word is that of an evil tree..." (14:26), stating that the evil tree refers to the two most wicked clans of the Quraysh.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, ‘Abd al-Razzaq, Sa‘id ibn Mansur, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ that he said: "It was a vision of the eye (رؤيا عين) shown to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) on the night he was taken on the night journey to Jerusalem, and it was not a dream in sleep." Regarding "the cursed tree in the Qur'an," he said: "It is the tree of Zaqqum."

I say: This meaning is also narrated from Ibn Sa‘d, Abu Ya‘la, and Ibn ‘Asakir from Umm Hani. You have already seen the status of this narration in the discussion on the commentary of the verse.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ that he said: "The Messenger of Allah was shown that he and his companions entered Mecca while he was in Medina at that time. So he traveled to Mecca before the appointed time, but the polytheists turned him back. Some people said: 'He has been turned back, yet he had told us that he would enter it.' So his return was their trial (فتنة)."

I say: What applies to this narration has already been mentioned in the commentary of the verse, in addition to the fact that it contradicts what preceded it.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Husayn ibn Sa‘id in the book al-Zuhd, from ‘Uthman ibn ‘Isa from ‘Umar ibn Udhaynah from Sulayman ibn Qays who said: "I heard the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) saying: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: Indeed, Allah has forbidden Paradise to every foul-mouthed (فحاش), obscene person of little modesty who does not care what he says or what is said to him. For if you were to investigate him, you would find him to be either a product of fornication (لغية) or a partnership of the Devil (شرك شيطان).'"

A man asked: "O Messenger of Allah, is there a partnership of the Devil among the people?" He replied: "Do you not read the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْوَالِ وَالْأَوْلَادِ (and share with them in wealth and children)?"

He asked: "Who is the one who does not care what he says or what is said to him?" He replied: "Yes, the one who attacks people and speaks about them while knowing that they will not leave him alone; that is the one who does not care what he says or what is said to him."

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): "I asked him about the partnership of the Devil (شرك الشيطان) in His saying: وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْوَالِ وَالْأَوْلَادِ. He said: 'Whatever wealth is forbidden (حرام) is a partnership of the Devil.' He said: 'And he is with the man until he has intercourse, so it [the child] is from his sperm and the man's sperm if it was forbidden.'"

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous, and they are of the nature of mentioning specific instances (المصاديق). The comprehensive meaning for them has already been presented.

What is mentioned in them regarding his partnership with the man in intercourse, the sperm, and so on, is a metaphor (كناية) for his having a portion in all of that. It is a form of representation (التمثيل) by which the intended meaning is clarified, and its parallels are many in the narrations.

Ayahs

رَّبُّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُزْجِى لَكُمُ ٱلْفُلْكَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ لِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا (٦٦)
Your Lord is He who drives for you the ships in the sea that you may seek of His bounty. Indeed, He is most merciful to you. (66)
وَإِذَا مَسَّكُمُ ٱلضُّرُّ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ ضَلَّ مَن تَدْعُونَ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ ۖ فَلَمَّا نَجَّىٰكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْبَرِّ أَعْرَضْتُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ كَفُورًا (٦٧)
When distress befalls you at sea, those whom you invoke besides Him are forsaken. But when He delivers you to land, you are disregardful [of Him]. Man is very ungrateful. (67)
أَفَأَمِنتُمْ أَن يَخْسِفَ بِكُمْ جَانِبَ ٱلْبَرِّ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حَاصِبًا ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُوا۟ لَكُمْ وَكِيلًا (٦٨)
Do you feel secure that He will not make the coastland swallow you, or He will not unleash upon you a rain of stones? Then you will not find any defender for yourselves. (68)
أَمْ أَمِنتُمْ أَن يُعِيدَكُمْ فِيهِ تَارَةً أُخْرَىٰ فَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ قَاصِفًا مِّنَ ٱلرِّيحِ فَيُغْرِقَكُم بِمَا كَفَرْتُمْ ۙ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُوا۟ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا بِهِۦ تَبِيعًا (٦٩)
Do you feel secure that He will not send you back into it another time and unleash against you a shattering gale and drown you because of your unfaith? Then you will not find for yourselves any redresser against Us. (69)
۞ وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ وَحَمَلْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَفَضَّلْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا تَفْضِيلًا (٧٠)
Certainly We have honoured the Children of Adam, and carried them over land and sea, and provided them with all the good things, and preferred them with a complete preference over many of those We have created. (70)
يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا۟ كُلَّ أُنَاسٍۭ بِإِمَـٰمِهِمْ ۖ فَمَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَقْرَءُونَ كِتَـٰبَهُمْ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا (٧١)
The day We shall summon every group of people along with their imam, then whoever is given his book in his right hand—they will read it, and they will not be wronged so much as a single date-thread. (71)
وَمَن كَانَ فِى هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَعْمَىٰ فَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَعْمَىٰ وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا (٧٢)
But whoever has been blind in this [world], will be blind in the Hereafter, and [even] more astray from the [right] way. (72)

(Commentary)

These verses serve as a completion to the preceding ones, establishing for Allah, the Glorified, the power to respond to supplication and remove harm—attributes that the previous section denied regarding their idols and statues. The preceding verses began with His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ ادْعُوا الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ فَلا يَمْلِكُونَ كَشْفَ الضُّرِّ عَنْكُمْ وَلا تَحْوِيلاً — "Say, ‘Invoke those whom you claim [to be gods] besides Him: they have no power to remove your distress, nor to bring about any change’" (17:56). These verses open with His saying: رَبُّكُمُ الَّذِي يُزْجِي لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ فِي الْبَحْرِ — "Your Lord is He who drives for you the ships in the sea..." (17:66), and so on.

We have said they are like a completion to the explanation of the previous verses—even though the content of each of the two sections constitutes a complete proof in its own right, one negating the divinity of their gods and the other establishing the divinity of Allah, the Glorified—because the first section opens with the command "Say" (قُلِ), while the second does not. The apparent meaning is that the two sections together form a single argument that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to present to the polytheists to compel them toward monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts.

This is supported by the preceding context, which began with His saying: قُلْ لَوْ كانَ مَعَهُ آلِهَةٌ كَما يَقُولُونَ إِذاً لَابْتَغَوْا إِلى ذِي الْعَرْشِ سَبِيلاً — "Say, ‘If there were [other] gods with Him, as they say, they would have sought a way to the Lord of the Throne’" (17:42). This was followed by His saying a second time: وَقالُوا أَإِذا كُنَّا عِظاماً — "They say, ‘What, when we have become bones...’" (17:49), until He said: قُلْ كُونُوا حِجارَةً أَوْ حَدِيداً — "Say, ‘Be you stones or iron’" (17:50).

He concluded the verses with His saying: يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ — "The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam..." (17:71), thereby indicating that the guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — and the misguidance (الضلالة) mentioned in this world will necessarily accompany man in the Hereafter. Thus, the next realm is patterned after the first; whoever was insightful in this world will be insightful in the Hereafter, and whoever was blind in this world will be blind in the Hereafter and further astray from the path.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبُّكُمُ الَّذِي يُزْجِي لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ فِي الْبَحْرِ لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ إِنَّهُ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً — "Your Lord is He who drives for you the ships in the sea that you may seek His grace. Indeed He is All-merciful to you" (17:66). Driving (الإزجاء), according to Majma‘ al-Bayan, is to urge something forward stage by stage. What is meant here is the movement of ships in the sea by sending winds and the like, and by making the water fluid and yielding so that it accepts flow and cleavage. The term al-fulk (الفلك) is a collective noun for ships.

"Seeking grace" (ابتغاء الفضل) refers to seeking sustenance (الرزق), for the Generous One usually bestows that which exceeds His own need; thus, the "grace" (الفضل) of a thing is its surplus or remainder. The preposition "from" (من) indicates the starting point, though it is sometimes said to denote partitivity. The end of the verse is a justification for the decree based on mercy, and the meaning is clear. This verse serves as a preamble to the one following it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا مَسَّكُمُ الضُّرُّ فِي الْبَحْرِ ضَلَّ مَنْ تَدْعُونَ إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ — "When distress befalls you at sea, those whom you invoke forsake you, except Him..." (17:67) to the end of the verse. Distress (الضر) refers to hardship. The "touch of distress at sea" is the fear of drowning when one is on the verge of it due to gale-force winds, the tossing of waves, and the like.

Regarding His saying: ضَلَّ مَنْ تَدْعُونَ إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ — "those whom you invoke forsake you, except Him," the intended meaning of "forsaking" or "going astray" (الضلال)—according to what has been mentioned—is their departure from one's thoughts, rather than straying from a path. It is also said to mean "loss," as in the expression "such a thing went astray (ضل) from so-and-so," meaning it was lost to him. In any case, it returns to the meaning of forgetting.

The "invocation" (الدعاء) intended here is the supplication of request (دعاء المسألة) rather than the supplication of worship (دعاء العبادة). Thus, the phrase "those whom you invoke" (مَنْ تَدْعُونَ) encompasses both the True God and the false deities invoked by the polytheists. The exception is connected (متصل). The meaning is: when the situation becomes dire for you at sea as you face drowning, you forget every deity you used to invoke and ask for your needs, except Allah.

It has also been said that the intended meaning is the supplication of worship rather than request; thus, it would specifically refer to those they worship besides Allah, making the exception disconnected (منقطع). The meaning would then be: when distress touches you at sea, the deities you worship vanish from your thoughts, but Allah, the Glorified, is never absent from you nor forgotten.

However, it appears that "going astray" (الضلال) carries its well-known meaning, which is the opposite of guidance (الهدى). The discourse is built upon a subtle metaphor: it is as if when man is touched by distress at sea and it occurs to his heart to supplicate for the removal of his distress, he seeks out his deities whom he used to invoke and continued to invoke previously.
