They, in turn, begin to hasten toward him, racing to cross the path to his memory so that he might remember them, invoke them, and seek their aid. Yet, they all lose the way (يضلون الطريق) and fail to reach his memory, so he forgets them. Meanwhile, Allah, the Glorified, is witnessed by his heart and present in his memory; man remembers Him at that moment and invokes Him, though he had been turning away from Him, so He answers him and delivers him to the land.

By this, it becomes clear that "going astray" (الضلال) carries its well-known meaning, and that "those whom you invoke" refers only to their deities besides Allah. Thus, the exception is disconnected (منقطع). The reason for making the exception disconnected is that the metaphorical imagery upon which the discourse is built is not suitable for His sanctuary of holiness, as He is transcendent above striving, being on a path, crossing it, and the like.

Furthermore, His saying: فَلَمَّا نَجَّاكُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ أَعْرَضْتُمْ — "But when He delivers you to land, you turn away" (17:67) makes it apparent that the "invocation" intended is the supplication of request, and that on land—that is, in their normal state, not the state of distress—they turn away from Him, the Exalted, and do not invoke Him. Therefore, His saying: مَنْ تَدْعُونَ — "those whom you invoke," which suggests a continuity of invocation, refers to their deities whom they used to invoke; thus, the exception of Him, the Exalted, is a disconnected exception.

His saying: فَلَمَّا نَجَّاكُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ أَعْرَضْتُمْ — "But when He delivers you to land, you turn away" means: when He saved you from drowning and removed the distress from you, returning you to the land, you turned away from Him or from supplicating to Him. This contains an indication that Allah, the Exalted, is never truly forgotten by man in any state, and that his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides him to supplicate to Him in both adversity and prosperity, hardship and ease. This is because "turning away" (الإعراض) can only occur from something that is established and present. Thus, His saying that man invokes Him in distress and turns away from Him after its removal implies that he is perpetually guided toward Him by his primordial nature.

His saying: وَكانَ الْإِنْسانُ كَفُوراً — "and man is very ungrateful" (17:67) means that ingratitude is the habit of man insofar as he possesses human nature. He attaches himself to apparent causes and thus forgets the Causer of causes, failing to thank Him, the Exalted, while he fluctuates within His manifest and hidden blessings.

Concluding the discourse with this sentence serves as a reminder that man's turning away from his Lord in states other than distress is not an instinctive or primordial state for him—such that his forgetting his Lord could be used as an argument to negate Lordship (الربوبية). Rather, it is an evil habit of man into which he is plunged by the ingratitude for blessings.

The verse contains a proof for His uniqueness in His Lordship. Its summary is that when man is cut off from all apparent causes and despairs of them, he does not cease to be attached to a cause in principle, nor does his hope for salvation vanish entirely. Rather, he hopes for salvation and his heart attaches itself to a cause that is capable of what all other causes are incapable of. This primordial attachment would have no meaning if there were not a Cause above all causes to whom all matters return, and that is Allah, the Glorified. Nothing turns man away from Him except preoccupation with the ornaments of the life of this world, attachment to apparent causes, and heedlessness of what lies beyond them.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَأَمِنْتُمْ أَنْ يَخْسِفَ بِكُمْ جانِبَ الْبَرِّ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حاصِباً ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُوا لَكُمْ وَكِيلاً — "Do you feel secure that He will not cause a side of the land to swallow you up, or unleash a squall of pebbles against you, then you will not find any guardian for yourselves?" (17:68). The "eclipse" (خسوف) of the moon is the hiding of its disk by darkness and shadow; "Allah caused the earth to swallow him" (خسف الله به الأرض) means He hid him within it. The ḥāṣib (الحاصب), as in al-Majma‘, is a wind that pelts with pebbles and small stones. It is also said that the ḥāṣib is a destructive wind on land, while the qāṣif (القاصف) is a destructive wind at sea.

The interrogative is for rebuke (التوبيخ). Allah, the Exalted, rebukes them for turning away from supplicating to Him on land, for they have no security from destructive events on land just as they had no security when distress touched them at sea. They have no knowledge of what will happen to them or against them. It is possible that Allah might cause a side of the land to swallow them or send a pebble-scattering wind upon them, destroying them thereby, and then they would find no guardian (وكيل) for themselves to repel the hardship and calamity or to restore security and peace to them.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ أَمِنْتُمْ أَنْ يُعِيدَكُمْ فِيهِ تارَةً أُخْرى — "Or do you feel secure that He will not send you back into it another time..." (17:69) to the end of the verse. Al-qaṣf (القصف) is to break with force; a "shattering wind" (قاصف الريح) is one that breaks ships and buildings. It is also said that the qāṣif is a destructive wind at sea. The tabī‘ (التبيع) is the follower (التابع) who pursues something. The pronoun in "into it" (فِيهِ) refers to the sea, and the pronoun in "for it" (بِهِ) refers to the drowning, the sending, or both together considering what occurred; there are proponents for each view. The verse is a continuation of the rebuke.

The meaning is: Or do you feel secure, by your delivery to the land, that Allah will not return you to the sea another time and send upon you a wind that shatters ships or destroys, thus drowning you because of your disbelief? Then, because of the drowning, you would not find anyone to pursue Allah on your behalf, asking Him why He did this to you, or calling Him to account for what He did.

In His saying: ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُوا لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا بِهِ تَبِيعاً — "then you will not find for yourselves any pursuer against Us for it," there is a transition (التفات) from the third person to the first person plural. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is the manifestation of greatness and majesty over the opponent, which is appropriate in this context, and also to serve as a preamble for the first person plural style in the following verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنا بَنِي آدَمَ وَحَمَلْناهُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلى كَثِيرٍ مِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا تَفْضِيلاً — "Certainly We have honored the Children of Adam, and carried them over land and sea, and provided them with all the good things, and preferred them with a complete preference over many of those We have created" (17:70). The verse is framed as an expression of favor (الامتنان) mixed with reproach. It is as if He, the Exalted, having mentioned the abundance of His blessings and the succession of His grace and mercy upon man—carrying him at sea to seek His grace and sustenance, and his state of ease on land, followed by his forgetting his Lord and turning away from supplicating to Him when He saves him and removes his distress out of ingratitude, even though he perpetually fluctuates within His innumerable blessings—now points to the totality of His honoring and preferring him. This is so that the extent of His care for man may be known, as well as man's ingratitude for His blessings despite their abundance and reach.

By this, it becomes clear that the verse is intended to describe the state of humanity in general, setting aside the specific divine honor, proximity, and purely spiritual virtue that characterizes some of them. Thus, the discourse encompasses polytheists, disbelievers, and the profligate; otherwise, the sense of favor and reproach would not be complete.

Regarding His saying: وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنا بَنِي آدَمَ — "Certainly We have honored the Children of Adam," honoring (التكريم) refers to singling out a thing for care and ennobling it with that which is specific to it and not found in others. By this, it differs from preference (التفضيل); for honoring is an intrinsic (نفسي) meaning—making the thing noble and possessing honor in itself—while preference is a relative (إضافي) meaning—singling it out for an increase in bestowal compared to others, while both share in the basic bestowal. Man is distinguished among cosmic beings by the intellect (العقل) and exceeds others in all attributes, states, and actions that exist among them.

This becomes manifest by comparing how man innovates in his food, drink, clothing, dwelling, and marriage; the order and management he brings to his society; and the means he employs for his objectives by utilizing other cosmic beings. Compare this to what other animals, plants, and the like possess; they have only simple, rudimentary modes of behavior, or nearly so, and they remain in their fixed stations assigned to them since their creation without any perceptible change or transformation. Man, however, has traveled toward distant goals in all aspects of his perfective life and continues to strive and ascend.

In summary, the Children of Adam are honored by what Allah has specifically granted them among all other cosmic beings, which is that by which they are distinguished from others: the intellect (العقل), through which they recognize truth from falsehood, good from evil, and the beneficial from the harmful.

As for what the commentators have mentioned or what has appeared in narrations—that the honor Allah bestowed upon them is speech, or an upright and extended stature, or fingers with which they do as they wish, or eating with the hand, or writing, or a beautiful form, or dominion over other creatures and their subjugation to him, or that Allah created their father Adam with His hand, or that He made Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from among them—all of these, or whatever else has been mentioned, are cited by way of example.

Some of these are branches of the intellect, such as writing, speech, and dominion over other creatures. Others are instances of preference (التفضيل) rather than honoring (التكريم), and the difference between them has already been mentioned. Still others fall outside the scope of the verse, such as the honor of creating their father Adam (عليه السلام) with His hand or making Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from among them; for these belong to otherworldly honor and spiritual ennoblement, which are outside the scope of this verse, as previously stated.

By this, the error in the statement of some—that the honoring is through all of these—becomes clear. The author of Rūḥ al-Ma‘ānī erred when he said after mentioning the various views: "All of them are in reality by way of example. Whoever claims it is restricted to one thing, like Ibn ‘Atiyyah who said: 'The honoring is through the intellect and nothing else,' has made a false claim, intended an excess, and contradicted explicit reason and sound transmission." End quote. The reason for his error is manifest from what has preceded.

His saying: وَحَمَلْناهُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ — "and carried them over land and sea" means: We carried them upon ships, beasts, and other means that they ride to their destinations and to seek the grace and sustenance of their Lord. This is one of the manifestations of their honoring.

His saying: وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ — "and provided them with all the good things" means: from the things they find good among the types of blessings—fruits, produce, and all else they enjoy and find delicious that can be termed sustenance. This is also one of the manifestations of honoring. Thus, the likeness of man in this divine honoring is like one who is invited to a banquet—which is an honor—then a mount is sent for him to ride to attend it—which is an honor—then various types of good and delicious foods and dishes are presented to him—which is an honor.

By this, it becomes clear that the conjunction of His saying "and We carried them..." and "and We provided them..." to the honoring is of the type of conjoining specific instances that follow from the general title abstracted from them.

His saying: وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلى كَثِيرٍ مِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا تَفْضِيلاً — "and preferred them with a complete preference over many of those We have created." It is not unlikely that what is meant by "those We have created" are the types of sentient animals and the jinn (الجن) — beings created from smokeless fire that are hidden from human sight — whom the Quran establishes. For Allah, the Glorified, considers the types of animals to be earthly communities like the human community and treats them as possessors of intellect, as He said: وَما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا طائِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَناحَيْهِ إِلَّا أُمَمٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ ما فَرَّطْنا فِي الْكِتابِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ — "There is no animal on land, nor a bird that flies with its wings, but they are communities like yourselves. We have not omitted anything from the Book. Then they will be mustered toward their Lord" (6:38).

This is most appropriate to the meaning of the verse. You have already learned that the purpose is to explain what Allah has honored the Children of Adam with and how He preferred them over other cosmic beings, which—as far as we know—are animals and jinn. As for the angels (الملائكة), they are not among the cosmic beings that fall under the influence of the material system governing the world of matter.

Thus, the meaning is: We preferred the Children of Adam over many of those We created, namely animals and jinn. As for those other than the "many"—the angels—they are outside the scope of the discourse because they are luminous beings, not cosmic ones, nor are they included in the course of the cosmic system. The verse only speaks of man insofar as he is one of the cosmic beings who has been favored with intrinsic and relative blessings.

From what has preceded, several points have become clear:

First: Both honoring (التكريم) and preference (التفضيل) in the verse refer to a type of divine gift bestowed upon man. As for his honoring, it is in that which is specific to his species—a gift that does not extend to others—namely the intellect (العقل) by which he distinguishes good from evil, beneficial from harmful, and beautiful from ugly. From this branch out other gifts, such as dominion over others and utilizing them for his purposes, speech, writing, and more.

As for his preference, it is in that which he exceeds others in matters shared between him and them. For instance, an animal feeds on what it finds of meat, fruit, grain, or grass in a simple manner. Man also feeds on these but exceeds the animal by what he innovates of various types of cooked and uncooked food in different ways, inventive arts, and innumerable delicious tastes that continue to increase in type and variety. The same applies to his drink, clothing, dwelling, marriage, and his domestic and civil society, and so on.

It is said in Majma‘ al-Bayan: "If it is asked: 'If the meaning of honoring and preference is the same, what is the meaning of the repetition?' The answer is that His saying 'We honored' (كَرَّمْنا) informs of the blessing but does not inform of the preference (التفضل) — bestowal without prior right — so the word 'preference' (التفضيل) was brought to indicate it. It is also said that honoring pertains to the blessings of this world, while preference pertains to the blessings of the Hereafter. And it is said that honoring is through the blessings by which religious obligation (التكليف) becomes valid, while preference is through the obligation itself by which He has exposed them to high stations." End quote.

As for his statement that preference indicates an additional point beyond the meaning of honoring—namely that it is a favor and a bestowal not out of entitlement—this is rejected. Preference, just as it can be without entitlement from the one preferred, can also be due to entitlement. As for what he narrated from others, it is a claim without proof.

Al-Razi said in his Tafsir regarding the difference between them: "The closest view in this is to say that He, the Exalted, preferred man over other animals by creative, natural, and essential matters such as intellect, speech, writing, a beautiful form, and an extended stature. Then He, the Mighty and Majestic, exposed him—by means of intellect and understanding—to the acquisition of true beliefs and virtuous character. The first is honoring and the second is preference. It is as if it were said: 'We preferred them by exposing them to the acquisition of that which contains salvation and proximity by means of the principles of that which We honored them with.' Thus, they must be grateful and direct what was created for them toward that for which it was created; so they should unify Allah, not associate anything with Him, and abandon the worship of others that they are upon." End quote.

Its summary is the distinction between honoring and preference by saying that the first is in essential matters or the instincts that follow them, while the second is in acquired matters. You are well aware that although man possesses both essential divine gifts and existential perfections as well as acquired ones as he mentioned, the restriction of honoring to the first type and preference to the second is supported neither by language nor by convention. Thus, the correct view is what we have previously presented.

Second: The verse considers human perfection from the perspective of his cosmic existence, his honoring, and his preference in comparison to other cosmic beings that fall under the cosmic system. The angels, who are outside the cosmic system, are outside the scope of the discourse. The meaning of preferring man over "many of those We created" is his preference over those other than the angels among cosmic beings. As for the angels, their existence is not of this kind; thus, they are not addressed in this regard at all.

By this, the invalidity of the argument used by some—that the verse proves angels are better than man, even the prophets (عليه السلام)—becomes clear. He said: "Because His saying, the Exalted: 'and We preferred them over many of those We created' indicates that there are those whom He did not prefer them over, and these can only be the angels, because the Children of Adam are better than every animal except the angels by consensus."

The reason for its invalidity is that what the verse addresses is only preference from the perspective of cosmic, worldly existence, and the angels do not exist in this mode of existence. To this returns the answer given by some: that preference in the verse does not refer to reward (الثواب), because reward cannot be a matter of preference initially; rather, what is meant is that by which Allah preferred them of the various types of blessings they were given in this world.

As for their answer that "many" (كثير) in the verse means "all," and that "from" (من) is explanatory (بيانية)—the meaning being: "We preferred them over those We created, and they are many"—this is a weak view supported neither by their language nor by the context of the verse. As for the claim that it is like the expression "I bestowed upon him the broad (العريض) of my status" or "I permitted him the inaccessible (المنيع) of my sanctuary," where it is not meant that I bestowed the broad part of my status and withheld what is not broad, or permitted the inaccessible part of my sanctuary and did not permit what is not inaccessible—rather, it means I bestowed my status which is characterized as broad and permitted my sanctuary which is inaccessible—this is refuted. If what is meant by the explanation of the two examples is that the attention in the speech is directed toward taking all status as broad and all sanctuary as inaccessible, then status and sanctuary would not be divided into broad and non-broad, or inaccessible and non-inaccessible, and it would not apply to the case of the verse where it is claimed that a part was mentioned while the whole was intended. If it is meant that the sense is "I bestowed upon him the broad of my status, so how much more so that which is not broad," then the inclusion of the whole in the ruling of the part would be by way of priority (الأولوية), which is certainly not permissible in the case of the verse.

It is sometimes answered that even if we concede that "many" refers to those other than the angels and the word "from" (من) is partitive, the most this implies is that the verse is silent regarding the preference of the Children of Adam over the angels. This is more general than the preference of the angel over man, as equality is possible. Even if it were conceded that it indicates preference, the most it would indicate is the preference of the genus of angels over the genus of the Children of Adam, which does not contradict some of the Children of Adam being better than the angels, such as the prophets (عليه السلام).

The truth, as you have learned, is that the verse does not address preference from the perspective of reward and otherworldly virtue. In other words, it addresses preference from the perspective of cosmic existence. "Many of those We created" refers to those other than the angels, "from" (من) is partitive, and "those We created" refers to the angels and others among humans, animals, and jinn. Man is preferred according to his cosmic existence over animals and jinn. This is the case, and a discussion on the meaning of man's preference over the angel will follow, Allah willing.

A Discussion on the Virtue between Man and the Angel

The Muslims have differed as to whether man or the angel is better. The well-known view attributed to the Ash‘arites is that man is better, meaning the believers among them; for no two people disagree that among men are those who are more astray than cattle—namely the deniers among them—so how could they be preferred over the near-stationed angels? This has been argued using the noble verse: وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلى كَثِيرٍ مِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا تَفْضِيلاً — "and preferred them with a complete preference over many of those We have created," taking "many" to mean "all," as we alluded to in the commentary on the verse, and by what has been narrated that the believer is more honorable to Allah than the angels.

This is also the well-known position of the Shi‘a. They sometimes argue that the angel is naturally disposed toward obedience without the possibility of disobedience, whereas man, by virtue of his choice, is equally disposed toward obedience and disobedience. He is composed of divine and satanic powers, and fashioned from intellect, lust, and anger. Thus, the obedient believing man obeys while not being prevented from disobedience, unlike the angel; therefore, he is better than the angel.

Nevertheless, the view of man's superiority in the sense mentioned is not unanimous among them. Some Ash‘arites, such as al-Zajjaj, held the superiority of the angel absolutely, and this is attributed to Ibn ‘Abbas.

Others held the superiority of the messengers among humans absolutely, then the messengers among the angels over all other humans and angels, then the general angels over the general humans.

Others held the superiority of the Cherubim (الكروبيين) among the angels absolutely, then the messengers among humans, then the perfect among them, then the generality of angels over the generality of humans, as Imam al-Razi says and is attributed to al-Ghazali.

The Mu‘tazilites went toward the superiority of the angels over humans, arguing from the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنا بَنِي آدَمَ — "Certainly We have honored the Children of Adam" to His saying: وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلى كَثِيرٍ مِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا تَفْضِيلاً — "and preferred them with a complete preference over many of those We have created." The presentation of their argument has already passed in the commentary on the verse.

Al-Zamakhshari went to extremes in reviling those who hold the superiority of man over the angel among those who interpreted "many" in the verse as "all." He said in al-Kashshaf, under His saying, the Exalted: وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلى كَثِيرٍ مِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا — "and preferred them over many of those We have created": "It refers to those other than the angels. It is enough of a preference for the Children of Adam that the angels are raised above them, given who they are and their station with Allah.

It is a wonder how the Compulsionists (المجبرة) reversed everything and were so stubborn that the habit of stubbornness emboldened them to the enormity of preferring man over the angel. This, after they heard Allah's glorification of their matter, His frequent mention of them with veneration, and after they knew where He settled them, how He brought them near, and how He placed them in relation to His prophets as His prophets are in relation to their communities.

Then their excessive prejudice against them led them to fabricate sayings and reports, including: 'The angels said: "Our Lord, You gave the Children of Adam the world to eat from and enjoy, and You did not give us that; so give it to us in the Hereafter." He said: "By My might and majesty, I shall not make the progeny of one I created with My hands like one to whom I said: 'Be,' and he was."' And they narrated from Abu Hurayrah that he said: 'The believer is more honorable to Allah than the angels who are with Him.'

Among their transgressions is that they interpreted 'many' as 'all' in this verse. They were forsaken until they were stripped of taste, so they did not feel the ugliness of their saying: 'and We preferred them over all of those We created.' Indeed, the meaning of their saying 'over all of those We created' is more distressing to their throats and more irritating to their eyes, but they do not perceive. Look at their artifice and their clinging to far-fetched interpretations in enmity toward the Supreme Assembly (الملأ الأعلى). It is as if Gabriel (عليه السلام) enraged them when he destroyed the cities of the people of Lot, so that malice does not leave their hearts." End quote.

What he alluded to regarding the narration of the angels' request that the Hereafter be made for them as the world was made for the Children of Adam was narrated from Ibn ‘Umar, Anas bin Malik, Zayd bin Aslam, and Jabir bin ‘Abdullah al-Ansari from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The wording of the last one is: "When Allah created Adam and his progeny, the angels said: 'O Lord, You created them to eat, drink, marry, and ride horses; so make the world for them and the Hereafter for us.' Allah, the Exalted, said: 'I shall not make one I created with My hands like one to whom I said: "Be," and he was.'"

The text of the narration is not without issues. Eating, drinking, marriage, and the like in man are material completions; man only finds pleasure in them because he seeks the survival of his person or species through the material structure Allah equipped him with. The angels, in their original existence, possess the perfection of that which man seeks through his material powers and his tedious, exhausting labors. They are transcendent above following the material system current in the universe. Thus, it is impossible for them to ask for that; they are not deprived such that they would be eager for it, hope for it, or wish for it.

A similar point applies to their previous argument for the superiority of man over the angel—that man's existence is composed of powers calling to obedience and powers calling to disobedience. If he chooses obedience over disobedience and inclines toward submission and servitude, his obedience is better than the obedience of the angels who are naturally disposed toward obedience and fashioned to avoid disobedience; thus, he is closer in proximity and greater in reward.

This is built upon a rational principle recognized in human society: that obedience—which is the compliance with a master's command and prohibition, and which possesses virtue and honor over disobedience, and by which one deserves reward if any is deserved—only entails its effect if the person addressed is in a position where action and omission are permissible for him, being equally disposed toward both sides. The closer he is to disobedience than to obedience, the stronger the effect, and vice versa. Thus, in complying with the prohibition of adultery, for example, the impotent, the decrepit old man, and the one for whom its preliminaries are difficult are not equal to the strong youth for whom most obstacles have been removed, and who has no barrier against it except the godwariness (تقوى) of Allah. Some of these omissions are not considered obedience, some are obedience, and some are the best of obedience according to this scale.

Since the angels have no way to disobey due to their lack of the instincts of lust and anger and their transcendence above the soul's desire, their compliance with divine commands is more like the compliance of the impotent and the decrepit old man with the prohibition of adultery. Thus, the virtue belongs to man in his obedience over them.

The flaw in this is that if it were true, the obedience of the angels would have no virtue at all, since they have no way to disobey nor do they possess a station of equal disposition. They would have no intrinsic honor or essential value, for there would be no honor here except through obedience that is contrasted with disobedience. Calling the essential compliance that never deviates from the essence "obedience" would be metaphorical. If it were so, there would be no cause for their proximity to their Lord, nor any station for their deeds.

However, Allah, the Glorified, has placed them in the station of proximity, settled them in the enclosures of holiness and the stations of intimacy, and made them the treasurers of His secret, the carriers of His command, and intermediaries between Him and His creation. Is all of this due to an arbitrary will from Him, without any suitability on their part or entitlement from their essences?

Allah has praised them with the most abundant praise, saying of them: بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ — "Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command" (21:26-27). And He said: لا يَعْصُونَ اللهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ — "they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them, and they carry out what they are commanded" (66:6). Thus, He described their essences as honored without any restriction and praised their obedience and their refraining from disobedience.

He said, praising their worship and their humility before their Lord: وَهُمْ مِنْ خَشْيَتِهِ مُشْفِقُونَ — "and they are apprehensive for fear of Him" (21:28). And He said: فَإِنِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَالَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَهُمْ لا يَسْأَمُونَ — "But if they are disdainful, those who are with your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they do not tire" (41:38). And He said: وَاذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ فِي نَفْسِكَ — "And remember your Lord within your soul..." until He said: وَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْغافِلِينَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عن عِبادَتِهِ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُ وَلَهُ يَسْجُدُونَ — "...and do not be among the heedless. Indeed those who are with your Lord are not too proud to worship Him; they glorify Him and prostrate before Him" (7:205-206). Thus, He commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to remember Him as they remember Him and to worship Him as they worship Him.

The truth of the matter is that the fact that an action is permissible to perform or omit, and that man stands in a position of equal disposition, is not in itself the criterion for the superiority of his obedience. Rather, it is because of what this reveals of the purity of his nature and the goodness of his inner self. The proof for this is that there is no value to obedience when the malice of the obedient one's soul and the ugliness of his inner self are known, even if he reaches the utmost in purifying the action and exerting effort in it—such as the obedience of the hypocrite and the one with a diseased heart whose work is nullified before Allah and whose good deed is erased from the register of deeds. Thus, the purity of the obedient one's soul, the beauty of his essence, and his sincerity in his servitude—which is revealed by his turning from disobedience to obedience and his enduring hardships in that—is what necessitates the preciousness of his work and the virtue of his obedience.

On this basis, the essences of the angels—which have no foundation except purity and honor, and whose actions are governed only by the humility of servitude and sincerity of intention—are better than the essence of man, which is clouded by desire and mixed with anger and lust, and his actions, which are rarely free from the hidden aspects of polytheism (الشرك), the mark of the soul, and the intrusion of nature.

Thus, the angelic constitution is better than the human constitution, and angelic deeds performed purely for the sake of Allah are better than the deeds of man, which contain the color of his constitution and a mixture of his essence. The perfection that man seeks for his essence through his obedience—which is reward—was given to the angel in his original existence, as previously alluded to.

Yes, since man only attains essential perfection gradually through what his essence acquires of readiness, quickly or slowly, it is possible that he might attain, through his readiness, a station of proximity and a realm of perfection above what the angel attained through the splendor of his essence in his original existence. The apparent meaning of His speech, the Exalted, confirms this possibility.

How could it be otherwise, when He, the Glorified, mentions in the story of making man a vicegerent (خليفة) on earth the virtue of man and his capacity for what the angels could not bear—the knowledge of all the names—and that it is a station of perfection that their glorifying His praise and their sanctifying Him cannot reach? He purifies him from the corruption on earth and the shedding of blood that will manifest from him, as He said: وَإِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً قالُوا أَتَجْعَلُ فِيها مَنْ يُفْسِدُ فِيها وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّماءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ قالَ إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ — "When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’ He said, ‘Indeed I know you do not know’" (2:30) to the end of the verses. We have detailed the discussion on this under the verses in the first volume of the book.

Then He, the Glorified, mentioned the command to the angels to prostrate to Adam, and then their prostrating to him all together. He said: فَسَجَدَ الْمَلائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ — "Thereat the angels prostrated, all of them together" (15:30). We clarified in the commentary on the verses of the story in Surah al-A‘raf that the prostration was only a submission from them to the station of human perfection, and Adam (عليه السلام) was only a qiblah (قبلة) — a direction of focus — for them, representing humanity before the angels. This is what the apparent meaning of His speech, the Exalted, provides, and there are reports that support it. The inquiry also has a rational dimension that is referred to in its proper places.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ — "The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam" (17:71). The "day" is the Day of Resurrection, and the adverbial phrase is connected to something omitted, meaning: "Remember such a day." The imam (الإمام) is the one who is followed. Allah, the Glorified, has called by this name individuals from among humans who guide people by Allah's command, as in His saying: قالَ إِنِّي جاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِماماً — "He said, ‘I am making you an imam of mankind’" (2:124), and His saying: وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا — "We made them imams, guiding by Our command" (21:73). He also called other individuals who are followed in misguidance by this name, as in His saying: فَقاتِلُوا أَئِمَّةَ الْكُفْرِ — "so fight the leaders of unfaith" (9:12). He also called the Torah by this name, as in His saying: وَمِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتابُ مُوسى إِماماً وَرَحْمَةً — "and before it was the Book of Moses, a guide and a mercy" (11:17). It is sometimes inferred from this that the heavenly books containing the Law (الشريعة), such as the books of Noah, Abraham, Jesus, and Muhammad (عليه السلام), are all imams.

He also called the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) by this name, as is apparent from His saying, the Exalted: وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ — "and We have figured everything in a manifest Book" (36:12). Since the apparent meaning of the verse is that every group of people has an imam other than that of others—as inferred from the addition of "imam" to the pronoun referring to "every group of people"—it is not appropriate for the "imam" in the verse to mean the Tablet, as it is one and not specific to some people over others.

Also, the apparent meaning of the verse is that this summons encompasses all people from the first to the last. It has already been mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ — "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book..." (2:213) that the first of the heavenly books containing the Law is the Book of Noah (عليه السلام), and there was no book before it in this regard. By this, the inappropriateness of the "imam" in the verse being the Book becomes clear; otherwise, those before Noah would be excluded from the scope of the summons in the verse.

Thus, it is determined that what is meant by the "imam" of every group of people is the one they follow in the paths of truth and falsehood, as previously mentioned that the Quran calls both of them "imams." Or it refers specifically to the Imam of Truth, who is the one Allah, the Glorified, chooses in every age to guide its people by His command, whether he be a prophet like Abraham and Muhammad (عليه السلام) or other than a prophet. The detailed discussion on this has already passed in the commentary on His saying: وَإِذِ ابْتَلى إِبْراهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِماتٍ فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ قالَ إِنِّي جاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِماماً قالَ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي قالَ لا يَنالُ عَهْدِي الظَّالِمِينَ — "And when his Lord tested Abraham with certain words, and he fulfilled them, He said, ‘I am making you an imam of mankind.’ He said, ‘And from my offspring?’ He said, ‘My pledge does not extend to the wrongdoers’" (2:124).

However, what is inferred from verses such as His saying regarding Pharaoh—who is among the imams of misguidance: يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ — "On the Day of Resurrection he will lead his people and conduct them into the Fire" (11:98), and His saying: لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلى بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ — "so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and pile up the bad on one another, and heap them all together, and cast them into hell" (8:37), and other verses—which are many—is that the people of misguidance do not part from their followed leaders on the Day of Resurrection. This necessitates that they accompany them in the summons and the presentation.

Furthermore, His saying: بِإِمامِهِمْ — "with their imam" is absolute, not restricted to the Imam of Truth whom Allah made an imam guiding by His command. He has called the one followed in misguidance an "imam" just as He called the one followed in guidance an "imam." The context of the end of the verse and the second verse also suggests that the imam by whom they are summoned is the one whom the people took as an imam and followed in the world, not necessarily the one Allah chose for the Imamate and appointed for guidance by His command, whether the people followed him or rejected him.

Thus, it appears that what is meant by the "imam" of every group of people in the verse is the one they followed, whether he was an imam of truth or an imam of falsehood. It is not as some think—that they are called by the names of their imams, so it is said: "O community of Abraham," "O community of Muhammad," "O family of Pharaoh," or "O family of so-and-so." This does not suit the branching detail in the verse, namely His saying: فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ — "As for those who are given their record in their right hand..." and وَمَنْ كانَ فِي هذِهِ أَعْمى — "But whoever is blind in this [world]..." etc. For there is no branching relationship between the summons by the imam in this sense and the giving of the record in the right hand or blindness.

Rather, what is meant by the summons—as suggested by the context of the end—is the presentation (الإحضار). They are presented with their imam; then those who followed an imam of truth take their record in their right hand, and the blindness of those who were blind to the recognition of the Imam of Truth in the world and following him becomes manifest. This is what reflection on the verse provides.

The commentators have various and differing views in interpreting the "imam" in the verse:

Among them: that the "imam" refers to the Book that is followed, such as the Torah, the Gospel, and the Quran. Thus, on the Day of Resurrection, it will be called: "O people of the Torah," "O people of the Gospel," and "O people of the Quran." Its explanation and the refutation of it have already passed.

Among them: that the "imam" refers to the Prophet for those who were on the truth, and Satan and the imam of misguidance for those who sought falsehood. Thus, it will be said: "Bring the followers of Abraham," "Bring the followers of Moses," "Bring the followers of Muhammad." Then the people of truth who followed them will stand and be given their records of deeds in their right hands. Then it will be said: "Bring the followers of Satan," "Bring the followers of the leaders of misguidance."

The flaw in this is that it is built upon taking "imam" in the verse in its conventional sense—the one followed among rational beings. There is no way to this given the existence of a specific meaning for it in the convention of the Quran, which is the one who guides by Allah's command and the one followed in misguidance.

Among them: that the "imam" refers to their record of deeds (كتاب أعمالهم). Thus, it will be said: "O companions of the record of good," and "O companions of the record of evil." The reason for its being called an "imam" is that they follow what it decrees of Paradise or Fire.

The flaw in this is that there is no meaning in calling the record of deeds an "imam" when it follows the action of man, whether good or evil. It is more appropriate to call it a follower (تابع) than to call it followed (متبوع). As for the final justification, the one followed in the decree is what Allah, the Glorified, judges after the spreading of the scrolls, the questioning, the weighing, and the witnessing. As for the Book, it only contains the texts of the deeds of good and evil without the final judgment.

From this, it becomes clear that the "imam" is neither the Preserved Tablet nor the record of the community's deeds—which is referred to in His saying: كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى إِلى كِتابِهَا — "Every nation will be summoned to its book" (45:28)—due to its lack of suitability with His saying at the end: فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ — "As for those who are given their record in their right hand," which is apparent in the individual rather than the group.

Among them: that it refers to mothers—by making imām (إمام) the plural of umm (أم). Thus, it will be said: "O son of so-and-so (female)," and it will not be said: "O son of so-and-so (male)." They have narrated a narration regarding this.

The flaw in this is that it does not suit the wording of the verse. It said: نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ — "We shall summon every group of people with their imam," and it did not say: "We summon the people with their imam" or "We summon every person with his mother." If it were as claimed, one of the latter two expressions would have been determined. As for the narration alluded to, assuming its authenticity and acceptance, it is an independent narration not concerning the interpretation of the verse.

Furthermore, the plural of umm as imām is a rare linguistic usage that the speech of Allah, the Exalted, is not carried upon. Al-Kashshaf counted this view among the innovations of interpretation.

Among them: that it refers to the one followed and the one pursued, whether rational or otherwise, whether truth or falsehood—such as the Prophet, the wali (الولي) — the divinely appointed guardian and leader —, Satan, the leaders of misguidance and religions of truth and falsehood, the heavenly books and books of misguidance, and good and evil traditions. Perhaps the summons of every group of people with their imam in this way is a metaphor for the accompaniment of every follower on the Day of Resurrection with the one he followed. The preposition "with" (بـ) denotes accompaniment.

The flaw in this is what we raised against the view that it refers to the prophets and leaders of misguidance. Carrying it upon the linguistic meaning is only appropriate when the Quran does not have a specific convention for it. You have already learned that the "imam" in the convention of the Quran is the one who guides by Allah's command or the one followed in misguidance. It is possible that the "with" (بـ) in "with their imam" (بِإِمامِهِمْ) denotes the instrument; so understand that.

Furthermore, the guidance of the Book, the tradition, the religion, and so on, in reality, returns to the guidance of the Imam. Likewise, the Prophet only guides insofar as he is an Imam guiding by Allah's command. As for his informing of the knowledge of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — or his delivering what he was sent with, he is a Prophet or a Messenger and not an Imam. Similarly, the misguidance of false schools, books of misguidance, and innovated traditions is, in reality, the misguidance of their founders and innovators.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَأُولئِكَ يَقْرَؤُنَ كِتابَهُمْ وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلاً — "As for those who are given their record in their right hand, they will read their record, and they will not be wronged so much as a thread" (17:71). The fatīl (الفتيل) is the twisted thread in the cleft of a date-stone. It is also said that the fatīl is what is in the belly of the stone, the naqīr (النقير) is on its back, and the qaṭmīr (القطمير) is the skin of the stone.

The branching of the detail upon their summons with their imam is proof that their following is what necessitates their division into two parts and their separation into two groups: those given their record in their right hand, and those who were blind and further astray from the path. Thus, the imam is of two kinds: an imam of guidance and an imam of misguidance. This is what we previously presented—that the branching of the detail testifies to the "imam" being more general than the Imam of Guidance.

It is also testified to by the replacement of the giving of the record in the left hand or from behind the back—as occurs in other places—with His saying: وَمَنْ كانَ فِي هذِهِ أَعْمى فَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ أَعْمى — "But whoever is blind in this [world] will be blind in the Hereafter..." (17:72).

The meaning—with the aid of the context—is: they will then separate into two groups. Those who were given the record of their deeds in their right hands—those will read their record joyfully, with good tidings, and happy with their felicity. They will not be wronged even the amount of a thread; rather, they will be paid their rewards in full and complete.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ كانَ فِي هذِهِ أَعْمى فَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ أَعْمى وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلاً — "But whoever is blind in this [world] will be blind in the Hereafter, and even more astray from the [right] path" (17:72). The contrast between His sayings "in this" (فِي هذِهِ) and "in the Hereafter" (فِي الْآخِرَةِ) is proof that the indication "in this" refers to the world. Just as the verse is framed to explain the correspondence between the life of this world and the Hereafter, it is proof that what is meant by the blindness of the Hereafter is the blindness of insight, just as what is meant by the blindness of the world is that. He, the Exalted, said: فَإِنَّها لا تَعْمَى الْأَبْصارُ وَلكِنْ تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ — "For indeed it is not the eyes that turn blind, but the hearts turn blind that are in the breasts" (22:46). This is also supported by following the blindness of the Hereafter with His saying: "and even more astray from the path."

The meaning is: whoever was in this life of the world not recognizing the Imam of Truth and not traveling the path of truth, he will be in the life of the Hereafter not finding felicity and success, nor guided to forgiveness and mercy.

From what has preceded, the flaw in the statement of some becomes clear: that the indication in His saying "in this" refers to the mentioned blessings, and the meaning is: whoever was blind in these blessings that he was provided, not recognizing them and not thanking Allah for what He bestowed, he will be blind in the Hereafter.

Likewise, what some mentioned—that the blindness of the world refers to the blindness of insight and the blindness of the Hereafter refers to the blindness of sight—has already had its reason for invalidity mentioned. Furthermore, the blindness of sight in the Hereafter sometimes returns to the blindness of insight because of His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ — "the day when the secrets are examined" (86:9).

The apparent view of some commentators is that the second "blind" (أَعْمى) in the verse conveys the meaning of superlative preference, as he interpreted it to mean that in the Hereafter he is more blind and further astray from the path. The context supports him in that.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Amālī of the Shaykh, with its chain of transmission from Zayd bin ‘Ali from his father (عليه السلام): regarding His saying: وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنا بَنِي آدَمَ — "Certainly We have honored the Children of Adam," he says: "We preferred the Children of Adam over the rest of creation." وَحَمَلْناهُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ — "and carried them over land and sea," he says: "upon the wet and the dry." وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ — "and provided them with all the good things," he says: "from the good things of all fruits." وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ — "and preferred them," he says: "There is no beast nor bird—except that it eats and drinks with its mouth—it does not raise its hand to its mouth with food or drink, except for the son of Adam—for he raises his food to his mouth with his hand. This is from the preference."

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Jabir from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام): وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلى كَثِيرٍ مِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا تَفْضِيلاً — "and preferred them with a complete preference over many of those We have created." He said: "Everything was created prone, except for man, who was created upright."

I say: What is in the two narrations is by way of mentioning some of the specific instances. The proof for this is his saying at the end of the first narration: "This is from the preference."

In the same source, narrated from al-Fudayl, who said: I asked Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ "The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam" (17:71). He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) will come among his people, ‘Ali (peace be upon him) among his people, al-Hasan among his people, and al-Husayn among his people—and everyone who died while living under the authority of an Imam (بين ظهراني إمام) shall come with him."

In Tafsir al-Burhan, narrated from Ibn Shahrashub from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Do you not praise Allah? When the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) comes, every people will be summoned to those whom they took as guardians (يتولونه); we shall seek refuge in the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and you shall seek refuge in us."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Majma‘ from him (peace be upon him). In this, there is an indication that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآle; وسلم) is the Imam of the Imams, just as He is the Witness over the witnesses, and that the rule of being summoned by the Imam applies among the Imams themselves.

In Majma‘ al-Bayan, both the elite (الخاص) and the general (العام) have narrated from ‘Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (peace be upon him) through authentic chains of transmission (الأسانيد الصحيحة) that he narrated from his forefathers from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said regarding this verse: "Every group of people will be summoned by the Imam of their time (إمام زمانهم), the Book of their Lord, and the Sunnah (السنة) — the established practice and tradition — of their Prophet."

I say: It was also narrated in Tafsir al-Burhan from Ibn Shahrashub from him, from his forefathers, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with the same wording, and he also attributed it to the narrations of both the elite and the general.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from ‘Ali, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said regarding: يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ "The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam" (17:71): "Every people will be summoned by the Imam of their time, the Book of their Lord, and the Sunnah of their Prophet."

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from ‘Ammar al-Sabati from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The earth is never left without an Imam who permits what Allah has made lawful (حلال) and forbids what He has made unlawful (حرام); and that is the saying of Allah: يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ 'The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam' (17:71)." Then he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Whoever dies without an Imam dies the death of the Age of Ignorance (جاهلية)." — the period of spiritual and moral darkness preceding the advent of Islam. (The narration continues).

I say: The basis for arguing from this verse is the universality of the summons mentioned therein for all people.

In the same source, narrated from Isma‘il ibn Hammam from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ "The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam" (17:71). He said: "When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah will say: 'Is it not justice from your Lord that every people should follow those whom they took as guardians?' They will say: 'Yes.' He will then say: 'Distinguish yourselves,' and they shall be distinguished."

I say: In this, there is support for what we previously established: that what is meant by the "summons by the Imam" is their being brought forth in his presence, rather than merely being called by name. The narrations regarding the aforementioned meanings are numerous.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلاً "and they shall not be wronged [so much as] a thread of a date-stone" (17:71). He said: "It is the thin skin on the back of the date-stone."

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from al-Muthanna from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him). He said: Abu Basir asked him—while I was listening—saying: "A man has a hundred thousand [dirhams] and says: 'I will perform the Hajj (الحج) — the prescribed pilgrimage to the House of Allah — this year, I will perform the Hajj this year,' until death comes to him, yet he was prevented by some affliction and did not perform the Hajj of Islam." He (peace be upon him) replied: "O Abu Basir, have you not heard the saying of Allah: وَمَنْ كانَ فِي هذِهِ أَعْمى فَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ أَعْمى وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلاً 'But whoever is blind in this [world] will be blind in the Hereafter, and even more astray from the [right] way' (17:72)? He was blind to one of the obligatory duties (فرائض) of Allah."

Ayahs

وَإِن كَادُوا۟ لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ لِتَفْتَرِىَ عَلَيْنَا غَيْرَهُۥ ۖ وَإِذًا لَّٱتَّخَذُوكَ خَلِيلًا (٧٣)
They were about to beguile you from what Allah has revealed to you so that you may fabricate something other than that against Us, whereat they would have befriended you. (73)
وَلَوْلَآ أَن ثَبَّتْنَـٰكَ لَقَدْ كِدتَّ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْـًٔا قَلِيلًا (٧٤)
And had We not fortified you, certainly you might have inclined toward them a bit. (74)
إِذًا لَّأَذَقْنَـٰكَ ضِعْفَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ وَضِعْفَ ٱلْمَمَاتِ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنَا نَصِيرًا (٧٥)
Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death, and then you would have not found for yourself any helper against Us. (75)
وَإِن كَادُوا۟ لَيَسْتَفِزُّونَكَ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُخْرِجُوكَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَإِذًا لَّا يَلْبَثُونَ خِلَـٰفَكَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٧٦)
They were about to hound you out of the land, to expel you from it, but then they would not have stayed after you but a little. (76)
سُنَّةَ مَن قَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِن رُّسُلِنَا ۖ وَلَا تَجِدُ لِسُنَّتِنَا تَحْوِيلًا (٧٧)
A precedent concerning those We have sent before you from among Our apostles, and you will not find any change in Our precedent. (77)
أَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِدُلُوكِ ٱلشَّمْسِ إِلَىٰ غَسَقِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَقُرْءَانَ ٱلْفَجْرِ ۖ إِنَّ قُرْءَانَ ٱلْفَجْرِ كَانَ مَشْهُودًا (٧٨)
Maintain the prayer [during the period] from the sun’s decline till the darkness of the night, and [observe particularly] the dawn recital. Indeed the dawn recital is attended [by angels]. (78)
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَتَهَجَّدْ بِهِۦ نَافِلَةً لَّكَ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقَامًا مَّحْمُودًا (٧٩)
And keep vigil for a part of the night, as a supererogatory [devotion] for you. It may be that your Lord will raise you to a praiseworthy station. (79)
وَقُل رَّبِّ أَدْخِلْنِى مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ وَأَخْرِجْنِى مُخْرَجَ صِدْقٍ وَٱجْعَل لِّى مِن لَّدُنكَ سُلْطَـٰنًا نَّصِيرًا (٨٠)
And say, ‘My Lord! ‘Admit me with a worthy entrance, and bring me out with a worthy departure, and render me a favourable authority from Yourself.’ (80)
وَقُلْ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ كَانَ زَهُوقًا (٨١)
And say, ‘The truth has come, and falsehood has vanished. Indeed falsehood is bound to vanish.’ (81)

(Commentary)

The verses mention some of the plots of the polytheists (المشركين) against the Qur’an and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — after having censured them for their persistence in denying monotheism (التوحيد) and the Resurrection (المعاد) and establishing proofs against them therein — as they sought to tempt the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) away from some of what was revealed to him so that he might compromise with them to some extent, and they sought to expel him from Mecca.

Allah threatened the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with the severest of warnings should he incline toward leaning (الركون) to them even slightly, and He threatened them with destruction for having expelled the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

The verses also contain an injunction to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the prayers (الصلوات) and seeking refuge with his Lord in his entering and his exiting, and an announcement of the manifestation of the Truth (الحق).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ عَنِ الَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ لِتَفْتَرِيَ عَلَيْنا غَيْرَهُ وَإِذاً لَاتَّخَذُوكَ خَلِيلاً "Indeed they were about to beguile you from what We have revealed to you, so that you may forge against Us something else, and then they would have surely taken you for a friend." (17:73). The particle in (إن) is the lightened form (مخففة), as evidenced by the lam (اللام) in layaftinunaka (لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ). Temptation (الفتنة) means causing one to slip or turning one away. The friend (الخليل) is derived from khullah (الخلة) meaning friendship (الصداقة); it is sometimes said to be from khallah (الخلة) meaning need (الحاجة), but this is far-fetched.

The apparent context suggests that "what We have revealed to you" refers to the Qur’an, including its contents of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — the negation of partners, and righteous conduct. This supports what has been mentioned in some reports regarding the occasions of revelation (شأن النزول): that the polytheists asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to cease mentioning their gods disparagingly and to distance himself from his believing slaves and the lowly among them so that they might sit with him and listen to him; thus, these verses were revealed.

The meaning is: Indeed, the polytheists came close to making you slip and turning you away from what We revealed to you, so that you might adopt a conduct and action that contradicts it, which would then be a forgery (افتراء) against Us because of its attribution to Us through your action; and in that case, they would have taken you as a friend.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً "Had We not fortified you, you were certainly about to incline toward them a little." (17:74). Fortification (التثبيت) — as the context indicates — is the divine protection (العصمة الإلهية) — the state of being divinely preserved from error and sin. Making the response to "had not" (لو لا) His saying: "you were certainly about to incline" (لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ), rather than the act of inclining itself — and leaning (الركون) is inclination (الميل) or the slightest degree of inclination — is proof that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) neither inclined nor was he about to do so. This is reinforced by attributing the leaning "toward them" rather than to their requests.

The meaning is: Had We not fortified you with Our protection (عصمتنا), you would have come close to inclining toward them slightly; but We fortified you, so you did not even come close to the slightest inclination toward them, let alone granting what they asked. Thus, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not grant their requests, nor did he incline toward them even slightly, nor was he even about to incline.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَياةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَماتِ ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنا نَصِيراً "Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death, and then you would not have found for yourself any helper against Us." (17:75). The context of the verse is one of warning (توعد). What is meant by the "double of life and death" is the doubled punishment (عذاب) of life and death. The meaning is: Had you happened to incline toward them with some inclination, We would have made you taste the double of the punishment with which We punish the criminals in their lives, and the double of what We punish them with in their deaths — that is, the double of the punishment of this world and the Hereafter.

It is narrated in al-Majma‘ from Aban ibn Taghlib that "double" (الضعف) refers to a punishment whose pain is doubled. The meaning would be: We would have made you taste the punishment of this world and the punishment of the Hereafter. He cited the poet's words regarding the killing of Malik:

For the killing of Malik when he departed from me, I spend the night in a painful double (ضعف أليم).

Meaning: in a painful punishment.

The conclusion of the verse, ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنا نَصِيراً "and then you would not have found for yourself any helper against Us," is an intensification of the threat, meaning that the punishment would then be inevitable with no escape from it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَسْتَفِزُّونَكَ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ لِيُخْرِجُوكَ مِنْها وَإِذاً لا يَلْبَثُونَ خِلافَكَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً "Indeed they were about to unsettle you from the land to expel you from it, and then they would not have stayed after you but for a little while." (17:76). Provocation (الاستفزاز) means unsettling and moving someone with lightness and ease. The lam (اللام) in "the land" (الْأَرْضِ) is for specific reference (للعهد), referring to Mecca. "After" (خلاف) means following [your departure], and "little" (قليلاً) refers to a short period of time.

The meaning is: Indeed, the polytheists were close to unsettling you from the land of Mecca to expel you from it; and had that occurred and you had left it, they would not have remained there after you except for a little while, for they would surely have been destroyed.

It has been said that those who were about to unsettle him were the Jews, who wanted to expel him from Medina. It has also been said that it refers to both the polytheists and the Jews, who both wanted to expel him from the land of the Arabs.

This is rendered unlikely by the fact that the Surah is Meccan, the verses share a single context, and the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) trial with the Jews only occurred in Medina after the Migration (الهجرة).

His saying, the Exalted: سُنَّةَ مَنْ قَدْ أَرْسَلْنا قَبْلَكَ مِنْ رُسُلِنا وَلا تَجِدُ لِسُنَّتِنا تَحْوِيلاً "[This is] the precedent for those of Our apostles whom We have sent before you, and you will not find any change in Our precedent." (17:77). Alteration (التحويل) is the shifting of a thing from one state to another. His saying "precedent" (سُنَّةَ) means "according to the precedent of those We have sent," and it relates to His saying "they would not have stayed" (لا يَلْبَثُونَ); that is, they would not stay after you except for a little while, according to the precedent of those of Our messengers We sent before you.

This precedent (السنة) — which is the destruction of the polytheists who expel their messenger from their lands and drive him out from among them — is a precedent of Allah, the Glorified. He attributed it to His messengers only because it is a precedent established for their sake, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: وَلا تَجِدُ لِسُنَّتِنا تَحْوِيلاً "and you will not find any change in Our precedent." Allah has said: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِرُسُلِهِمْ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا فَأَوْحى إِلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ لَنُهْلِكَنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ "The faithless said to their apostles, ‘We will surely expel you from our land, or you shall revert to our creed.’ Then their Lord revealed to them: ‘We will surely destroy the wrongdoers.’" (14:13).

The meaning is: And then We would destroy them according to Our precedent which We established for the sake of those of Our messengers We sent before you and which We carried out; and you shall find no alteration or substitution in Our precedent.

His saying, the Exalted: أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِدُلُوكِ الشَّمْسِ إِلى غَسَقِ اللَّيْلِ وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً "Maintain the prayer from the sun’s decline till the darkness of the night, and [observe particularly] the dawn recital. Indeed the dawn recital is attended [by angels]." (17:78). It is stated in Majma‘ al-Bayan: Duluk (الدلوك) is the decline [of the sun from its zenith]. al-Mubarrad said: The duluk of the sun is from its decline until its setting. It is also said to be the setting itself. Its root is from dalk (rubbing); the decline was called duluk because the one looking at it rubs his eyes due to the intensity of its rays, and the setting was called duluk because the observer rubs his eyes to fix his gaze upon it. [End of quote].

He also said: The ghasaq (غسق) of the night is the appearance of its darkness. It is said: "the wound ghasaqat" when it bursts and what is inside it appears. [End of quote]. In al-Mufradat, it says: The ghasaq of the night is the intensity of its darkness. [End of quote].

The commentators have differed in interpreting the beginning of the verse. What is narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) through Shi‘i chains is the interpretation of the sun's duluk as its decline (زوال) and the ghasaq of the night as its midpoint. Reference to these narrations will come in the "Discussion on Traditions," God willing.

Accordingly, the verse encompasses the time between the sun's decline and the middle of the night. The daily obligatory prayers (الفرائض اليومية) occurring within this span of time are four: the Noon (الظهر), Afternoon (العصر), Sunset (المغرب), and Night (العشاء) prayers. By adding the Dawn prayer (صلاة الصبح), indicated by His saying "and the dawn recital" (وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ) etc., the five daily prayers are completed.

His saying "and the dawn recital" (وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ) is conjoined to "the prayer" (الصلاة); meaning: and maintain the dawn recital. What is meant by it is the Dawn prayer (صلاة الصبح) because of the recitation (القرائة) it contains. The narrations are in agreement that the Dawn prayer is what is meant by "the dawn recital."

Similarly, the narrations from the paths of both groups [Shi‘a and Sunni] agree on interpreting the conclusion of the verse, إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً "Indeed the dawn recital is attended," as being witnessed by the angels of the night and the angels of the day. We shall refer to some of these narrations shortly, God willing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَتَهَجَّدْ بِهِ نافِلَةً لَكَ عَسى أَنْ يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقاماً مَحْمُوداً "And keep vigil for a part of the night, as a supererogatory [devotion] for you. It may be that your Lord will raise you to a praiseworthy station." (17:79). Tahajjud (التهجد) is from hujud (الهجود), which originally means sleep. The meaning of tahajjud is wakefulness and staying up after sleep, as mentioned by several [scholars]. The pronoun in "with it" (بِهِ) refers to the Qur’an or to the "part" understood from His saying "and of the night" (وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ). Nafilah (النافلة) is from nafl (النفل), which means increase or excess. It is sometimes said that His saying "and of the night" is a form of incitement (إغراء), similar to saying: "Keep to the night." The fa (الفاء) in His saying "so keep vigil with it" (فَتَهَجَّدْ بِهِ) is like that in His saying: فَإِيَّايَ فَارْهَبُونِ "so fear Me alone" (16:51).

The meaning is: Stay awake for part of the night after your sleep with the Qur’an — which is the prayer — it being an additional prayer for you beyond the obligatory one.

His saying: عَسى أَنْ يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقاماً مَحْمُوداً "It may be that your Lord will raise you to a praiseworthy station." It is possible that "station" (المقام) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) meaning "raising" (البعث), thus being a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) for "will raise you" (يبعثك) from a different root. The meaning would be: It may be that your Lord will raise you with a praiseworthy raising. It is also possible that it is a noun of place (اسم مكان), with "raising" (البعث) meaning "establishing" or implying the meaning of "granting" and the like. The meaning would then be: It may be that your Lord will establish you in a praiseworthy station, or raise you while granting you a praiseworthy station.

The Glorified has described his station as being "praiseworthy" (محموداً) and left the statement absolute without restriction. This implies that it is a station praised by all, and all would only offer such praise if it were deemed good by all and beneficial to everyone. Therefore, they have interpreted the "praiseworthy station" (المقام المحمود) as the station for which all creation praises him, which is the station of the Great Intercession (الشفاعة الكبرى) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on the Day of Resurrection. The narrations from the paths of both groups, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام), are in agreement on this interpretation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقُلْ رَبِّ أَدْخِلْنِي مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ وَأَخْرِجْنِي مُخْرَجَ صِدْقٍ وَاجْعَلْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ سُلْطاناً نَصِيراً "And say, ‘My Lord! Admit me with a worthy admission, and bring me out with a worthy departure, and make for me from Your presence a helping authority.’" (17:80). Mudkhal (المدخل) — with a dammah on the mim and a fathah on the kha — is a mim-infinitive meaning "admitting" (الإدخال), and similarly mukhraj (المخرج) means "bringing out" (الإخراج). The significance of attributing admission and departure to "truth" (الصدق) is that the entering and exiting in every matter should be characterized by truthfulness, proceeding according to reality without the outward contradicting the inward or some parts opposing others — such as a person calling to Allah with his tongue while desiring in his heart to dominate people, or being sincere in part of his call to Allah while associating others with Him in another part.

In short, it is that truth (الصدق) be seen in every entry and exit of his, and that it encompass his entire existence, such that he says what he does and does what he says, and neither says nor does anything except what he sees and believes in. This is the station of the truthful (الصديقين).

The meaning returns to something like our saying: "O Allah, take charge of my affairs as You take charge of the affairs of the truthful."

His saying: وَاجْعَلْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ سُلْطاناً نَصِيراً "and make for me from Your presence a helping authority," means an authority that aids me in the matters I intend and the works I am engaged in, so that I am not overcome in my call by a false argument, nor tempted by a trial or a plot devised against me by Your enemies, nor led astray by the prompting and whispering of a devil.

The verse — as you see — is absolute, commanding the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to ask his Lord to take charge of his affairs in every entry and exit with truth, and to grant him an authority from Himself that aids him, so that he does not deviate from the truth nor prevail through falsehood. Thus, there is no basis for what some commentators have mentioned: that the entry and exit refer to entering Medina during the Migration and exiting it toward Mecca for the Conquest, or that they refer to entering the grave at death and exiting it at the Resurrection.

Indeed, since the verse follows His saying "Indeed they were about to beguile you" and "Indeed they were about to unsettle you," and is within their context, it alludes to the command for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to seek refuge with his Lord in every matter of the mission that concerns him or that he is engaged in, and in the entering and exiting of every place he inhabits, enters, or leaves; and this is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقُلْ جاءَ الْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ الْباطِلُ إِنَّ الْباطِلَ كانَ زَهُوقاً "And say, ‘The truth has come, and falsehood has vanished. Indeed falsehood is bound to vanish.’" (17:81). It is stated in al-Majma‘: al-Zahuq (الزهوق) is destruction and nullification. It is said: "his soul zahaqat" when it departs, as if it has departed toward destruction. [End of quote]. The meaning is clear.

In the verse is a command for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to announce the manifestation of the Truth. Because the verse occurs in the context of what preceded — from His saying "Indeed they were about to beguile you" to the end of the verses — it is a command to make the polytheists despair of [influencing] him and to alert them to be certain that they have no hope in him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

The verse also contains an indication that falsehood (الباطل) has no permanence, as He, the Exalted, said in another place: وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ "And the parable of a bad word is that of a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, it has no stability." (14:26).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding the occasion of revelation for His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ عَنِ الَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ (the verses): "They said to him: 'Cease reviling our gods and mocking our ideals — and drive away these slaves and lowly ones whose smell is that of body odor — so that we may sit with you and listen to you.' He hoped for their submission to Islam, so the verse was revealed."

I say: A similar report was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abi Hatim from Sa‘id ibn Nufayr. As for what was narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas: "That Umayyah ibn Khalaf, Abu Jahl ibn Hisham, and men from Quraysh came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'Come and touch our gods and we will enter into your religion with you.' It was difficult for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be separated from his people and he loved their submission to Islam, so he felt soft toward them. Then Allah revealed: وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ — until His saying — نَصِيراً." This does not align with the apparent meaning of the verses, as they negate even the proximity of leaning from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), let alone the leaning itself.

The same applies to what was narrated by al-Tabari and Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn ‘Abbas: "That the tribe of Thaqif said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'Grant us a year's respite so that gifts may be offered to our gods — and when we have collected what is offered to the gods, we will secure it — then we will submit to Islam and break the idols.' He intended to grant them the respite, so there was revealed: وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ (the verse)."

Likewise, what is in the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi from Abu Ya‘qub from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding the verse: "He said: On the day of the Conquest, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) removed idols from the Mosque — and among them was an idol on al-Marwah. Quraysh asked him to leave it and he felt shy — then he ordered it to be broken, so this verse was revealed."

There are other reports similar to these two in meaning. These are narrations that do not align with the apparent meaning of the Book. Far be it from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to intend such innovations (البدع) while Allah, the Glorified, negates from him even the proximity of leaning and slight inclination, let alone intending the action.

Furthermore, these incidents are among the events that occurred after the Migration, while the Surah is Meccan. In al-‘Uyun, with its chain to ‘Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه‌السلام), regarding what al-Ma’mun asked him: "He said to him: 'Tell me about the saying of Allah: عَفَا اللهُ عَنْكَ لِمَ أَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ (Allah forgive you! Why did you give them leave?).' al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) said: 'This is among what was revealed as "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor" — Allah addressed His Prophet with that but intended his community.
