Likewise is His saying: لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ (If you ascribe a partner to Allah, your charitable deeds will surely go for nothing, and you will surely be among the losers) and His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً.' He [al-Ma’mun] said: 'You have spoken the truth, O son of the Messenger of Allah.'"

In al-Majma‘, from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ (the verse): "He said: When this verse was revealed, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'O Allah, never entrust me to myself for even the blink of an eye.'"

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib from ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام): "I said to him: 'When was the prayer made obligatory upon the Muslims as they are today?' He said: 'In Medina, when the mission became manifest and Islam grew strong — then Allah prescribed Jihad for the Muslims, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) added seven units (ركعات) to the prayer: two units in the Noon prayer, two in the Afternoon, one in the Sunset, and two in the Night prayer. He left the Dawn prayer as it was prescribed in Mecca — to hasten the descent of the angels of the day to the earth — and to hasten the ascent of the angels of the night to the heaven — so the angels of the night and the angels of the day — would witness the Dawn with the Messenger of Allah. Therefore, Allah said: وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً; the Muslims witness it, and the angels of the night and the day witness it.'"

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِدُلُوكِ الشَّمْسِ إِلى غَسَقِ اللَّيْلِ وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ: "He said: In the verse is an explanation of the obligation of the five prayers and an explanation of their times. This is supported by what al-‘Ayyashi narrated with a chain from ‘Ubaydah ibn Zurarah from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding this verse."

He said: "Indeed, Allah made obligatory four prayers whose initial time is from the sun's decline until the middle of the night. Among them are two prayers whose initial time is from the sun's decline until its setting, except that this one is before that one; and among them are two prayers whose initial time is from the sun's setting until the middle of the night, except that this one is before that one."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ibn Mas‘ud: "He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Gabriel came to me at the duluk of the sun when it declined — and he prayed the Noon prayer with me.'"

In the same work, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in Nawadir al-Usul, Ibn Jarir, al-Tabari, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Abu al-Darda’: "He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited: إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً. He said: 'Allah witnesses it, as do the angels of the night and the angels of the day.'"

I say: The interpretation of the "dawn recital" being "attended" (مشهوداً) as the witnessing of the angels of the night and the angels of the day in the narrations of both groups almost reaches the level of being widely transmitted (تواتر). To this has been added in some of them the witnessing of Allah, as in this narration, and in some of them the witnessing of the Muslims, as in what preceded.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from ‘Ubayd ibn Zurarah: "He said: Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) was asked about the believer: 'Does he have [the right of] intercession (شفاعة)?' He said: 'Yes.' A man from the group said to him: 'Does the believer need the intercession of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)?' He said: 'Yes, the believers have faults and sins — and there is no one but that he will need the intercession of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on that day.' He said: And a man asked him about the saying of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and no boast.' He said: 'Yes — he will take the ring of the gate of Paradise and open it, then fall prostrate. Allah will say: "Raise your head; intercede, and your intercession will be accepted; ask, and you shall be given." So he will raise his head — then fall prostrate again. Allah will say: "Raise your head; intercede, and your intercession will be accepted; and ask, and you shall be given." Then he will raise his head and intercede and be granted intercession, and ask and be given.'"

In the same work, from Sama‘ah ibn Mihran from Abu Ibrahim (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of Allah: عَسى أَنْ يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقاماً مَحْمُوداً: "He said: People will stand on the Day of Resurrection for the duration of forty days — and the sun will be commanded, so it will descend over the heads of the servants and sweat will bridle them — and the earth will be commanded not to accept anything of their sweat — so they will come to Adam and seek his intercession, and he will direct them to Noah — and Noah will direct them to Abraham, and Abraham will direct them to Moses, and Moses will direct them to Jesus, and Jesus will direct them to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He will say: 'You must go to Muhammad, the Seal of the Prophets.' Then Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) will say: 'I am for it.'

He will proceed until he comes to the gate of Paradise and knocks. It will be said to him: 'Who is this?' — and Allah knows best. He will say: 'Muhammad.' He will say: 'Open.' When the gate is opened, he will face his Lord and fall prostrate — and he will not raise his head until it is said to him: 'Speak and ask, you shall be given; and intercede, your intercession will be accepted.' He will raise his head, face his Lord, and fall prostrate again. He will be told the same — then he will raise his head until he even intercedes for those who have been burned by the Fire — so no one among the people on the Day of Resurrection in all the nations will be more distinguished than Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — and that is the saying of Allah, the Exalted: عَسى أَنْ يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقاماً مَحْمُوداً."

I say: His saying: "until he even intercedes for those who have been burned by the Fire" means some of those who entered the Fire. Regarding the meaning of this narration, there are several narrations from the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bukhari, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn ‘Umar: "He said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'The sun will draw near until the sweat reaches half the ear — while they are in that state, they will seek help from Adam (عليه‌السلام), and he will say: "I am not the one for that," then Moses (عليه‌السلام) who will say the same, then Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — so he will intercede and Allah will judge between the creation. He will walk until he takes the ring of the gate of Paradise, and on that day Allah will raise him to a [praiseworthy] station.'"

In the same work, Ibn Jarir and al-Bayhaqi in Shu‘ab al-Iman extracted from Abu Hurayrah that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The praiseworthy station is the intercession."

In the same work, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Sa‘d ibn Abi Waqqas: "He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was asked about the praiseworthy station, and he said: 'It is the intercession.'"

I say: The narrations with the aforementioned contents are numerous.

Ayahs

وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مَا هُوَ شِفَآءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۙ وَلَا يَزِيدُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا خَسَارًا (٨٢)
We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the faithful; and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss. (82)
وَإِذَآ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَى ٱلْإِنسَـٰنِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَـَٔا بِجَانِبِهِۦ ۖ وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ كَانَ يَـُٔوسًا (٨٣)
When We bless man, he is disregardful and turns aside; but when an ill befalls him, he is despondent. (83)
قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلَىٰ شَاكِلَتِهِۦ فَرَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ هُوَ أَهْدَىٰ سَبِيلًا (٨٤)
Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character. Your Lord knows best who is better guided with regard to the way.’ (84)
وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلرُّوحِ ۖ قُلِ ٱلرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّى وَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٨٥)
They question you concerning the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of the command of my Lord, and you have not been given of the knowledge except a few [of you].’ (85)
وَلَئِن شِئْنَا لَنَذْهَبَنَّ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُ لَكَ بِهِۦ عَلَيْنَا وَكِيلًا (٨٦)
If We wish, We would take away what We have revealed to you. Then you would not find for yourself any defender against Us, (86)
إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّ فَضْلَهُۥ كَانَ عَلَيْكَ كَبِيرًا (٨٧)
except a mercy from your Lord. Indeed His grace has been great upon you. (87)
قُل لَّئِنِ ٱجْتَمَعَتِ ٱلْإِنسُ وَٱلْجِنُّ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ لَا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِۦ وَلَوْ كَانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيرًا (٨٨)
Say, ‘Should all humans and jinn rally to bring the like of this Quran, they will not bring its like, even if they assisted one another.’ (88)
وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا لِلنَّاسِ فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مِن كُلِّ مَثَلٍ فَأَبَىٰٓ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُورًا (٨٩)
We have certainly interspersed this Quran with every [kind of] parable for the people, but most people are only intent on ingratitude. (89)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّىٰ تَفْجُرَ لَنَا مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ يَنۢبُوعًا (٩٠)
They say, ‘We will not believe you until you make a spring gush forth for us from the ground. (90)
أَوْ تَكُونَ لَكَ جَنَّةٌ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَعِنَبٍ فَتُفَجِّرَ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ خِلَـٰلَهَا تَفْجِيرًا (٩١)
Or until you have a garden of date palms and vines and you make streams gush through it. (91)
أَوْ تُسْقِطَ ٱلسَّمَآءَ كَمَا زَعَمْتَ عَلَيْنَا كِسَفًا أَوْ تَأْتِىَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ قَبِيلًا (٩٢)
Or until you cause the sky to fall in fragments upon us, just as you have averred. Or until you bring Allah and the angels [right] in front of us. (92)
أَوْ يَكُونَ لَكَ بَيْتٌ مِّن زُخْرُفٍ أَوْ تَرْقَىٰ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَلَن نُّؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّىٰ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا كِتَـٰبًا نَّقْرَؤُهُۥ ۗ قُلْ سُبْحَانَ رَبِّى هَلْ كُنتُ إِلَّا بَشَرًا رَّسُولًا (٩٣)
Or until you have a house of gold, or you ascend into the sky. And we will not believe your ascension until you bring down for us a book that we may read.’ Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human apostle?!’ (93)
وَمَا مَنَعَ ٱلنَّاسَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ بَشَرًا رَّسُولًا (٩٤)
Nothing has kept these people from believing when guidance came to them, but their saying, ‘Has Allah sent a human as an apostle?!’ (94)
قُل لَّوْ كَانَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةٌ يَمْشُونَ مُطْمَئِنِّينَ لَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَلَكًا رَّسُولًا (٩٥)
Say, ‘Had there been angels in the earth, walking around and residing [in it like humans do], We would have sent down to them an angel from the heaven as apostle.’ (95)
قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا (٩٦)
Say, ‘Allah suffices as witness between me and you. Indeed He is well aware His servants and a keen observer.’ (96)
وَمَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِهِۦ ۖ وَنَحْشُرُهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ عُمْيًا وَبُكْمًا وَصُمًّا ۖ مَّأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ كُلَّمَا خَبَتْ زِدْنَـٰهُمْ سَعِيرًا (٩٧)
Whomever Allah guides is rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—you will never for find them any guardians besides Him. On the Day of Resurrection, We shall muster them [scrambling] on their faces, blind, dumb, and deaf. Their refuge shall be hell. Whenever it subsides, We shall intensify the blaze for them. (97)
ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآؤُهُم بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا كُنَّا عِظَـٰمًا وَرُفَـٰتًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقًا جَدِيدًا (٩٨)
That is their requital because they defied Our signs and said, ‘What, when we have become bones and dust, shall we really be raised in a new creation?’ (98)
۞ أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ قَادِرٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُمْ وَجَعَلَ لَهُمْ أَجَلًا لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ فَأَبَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ إِلَّا كُفُورًا (٩٩)
Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth, is able to create the like of them? He has appointed for them a term, in which there is no doubt; yet the wrongdoers are only intent on ingratitude. (99)
قُل لَّوْ أَنتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزَآئِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّىٓ إِذًا لَّأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ ٱلْإِنفَاقِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ قَتُورًا (١٠٠)
Say, ‘Even if you possessed the treasuries of my Lord’s mercy, you would withhold them for the fear of being spent, and man is very niggardly.’ (100)

(Commentary)

This is a return, after another return, to the discourse concerning the Qur’an, its status as a sign (آية) of prophethood, and the mercy and blessing that accompany it. The discourse on this was previously opened with His saying: إِنَّ هَذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَهْدِي لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ — "Indeed this Quran guides to that which is most upright" (17:9). Then He returned to it with His saying: وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا فِي هَذَا الْقُرْآنِ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا — "Certainly We have dispensed [various metaphors] in this Quran so that they may take admonition" (17:41), and His saying: وَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ — "When you recite the Quran..." (17:45), and His saying: وَمَا مَنَعَنَا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآيَاتِ — "Nothing kept Us from sending the signs..." (17:59).

In these verses, He explains that the Qur’an is a healing (شفاء) and a mercy (رحمة); in other words, it is a reformer for the one whose soul is sound, and a cause of loss for the wrongdoers (الظالمين). He also explains that it is a miraculous sign of prophethood. Then He mentions the signs they used to propose to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), the response to them, and the discourse related to that.

Included in these verses is the mention of their question regarding the spirit (الروح) and the answer to it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ مَا هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَساراً — "We send down in the Quran that which is a healing and mercy for the faithful, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss" (17:82). The word "from" (مِنْ) is explicative (بيانية), clarifying the relative pronoun, namely His saying: "that which is a healing" (مَا هُوَ شِفاءٌ), meaning: We send down that which is a healing and a mercy, and it is the Qur’an.

Describing the Qur’an as a healing (شفاء) — and healing only occurs from an illness — is evidence that hearts possess states to which the Qur’an stands as a healing medicine stands to a disease. This is what is understood from the speech of the Glorified, where He mentions that the true religion (الدين الحق) is primordial (فطري) to the human being. Just as the human constitution, which was fashioned upon its original creation (الخلقة الأصلية) before being afflicted by contrary states and effects divergent from its initial balance, possesses a natural rectitude (استقامة طبيعية) according to which it proceeds through the stages of life; likewise, according to its original creation, it possesses true beliefs regarding the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد) and the fundamental branches of knowledge (أصول المعارف) derived from them, as well as virtuous and pure character traits (أخلاق فاضلة زاكية) that harmonize with it, resulting in suitable states and actions.

Thus, the human being possesses a spiritual and meaningful health and rectitude (صحة واستقامة روحية معنوية), just as he possesses a physical and formal health and rectitude (صحة واستقامة جسمية صورية). He also suffers from spiritual diseases and ailments (أمراض وأدواء روحية) due to the disruption of spiritual health, just as he suffers from physical diseases and ailments due to the disruption of physical health. For every ailment there is a medicine, and for every disease there is a healing.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned regarding some of the believers that in their hearts is a disease (مرض), which is other than overt disbelief (الكفر) and hypocrisy (النفاق), as indicated by His saying: لَئِنْ لَمْ يَنْتَهِ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَالْمُرْجِفُونَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ لَنُغْرِيَنَّكَ بِهِمْ — "If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness and the rumourmongers in the city do not desist, We will surely rouse you against them" (33:60), and His saying: وَلِيَقُولَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَالْكافِرُونَ ما ذا أَرادَ اللهُ بِهذا مَثَلاً — "...and so that those in whose hearts is a sickness and the faithless may say, ‘What did Allah mean by this allegory?’" (74:31).

This thing called "disease" is nothing other than that which disrupts the firmness of the heart and the rectitude of the soul, such as types of doubt (الشك) and uncertainty (الريب) that cause internal agitation, the wavering of the inner secret (تزلزل السر), the inclination toward falsehood, and the following of passion (اتباع الهوى). These are things that can coexist with the faith of the general believers who occupy the lowest ranks of faith (الإيمان), and are considered a deficiency and a form of polytheism (شرك) in relation to the higher ranks of faith. Indeed, the Exalted has said: وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ — "And most of them do not believe in Allah without being polytheists" (12:106), and He said: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً — "But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their souls any burning regret over your verdict, and submit in total submission" (4:65).

The Noble Qur’an removes, through its definitive proofs (حججه القاطعة) and radiant demonstrations (براهينه الساطعة), the various types of doubts and ambiguities that obstruct the path of true beliefs and real knowledge. It repels, through its healing exhortations, its stories, lessons, parables, promises, threats, warnings, glad tidings, rulings, and laws, the maladies and afflictions of the hearts. Thus, the Qur’an is a healing for the believers.

As for its being a mercy (رحمة) for the believers — and mercy is the bestowing of that which completes a deficiency and removes a need — it is because the Qur’an illuminates the hearts with the light of knowledge (العلم) and certainty (اليقين) after removing from them the darknesses of ignorance, blindness, doubt, and uncertainty. It adorns them with virtuous dispositions (الملكات الفاضلة) and noble, pure states after washing away the filth of base forms and lowly attributes.

Insofar as it is a healing, it removes various diseases and ailments; and insofar as it is a mercy, it restores to the soul the health and original primordial rectitude (الاستقامة الأصلية الفطرية) it had lost. By being a healing, it purifies the locus from the obstacles contrary to felicity (السعادة) and prepares it to receive it; and by being a mercy, it clothes it in the garment of felicity and blesses it with the bounty of rectitude.

Thus, the Qur’an is a healing and a mercy for diseased hearts, just as it is a guidance (هدى) and a mercy for souls not secure from misguidance. Through this, the subtle point (النكتة) in the sequencing of mercy after healing in His saying: "that which is a healing and mercy for the faithful" (مَا هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ) becomes clear. It is like His saying: هُدىً وَرَحْمَةً لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "...a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith" (12:111) and His saying: وَمَغْفِرَةً وَرَحْمَةً — "...forgiveness and mercy" (4:96).

The meaning of His saying: "We send down in the Quran that which is a healing and mercy for the faithful" (وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ مَا هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ) is: We send down to you a matter that heals the diseases of the hearts, removes them, and restores them to a state of health and rectitude, so they may enjoy the blessing of felicity and dignity.

And His saying: وَلا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَساراً — "...and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss" (17:82). The context indicates that the intent is to explain the effect the Qur’an has on those other than the believers, in contrast to its beautiful effect on the believers. Thus, the "wrongdoers" (الظالمين) refers to the non-believers, who are the disbelievers (الكفار), rather than polytheists (المشركين) specifically, as appears from some commentators. The ruling was attached to the attribute, namely "wrongdoing" (الظلم), to suggest the cause (التعليل); that is, the Qur’an only increases them in loss because of their wrongdoing through disbelief.

Loss (الخسار) is the diminution of capital (رأس المال). The disbelievers possess a capital according to their origin, which is the primordial religion (الدين الفطري) with which their simple souls were inspired. Then, by their disbelief in Allah and His signs, they lost out on it and diminished it. Furthermore, their disbelief in the Qur’an and their turning away from it due to their wrongdoing increases them in loss upon loss and diminution upon diminution, if any remnant of the primordial gift (موهبة الفطرة) remained with them. The context of negation and exception (النفي والاستثناء) points to this subtle nuance, where it is said: "and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss" (وَلا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَساراً), rather than saying: "and it increases the wrongdoers in loss."

From this, it becomes clear that the sum total of the verse's meaning is that the Qur’an increases the believers in health and rectitude upon their health and rectitude through faith, and in felicity upon their felicity. If it increases the disbelievers in anything, it only increases them in deficiency and loss.

The commentators have other views regarding the meaning of the beginning and end of the verse, which we have overlooked; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to their writings.

Among what they mentioned is that "healing" (الشفاء) in the verse is more general than the healing of spiritual diseases like ignorance, doubt, uncertainty, and base psychological dispositions; it also includes the healing of physical diseases through seeking blessings (التبرك) from its noble verses by recitation and writing.

There is no harm in this, but if such generalization were correct, it should be correct for both the beginning and the end of the verse. For just as it is used to repel diseases and maladies through recitation or writing, it is also used to repel enemies, lift the oppression of the wrongdoers, and nullify the plots of the disbelievers, thereby increasing the wrongdoers in loss just as it benefits the believers with healing. The attribution of the increase in their loss to the Qur’an, while it is in reality based on their own poor choice and the wretchedness of their souls, is a type of metaphor (مجاز).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا أَنْعَمْنا عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَأى بِجانِبِهِ وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ كانَ يَؤُساً — "When We bless man, he is disregardful and turns aside; but when ill fortune visits him, he is despondent" (17:83). It is said in al-Mufradat: العرض (breadth) is the opposite of length; originally it is said of bodies, then used for other things... until he said: أعرض means he showed his side (عرضه), i.e., his flank. When it is said: "Such a thing appeared (أعرض) to me," it means its side became visible so it could be taken. When it is said: "He turned away (أعرض) from me," it means he turned back, showing his side. (End of the relevant portion).

And النأي means distance. "He turned aside (نأى بجانبه)" means he took for himself a direction far from Us. The combination of His saying: "he is disregardful and turns aside" (أَعْرَضَ وَنَأى بِجانِبِهِ) depicts the state of the human being in his distancing and cutting himself off from his Lord when He bestows blessings upon him. It is like one who turns his face away from his companion and takes a position far from him. Some commentators have mentioned that His saying: "turned aside" (نأى بجانبه) is an allusion (كناية) to arrogance and self-exaltation.

His saying: وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ كانَ يَؤُساً — "...but when ill fortune visits him, he is despondent." That is, when evil touches him even slightly, like a mere touch (المس), he becomes despairing, having cut off hope for good, which is the blessing. Evil (الشر) was not attributed to Him, the Exalted, as the blessing was, out of transcendence (تنزيهاً) for Him, the Exalted, from having evil ascribed to Him. Also, the existence of evil is a relative matter (أمر نسبي), not an essential one (نفسي). What is realized as evil in the world, such as death, disease, poverty, deficiency, and so on, is only evil in relation to its specific instance. As for its relation to other things, and especially to the general order (النظام العام) prevailing in the cosmos, it is part of the good that is indispensable in the universal governance (التدبير الكلي). Thus, what is good is that which Divine Providence (العناية الإلهية) has specifically targeted and is intended essentially (مراد بالذات); and what is evil is that which Providence has targeted for the sake of something else, and is decreed accidentally (مقضي بالعرض).

The meaning is: when We bestow blessings upon the human being — this creature situated in the flow of causes — he becomes occupied with the outward causes and clings to them, thus forgetting Us, neither remembering Us nor thanking Us. And when a small amount of evil reaches him, such that good is stripped from him and its causes vanish, and he sees that, he becomes intensely despairing of good because he was attached to its causes, and he sees the nullification of those causes while not seeing his Lord's hand in that.

The verse describes the state of the ordinary human being situated in a vital society governed by convention (العرف) and habit (العادة). When divine blessings of wealth, status, children, and others follow one another upon him, and the outward causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) harmonize with that, he becomes occupied with them and his heart becomes attached to them, leaving him no room to occupy himself with the remembrance of his Lord and gratitude for what He bestowed. When evil touches him and some of the gifted blessings are stripped away, he despairs of good and does not console himself with hope, because he only sees good through outward causes, none of which he finds in existence at that time.

This state is different from the state of the primordial human being (الإنسان الفطري) whose mind is not clouded by social forms and etiquettes, nor governed by convention and habit — either by a divine support (تأييد إلهي) that accompanies and rectifies him, or by the occurrence of a necessity (اضطرار) that makes him forget outward causes, so he returns to the simplicity of his primordial nature (الفطرة) and calls upon his Lord, asking Him to remove his harm. Thus, the human being has two states: a primordial state that guides him to return to his Lord when harm touches him and evil descends, and a habitual state in which causes intervene between him and his Lord, occupying him and diverting him from returning to Him with remembrance and gratitude. The verse describes his second state, not the first.

From here, it becomes clear that there is no contradiction between this verse and the verses indicating that when harm touches the human being, he returns to his Lord, such as His saying previously: وَإِذا مَسَّكُمُ الضُّرُّ فِي الْبَحْرِ ضَلَّ مَنْ تَدْعُونَ إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ — "When distress befalls you at sea, those whom you invoke forsake you, except Him" (17:67), and His saying: وَإِذا مَسَّ الْإِنْسانَ الضُّرُّ دَعانا لِجَنْبِهِ أَوْ قاعِداً أَوْ قائِماً — "When distress befalls man, he supplicates Us, [lying] on his side, or sitting, or standing" (10:12), and others.

The connection of the verse to what preceded it also becomes clear: it is connected to the previous verse through its ending, namely His saying: "and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss" (وَلا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَساراً). The sum total is that this loss is not far from them, for it is the state of the human being to be occupied by outward causes when divine blessings descend, thus turning away from his Lord, disregarding Him, and turning aside, and to despair when evil touches him.

Philosophical Discussion

They have mentioned that evils (الشرور) are included in the Divine Decree (القضاء الإلهي) accidentally (بالعرض). They have presented the following in its explanation: It is narrated from Plato that evil is non-existence (عدم). He explained this with examples: in a killing by a sword, for instance, there is an evil. This evil does not lie in the striker's ability to perform the strike, nor in his courage, nor in the strength of his arm muscles, for all of that is a perfection (كمال) for him and is not part of evil at all. It does not lie in the sharpness of the sword, the precision of its tip, or its being a cutting tool, for that is part of its perfection and goodness. It does not lie in the neck of the slain person being affected by the cutting tool, for it is part of its perfection to be so. Thus, nothing remains for evil except the departure of the slain person's soul and the nullification of his life, which is a non-existent (عدمي) matter. The same applies to all other examples: evil is non-existence.

Furthermore, since the evils in the world are linked to occurring events and surrounded by them, they are relative non-existences (أعدام مضافة), not absolute non-existence (عدم مطلق). Thus, they have a share of existence and occurrence, such as types of loss, deficiency, death, and corruption occurring externally and included in the general cosmic order. Therefore, they have a connection to the Divine Decree governing the cosmos, but they are included in the Decree accidentally, not essentially (بالذات).

This is because what you conceive of as non-existence is either absolute non-existence — which is the non-existence of the contradiction to existence — or relative to a potentiality (ملكة), which is the non-existence of a perfection of existence from that which is supposed to possess it, like blindness, which is the non-existence of sight in that which is supposed to be seeing.

The first category is either the non-existence of a thing taken in relation to its quiddity (ماهيته), such as the non-existence of Zayd taken in relation to the quiddity of himself. This is a mental consideration (اعتبار عقلي) and is not part of the occurrence of evil at all, as there is no common subject between two contradictories. Yes, non-existence may sometimes be restricted and measured against a thing, thus becoming an evil, such as the non-existence of Zayd after his existence; this returns in reality to non-existence relative to a potentiality, the ruling of which follows.

Or it is the non-existence of a thing taken in relation to another thing, such as the lack of the perfections of Necessary Existence (الوجود الواجبي) in contingent quiddities (الماهيات الإمكانية), and the lack in every quiddity of the existence of another quiddity specific to it — such as the plant lacking the existence of the animal, or the cow lacking the existence of the horse. This type of non-existence is among the concomitants of quiddities, which are conceptual (اعتبارية) and not ontologically originated (غير مجعولة).

The second category, which is non-existence relative to a potentiality, is the lack in a certain thing of some perfection of its existence that is supposed to exist for it and by which it is supposed to be characterized — such as the types of corruption (الفساد) that afflict things, and the deficiencies, flaws, maladies, diseases, ailments, and pains that occur to them. This category of evils only occurs in material things and is attributed to the deficiency of predispositions (الاستعدادات) at their various levels, not to the bestowing of the Principle of Existence (مبدأ الوجود); for the cause of non-existence is non-existence, just as the cause of existence is existence.

Thus, that which the word of creation (الإيجاد) and the Divine Will have targeted, and which the Decree has essentially encompassed in matters accompanied by some evil, is only the measure of existence with which it was clothed according to its predisposition and the extent of its receptivity. As for the non-existence that accompanies it, it is only attributed to its lack of receptivity and the deficiency of its predisposition. Yes, ontological origination (الجعل) and bestowing (الإفاضة) are attributed to it accidentally due to a type of unity (اتحاد) between it and the existence that accompanies it.

In another explanation, matters are of five categories: that which is pure good (خير محض); that whose good is greater than its evil; that whose good and evil are equal; that whose evil is greater than its good; and that which is pure evil. None of the last three exist, as they would necessitate preferring without a preferrer (الترجيح من غير مرجح) or preferring the outweighed over the outweighing (ترجيح المرجوح على الراجح). It is necessary, in view of Divine Wisdom (الحكمة الإلهية) emanating from Necessary Power and Knowledge and Generosity unmixed with stinginess, to bestow what is most beneficial (الأصلح) in the most perfect order, and to bring into existence that which is pure good and that whose good is greater than its evil; for leaving the first would be pure evil, and leaving the second would be much evil.

Thus, the evil that exists is rare and little in relation to the good that exists, and the little evil only exists as a consequence of the much good.

It is narrated from Imam al-Razi that there is no place for this discussion among them, based on what they held that He, the Exalted, is a complete cause (علة تامة) for the world, and the impossibility of the complete cause being separated from its effect. Thus, He is necessitated (موجب) in His act, not choosing (مختار). Therefore, He must bring into existence that for which He is a cause, whether good or evil, without choice in preference.

It was hidden from him that this necessity (الوجوب) only subsists in the effect, received from the cause, just as it receives its existence from it. It is impossible for what the cause bestows to return and overpower the cause, forcing it to act and overcoming it by its limitation.

The author of Ruh al-Ma'ani was fair when he first pointed to something similar to the aforementioned discussion, saying: "It is not hidden that this is only completed based on the view that it is impossible for His Will, the Exalted, to be equally related to a thing and its opposite without a motive or benefit, as is the school of the Ash'arites. Otherwise, it might be said: if the Actor of all is choosing, He may choose whichever He wishes of goods and evils. However, the sages and the pillars of Islam said: His choice, the Exalted, is higher than this pattern; the affairs of the world are tied to universal laws, and His acts, the Exalted, are linked to manifest and hidden wisdoms and benefits."

Then he said: "And the Imam's saying: 'Since the philosophers held necessity and compulsion in acts, their delving into this topic is part of superfluity (الفضول) and misguidance, because the question "why" regarding their issuance does not apply, just like the issuance of burning from fire, because it issues from it by its essence'—"

"—is born of prejudice, because their researchers establish choice (الاختيار). The issuance of acts from Allah, the Exalted, according to them, is not like the issuance of burning from fire. After assuming submission, their discussion of how evil occurs in this world is because the Creator, blessed be His Name, is pure, simple good according to them, and they do not allow evil from that which has no evil aspect at all. Thus, at first glance, what the Dualists (الثنوية) fabricated of two principles, good and evil, would be incumbent upon them. So they freed themselves through that discussion; thus it is a merit (فضل), not superfluity." (End).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلَتِهِ فَرَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ هُوَ أَهْدى سَبِيلاً — "Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character. Your Lord knows best who is better guided in his way’" (17:84). المشاكلة (character/disposition) — according to what is in al-Mufradat — is from al-shakl, which is the hobbling of an animal; that with which it is hobbled is called a shikal with a kasrah on the shin. الشاكلة is the innate disposition (السجية); it was so named because it "hobbles" the human being to proceed according to what suits it and what it necessitates.

In al-Majma': الشاكلة is the way (الطريقة) and the school (المذهب). It is said: "This is a path with many branches (شواكل)," meaning many paths branch off from it. (End). It is as if they were both named so because of the "hobbling" of the travelers and adherents by their commitment to them and not deviating from them. It was also said: الشاكلة is from al-shakl with a fathah on the shin, meaning "like" (المثل). It was also said: it is from الشكل with a kasrah on the shin, meaning "form" (الهيأة).

In any case, the noble verse arranges the human being's action upon his character (شاكلته), meaning that the action suits and agrees with it. In relation to the action, it is like the spirit pervading the body, which represents through its limbs and actions the states of the spiritual soul. It has been established through experience and scientific research that there is a specific link between psychological dispositions (الملكات) and states and actions. The action of the brave, courageous person and the coward are not equal when they face a terrifying situation; nor is the action of the generous, noble person and the stingy, base person in matters of spending, and so on. There is also a specific link between psychological attributes and the type of human physical constitution. Among temperaments (الأمزجة) are those to which anger and love of revenge naturally hasten; among them are those in which the desire for food or marriage or other things boils and seethes, such that the soul yearns for it at the slightest cause that calls and moves it; and among them are others. Thus, the formation of dispositions differs according to what suits the instance in terms of speed and slowness.

Despite all this, the call of a temperament suitable for a disposition or an action does not exit the limit of predisposition (الاقتضاء) to the limit of complete causality (العلية التامة), such that an action contrary to the requirement of nature would exit from possibility to impossibility and choice (الاختيار) would be nullified. The action remains within its choosability, even if in some instances it is extremely difficult.

His speech, the Glorified, supports what preceded according to what contemplation yields. For He, the Glorified, is the One who says: وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِداً — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord; and as for that which is bad, it does not come out except scantily" (7:58). Joining this verse to the verses indicating the universality of the call, such as His saying: لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ — "...that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach" (6:19), yields that the influence of human constitutions on attributes and actions is by way of predisposition, not complete causality, as is apparent.

How could it be otherwise, when He, the Exalted, considers religion to be primordial (فطرياً), heralded by the creation (الخلقة) for which there is no substitution or change? He said: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah upon which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion" (30:30). And He said: ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ — "Then He made the way easy for him" (80:20). The call of the primordial nature (الفطرة) to the true religion and the moderate path (السنة المعتدلة) cannot coexist with a call of the constitution to evil, corruption, and deviation from moderation by way of complete causality.

As for the saying of the one who says: "Felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) are essential (ذاتيتان), never failing their necessitated subject, like the evenness of four and the oddness of three, or they are decreed by an eternal, necessary decree; and the call is only to complete the proof (إتمام الحجة), not for the possibility of change or hope of transformation from one state to another, for the matter is already settled"—

—it is refuted by the fact that the validity of establishing the proof (إقامة الحجة) is itself a proof for the non-necessity of the felicity of the felicitous and the wretchedness of the wretched. For if felicity and wretchedness were among the concomitants of essences (لوازم الذوات), they would not need a proof to attach to them, as there is no proof in essential matters; thus the proof would be futile. Likewise, if they were necessary for the essences by an eternal necessary decree, not by an essential predisposition of the essences, the proof would be for the people against Allah, the Glorified; thus the proof from Him, the Exalted, would be futile. So the validity of establishing the proof from His side, the Glorified, reveals the non-necessity of anything of felicity and wretchedness in view of the human essence, regardless of his good and evil actions and his true and false beliefs.

Furthermore, the human being's recourse to the primordial nature for the goals of life through things like teaching, training, warning, glad tidings, promises, threats, commands, prohibitions, and so on, is the clearest evidence that the human being in himself is at the meeting point of two lines and the branching of two paths: felicity and wretchedness. It is within his power to choose whichever of them he wishes and to tread whichever of them he wants, and for every effort there is a suitable reward. The Exalted said: وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى وَأَنَّ سَعْيَهُ سَوْفَ يُرى ثُمَّ يُجْزاهُ الْجَزاءَ الْأَوْفى — "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for, and that he will soon be shown his endeavour, then he will be rewarded for it with the fullest reward" (53:39-41). This is one type of link established between actions and dispositions and between essences. There is another type of link established between actions and dispositions and between the conditions, states, and factors external to the human essence that are established in the context of life and the atmosphere of living, such as etiquettes, paths, forms, and traditional customs. These call the human being to what agrees with them and deter him from opposing them, and they do not delay in fashioning him into a new, second image, such that his actions correspond to the surrounding conditions and states gathered and harmonized in the context of his life.

This link is mostly by way of predisposition, except that it may sometimes become so established that there is no hope for its removal, due to the rooting of base or virtuous dispositions in the human soul. In His speech, the Exalted, there is that which points to this, such as His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ خَتَمَ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلى سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلى أَبْصارِهِمْ غِشاوَةٌ — "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith. Allah has set a seal on their hearts and their hearing, and there is a blindfold on their sight" (2:6-7), and others.

This does not harm the validity of establishing the proof against them through calling, warning, and glad tidings, because the impossibility of the call's effect on them is based on their own poor choice, and impossibility through choice (الامتناع بالاختيار) does not negate choice.

It has become clear from what we have presented at length that the human being has character (شاكلة) after character. There is a character prepared by the type of his creation and the specificity of his physical constitution; this is a moral personality (شخصية خلقية) resulting from the interaction of his bodily systems with one another, like the temperament (المزاج), which is a middle quality resulting from the interaction of contrary qualities with one another.

There is another, second character, which is a moral personality resulting from the modes of influence of external factors on the human soul, based on what is in it of the first character, if it exists.

The human being, upon whatever character is attained and whatever psychological trait and internal spiritual actuality (فعلية) he possesses, his action proceeds according to it and his deeds represent and recount it. Just as the arrogant, conceited person's state is apparent in his speaking, silence, standing, sitting, movement, and stillness; and the lowly, wretched person's lowliness and wretchedness are manifest in all his actions; and likewise the brave, the coward, the generous, the stingy, the patient, the dignified, the hasty, and so on. How could it be otherwise, when the act represents its actor, the outward is the title of the inward, and the form is the evidence of the meaning?

His speech, the Glorified, confirms this and builds its proofs upon it in many instances, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ وَلَا الظُّلُماتُ وَلَا النُّورُ وَلَا الظِّلُّ وَلَا الْحَرُورُ وَما يَسْتَوِي الْأَحْياءُ وَلَا الْأَمْواتُ — "The blind and the seeing are not equal, nor are darkness and light, nor are the shade and the scorching sun. The living and the dead are not equal" (35:19-22), and His saying: الْخَبِيثاتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَالْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثاتِ وَالطَّيِّباتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَالطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّباتِ — "Vicious women are for vicious men, and vicious men for vicious women. Good women are for good men, and good men for good women" (24:26), among many other verses.

His saying, the Exalted: "Everyone acts according to his character" (كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلَتِهِ) is definitive (محكم) in its meaning, regardless of which meaning we assign to "character." However, the connection of the verse to His saying: "We send down in the Quran that which is a healing and mercy for the faithful, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss" (17:82), and its occurrence in the context that Allah, the Glorified, grants profit to the believers and heals them through the Noble Qur’an and the true call, while causing loss to the wrongdoers because of their wrongdoing, makes it more likely that the intended meaning of "character" is the second meaning: the moral personality attained by the human being from the sum of his instincts and the external factors acting upon him.

It is as if when He, the Exalted, mentioned the believers' benefit from His speech as healing and mercy, and the deprivation of the wrongdoers from that and the increase in their loss, an objector challenged this differentiation, arguing that if He had made both groups equal in healing and mercy, that would have been more fulfilling for the purpose of the message and more beneficial for the state of the call. So He commanded His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to answer them regarding that.

He said: قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلَتِهِ — "Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character.’" That is, your actions issue according to the character and existing actuality you possess. Whoever possesses a just character, his guidance to the word of truth and righteous action is easy, and he benefits from the true call. Whoever possesses a wrongdoing character, it is difficult for him to clothe himself in true speech and righteous action, and he gains nothing from hearing the true call except loss. And Allah, who is your Lord, the Knower of your secrets, the Disposer of your affairs, knows best who possesses a just character and is "better guided in his way" (أَهْدى سَبِيلاً) and closer to benefiting from the word of truth. That which He knew and informed of is that the believers are better guided in their way; thus healing and mercy through the Qur’an He sends down are specific to them, and nothing remains for the disbelievers, the people of wrongdoing, except increased loss, unless they tear themselves away from their wrongdoing and thus benefit from it.

From here, the subtle point in using the superlative form (صيغة التفضيل) in His saying: "better guided in his way" (أَهْدى سَبِيلاً) becomes clear. This is because, as previously mentioned, the character is not necessitating in its call to what suits it. Thus, the wrongdoing character, although it is misguiding and calls to evil action, does not make misguidance inevitable; it still contains a trace of guidance, even if weak. The just character is "better guided" than it. So understand.

Imam al-Razi mentioned in his commentary a summary of which is: the verse indicates that the human rational souls (النفوس الناطقة) differ in their quiddity (الماهية). This is because He, the Exalted, explained in the previous verse that the Qur’an, in relation to some souls, yields healing and mercy, and in relation to others, yields loss and disgrace. Then He followed it with His saying: "Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character’" (قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلَتِهِ). Its meaning is that it is fitting for those pure souls that the effects of intelligence and perfection appear in them from the Qur’an, and for those murky souls that the effects of disgrace and misguidance appear in them from it, just as the sun hardens salt, softens fat, whitens the fuller's garment, and blackens his face.

This is only completed if the spirits and souls differ in their quiddities; some are radiant and clear, in which "light upon light" appears from the Qur’an, and some are murky and dark, in which "misguidance upon misguidance" and "exemplary punishment upon exemplary punishment" appear from it. (End).

The critique of this is: if he had established the proof for the difference in the quiddities of souls after the rooting of their dispositions and their being fashioned by their forms, it would have had a basis. As for the simple souls (النفوس الساذجة) before the rooting of dispositions, they do not differ in their effects with a necessary difference such that the proof would apply to them. You have already learned that the verse only addresses the state of the human being after the attainment of his character and moral personality resulting from the sum of his instincts and the external factors acting upon him, which call to a type of action by way of predisposition. So observe carefully.

"Philosophical Discussion"

The sages have mentioned that between the act and its actor — by which they mean the effect (المعلول) and its efficient cause (العلة الفاعلة) — there is an existential homogeneity (سنخية وجودية) and an essential link (رابطة ذاتية) by which the existence of the act becomes, as it were, a lower level of the existence of its actor, and the existence of the actor becomes, as it were, a higher level of the existence of its act. Indeed, the matter is so based on the primacy of existence (أصالة الوجود) and its gradation (تشكيكه).

They explained this by saying that if there were no essential suitability (مناسبة ذاتية) and factual specificity (خصوصية واقعية) between the effect-act and its efficient cause, by which each is specific to the other, the relation of the actor to his act would be like his relation to anything else, just as the relation of the act to its actor would be like its relation to anything else. Thus, the attribution of the act's issuance to its actor would have no meaning. A similar proof applies to the effect in relation to other causes and establishes the link between it and them. However, since the efficient cause is that which necessitates the existence of the effect, and the giver of a thing is not lacking it, the efficient cause possesses the perfection of the effect's existence, and the effect represents its existence at a lower level.

Sadr al-Muta'allihin explained this in a more precise and subtle way, which is that the effect is in need in its existence of the efficient cause, its essence being dependent upon it. it is not permissible for this poverty and dependence to be posterior to the level of its essence, such that there would be an essence and then poverty and dependence; otherwise, it would be independent in its essence from the cause and self-subsistent without it, thus not being an effect. Therefore, its essence is identical to poverty and dependence (عين الفقر والتعلق). It has no existence except the relational, non-independent existence (الرابط غير المستقل). What appears in it of independent existence, which was assumed with it initially, is only the independence of its cause. Thus, the existence of the effect mimics the existence of its cause and represents it at the level of existence that it possesses.

(Follow-up to the Previous Discussion from the Perspective of the Qur’an)

Contemplation of the Qur’anic verses leaves no doubt that the Noble Qur’an considers things, in their various aspects and diverse types, as signs (آيات) of Him, the Exalted, pointing to His names and attributes. There is nothing except that it is a sign in its existence and in any assumed aspect of its existence for Him, the Exalted, pointing to the court of His greatness and majesty. The sign (الآية), which is the mark (العلامة) pointing to something, insofar as it is a sign, its existence is mirror-like (مرآتي). For its existence is within the "Possessor of the Sign," who is its signified, and it is not independent of Him; for if it were independent in its existence or in an aspect of its existence, it would not be, from that aspect, pointing to Him, indicating Him, or a sign for Him.

Thus, things, insofar as they are created by Him, the Exalted, are His acts. They mimic, through their existence and the attributes of their existence, His existence, the Glorified, and His noble attributes. This is what is meant by the homogeneity of the act with its actor, not that the act possesses the identity of the actor or is identical to the reality of His essence, for necessity refutes that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي وَما أُوتِيتُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — "They question you concerning the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command, and you have not been given of knowledge except a little’" (17:85). الروح (the Spirit), according to what is known in the language, is the principle of life (مبدأ الحياة) by which the animal is able to perceive and move voluntarily. The word is used as both masculine and feminine. It is sometimes used metaphorically for matters through which desirable good effects appear, just as knowledge is considered life for the souls. The Exalted said: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأحيَيْناهُ — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life..." (6:122), i.e., through guidance to faith. Based on this meaning, a group has interpreted His saying: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ — "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command..." (16:2), i.e., with revelation (الوحي); and His saying: وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا — "Thus have We revealed to you a Spirit of Our command" (42:52), i.e., the Qur’an, which is revelation. They mentioned that He, the Exalted, named the revelation or the Qur’an "Spirit" because through it is the life of dead souls, just as through the well-known spirit is the life of dead bodies.

In any case, the mention of the Spirit has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted, in many verses, both Meccan and Medinan. In all of them, it does not refer to the meaning we find in the animal, which is the principle of life from which perception and voluntary movement branch out, as in His saying: يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا — "On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in ranks..." (78:38), and His saying: تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ فِيها بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ — "In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command" (97:4). There is no doubt that what is intended by it in the verse is other than the animal spirit and other than the angels. The narration from Ali (peace be upon him) has already been mentioned, where he argued from His saying, the Exalted: "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants" (16:2) that the Spirit is other than the angels. He described it sometimes as "Holy" (القدس) and sometimes as "Trustworthy" (الأمانة), as will come, due to its purity from betrayal and all other spiritual filth, flaws, and maladies from which human spirits are not free.

Although it is other than the angels, it accompanies them in revelation and transmission, as appears from His saying: "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants" (16:2). For He, the Exalted, said: مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ — "Whoever is an enemy of Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart by Allah’s leave" (2:97). Thus, He attributed the bringing down of the Qur’an upon his heart (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to Gabriel. Then He said: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ — "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down on your heart, so that you may be one of the warners, in a clear Arabic language" (26:193-195). And He said: قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord..." (16:102). Thus, He placed the Spirit — which is other than the angels in one sense — in the place of Gabriel, who is from the angels. So Gabriel descends with the Spirit, and the Spirit carries this recited, followed Qur’an.

Through this, the knot is untied in His saying, the Exalted: "Thus have We revealed to you a Spirit of Our command" (42:52). It becomes clear that the intent of "revealing the Spirit" in the verse is the sending down of the Holy Spirit to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and sending it down to him is the revelation of the Qur’an to him because it carries it, as has been explained. Thus, there is no reason for what some mentioned, as we quoted just now, that the intended meaning of "Spirit" in the verse is the Qur’an.

As for the attribution of revelation (الوحي) — which is hidden speech — to the Spirit in this sense — while it is among the existential beings and external entities — there is no harm in it. For these pure beings, just as they are sacred beings from His creation, the Exalted, they are also words (كلمات) from Him, the Exalted, as He said regarding Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him): وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِنْهُ — "...and His Word that He cast toward Mary and a spirit from Him" (4:171). Thus, He considered the Spirit a "word" pointing to the intent. So it is permissible for the Spirit to be considered a revelation, just as it was considered a word. He only named it a "word" from Him because it was only through the word of creation (كلمة الإيجاد) without the mediation of the ordinary cause in the existence of people, as evidenced by His saying: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى عِنْدَ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was" (3:59). He, the Glorified, increased the clarification of the reality of the Spirit where He said: "Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي). The appearance of "of" (مِنْ) is that it is for clarifying the genus (تبيين الجنس), as in its counterparts among the verses: يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ — "He casts the Spirit of His command..." (40:15), "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command," "We revealed to you a Spirit of Our command," "In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command." Thus, the Spirit is of the genus of the Command (الأمر).

Then He defined His Command in His saying: إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things" (36:82-83). He explained first that His Command is His saying to a thing: "Be" (كن), which is the word of creation (كلمة الإيجاد) that is the creation itself. الإيجاد (creation/bringing into existence) is the existence of the thing, but not from every aspect, rather from the aspect of its attribution to Him, the Exalted, and its subsistence through Him. Thus, His saying is His act.

Among the evidence that the existence of things is a "saying" for Him, the Exalted, from the aspect of its relation to Him while disregarding other existential causes, is His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ — "Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye" (54:50), where He likened His Command, after calling it "single," to the twinkling of an eye. This type of comparison is to negate gradualness (التدريف). Through this, it is known that in originated things there is a gradualness obtained through the mediation of cosmic causes corresponding to time and space, and an aspect stripped of gradualness, outside the realm of time and space; from that aspect, it is His Command, His saying, and His word. As for the aspect by which it is gradual, linked to cosmic causes, and corresponding to time and space, it is from that aspect part of the Creation (الخلق). The Exalted said: أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ — "Look! All creation and command belong to Him" (7:54). Thus, the Command (الأمر) is the existence of the thing from the aspect of its attribution to Him, the Exalted, alone; and the Creation (الخلق) is that from the aspect of its attribution to Him with the mediation of cosmic causes in it.

This is also understood from His saying: "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was" (3:59), where He first mentioned the creation of Adam and mentioned its link to dust, which is among the causes, then mentioned his existence and did not link it to anything except His saying: "Be" (كُنْ). So understand that. Similar to it is His saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلْناهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرارٍ مَكِينٍ ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً — "Then We made him a drop of fluid in a secure lodging. Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass. Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue..." until He said: ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ — "...then We produced him as another creation" (23:13-14). He considered his creation attributed to Himself, without the intervention of cosmic causes, as the "production of another creation."

Thus, it has become clear through all this that the Command (الأمر) is the celestial word of creation and His act, the Exalted, specific to Him, in which causes do not mediate, and which is not measured by time or space or other things.

Then He explained secondly that His Command in everything is the dominion (ملكوت) of that thing — and al-malakut is more emphatic than al-mulk (kingdom). For every thing there is a dominion, just as for it there is a command. The Exalted said: أَوَلَمْ يَنْظُرُوا فِي مَلَكُوتِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Have they not contemplated the dominion of the heavens and the earth..." (7:185), and He said: وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth..." (6:75), and He said: "In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command" (97:4).

It has been established by what has passed that the Command is the word of creation, and it is His act, the Exalted, specific to Him, in which cosmic causes with their gradual effects do not mediate. It is the existence higher than the realm of matter and the vessel of time. And the Spirit (الروح), according to its existence, is of the genus of the Command, from the dominion (الملكوت).

The Exalted has described the matter of the Spirit in His speech with various descriptions. He mentioned it individually in such as His saying: "On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in ranks" (78:38), and His saying: تَعْرُجُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ — "The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him..." (70:4).

It appears from His speech that some of it is with the angels, as in the verses quoted just now: "it is he [Gabriel] who has brought it down on your heart," "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down on your heart," "Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down," and His saying: فَأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَها بَشَراً سَوِيًّا — "...then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he embodied himself for her as a flawless human" (19:17).

Some of it is breathed into the human being generally. The Exalted said: ثُمَّ سَوَّاهُ وَنَفَخَ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِهِ — "Then He proportioned him and breathed into him of His Spirit" (32:9), and He said: فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي — "So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My Spirit..." (15:29, 38:72).

Some of it is with the believers, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ — "...It is they in whose hearts He has inscribed faith and whom He has strengthened with a Spirit from Him" (58:22). It is also suggested, indeed indicated, by His saying: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people..." (6:122). For the life mentioned in the verse is a new life, and life is a branch of the Spirit.

Some of it descended to the prophets (peace be upon them), as indicated by His saying: "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants, [saying]: ‘Warn...’" (16:2), and His saying: وَآتَيْنا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ الْبَيِّناتِ وَأَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ — "...and We gave Jesus, son of Mary, manifest proofs and confirmed him with the Holy Spirit" (2:87), and His saying: "Thus have We revealed to you a Spirit of Our command" (42:52), and others.

Among the Spirit is that which is suggested by the verses mentioning that in animals other than humans there is life, and in plants there is life; and life is apparently a branch of the Spirit.

Thus, through what we have presented at length, the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: "They question you concerning the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي) has become clear. The question was about the reality of the absolute Spirit mentioned in His speech, the Glorified, and the answer includes an explanation of the reality of the Spirit: that it is of the genus of the Command (الأمر) in the sense previously explained.

As for His saying: وَما أُوتِيتُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — "...and you have not been given of knowledge except a little." That is: what you possess of knowledge regarding the Spirit, which Allah has given you, is a little out of much. For it has a position in existence and properties and effects in the cosmos that are wondrous and marvelous, from which you are veiled.

The commentators have several views regarding the Spirit that was questioned and answered:

Some said: the intended Spirit is the one Allah mentions in His saying: "On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in ranks" and His saying: "The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him." They have no evidence for this.

Some said: the intended Spirit is Gabriel, for Allah named him "Spirit" in His saying: "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down on your heart." The critique of this is that the mere naming of him as "Spirit" in some of His speech does not necessitate that he is the one intended wherever it is mentioned. Furthermore, this naming has a specific meaning we alluded to in the previous discourse; were it not for that, Jesus and Gabriel would be one, because Allah named each of them "Spirit."

Some said: the intended Spirit is the Qur’an, because Allah named it "Spirit" in His saying: "Thus have We revealed to you a Spirit of Our command." Thus, the sum of the question and answer would be: they ask you about the Qur’an, is it from Allah or from yourself? So answer them that it is from my Lord's command; none other than Him is able to bring its like. It is thus a miraculous sign (آية معجزة) indicating the truth of my message, and you have not been given of knowledge regarding it except a little, without encompassing it such that you could bring its like. They said: the following verse: وَلَئِنْ شِئْنا لَنَذْهَبَنَّ بِالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ — "If We willed, We would surely take away what We have revealed to you" (17:86) supports this meaning.

The critique of this is that its being named "Spirit" in some of His speech does not necessitate that it is the one intended whenever it is used absolutely, as mentioned just now. Furthermore, you have already learned what is in the claim of this naming. Also, the following verse is not exclusively a support for this view, but rather harmonizes with some of the other views as well.

Some said: the intended Spirit is the human spirit (الروح الإنساني), for it is what is first understood from its absolute use. His saying: "Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي) is a withholding of explanation and a prohibition against delving into understanding the reality of the Spirit, for it is of Allah's command which He has reserved for His own knowledge and has not informed anyone of its reality. Then they differed regarding its reality: some said it is an airy body (جسم هوائي) circulating in the orifices of the body; some said it is an airy body in the form of the body, dwelling in it—

...and its departure is its death; others say it consists of original parts in the heart; others say it is an accident (عرض) — a non-essential quality existing in a substrate — in the body; and others say it is the body itself, among other views.

The problem with this is that the immediate linguistic sense (التبادر) — the spontaneous coming to mind of a meaning upon hearing a word — in the speech of the Exalted is denied here. Furthermore, contemplation (التدبر) of the verses addressing the matter of the Spirit (الروح), as we have previously established, refutes everything they have mentioned.

Some have said: What is meant by it is the Spirit in general as it occurs in His speech, and the question was only about whether it is eternal (قديم) or originated (محدث). Thus, the answer was that it originates from His command and His act, the Exalted, and His act is originated, not eternal.

The problem with this is that while generalizing the Spirit to all its occurrences in the speech of the Exalted is appropriate, reducing the question to the origination or eternity of the Spirit and directing the answer accordingly is a claim for which there is no evidence from the wording itself.

Furthermore, they differed regarding the meaning of His saying: "The Spirit is from the command of my Lord" (الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي). Is it an affirmative answer or a refusal to answer and a diversion from the question? There are two opinions. The aforementioned views on the meaning of the Spirit vary in their suitability with these two opinions; for some, the first opinion is necessary, and for others, the second. We have alluded to this within the various views.

Then, they had another disagreement regarding those addressed by His saying: "And you have not been given of knowledge except a little" (وَما أُوتِيتُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً). Were they the Jews, or the Quraysh—had they been the questioners under the instruction of the Jews—or were they the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and people other than the Prophet? It is more suitable to the context that the address be directed to the questioners and that the speech be the completion of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) words. The questioners were the Jews because they were known at that time for knowledge, and the speech affirms some knowledge for them, unlike the Quraysh and the disbelievers (كفار) of the Arabs, whom the Exalted described in some of His speech (1) as those who do not know.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ شِئْنا لَنَذْهَبَنَّ بِالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُ لَكَ بِهِ عَلَيْنا وَكِيلاً Withdrawn is what We have revealed to you, then you would not find for yourself any guardian against Us to maintain it. The speech is connected to what preceded it. Although the previous verse addressed the matter of the Spirit in general—which possesses different levels—that which applies to it according to the context of the preceding verses, which were set forth regarding the matter of the Qur’an, is the celestial Spirit descending upon the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who delivers the Qur’an to him.

(1) Surah al-Baqarah, Ayah 118.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: The Spirit descending upon you, delivering the Qur’an to you, is from Our command and is not outside Our power. I swear, if We willed, We could surely take away this Spirit, which is Our word delivered to you; then you would find no one to be a guardian (وكيل) for you against Us to defend you, demand it back from Us, or compel Us to return what We have taken away.

From this, it becomes clear, firstly: that what is meant by "that which We have revealed to you" is the divine Spirit, which is a word delivered from Allah to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), similar to His saying: وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا Thus have We revealed to you a Spirit of Our command (Ash-Shura: 52).

Secondly: that what is meant by the "guardian" (الوكيل) is one to demand and return what Allah has taken away, rather than a guardian for the preservation and recitation of the Qur’an, as some commentators have interpreted. That interpretation is based on explaining "that which We have revealed to you" as the Qur’an itself, rather than the Spirit that descends with it, as we previously stated.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِنَّ فَضْلَهُ كانَ عَلَيْكَ كَبِيراً except as a mercy from your Lord. Indeed His grace has been great toward you. This is an exception (استثناء) from a deleted clause indicated by the context. The estimation is: You were not singled out with what you were singled out with, nor given what you were given of the descent of the Spirit and its constant presence with you, except as a mercy from your Lord. Then He justified it by His saying: "Indeed His grace has been great toward you," which is mentioned in the context of conferring a favor (الامتنان).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلى أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً Say, ‘Should entire mankind and jinn muster to produce the like of this Qur’an, they will not produce the like of it, even if they should back each other up.’ The "backer" (الظهير) is the helper (المعين), derived from "back" (الظهر), just as "leader" (الرئيس) is from "head" (الرأس). His saying "the like of it" (بِمِثْلِهِ) places the manifest noun in the position of the pronoun, and its pronoun refers back to the Qur’an.

In the verse is an explicit challenge (تحدٍ). It is apparent that the challenge (التحدي) — the divine provocation of opponents to produce something similar to the Qur'an — pertains to all the perfections of the Qur’an regarding its wording and meaning, not its eloquence (فصاحة) and rhetoric (بلاغة) alone. This is because the joining of non-native speakers to them would not benefit them at all in countering rhetoric, yet the verse emphasizes the gathering of both humans and jinn (الثقلين) and their aiding one another.

Furthermore, the verse is apparent in the perpetuity of the challenge. The pure Arabs, the masters of eloquence and rhetoric, have passed away today and no trace of them remains, yet the Qur’an remains in its inimitability (إعجاز), challenging by itself just as it was.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنا لِلنَّاسِ فِي هذَا الْقُرْآنِ مِنْ كُلِّ مَثَلٍ فَأَبى أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُوراً Certainly We have cited in this Qur’an every [kind of] parable for mankind, but most people are averse to anything but ingratitude. The "diversification" (تصريف) of parables is their repetition, reiteration, and transformation from one explanation to another and from one style to another. A "parable" (المثل) is the description of the intended meaning by what represents it and brings it closer to the listener's mind. The word "from" (مِنْ) in His saying "from every parable" (مِنْ كُلِّ مَثَلٍ) denotes the starting point of the goal (ابتداء الغاية). What is meant is every parable that clarifies for them the way of truth and paves for them the path of faith and gratitude, as evidenced by His saying: "but most people are averse to anything but ingratitude." The speech is intended for rebuke and blame.

In His saying "most people" (أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ), the manifest noun is placed in the position of the pronoun; the original form would be "most of them." Perhaps the reason for this is to indicate that this is the implication of their being "people" (ناساً), as previously mentioned in His saying: وَكانَ الْإِنْسانُ كَفُوراً and man is very ungrateful (Al-Isra: 67).

The meaning is: I swear, We have repeated for people in this Qur’an every parable that clarifies the truth for them and invites them to faith in Us and gratitude for Our blessings, but most people refused anything but to disbelieve and not be grateful.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى تَفْجُرَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ يَنْبُوعاً They say, ‘We will not believe you until you make a spring gush forth from the ground for us, — until His saying — كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ a book that we may read.’ "Cleaving" (الفجر) is opening and splitting, as is "causing to gush" (التفجير), except that the latter implies intensification and abundance. A "spring" (الينبوع) is a fount whose water does not dry up. "Amidst" (خلال) something means its middle and its intervals. "Fragments" (الكسف) is the plural of kisfah, like qita‘ is the plural of qit‘ah (piece), in both weight and meaning. "Face to face" (القبيل) means the one opposite, like al-‘ashir and al-mu‘ashir. "Gold" (الزخرف) is gold. "Ascending" (الرقي) is climbing and rising.

The verses recount the miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) that the Quraysh proposed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), making their faith in him contingent upon them, while belittling the Qur’an, which is a perpetual miracle.
