The meaning: "And they said" (وَقالُوا), i.e., the Quraysh said, "We will never believe you" (لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ), O Muhammad, "until you cleave" (حَتَّى تَفْجُرَ) and split "for us from the ground" (لَنا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ) — the land of Mecca due to its scarcity of water — "a spring" (يَنْبُوعاً), a fount whose water does not dry up; "or there be" (أَوْ تَكُونَ) through a miracle "for you a garden of palms and vines, and you make rivers gush forth" (لَكَ جَنَّةٌ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَعِنَبٍ فَتُفَجِّرَ الْأَنْهارَ), i.e., split them or make them flow, "amidst them" (خِلالَها), i.e., in the middle of that garden and its intervals, "abundantly" (تَفْجِيراً); "or you cause the sky to fall, as you claimed" (أَوْ تُسْقِطَ السَّماءَ كَما زَعَمْتَ), i.e., similar to what you claimed, referring (1) to the saying of the Exalted: أَوْ نُسْقِطْ عَلَيْهِمْ كِسَفاً مِنَ السَّماءِ or drop on them fragments from the sky (Saba: 9), "upon us in fragments" (عَلَيْنا كِسَفاً) and pieces; "or you bring Allah and the angels face to face" (أَوْ تَأْتِيَ بِاللهِ وَالملائِكَةِ قَبِيلاً), opposite us so that we may view them and witness them;

(1) Thus, the verse is not devoid of an indication that Surah Saba preceded this Surah in revelation.

"or you have a house of gold" (أَوْ يَكُونَ لَكَ بَيْتٌ مِنْ زُخْرُفٍ); "or you ascend" (أَوْ تَرْقى) and climb "into the sky, and we will not believe your ascension" (فِي السَّماءِ وَلَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ) and climbing "until you bring down to us" (حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا) from it "a book that we may read" (كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ) and recite.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولاً Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human messenger?’ In this is His command to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to answer what they proposed to him and to alert them to their ignorance and obstinacy (مكابرتهم) in matters that are not hidden from any observer. They asked him for momentous things, most of which only the divine Unseen power (القدرة الغيبية الإلهية) is capable of, and among them were things essentially impossible (مستحيل بالذات), such as bringing Allah and the angels face to face. They were not satisfied with this much, nor were they content until they made him the one questioned, the one responsible for that, and the one responding to what they asked. They did not say, "We will not believe you until you ask your Lord to do such and such," but rather they said: "We will not believe you until you cleave..." etc., "or you have..." etc., "or you cause the sky to fall..." etc., "or you bring Allah..." etc., "or you have..." etc., "or you ascend into the sky, and we will not believe your ascension until you bring down to us a book that we may read."

If they wanted that from him because he is a human, then where is a human in relation to this absolute, infinite power that encompasses even the essentially impossible? And if they wanted that from him because he claims messengership (الرسالة), then messengership only necessitates carrying what Allah has burdened him with of His command and sending him to convey it through warning and glad tidings, not delegating the Unseen power to him or enabling him to create whatever he wants and bring into existence whatever they wish. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) does not claim that for himself, so their proposal of what they proposed, despite the clarity of the matter, is a strange proposal indeed.

Therefore, He commanded him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to hasten in answering them, firstly by glorifying his Lord (تنزيه ربه) from what this proposal of theirs implies of haphazardness and the delegation of power to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It is not unlikely that wonder (التعجب) can be inferred from it, as the context is suitable for that.

Secondly: to answer with his saying in the form of a question: "Am I anything but a human messenger?" This supports the view that his saying "Immaculate is my Lord" (سُبْحانَ رَبِّي) occurs in the place of wonder. That is: If you have proposed these things to me and demanded them from me as I am Muhammad, then I am only a human, and a human has no power over any of these things. And if you proposed them because I am a messenger claiming messengership, then the role of a messenger is nothing but to carry the message and convey it, not to assume absolute Unseen power.

It has become clear through this explanation that both "human" (بَشَراً) and "messenger" (رَسُولاً) are essential for the soundness of the answer to their proposal. As for his saying "human," it is to refute their proposal that he bring these signs through his own inherent power. As for his saying "messenger," it is to refute the proposal of bringing them through a power acquired from his Lord.

Some have mentioned a summary of which is: the pillar of the speech is his saying "messenger," and his saying "human" is a preamble to it, refuting their denial of the possibility of a messenger being a human, and indicating that the messengers before him were likewise. The meaning according to this would be: Am I anything but a human messenger like the other messengers? They did not bring anything except what He made to flow at their hands, without it being delegated to them or them dictating anything to their Lord.

He said: Making "human" and "messenger" both pillars of the speech contradicts what appears from the reports (الآثار). Firstly, what has been narrated in the reports is that they asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to ask his Lord to do such and such; they did not ask him to bring anything from himself such that it would be refuted by affirming his humanity. Secondly, it necessitates that "messenger" be a second predicate (خبر بعد خبر), and the sound taste (الذوق السليم) would reject them being two predicates. [End of summary].

The response to this is, firstly: taking his saying "human" as a refutation of their claim that it is impermissible for a messenger to be a human—when the verses do not contain this claim either explicitly or implicitly—is an imposition without evidence.

Secondly: what he mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse—"Am I anything but a human messenger like the other messengers, and they did not bring except such and such"—the pillar of the speech in it is the comparison found in his saying "like the other messengers," not his saying "messenger." Deleting the pillar of the speech corrupts the context; so understand that.

Thirdly: the fact that the reports state they only asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to ask his Lord to bring those signs, without asking him himself to bring them, does not contradict the text of the Book which states otherwise. What Allah recounted from them is that they said: "We will not believe you until you cleave for us..." etc., "then you make rivers gush forth..." etc., "or you cause the sky to fall..." etc. This is a strange exaggeration regarding the reports, making them judge over the Book of Allah and giving them precedence over it even in cases of contradiction.

Fourthly: the rejection by "sound taste" of the permissibility of "messenger" being a second predicate has no apparent basis.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما مَنَعَ النَّاسَ أَنْ يُؤْمِنُوا إِذْ جاءَهُمُ الْهُدى إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا أَبَعَثَ اللهُ بَشَراً رَسُولاً Nothing prevented the people from believing when guidance came to them but that they said, ‘Has Allah sent a human as messenger?’

The question in His saying "Has Allah sent a human as messenger?" is for denial (الإنكار). The sentence "they said, 'Has Allah sent...'" etc., is a recounting of their state according to their belief, even if they did not utter these exact words.

The denial of prophethood (النبوة) and messengership (الرسالة) while affirming the Deity is among the beliefs of idolatry (الوثنية). This is a context-clue that what is meant by "the people" is the idolaters, and the "faith" (الإيمان) they were prevented from is faith in the messenger.

So the meaning of the verse is: Nothing prevented the idolaters—and the Quraysh and the general Arabs were among them then—from believing in the messengership—or your messengership—except their denial of the messengership of a human. Therefore, they used to reject the call of their messengers—as Allah recounted—with the like of their saying: لَوْ شاءَ رَبُّنا لَأَنْزَلَ مَلائِكَةً فَإِنَّا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ كافِرُونَ ‘Had our Lord wished, He would have sent down angels. So we indeed disbelieve in what you have been sent with.’ (Fussilat: 14).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَوْ كانَ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَلائِكَةٌ يَمْشُونَ مُطْمَئِنِّينَ لَنَزَّلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ مَلَكاً رَسُولاً Say, ‘Had there been angels walking at peace on the earth, We would have surely sent down an angel to them from the sky as messenger.’ The Glorified commanded His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to refute their saying and their denial of the messengership of a human and the descent of revelation (الوحي) by stating that divine care (العناية الإلهية) has been attached to the guidance of the people of the earth, and that does not happen except through a celestial revelation, not from themselves. Thus, the humans residing on the earth have no choice but to receive a celestial revelation through the descent of an angel messenger to them, and their prophet is singled out for that.

This is a characteristic of earthly life and material living, which stands in need of divine guidance (هداية إلهية) to which there is no path except by the descent of revelation from the sky. Even if a group of angels inhabited the earth and took up an earthly, material life, We would have sent down to them from the sky an angel messenger, just as We send down to humans an angel messenger.

The concern in the noble verse—as you see—is attached to two aspects: one is the life being earthly and material, and the other is that the guidance necessitated by divine care must be through a revelation descending from the sky via the messengership of one of the angels.

The matter being so, these two aspects—namely, the life of the species being earthly and material, and the necessity of their guidance through a celestial intermediary and a high angel—are the two primary premises in the proof (البرهان) for the existence of messengership and its necessity.

As for what the commentators insisted upon regarding restricting the meaning of the verse to the necessity of the messenger being of the same genus as those to whom he is sent—such as a human for humans and an angel for angels—it is not of such importance. Therefore, it was not explicitly stated in the noble verse.

That is because the messenger to humans—who is the one who teaches them and nurtures them—being from among themselves is a necessary consequence of their life being earthly and the revelation descending upon them being by means of a celestial angel. The variation among material individuals of the species in terms of felicity and wretchedness, perfection and deficiency, and purity or impurity of the inner self is necessary. The angel delivering the revelation and what he carries of it is pure and wholesome; "none touch it except the purified." Thus, the angel descending with revelation, though he descends to the species, none touch him except individuals among them who are purified from the impurities and stains of matter, sanctified from the touch of Satan; and they are the messengers (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

To clarify the position: the implication of divine care is the guidance of every species among the species of creation to its perfection and felicity. Man, who is one of these species, is not an exception to this universal rule. His felicity in life is not complete unless he lives a social life governed by laws and traditions (سنن) that guarantee the felicity of his life in this world and after it, and remove the necessary differences arising between individuals. Since his life is a conscious life, he must be equipped with that by which he receives these laws and traditions. What he is equipped with of the intellect (العقل) that distinguishes between his good and evil is not sufficient, for the intellect itself leads him to difference. Thus, he must be equipped with another consciousness (شعور) by which he receives what Allah pours forth of the gnoses (المعارف) and laws that remove difference and guarantee his felicity and perfection; and that is the consciousness of revelation (شعور الوحي), and the human characterized by it is the prophet.

This is a complete rational proof (برهان عقلي) taken from His speech. We have presented it and detailed the discourse on it in the discussions on Prophethood in the second volume and within the stories of Noah in the tenth volume of the book.

As for the verse we are currently considering—namely His saying: "Say, ‘Had there been angels walking at peace on the earth...’" etc.—it adds something to the aforementioned meaning of the proof: that the delivery of revelation to humans must be by the descent of an angel from the sky to them.

This is because the summary of the content of the verse and what preceded it is that what prevents people from believing in your messengership is that they deem the messengership of a human from the side of Allah, the Glorified, to be impossible. They have erred in that, for the requirement of earthly life and Allah's care for the guidance of His servants is that He send down to some of them an angel from the sky as a messenger. Even if the angels were like humans living on the earth, Allah would have sent down to some of them—who would be their messenger—an angel from the sky as a messenger carrying His revelation.

This, as you see, provides firstly: the meaning of human messengership, which is that the messenger is a human upon whom an angel descends from the sky with the religion of Allah, and then he conveys it to the people by Allah's command.

And it points secondly: to the proof of messengership, that the earthly life of man and the Lordly care attached to the guidance of His servants and bringing them to their goals cannot do without the descent of a celestial religion upon them. The angels are the intermediaries for the descent of celestial blessings to the earth; thus, the religion necessarily descends upon people by means of the angel, which is his messengership. The one who witnesses it and receives what he descends with—and it can only be some people, not all of them, due to the need for inner purity and a spirit from the command of Allah—is the human messenger.

It was expected from the context that it would be said: "Allah would have raised among them an angel messenger," corresponding to their saying recounted in the previous verse: "Has Allah sent a human as messenger?" However, it shifted to the like of His saying: "We would have surely sent down an angel to them from the sky as messenger" to be, firstly, more decisive in resolving the doubt and cutting off the illusion. For the general idolaters among the Brahmins, Buddhists, and Sabaeans—as witnessed by what is in their sacred books—do not shy away from prophethood in the sense of the raising of a perfect human for the perfection of people; they express it as the appearance of the Savior or the Reformer, and the descent of the Deity to the earth and His appearance to its people in the form of an earthly being. Buddha and Yuzasaf—according to what is said—were among them. The worshipped one for them, in any case, is the angel, the jinn, or the human absorbed in Him, rather than Allah, the Glorified.

They only completely refuse the messengership of an angel—who is one of the worshipped deities for them—to a human with a religion in which Allah alone is worshipped, while He is a Deity not worshipped by them. Thus, in the explicit mention of the messengership of the celestial angel to the earthly human from Allah, there is a text of complete opposition to them.

And it is, secondly, to indicate that the messengership of the angel is in reality to the general human, except that the one who is fit to receive revelation from him is the messenger from among them. As for others, they are deprived of that due to their lack of readiness for it. Thus, the effusion (الفيض) is general, even if the recipient of the effusion (المستفيض) is specific. The Exalted said: وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً and the bounty of your Lord is not forbidden [to anyone] (Al-Isra: 20), and He said: قالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ حَتَّى نُؤْتى مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ رُسُلُ اللهِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسالَتَهُ They say, ‘We will not believe until we are given the like of what was given to Allah’s apostles.’ Allah knows best where to place His apostleship. (Al-An‘am: 124).

The verse, with the meaning of messengership it provides, supports what has been narrated from the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them) regarding the difference between the Messenger (الرسول) and the Prophet (النبي): that the Messenger is the one who sees the angel and hears from him, while the Prophet sees in a dream and does not witness [the angel] directly. We have presented some of these reports during the discussions on Prophethood in the second volume of the book.

Among the most elegant and concise expressions in the verse is His expression of earthly life by His saying: "on the earth, angels walking at peace" (فِي الْأَرْضِ مَلائِكَةٌ يَمْشُونَ مُطْمَئِنِّينَ). For spatial movement on the earth while being under the influence of earthly gravity is among the clearest characteristics of earthly material life.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ كَفى بِاللهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ بِعِبادِهِ خَبِيراً بَصِيراً Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you. Indeed He is all-knowing and all-seeing of His servants.’ When he had argued against them with what he argued, and explained to them what he explained regarding the matter of the miracle of his messengership—which is the Qur’an with which he challenged them—while they, in their obstinacy, denial, and stubbornness, pay no heed to it and propose other haphazard things to him, respecting no truth and desisting from no falsehood, He commanded that the matter be returned to the testimony of Allah. For He is a witness to what occurred from him and from them; he has conveyed what he was sent with, called, argued, and left no excuse. They have heard, the proof has been completed against them, and they have waxed proud and rebelled. Thus, the speech is in the sense of announcing the cessation of argumentation, leaving the dispute, and returning the matter to the Owner of the matter, so let Him judge what He will judge.

It is said that what is meant by the verse is seeking testimony from Allah, the Glorified, for the truth of the call and the correctness of the messengership. It is as if he says: It is sufficient proof for me that Allah is a witness to my messengership; this is His speech explicitly stating that. If you say: It is not His speech but rather something you have fabricated, then bring the like of it—and you will never bring the like of it even if the two heavy groups (humans and jinn) were your helpers and supporters aiding you.

This is good in itself, except that the end of the verse, as has been said, does not suit it—namely His saying: "between me and you" and His saying: "Indeed He is all-knowing and all-seeing of His servants." Rather, it would have been closer to say: "as a witness for me against you" or "to my messengership" or the like.

This verse and the two verses before it are rhymed with the word "messenger" (رَسُولاً). This is the only instance in the Qur’an where three consecutive verses agree in a single rhyme, as we shall mention.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَهْدِ اللهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِ وَمَنْ يُضْلِلْ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُمْ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِهِ Whomever Allah guides is the rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—you will never find for them any guardians besides Him. — etc. It is—as the context suggests—from the completion of the final address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with His saying: "Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you.’" It is an allusion to the fact that the proof has been completed against them and misguidance (الضلالة) has become due for them, so there is no hope for their guidance.

The summary of the meaning: Address them by announcing the cessation of argumentation, for guidance (الهداية) belongs to Allah the Exalted; no one shares it with Him. So whomever He guides is the guided one, none other; and whomever He leads astray and does not guide, you will never find for him, O Muhammad, any guardians besides Him to guide them. Allah does not guide these people, so turn away from them and do not burden yourself in calling them in the hope that they might believe.

From here it becomes clear that the saying of some—that the verse is a new sentence not included in the scope of "Say"—is misplaced.

He used "guardians" (أولياء) in the plural form, even though the singular would be more eloquent and comprehensive, to indicate that if there were a guardian for them besides Allah, it would be either their deities—which are many—or other cosmic causes—which are also many.

In His saying: "Whomever Allah guides is the rightly guided" (وَمَنْ يَهْدِ اللهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِ) etc., there is a shift (التفات) from the first-person plural to the third-person singular. The context was that of the first-person plural. Perhaps the reason for this is that if it were said: "Whomever We guide and whomever We lead astray" in the first-person plural, it might suggest the participation of the angels in the matter of guidance and misguidance, thus suggesting a contradiction in His saying: "you will never find for them any guardians besides Him," for the guardians according to them are the angels, and they take them as deities and worship them.

His saying: وَنَحْشُرُهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عَلى وُجُوهِهِمْ and We shall muster them on the Day of Resurrection, [fallen] on their faces, to the end of the two verses. "The blind" (العمى), "the dumb" (البكم), and "the deaf" (الصم) are the plurals of a‘ma, abkam, and asamm. The "subsiding" (خبو) of the fire is the settling of its flame. "The Blaze" (السعير) is the flame of the fire. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللهَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ قادِرٌ عَلى أَنْ يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُمْ Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth, is able to create the like of them? to the end of the verse. "Ingratitude" (الكفور) is denial. It is an argument from Him, the Exalted, for the Resurrection (البعث) after death. Their saying: أَإِذا كُنَّا عِظاماً وَرُفاتاً أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقاً جَدِيداً ‘What, when we have become bones and decayed parts, shall we really be raised as a new creation?’ was an expression of deeming it remote, based on the impossibility of this worldly body returning after its disintegration and becoming bones and decayed parts to what it was through a new creation. Thus, He argued against them that the creation of the body initially proves the power over it and over its like, which is the new creation for the Resurrection; for the ruling of likes is one.

The similarity (المماثلة) is only from the perspective of comparing the new body with the first body, setting aside the soul (النفس) which is the preserver of the unity and personality of the human being. This does not contradict the fact that the person in the Hereafter is the very same person as in this world, not a "like," because the criterion of unity and personality is the human soul, and it is preserved with Allah the Glorified, not perishing or becoming non-existent. When it attaches to the newly created body, it is the same worldly human, just as the human in this world is a single personal entity remaining in his personal unity despite the change of the body with all its parts time after time.

The evidence that the soul, which is the reality of the human, is preserved with Allah despite the scattering of the body's parts and the corruption of its form, is the saying of the Exalted: وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we be in a new creation?’ Indeed, they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you.’ (As-Sajdah: 10-11). Here they raised a problem regarding the Return (المعاد) that it is a renewal of creation after the annihilation of the human through the scattering of his body's parts. The answer was that the angel of death takes the human and receives him fully and completely; thus he is not lost nor does he disintegrate. What is lost is only his body, and there is no harm in that, for Allah will renew it.

The evidence that the resurrected human is the very same worldly human, not a "like," is all the verses of the Resurrection indicating the return of the human to Him, the Exalted, his being raised, questioned, accounted, and recompensed for what he did.

All of this witnesses that the meaning of similarity is what we have mentioned. He only addressed the matter of the body—leading to the mention of similarity—to correspond to the core of the problem they raised in their saying: "What, when we have become bones and decayed parts, shall we really be raised as a new creation?" They did not include in their saying anything but the states of the body, not the soul taken from it. When the soul is set aside, the body is a "like" of the body, even if it is the very same when the soul is considered.

Some have mentioned: What is meant by "the like of them" is themselves; it is of the sort of their saying: "The like of you does not do this," meaning "You do not do it." There is room for discussion here. It appears that the concern in this construction is that "the like of you," due to possessing the like of the attribute found in you, does not do this; so you do not do it because of your attribute. In it is the negation of the act by negating its cause by way of allusion (كناية), and it is more emphatic than our saying: "You do not do it."

And His saying: وَجَعَلَ لَهُمْ أَجَلاً لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ and He has appointed for them a term in which there is no doubt. It appears that what is meant by the "term" (الأجل) is the time of death. For the term is either the sum total of the duration of worldly life—which is limited by death—or the final time of life, which is coincided by death. In any case, the reminder of death, in which there is no doubt, is so that they may take heed by it and desist from audacity against Allah and denying His signs; for He is able to resurrect them and take vengeance upon them for what they have done.

So His saying: "and He has appointed for them a term in which there is no doubt" looks toward His saying at the beginning of the previous verse: "That is their recompense because they disbelieved in Our signs." It is similar to His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — until He said — أَوَلَمْ يَنْظُرُوا فِي مَلَكُوتِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — until He said — وَأَنْ عَسى أَنْ يَكُونَ قَدِ اقْتَرَبَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَهُ يُؤْمِنُونَ As for those who deny Our signs, We will draw them on [to ruin] gradually, from whence they do not know... Have they not contemplated the dominion of the heavens and the earth... and that maybe their term has already drawn near? So in what discourse will they believe after this? (Al-A‘raf: 182-185).

Some have allowed that what is meant by the "term" is the Day of Resurrection. This does not suit the context, for the preceding speech recounts their denial of the Resurrection and then argues against them with [divine] power; thus, it is not appropriate to take the Resurrection as a given in which there is no doubt.

Similar to this is the assertion of some that His saying: "and He has appointed for them a term in which there is no doubt" is another argument set forth to prove the Day of Resurrection, according to both interpretations of the term being the day of death or the Day of Resurrection. This is an affectation that yields no benefit at all, so there is no reason to be occupied with it.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَوْ أَنْتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزائِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّي إِذاً لَأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ الْإِنْفاقِ وَكانَ الْإِنْسانُ قَتُوراً Say, ‘If you possessed the treasuries of my Lord’s mercy, you would surely hold them back for fear of spending, and man is very tightfisted.’ Al-qatur is interpreted as the miser who is extreme in withholding. It is said in al-Majma‘: al-qatr is constriction, and al-qatur is the intensive form from it. It is said: qatara, yaqturu, taqattara, aqtara, and qattara when one is stingy in spending. [End].

This is a rebuke to them for their prevention of the messengership of a human, which was previously reported from them by His saying: "Nothing prevented the people from believing when guidance came to them but that they said, ‘Has Allah sent a human as messenger?’" The meaning of the verse is clear.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Mas‘adah ibn Sadaqah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Healing is only in the knowledge of the Qur’an, because of His saying: ما هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (that which is a healing and a mercy for the believers)." [The tradition].

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Sufyan ibn ‘Uyaynah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): He said: "Intention (النية) is better than action; indeed, intention is the action." Then he recited the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلَتِهِ (Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character’), meaning according to his intention.

I say: His saying "intention is the action" points to their unity—the unity of the title (العنوان) and that which is titled (معنونه).

In it, with its chain of narration to Abu Hashim, who said: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "The people of the Fire will only abide forever in the Fire because their intentions in the world were that if they were to live forever in it, they would disobey Allah forever. And the people of Paradise will only abide forever in Paradise because their intentions in the world were that if they were to remain in it, they would obey Allah forever. So by intentions, these and those abide forever." Then he recited the saying of the Exalted: قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلَتِهِ (Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character’). He said: "According to his intention."

I say: This is an allusion to the rootedness of the dispositions (الملكات) such that the readiness for their opposite is nullified in the soul. Al-Ayyashi also narrated the tradition in his Tafsir from Abu Hashim from him (peace be upon him).

In ad-Durr al-Manthur: regarding the saying of the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ (They ask you about the Spirit): Ahmad, at-Tirmidhi (who graded it authentic), an-Nasa’i, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Hibban, Abu ash-Shaykh in al-‘Azamah, al-Hakim (who graded it authentic), Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym, and al-Bayhaqi (both in ad-Dala’il) narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: The Quraysh said to the Jews: "Give us something to ask this man." They said: "Ask him about the Spirit." So they asked him, and there was revealed: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي ـ وَما أُوتِيتُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً (They ask you about the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command, and you have not been given of knowledge except a little’). They said: "We have been given much knowledge; we have been given the Torah—and whoever is given the Torah has been given much good." So Allah revealed: قُلْ لَوْ كانَ الْبَحْرُ مِداداً لِكَلِماتِ رَبِّي ـ لَنَفِدَ الْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَنْ تَنْفَدَ كَلِماتُ رَبِّي وَلَوْ جِئْنا بِمِثْلِهِ مَدَداً (Say, ‘If the sea were ink for the words of my Lord, the sea would surely be spent before the words of my Lord are spent, even if We brought another like it for replenishment’).

I say: It has been narrated through other paths from ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud and from ‘Abdur-Rahman ibn ‘Abdillah ibn Umm al-Hakam: that the question was only from the Jews in Medina—and the verse was revealed there. However, the fact that the Surah is Meccan and the unity of the context of its verses does not suit that.

In it, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn al-Anbari in Kitab al-Addad, Abu ash-Shaykh in al-‘Azamah, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma’ wa as-Sifat narrated from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib: regarding the saying: وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ (And they ask you about the Spirit). He said: "It is an angel from among the angels who has seventy thousand faces—for each face there are seventy thousand tongues—for each tongue there are seventy thousand languages—he glorifies Allah the Exalted with all those languages. Allah the Exalted creates from every glorification an angel who flies with the angels until the Day of Resurrection."

I say: The Spirit being from the angels does not agree with the apparent sense of several verses of the Book, such as His saying: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ (He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command) (An-Nahl: 2) and other verses. The tradition of ‘Ali (peace be upon him) has already preceded under the saying of the Exalted: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ in Surah an-Nahl, in which is his denial that the Spirit is an angel and his argument for that using the verse. Thus, the consideration in the matter of the Spirit is what follows.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Abu Basir, who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي (They ask you about the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’). He said: "A creation greater than Gabriel and Michael; it was with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and it is with the Imams—and it is from the Realm of Sovereignty (الملكوت) — the celestial and spiritual world of divine authority."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, and the narration agrees with what has been previously clarified of the significance of the verses.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah and Humran from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them): regarding the saying: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ (They ask you about the Spirit). He said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, is One, Eternal (صمد)—and the Eternal is the thing that has no cavity (جوف). The Spirit is but a creation from His creation—it has sight, power, and support; He places it in the hearts of the messengers and the believers."

I say: He only addressed what he addressed at the beginning of the narration to refute what might be imagined from the like of His saying, the Exalted: وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي (and breathed into him of My Spirit), that there is a cavity and a breathed soul.

In it, from Abu Basir from one of the two (peace be upon them), who said: I asked him about His saying: وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي (And they ask you about the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’). What is the Spirit? He said: "That which is in the beasts and the people." I said: "And what is it?" He said: "From the Realm of Sovereignty (الملكوت), from the Power (القدرة)."

I say: These narrations support what preceded in the explanation of the verse: that the Spirit asked about is a vast reality possessing different levels. Also, the apparent sense of this narration is that the animal spirit is immaterial (مجرد), from the Realm of Sovereignty.

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas: that ‘Utbah and Shaybah, the two sons of Rabi‘ah, Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, a man from the Banu ‘Abd ad-Dar, Abu al-Bakhtari the brother of Banu Asad, al-Aswad ibn al-Muttalib, Rabi‘ah ibn al-Aswad, al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, Abu Jahl ibn Hisham, ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah, Umayyah ibn Khalaf, al-‘As ibn Wa’il, and Nabih and Munabbih, the two sons of al-Hajjaj as-Sahmi, gathered after sunset at the back of the Kaaba. Some of them said to others: "Send for Muhammad and speak to him and argue with him until you have left no excuse regarding him."

So they sent to him saying that the nobles of your people have gathered for you to speak to you. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came to them quickly, thinking that a new idea had occurred to them regarding his matter. He was solicitous for them, loving their guidance, and their stubbornness was hard upon him, until he sat with them.

They said: "O Muhammad, we have sent for you to leave no excuse for you. By Allah, we do not know of any man among the Arabs who has brought upon his people what you have brought upon your people. You have reviled the forefathers, criticized the religion, belittled the intellects, reviled the deities, and divided the community. There remains no ugly thing but that you have brought it between us and you. If you have only brought this discourse seeking wealth, we will gather for you from our wealth until you are the wealthiest of us. If you seek honor among us, we will make you our leader. If you want kingship, we will make you king over us. And if this which comes to you is a vision (رئي) that you see which has overcome you"—and they used to call the follower from the jinn a ri’y—"perhaps we will spend our wealth in seeking medicine until we cure you of it and leave no excuse regarding you."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "What you say is not in me. I have not brought you what I have brought you seeking your wealth, nor your property, nor kingship over you. But Allah has raised me to you as a messenger, sent down a book to me, and commanded me to be a bearer of glad tidings and a warner to you. So I have conveyed to you the message of my Lord and advised you. If you accept from me what I have brought you, it is your portion in this world and the Hereafter. And if you reject it, I will be patient for the command of Allah until Allah judges between me and you."

They said: "O Muhammad, if you are not accepting from us what we have offered you, then you know that there is no one among the people whose land is more constricted, whose wealth is less, or whose life is harder than ours. So ask your Lord who raised you with what He raised you with to move away from us these mountains that have constricted us, to expand our land for us, and to make rivers flow in it like the rivers of Syria and Iraq. And let Him raise for us those of our forefathers who have passed away, and let Qusay ibn Kilab be among those He raises for us, for he was a truthful elder; then we will ask them about what you say: is it truth or falsehood? If you do what we have asked you and they believe you, we will believe you and recognize by it your station with Allah and that He raised you as a messenger."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I was not raised for this. I have only come to you from Allah with what He raised me with. I have conveyed to you what I was sent with to you. If you accept it, it is your portion in this world and the Hereafter; and if you reject it, I will be patient for the command of Allah until Allah judges between me and you."

They said: "If you do not do this for us, then take for yourself: ask your Lord to send an angel to believe you in what you say and to argue back to us for you. And ask Him to make for you gardens, treasures, and palaces of gold and silver, and to enrich you by them from what we see you seeking; for you stand in the markets and seek a livelihood as we seek it, so that we may know your station from your Lord, if you are a messenger as you claim."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I am not doing that. I am not one who asks his Lord for this. I was not raised to you for this, but Allah raised me as a bearer of glad tidings and a warner. If you accept what I have brought you, it is your portion in this world and the Hereafter; and if you reject it, I will be patient for the command of Allah until Allah judges between me and you."

They said: "Then cause the sky to fall as you claimed that your Lord, if He wills, would do. For we will never believe you unless you do it." The Messenger of Allah said: "That is for Allah; if He wills, He will do that to you."

They said: "O Muhammad, your Lord knew that we would sit with you and ask you about what we asked you and demand from you what we demand. So He should have come forward to you and taught you what to argue back to us with, and informed you of what He is doing regarding that with us if we do not accept from you what you brought us. It has reached us that a man in Yamamah called ar-Rahman is the one who teaches you this. By Allah, we will never believe in ar-Rahman. We have left no excuse for you, O Muhammad. By Allah, we will not leave you and what you have done to us until we destroy you or you destroy us." And one of them said: "We will never believe you until you bring Allah and the angels face to face."

When they said that, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stood up from them, and ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah stood with them and said: "O Muhammad, your people offered you what they offered and you did not accept it from them. Then they asked you for things for themselves so that they might know your station with Allah, and you did not do that. Then they asked you to hasten what you threaten them with of punishment. By Allah, I will never believe in you until you take a ladder to the sky, then ascend it while I am watching until you reach it and bring with you a spread-out copy, with four angels with you witnessing for you that you are as you say. By Allah, even if you did that, I think I would not believe you."

Then he turned away from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) returned to his family sad and regretful for what he had missed of what he had hoped for from his people when they called him, and for what he saw of their following him. And there was revealed to him regarding what ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah said to him: وَقالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ — until His saying — بَشَراً رَسُولاً. [The tradition].

I say: What is mentioned in the narration regarding their dialogue with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and their questions does not correspond to the apparent sense of the verses, nor to the answer contained in them according to its apparent sense. This has been previously alluded to in the explanation of the verses.

The narration has been repeated through the paths of the Shi‘a and the Sunnis that the one who addressed the speech to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from among the group and asked him these questions was ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah al-Makhzumi, the brother of Umm Salamah, the wife of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In ad-Durr al-Manthur: regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَنَحْشُرُهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عَلى وُجُوهِهِمْ (and We shall muster them on the Day of Resurrection, [fallen] on their faces): Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, an-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim, Abu Nu‘aym in al-Ma‘rifah, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma’ wa as-Sifat narrated from Anas, who said: It was said: "O Messenger of Allah, how will the people be mustered on their faces?" He said: "The One who made them walk on their feet is able to make them walk on their faces."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ تِسْعَ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۖ فَسْـَٔلْ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِذْ جَآءَهُمْ فَقَالَ لَهُۥ فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّى لَأَظُنُّكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ مَسْحُورًا (١٠١)
Certainly We gave Moses nine manifest signs. So ask the Children of Israel. When he came to them, Pharaoh said to him, ‘O Moses, indeed I think you are bewitched.’ (101)
قَالَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَآ أَنزَلَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ بَصَآئِرَ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّكَ يَـٰفِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُورًا (١٠٢)
He said, ‘You certainly know that no one has sent these [signs] except the Lord of the heavens and the earth, as eye-openers, and I, O Pharaoh, indeed think you are doomed.’ (102)
فَأَرَادَ أَن يَسْتَفِزَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥ جَمِيعًا (١٠٣)
He desired to exterminate them from the land, so We drowned him and all those who were with him. (103)
وَقُلْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ لِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱسْكُنُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ جِئْنَا بِكُمْ لَفِيفًا (١٠٤)
After him We said to the Children of Israel, ‘Take up residence in the land, and when the occasion of the other [promise] comes, We shall gather you in mixed company.’ (104)
وَبِٱلْحَقِّ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ وَبِٱلْحَقِّ نَزَلَ ۗ وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا (١٠٥)
With the truth did We send it down, and with the truth did it descend, and We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and as a warner. (105)
وَقُرْءَانًا فَرَقْنَـٰهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْنَـٰهُ تَنزِيلًا (١٠٦)
We have sent the Quran in [discrete] parts so that you may recite it for the people a little at a time, and We have sent it down piecemeal. (106)
قُلْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَوْ لَا تُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦٓ إِذَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ سُجَّدًا (١٠٧)
Say, ‘Whether you believe in it, or do not believe in it, indeed when it is recited to those who were given knowledge before it, they fall down in prostration on their faces, (107)
وَيَقُولُونَ سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّنَآ إِن كَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّنَا لَمَفْعُولًا (١٠٨)
and say, ‘‘Immaculate is our Lord! Indeed Our Lord’s promise is bound to be fulfilled.’’ (108)
وَيَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ يَبْكُونَ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ خُشُوعًا ۩ (١٠٩)
Weeping, they fall down on their faces, and it increases them in humility.’ (109)
قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ أَوِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ ۖ أَيًّا مَّا تَدْعُوا۟ فَلَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَلَا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلَاتِكَ وَلَا تُخَافِتْ بِهَا وَٱبْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا (١١٠)
Say, ‘Invoke ‘‘Allah’’ or invoke ‘‘the All-beneficent.’’ Whichever [of His Names] you may invoke, to Him belong the Best Names.’ Be neither loud in your prayer, nor murmur it, but follow a middle course between these, (110)
وَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ شَرِيكٌ فِى ٱلْمُلْكِ وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلِىٌّ مِّنَ ٱلذُّلِّ ۖ وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيرًۢا (١١١)
and say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has neither taken any son, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, nor has He any wali out of weakness,’ and magnify Him with a magnification [worthy of Him]. (111)

(Commentary)

In these verses, there is a drawing of a parallel between the miracle of prophethood brought by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—which is the Quran—and the polytheists' (المشركين) turning away from it and their arbitrary proposal of other signs (آيات), and that which Moses (عليه‌السلام) brought of the signs of prophethood and Pharaoh’s turning away from them, accusing him of being bewitched (مسحور). Then, the discourse returns to describing the Quran, the reason for its gradual revelation in separate parts, and the divine knowledge (المعارف) associated with it.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسى تِسْعَ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ فَسْئَلْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِذْ جاءَهُمْ فَقالَ لَهُ فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا مُوسى مَسْحُوراً» — Certainly We gave Moses nine manifest signs. So ask the Children of Israel when he came to them, and Pharaoh said to him, ‘O Moses, I indeed think you are bewitched.’ (17:101). The signs given to Moses (عليه‌السلام) according to what the Quran narrates are more than nine; however, the signs he brought specifically for the mission to Pharaoh, as mentioned in the Quran, are nine. They are: the staff (العصا), the hand (اليد), the flood (الطوفان), the locusts (الجراد), the lice (القمل), the frogs (الضفدع), the blood (الدم), the years of drought (السنون), and the scarcity of fruits (نقص من الثمرات). It is apparent that these are what is intended by the "nine signs" mentioned in the verse, especially given what is narrated of Moses' speech to Pharaoh: «لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ ما أَنْزَلَ هؤُلاءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بَصائِرَ» — He said, ‘You certainly know that no one has sent these down except the Lord of the heavens and the earth as eye-openers.’ (17:102). As for signs other than these—such as the parting of the sea (البحر), the rock (الحجر), the reviving of the murdered man by the cow (إحياء المقتول بالبقرة), the reviving of those struck by the thunderbolt (الصاعقة) from his people, the raising of the mountain (نتق الجبل) above them, and others—they fall outside of these nine mentioned in the verse.

This is not contradicted by the fact that these signs appeared gradually, for this dialogue is extracted from the sum total of the disputation between Moses and Pharaoh throughout the duration of his mission.

Therefore, no weight is given to what some exegetes have mentioned in opposition to what we have enumerated, as there is no evidence for it. In the Torah, it is mentioned that the nine are: the staff, the blood, the frogs, the lice, the death of livestock, hail like fire sent down with a blazing fire that destroyed the plants and animals it passed, the locusts, the darkness, and the death of the firstborn of humans and all animals.

Perhaps the discrepancy between the Torah and the apparent meaning of the Quran regarding the nine signs is what necessitated the omission of the detailed list of the nine signs in the verse, so that the command to ask the Jews would remain valid; for despite the explicit contradiction, they would not have believed the Quran, but would have hastened to denial (التكذيب) before affirmation (التصديق).

And His saying: «إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا مُوسى مَسْحُوراً» — ‘I indeed think you are bewitched’ means: you have been affected by sorcery so your intellect has become disordered. This is similar in meaning to his saying narrated in another place: «إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ الَّذِي أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ» — ‘Indeed your apostle who has been sent to you is surely crazy.’ (26:27). It has also been said that "bewitched" (المسحور) means the sorcerer (الساحر), similar to al-maymun (الميمون) and al-mash’um (المشئوم) meaning the one who brings good or bad fortune; its origin is the use of the passive participle form for attribution. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ ما أَنْزَلَ هؤُلاءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بَصائِرَ وَإِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا فِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُوراً» — He said, ‘You certainly know that no one has sent these down except the Lord of the heavens and the earth as eye-openers, and I indeed think you are doomed, O Pharaoh.’ (17:102). The doomed (المثبور) is the one who is destroyed, derived from al-thubur (الثبور) meaning destruction (الهلاك). The meaning is: Moses said, addressing Pharaoh: You certainly know, O Pharaoh, that no one sent down these manifest signs except the Lord of the heavens and the earth; He sent them as eye-openers (بَصائِرَ) — clear proofs by which one gains insight to distinguish truth from falsehood — and I indeed think you, O Pharaoh, are doomed to destruction in the Hereafter due to your obstinacy and denial.

He used the word "think" (الظن) rather than "certainty" (اليقين) because judgment belongs to Allah, and to correspond with Pharaoh's words: «إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا مُوسى» — ‘I indeed think you, O Moses...’ etc. Furthermore, "thinking" (الظن) is sometimes used in contexts of certainty.

His saying, the Exalted: «فَأَرادَ أَنْ يَسْتَفِزَّهُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ فَأَغْرَقْناهُ وَمَنْ مَعَهُ جَمِيعاً» — So he desired to unsettle them from the land, but We drowned him and those who were with him, all together. (17:103). Unsettling (الاستفزاز) is to disturb and expel with violence. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقُلْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ اسْكُنُوا الْأَرْضَ فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ جِئْنا بِكُمْ لَفِيفاً» — After him We said to the Children of Israel, ‘Settle in the land, and when the promise of the Hereafter comes to pass, We will bring you as a mixed crowd.’ (17:104). The "land" they were commanded to settle in is the Holy Land (الأرض المقدسة) which Allah had ordained for them, as evidenced by His saying: «ادْخُلُوا الْأَرْضَ الْمُقَدَّسَةَ الَّتِي كَتَبَ اللهُ لَكُمْ» — ‘Enter the Holy Land which Allah has ordained for you’ (5:21), and other verses. Similarly, the "land" in the previous verse refers to the land in general or the land of Egypt, as evidenced by the context (السياق).

And His saying: «فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ» — ‘and when the promise of the Hereafter comes to pass’ means the promise of the second turn or the life of the Hereafter. According to what the exegetes have mentioned, it refers to the Day of Resurrection. And His saying: «جِئْنا بِكُمْ لَفِيفاً» — ‘We will bring you as a mixed crowd’ means gathered together, entwined with one another.

The meaning is: We said to the Children of Israel after the drowning of Pharaoh: Settle in the Holy Land—whereas Pharaoh had wanted to unsettle them from the land—and when the Day of Resurrection arrives, We shall bring you all together, entwined, for the reckoning and the final judgment.

It is not far-fetched that the "promise of the Hereafter" (وعد الآخرة) refers to what Allah, the Glorified, mentioned at the beginning of the Surah regarding His decree to the Children of Israel: «فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ لِيَسُوؤُا وُجُوهَكُمْ وَلِيَدْخُلُوا الْمَسْجِدَ كَما دَخَلُوهُ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَلِيُتَبِّرُوا ما عَلَوْا تَتْبِيراً» — ‘So when the second promise came to pass, [We sent them] to sadden your faces and to enter the Temple just as they had entered it the first time, and to destroy utterly whatever they conquered.’ (17:7). Although the majority of exegetes did not mention this, it would link the conclusion of the Surah back to its beginning. The meaning would then be: We commanded them after the drowning of Pharaoh to settle in the Holy Land from which Pharaoh was preventing you, and remain therein until the second promise comes, in which affliction shall envelop you through killing, captivity, and exile; then We shall gather you from it and bring you as a mixed crowd—referring to their captivity and exile to Babylon.

On this interpretation, the subtle point (النكتة) of branching His saying: «فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ» etc., from His saying: «وَقُلْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ اسْكُنُوا الْأَرْضَ» becomes clear, unlike the previous interpretation where no obvious subtle point results from this branching.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَبِالْحَقِّ أَنْزَلْناهُ وَبِالْحَقِّ نَزَلَ وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّراً وَنَذِيراً» — With the truth have We sent it down, and with the truth has it descended, and We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and as a warner. (17:105). Having finished the parallel, He returns to explaining the state of the Quran and mentioning its attributes. He mentions that He sent it down in a manner accompanied by the truth (الحق), and it descended from Him in a manner accompanied by the truth. Thus, it is protected from falsehood (الباطل) from the direction of the One who sent it down; it is not idle or nonsensical speech, nor has anything entered it that could ever corrupt it, nor has anyone shared in it such that they might abrogate it at any time. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is nothing but a messenger from Him, the Exalted, bringing good news and warning with it. He has no right to manipulate it by addition or omission, nor to abandon it entirely or partially based on the people's suggestions or his own desire, nor to turn away from it to ask Allah for another sign according to his desire or the people's desire, nor to compromise with them regarding it or be lenient in any of its divine knowledge or laws. All of this is because it is truth (حق) originating from a source of truth, and what is there after truth except error (الضلال)?

Thus, His saying: «وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ» etc., completes the preceding discourse. Its summary is that the Quran is a true sign (آية حقة), and no one has the right to manipulate it in any way; the Prophet and others are equal in this regard.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً» — A Quran which We have unfolded in parts so that you may read it to the people at intervals, and We have sent it down in successive revelations. (17:106). This is conjoined to what preceded it; meaning: We sent it down with the truth and We unfolded it as a Quran. It is said in al-Majma‘: The meaning of "We unfolded it" (فرقناه) is that We separated it and sent it down verse by verse and Surah by Surah. This is indicated by His saying: «عَلى مُكْثٍ» — ‘at intervals’. Al-mukth (المكث) can be pronounced with a damma or a fatha on the mim; they are two linguistic variants. [End of quote].

The term itself encompasses the descent of the Quranic knowledge (المعارف)—which is with Allah—into the mold of words and expressions that can only be received gradually and cannot be engaged with except with intervals and deliberation, so that it is easier for people to intellectually grasp and memorize it, as in His saying: «إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ» — ‘We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is with Us in the Mother of the Book, sublime and wise.’ (43:3-4).

It also encompasses the descent of the Quranic verses in separate installments (نجوما مفرقة), Surah by Surah and verse by verse, according to the people's progress in their readiness to receive the fundamental knowledge for belief (الاعتقاد) and the secondary laws for action (العمل), and as dictated by the public interests (المصالح). This was so that knowledge would be paired with action, and so that human nature would not recoil from it, by taking its knowledge and laws one after another, as would have happened had it been sent down all at once. The Torah was sent down all at once, and the Jews did not receive it with acceptance until the mountain was raised (نتق الجبل) above them as if it were a canopy.

However, what is more consistent with the context of the previous verses—which included their narrated proposal: «حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ» — ‘...until you bring down to us a book that we may read’ (17:93), which clearly suggests a proposal for the Quran to be sent down all at once—is that the "unfolding" (تفريق) of the Quran refers to its descent Surah by Surah and verse by verse according to the gradual occurrence of the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول). People repeatedly proposed that the Quran be sent down as a single whole, as in: «وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً» — ‘The faithless say, ‘Why was not the Quran sent down to him all at once?’’ (25:32), and His saying narrating from the People of the Book: «يَسْئَلُكَ أَهْلُ الْكِتابِ أَنْ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتاباً مِنَ السَّماءِ» — ‘The People of the Book ask you to bring down for them a Book from the sky.’ (4:153).

This is supported by the conclusion of the verse with His saying: «وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً» — ‘and We have sent it down in successive revelations’, for al-tanzil (التنزيل)—which is the gradual sending down of something—is more closely related to the second consideration than the first.

Nevertheless, the second consideration—which is the detailing and unfolding of the Quran according to its descent by sending down some of it after others without it being sent down all at once—necessitates the first consideration, which is its detailing and unfolding into various followed items of knowledge and laws after they were all integrated into a single reality, folded and gathered in their roots within a single, unique origin.

For this reason, Allah, the Glorified, detailed His Book into Surahs and verses after He clothed it in the garment of Arabic wording, so that it would be easy for people to understand it, as He said: «لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ» — ‘so that you may exercise your reason’. Then He categorized it into types and arranged it in an order, sending it down one after another when the need arose and according to the attainment of the people's varying readiness and the completion of their receptivity for each part. This took place over a full twenty-three years, so that instruction would be accompanied by spiritual training (التربية), and knowledge would be paired with action.

We shall return to the discussion of some matters related to the verse and the Surah in a separate discourse, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ آمِنُوا بِهِ أَوْ لا تُؤْمِنُوا» — Say, ‘Believe in it, or do not believe.’ (17:107) to the end of the three verses. What is intended by "those who were given knowledge before it" (الذين أوتوا العلم من قبله) are those who attained realization through knowledge of Allah and His signs before the descent of the Quran, whether they were Jews, Christians, or others. There is no reason for restriction, unless it is said that the context implies these were people of truth, and the religion that was not abrogated at that time was the religion of the Messiah (عليه‌السلام); thus, they would be the people of truth among the Christian scholars who did not deviate or change [the scripture].

In any case, the meaning of their being "given knowledge before it" is that they were prepared to understand the word of truth and accept it because they were equipped with knowledge of the reality of its meaning, which bequeathed to them the attribute of humility (الخشوع); thus, the Quran recited to them increases them in humility.

And His saying: «يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقانِ سُجَّداً» — ‘they fall down on their faces in prostration’. Faces (الأذقان) is the plural of dhaqan (ذقن), which is the chin where the two jawbones meet. Falling down (الخرور) on the chins means falling to the ground on their faces for prostration, as explained by His saying: «سُجَّداً» — ‘in prostration’. The chins were specified because the chin is the part of the face closest to the ground when falling upon it for prostration. It is sometimes said that "chins" refers to the faces, using the part to represent the whole metaphorically.

And His saying: «وَيَقُولُونَ سُبْحانَ رَبِّنا إِنْ كانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّنا لَمَفْعُولاً» — ‘and say, ‘Immaculate is our Lord! Indeed our Lord’s promise was bound to be fulfilled.’’ (17:108). That is, they glorify Him, the Exalted, from every deficiency and specifically from breaking a promise. The context of the previous verses indicates that the "promise" refers to His promise of the Resurrection (البعث), and this is in contrast to the polytheists' insistence on denying the Resurrection and the Return (المعاد), as repeated in the previous verses.

And His saying: «وَيَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقانِ يَبْكُونَ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ خُشُوعاً» — ‘They fall down on their faces weeping, and it increases them in humility.’ (17:109). The repetition of "falling down on their faces" and its association with weeping is to convey the meaning of submission (الخضوع) — the humility expressed through the body — just as the second clause conveys the meaning of humility (الخشوع) — the humility that resides in the heart. The summary of the verse is that they submit and are humble.

In the verse is the affirmation of the characteristic of the believers for them, which was referred to previously by His saying: «وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنُ ما هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — ‘We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the believers’ (17:82). Similarly, the verse negates the characteristic of the polytheists from them, which is the denial of the Resurrection.

In these three verses is an explanation that the Quran is independent of their faith; not because the faith of those given knowledge before it removes the need for the faith of others, but because their faith in it reveals that it is a Book of truth sent down with the truth. It has no need for its truthfulness, nor any lack in its perfection, for the faith of any believer or the affirmation of any affirmer. If they believe in it, it is for their own souls, and if they disbelieve in it, it is against them; it is neither for it nor against it.

He, the Glorified, mentioned their turning away from the Quran, their disbelief in it, their lack of regard for it being a sign, and their proposal of other signs. Then He explained for it such attributes of perfection and evidences of inimitability (الإعجاز) in its wording and meaning, and its profound effect on souls, and the manner of its descent, through which it became clear that it is truth which no falsehood or corruption can ever touch. Then He explained in these verses that it is independent of their faith; they are left to what they choose of faith or disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: «قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى» — Say, ‘Invoke ‘‘Allah’’ or invoke ‘‘the All-beneficent.’’ Whichever [of His names] you may invoke, to Him belong the Best Names.’ (17:110). The word "or" (أو) is for parity and permissibility. What is intended by "Allah" and "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) are the two names indicating the Named, rather than the Named Himself. The meaning is: Invoke by the name of Allah or by the name of the All-Beneficent, for the invocation is an invocation of Him.

And His saying: «أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا» — ‘Whichever you may invoke’ is a conditional clause, and "what" (ما) is a connective for emphasis, similar to His saying: «فَبِما رَحْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ» — ‘It is by Allah’s mercy...’ (3:159) and His saying: «عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ لَيُصْبِحُنَّ نادِمِينَ» — ‘In a little while they will surely become regretful.’ (23:40). "Whichever" (أَيًّا) is the conditional particle and is the object of "you invoke" (تَدْعُوا).

And His saying: «فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى» — ‘to Him belong the Best Names’ is the response to the condition (جواب الشرط). It is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect. The meaning is: Whichever of the two names you invoke, it is a most beautiful name belonging to Him, because all the Best Names (الأسماء الحسنى) belong to Him. The names indicating the named entities include those that are beautiful (حسنة), indicating what contains beauty, and those that are ugly (قبيحة), which are the opposite; there is no way for the ugly to be attributed to Him, the Exalted. Among the beautiful names are those that are "most beautiful" (أحسن), containing no taint of deficiency or ugliness, such as Wealth (الغنى) with which there is no poverty, Life (الحياة) with which there is no death, and Might (العزة) with which there is no humiliation. Others are beautiful where beauty predominates without being pure. To Allah, the Glorified, belong the Best Names (الأسماء الحسنى) — every name that is the most beautiful of names in its meaning — as indicated by the saying of the leaders of the religion: "Indeed Allah, the Exalted, is Wealthy but not like the wealthy, Living but not like the living, Mighty but not like the mighty, Knowing but not like the knowing," and so on. That is, He possesses the pure and absolute form of every perfection, untainted by its opposite.

The pronoun in His saying: «أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى» refers to the Essence (الذات) which transcends every name and description. It does not refer back to either of the two names, "Allah" or "the All-Beneficent," because what is intended by them—as mentioned—are the names rather than the transcendent Essence which is named by them. It would make no sense to say: Whichever of the two names you invoke, that name possesses all the Best Names or the rest of the Best Names. Rather, the meaning is: Whichever of His names you invoke, there is no barrier to it, because they are all His names, for they are Best Names and to Him belong the Best Names. They are the paths to invoking Him, and invoking them is invoking Him, for they are His names, and the name is the mirror of the Named and His title; so understand that.

The verse is among the most brilliant of Quranic verses, illuminating the reality of what the Holy Quran holds regarding the monotheism of the Essence (توحيد الذات) and the monotheism of worship (توحيد العبادة), in contrast to what the idolaters (الوثنية) hold regarding the monotheism of the Essence while associating partners in worship (تشريك العبادة).

For the idolaters—as a summary of their views was presented in the tenth volume of this book—believe that He, the Glorified, is an Essence transcending every limit and description, who then became determined by names, one name after another, and they call this "procreation" (تولدا). They believe that the angels and jinn are high manifestations (مظاهر) of His names, so they are His children who manage the universe. They believe that the worship of the worshippers and the orientation of those who orient themselves does not exceed the level of the names, nor does it surpass the rank of the "children" who are the manifestations of His names. For in what we worship, we only worship the God (الإله), or the Creator (الخالق), or the Provider (الرازق), or the Giver of Life (المحيي), or the Bringer of Death (المميت), and so on. All of these are names whose manifestations are the "children" from among the angels and jinn. As for the transcendent Essence, It is too high to be reached by sense, imagination, or intellect, and too exalted for any orientation, seeking, worship, or ritual to attach to It.

Thus, according to them, the invocation of every name is the worship of that name—meaning the angel or jinn who is the manifestation of that name—and he is the god (الإله) worshipped through that worship. Consequently, the gods multiply with the multiplication of the types of invocations for various needs. For this reason, when some of the polytheists heard the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) praying in his prayer: "O Allah, O All-Beneficent," they said: "Look at this Sabian; he forbids us from worshipping two gods while he himself invokes two gods."

The noble verse refutes them and reveals the error in their view by stating that these names are Best Names belonging to Him, the Exalted. They are purely owned by Him, having no independence from Him in description, nor are they separate from Him in essence or attribute; He owns them and they subsist through Him. They possess nothing but pure "path-ness" (الطريقية). Thus, invoking them is invoking Him, and orienting through them is orienting toward Him. How can it be correct for the name to veil the Named when it is nothing but a path indicating Him, a guide leading to Him, and a face for Him through which He manifests to others? Therefore, the invocation of many names does not contradict the monotheism of the worship of the Essence, just as it is impossible for worship to stop at the name and not exceed it.

From this explanation, the error in counting the names or their manifestations from among the angels and jinn as "children" of Him, the Exalted, becomes apparent. For the application of "offspring" (الولد) and "child" (الابن)—whether literally or as an honorific—necessitates a type of homogeneity and partnership between the child and the parent in the reality of the essence or in one of its perfections. The courtyard of His majesty is sanctified from anything else sharing with Him in essence or perfection; for what He possesses is for Himself, and what others possess is His, not for themselves.

Likewise, the error in attributing the management of the universe in its various forms to them becomes apparent. For these angels, as well as the names of which they are manifestations according to them, do not own anything for themselves, nor are they independent of Him in anything. Rather, they are honored servants; they do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command. The same applies to the jinn in what they do. In short, there is no cause among the active causes in the universe except that He, the Exalted, is the one who granted it power over what it does; He is the Owner of what He has granted, and the One powerful over that which He has empowered it.

This is what the following verse conveys: «وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلِيٌّ مِنَ الذُّلِ» — And say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has not taken any son, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, nor has He any ally [to help Him] out of weakness.’ (17:111). We shall repeat the reference to this, Allah willing.

In the verse is an indication that the word of majesty (لفظ الجلالة) — Allah — is among the Best Names. In its origin—al-ilah (الإله)—it is a description conveying the meaning of "being worshipped" (المعبودية), even if it became a proper noun (العلمية) through frequent usage. This is indicated by the correctness of applying attributes to it; one says: "Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful," but one does not say: "The All-Beneficent, Allah, the Most Merciful." In His speech, the Exalted: «بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ» — In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (1:1).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلاتِكَ وَلا تُخافِتْ بِها وَابْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلاً» — Do not be loud-voiced in your prayer, nor be low-voiced in it, and seek a way in between. (17:110). Loudness (الجهر) and whispering (الإخفات) are relative attributes that characterize sounds. Sometimes a third quality is considered between them, which is whispering relative to loudness and loudness relative to whispering. Thus, loudness (الجهر) is exaggeration in raising the voice, and whispering (الإخفات) is exaggeration in lowering it, and what is between them is moderation. The meaning of the verse would then be: Do not exaggerate in your prayer in loudness nor in whispering, but rather follow a way between them, which is moderation. It is called a "way" (سبيلا) because it is a practice (سنة) to be followed by him and those of his community of believers who emulate him.

This is if the "prayer" in His saying: «بِصَلاتِكَ» — ‘in your prayer’ is intended to be exhaustive, referring to every single prayer. However, if the collective whole is intended—and this is perhaps more apparent—the meaning would be: Do not be loud in all your prayers, nor be low-voiced in all of them, but rather take a middle way, being loud in some and low-voiced in others. This meaning is more appropriate in view of what is established in the Sunnah regarding loudness in some of the daily obligatory prayers, such as Dawn (الصبح), Sunset (المغرب), and Night (العشاء), and whispering in others.

Perhaps this interpretation is more fitting in view of the connection between the end of the verse and its beginning. Loudness in prayer suits His being High and Transcendent (علي متعال), and whispering suits His being Near (قريب), closer than the jugular vein. Thus, adopting both qualities in the prayers is a fulfillment of the right of all His names.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلِيٌّ مِنَ الذُّلِّ وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيراً» — And say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has not taken any son, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, nor has He any ally [to help Him] out of weakness,’ and magnify Him with a [worthy] magnification. (17:111). This is conjoined to His saying in the previous verse: «قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ». The summary of the discourse returns to: Say to them that these names you invoke and claim to be worshipped gods besides Him are only His names; they are owned by Him, owning neither themselves nor anything for themselves. Thus, invoking them is invoking Him, for He is the Worshipped in every case.

Then, praise Him and laud Him for what results from the absolute nature of His sovereignty (ملك). For nothing resembles Him in essence or attribute such that it could be a son to Him—if it were derived from Him in essence or attribute, as the idolaters and the People of the Book among the Christians and Jews, and the ancient Magians say regarding the angels, the jinn, the Messiah, Ezra, or the rabbis. Nor can there be a partner (شريك) if it shared in sovereignty without derivation, as the idolaters, dualists (الثنويون), and others among the worshippers of Satan say. Nor can there be an ally (ولي) for Him if it shared in sovereignty and surpassed Him, thus rectifying some part of His kingdom that He was unable to rectify.

In another way: nothing is of His genus such that it could be a son if it were below Him, or a partner if it were equal to Him in rank, or an ally if it were superior to Him in sovereignty.

The verse is, in reality, a laudation of Him, the Exalted, for His absolute sovereignty, from which follows the negation of the son, the partner, and the ally. For this reason, He commanded him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to use praise (التحميد) rather than glorification (التسبيح), even though the negation of the son, partner, and ally mentioned therein are negative attributes (صفات سلبية) for which glorification is more suitable than praise; so understand that.

He, the Glorified, concluded the verse with His saying: «وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيراً» — ‘and magnify Him with a [worthy] magnification.’ This magnification is left absolute after the description and sanctification; thus, it is a magnification beyond every description. Therefore, "Allah is Greater" (الله أكبر) has been interpreted as meaning He is greater than can be described, as narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام). If the meaning were that He is greater than everything, it would not be free from associating things with Him, the Exalted, in the meaning of greatness, while His courtyard is too mighty for anything to share with Him in any matter.

Among the subtle beauties of the Surah is the opening of its first verse with glorification (التسبيح) and the conclusion of its last verse with magnification (التكبير), while also opening it with praise (التحميد).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Sa‘id ibn Mansur, ibn al-Mundhir, and ibn Abi Hatim narrated from ‘Ali that he used to read: laqad ‘alimta (لقد علمتَ) — meaning with the fatha [on the ta]. ‘Ali said: "By Allah, the enemy of Allah [Pharaoh] did not know, but it was Moses who knew."

I say: This is a recitation attributed to him (عليه‌السلام).

In al-Kafi, from ‘Ali ibn Muhammad with his chain of transmission, he said: Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) was asked about someone who has an ailment on his forehead and cannot prostrate upon it. He said: "يضع ذقنه على الأرض إن الله عز وجل يقول ـ يخرُّون للأذقان سجداً" — "He places his chin on the ground, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: ‘they fall down on their faces (chins) in prostration.’"

I say: There are other narrations with the same meaning.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, ibn Jarir and ibn Mardawayh narrated from ibn ‘Abbas who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed in Mecca one day and invoked Allah. He said in his invocation: "O Allah, O All-Beneficent." The polytheists said: "Look at this Sabian; he forbids us from invoking two gods while he himself invokes two gods."
