So Allah sent down: «قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ» the verse.

I say: There are other narrations regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse that differ from this one and mention other things; however, this narration is the closest in its correspondence to the content of the verse.

In al-Tawhid, with a chain of transmission, and in al-Ihtijaj, as a mursal narration, from Hisham ibn al-Hakam who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) about the names of Allah, the Mighty is His mention, and their derivation. I said: "From what is 'Allah' derived?" He said: "O Hisham, 'Allah' is derived from ilah (إله), and ilah necessitates a 'worshipped one' (مألوها). The name is other than the Named. Whoever worships the name without the meaning has disbelieved and has not worshipped anything. Whoever worships the name and the meaning has disbelieved and has worshipped two. Whoever worships the meaning without the name, that is monotheism (التوحيد). Do you understand, O Hisham?"

He said: I said: "Tell me more." He said: "Indeed, to Allah, Blessed and Exalted, belong ninety-nine names. If the name were the Named, every name among them would be a god. But Allah is a meaning indicated by these names, and all of them are other than Him. O Hisham, 'bread' is the name of the food, 'water' is the name of the drink, 'garment' is the name of the clothing, and 'fire' is the name of that which burns." [The tradition continues].

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission from ibn Ri’ab from more than one person from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "من عبد الله بالتوهم فقد كفر ، ومن عبد الاسم ولم يعبد المعنى فقد كفر ، ومن عبد الاسم والمعنى فقد أشرك ، ومن عبد المعنى بإيقاع الأسماء عليه بصفاته ـ التي يصف بها نفسه فعقد عليه قلبه ـ ونطق به لسانه في سرائره وعلانيته ـ فأولئك أصحاب أمير المؤمنين" — "Whoever worships Allah through imagination has disbelieved. Whoever worships the name and does not worship the meaning has disbelieved. Whoever worships the name and the meaning has associated partners. Whoever worships the meaning by applying the names to It through His attributes—with which He describes Himself—and binds his heart to It, and his tongue speaks of It in his secrets and openly—those are the companions of the Commander of the Faithful." In another tradition: "Those are the true believers."

In the Tawhid of al-Bihar, in the chapter on the distinction between the name and the meaning, from al-Tawhid with its chain of transmission from Ibrahim ibn ‘Umar from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted, created a name with letters that are not described, with a word that is not spoken, with a person that is not embodied, with a likeness that is not characterized, and with a color that is not dyed. Dimensions are negated from it, limits are distanced from it, and the sense of every imaginer is veiled from it. It is hidden but not veiled. He made it a complete word consisting of four parts together—none of which is before the other. He manifested three things from it due to the need of the creation for them, and He veiled one of them—which is the Hidden, Treasured Name—by these three names that were manifested.

The manifested ones are: Allah, the Blessed (تبارك), and the Immaculate (سبحان). For each of these names there are four pillars, making twelve pillars. Then He created for each pillar thirty names and acts attributed to them. So He is the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful, the King, the Holy, the Creator, the Maker, the Shaper, the Living, the Self-Subsisting, whom neither slumber nor sleep overtakes, the All-Knowing, the All-Aware, the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing, the All-Wise, the All-Mighty, the All-Compelling, the All-Proud, the High, the Great, the All-Powerful, the Able, the Peace, the Faithful, the Guardian, the Maker, the Originator, the Incomparable, the Exalted, the Majestic, the Generous, the Provider, the Giver of Life, the Bringer of Death, the Resurrector, the Inheritor.

These names and whatever belongs to the Best Names—until they complete three hundred and sixty names—are attributions to these three names. These three names are pillars and veils for the One Name—the Hidden, Treasured one—veiled by these three names. That is His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: «قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ ـ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى»."

I say: The tradition is also narrated in al-Kafi from him (عليه‌السلام).

It has already been mentioned in the discussion of the Best Names in the eighth volume of this book that these words called the names of Allah are only the "names of the names" (أسماء الأسماء). That which they indicate and point to of the referent—meaning the Essence—is taken with the description of what the name is according to reality. Accordingly, some of the Best Names are identical to the Essence (عين الذات), which are those comprising a positive perfection attribute such as the Living, the All-Knowing, and the All-Powerful. Others are additional to the Essence and outside of It, which are those comprising a negative or active attribute, such as the Creator, the Provider, and "neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him." This is regarding the names. As for the "names of the names"—which are the words indicating the Essence taken with one of Its descriptions—there is no doubt that they are other than the Essence, and that they are originated words subsisting in the one who utters them.

However, there is a disagreement here from two perspectives:

First: Some ignorant people among the early theologians (متكلمي السلف) confused the names with the "names of the names." They thought that what was meant by the identity (عينية) of the names with the Essence was the identity of the "names of the names" with It. Thus, they held that the name is the Named, and accordingly, worshipping the name and invoking it would be identical to worshipping the Named. This view was prevalent in the early Abbasid era, and the two previous narrations—meaning the two narrations from al-Tawhid—are in refutation of it.

Second: What the idolaters hold, which is that Allah, the Glorified, is not the object of worshipful orientation; rather, it attaches to the names. Thus, the names or their manifestations from among the angels, jinn, and perfect humans are the ones invoked and are the worshipped gods besides Allah. You have already seen in the preceding commentary that His saying, the Exalted: «قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ» etc., is a refutation of this.

The last narration also reveals the manner of the names' emergence from the transcendent Essence, which is too high for knowledge to encompass, or for description and attribute to restrict, or for name and title to limit. Through the explanation in its beginning and end, it is explicit that what is meant by "names" therein are the names themselves, rather than the "names of the names." We have explained this to some extent in the conclusion of the discussion on the Best Names in the eighth volume of this book; so refer to it if you wish.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): regarding His saying, the Exalted: «وَلا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلاتِكَ وَلا تُخافِتْ بِها وَابْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلاً». He said: "كان رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله إذا كان بمكة جهر بصوته ـ فيعلم بمكانه المشركون فكانوا يؤذونه ـ فأنزلت هذه الآية عند ذلك" — "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in Mecca, he would be loud with his voice, so the polytheists would know his location and harm him. So this verse was sent down at that time."

I say: This meaning is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from ibn Mardawayh from ibn ‘Abbas. It is also narrated from ‘A’ishah that it was sent down regarding invocation (الدعاء), and there is no harm in this as it does not contradict [the other]. It is also narrated from her that it was sent down regarding the tashahhud.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Sama‘ah, he said: I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: «وَلا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلاتِكَ وَلا تُخافِتْ بِها». He said: "المخافتة ما دون سمعك والجهر أن ترفع صوتك شديدا" — "Whispering is what is below your own hearing, and loudness is to raise your voice intensely."

I say: In this is support for the first meaning mentioned in the exegesis of the verse.

In it, with its chain of transmission from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, he said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): "Must the Imam make those behind him hear, even if they are many?" He said: "ليقرأ وسطا يقول الله تبارك وتعالى : ولا تجهر بصلاتك ولا تخافت بها" — "He should recite moderately; Allah, Blessed and Exalted, says: ‘Do not be loud-voiced in your prayer, nor be low-voiced in it.’"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad and al-Tabarani narrated from Mu‘adh ibn Anas who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The verse of Might (آية العز) is: «وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً» the entire verse."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلِيٌّ مِنَ الذُّلِ». He said: "لم يذل فيحتاج إلى ولي ينصره" — "He was not humiliated such that He would need an ally to help Him."

"Another Traditionary and Quranic Research"

Related to His saying, the Exalted: «وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ» in three sections:

1 — The Holy Quran has parts by which it is known, such as the juz’, the hizb, the ‘ushr, and others. Those whose consideration traces back to a specific attention from the Mighty Book itself are two: the Surah and the Ayah (verse). Allah, the Glorified, has repeatedly mentioned them in His speech, such as His saying: «سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها» — ‘[This is] a surah which We have sent down’ (24:1) and His saying: «قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ» — ‘Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it’’ (10:38), and others.

Their usage has been frequent on the tongue of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the companions, and the Imams—a frequency that leaves no doubt that they have a reality in the Holy Quran. A Surah is a collection of divine speech beginning with the basmalah, aimed at explaining a purpose; this purpose identifies the Surah consistently and without exception, except for Bara’ah (Surah al-Tawbah). It has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) that it consists of verses from Surah al-Anfal. Other exceptions include what has been narrated from them (عليه‌السلام) that al-Duha and Alam Nashrah are one Surah, and that al-Fil and al-Ilaf are one Surah.

Similar is the case for the Ayah (verse). The application of "Ayah" to a segment of speech has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: «وَإِذا تُلِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُهُ زادَتْهُمْ إِيماناً» — ‘and when His signs are recited to them they increase them in faith’ (8:2), and His saying: «كِتابٌ فُصِّلَتْ آياتُهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا» — ‘a Book whose verses have been elaborated as an Arabic Quran’ (41:3). It has been narrated from Umm Salamah that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to stop at the ends of the verses. It is established that Surah al-Hamd is seven verses, and it is narrated from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that Surah al-Mulk is thirty verses, among other things indicating that numbering was applied to the verses in the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his Progeny.

What reflection on the division of Arabic speech into segments and sections naturally provides—especially in rhymed speech—and then contemplation on what has been narrated from the Prophet and his Progeny (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the number of verses, is that an Ayah of the Quran is a segment of speech upon which recitation should naturally rely by separating it from what precedes it and what follows it.

This varies according to the contexts, especially in rhymed contexts. Sometimes it is a single word, such as His saying: «مُدْهامَّتانِ» — ‘dark green’ (55:64). Sometimes it is two words or more, whether a complete sentence or not, such as His saying: «الرَّحْمنُ عَلَّمَ الْقُرْآنَ خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيانَ» — ‘The All-beneficent has taught the Quran. He created man, [and] taught him articulate speech.’ (55:1-4) and His saying: «الْحَاقَّةُ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ» — ‘The Inevitable! What is the Inevitable? And what will show you what the Inevitable is?’ (69:1-3). Sometimes it is long, like the Verse of Debt (آية الدين) in Surah al-Baqarah (2:282).

2 — As for the number of Quranic Surahs, they are one hundred and fourteen Surahs according to the standard arrangement in the mus’haf circulating among us, which corresponds to the Uthmanic mus’haf. The speech of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) regarding this has preceded: that they do not count Bara’ah as an independent Surah, and they count al-Duha and Alam Nashrah as one Surah, and al-Fil and al-Ilaf as one Surah.

As for the number of verses (الآي), no mutawatir text has been transmitted that defines the verses and distinguishes each verse from others, nor is there any solitary report (الآحاد) that can be relied upon. The clearest evidence for this is the disagreement among the scholars of numbering (أهل العدد) among themselves: the Meccans, the Medinans, the Syrians, the Basrans, and the Kufans.

Some said the total of the Quran is six thousand verses. Others said six thousand two hundred and four verses. It was also said: and fourteen; and said: and nineteen; and said: and twenty-five; and said: and thirty-six.

The Meccans narrated their numbering from ‘Abdullah ibn Kathir from Mujahid from ibn ‘Abbas from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b. The Medinans have two numberings: one ending at Abu Ja‘far Marthad ibn al-Qa‘qa‘ and Shaybah ibn Nassah, and the other at Isma‘il ibn Ja‘far ibn Abi Kathir al-Ansari. The people of Syria narrated their numbering from Abu al-Darda’. The numbering of the people of Basra ends at ‘Asim ibn al-‘Ajjaj al-Jahdari. The numbering of the people of Kufa is attributed to Hamzah, al-Kisa’i, and Khalaf. Hamzah said: "Ibn Abi Layla informed us of this numbering from Abu ‘Abd al-Rahman al-Sulami from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib."

In short, since the numbers do not end at a mutawatir text or a single report of significance that can be relied upon to distinguish each verse from its sister, there is no obligation to adopt any of them. Whatever among them is clear and manifest is accepted; otherwise, the contemplating researcher may choose what his investigation leads him to.

That which is narrated from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) regarding the Kufan numbering is contradicted by the fact that the basmalah is not counted in any of the Surahs except for Fatihat al-Kitab as one of its verses, despite what is narrated from him (عليه‌السلام) and from other Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) that the basmalah is a verse of the Quran and is part of every Surah that opens with it. This would necessitate an increase in the count by the number of basmalahs.

This is what deterred us from presenting the details of the numbering they mentioned here, and from mentioning the Surahs whose verse count they agreed upon—which are forty Surahs—and those whose count or verse-ends they disagreed upon—which are seventy-four Surahs—as well as what they agreed upon as being a complete verse or not being a verse, such as «الر» wherever it occurs in the Quran, and what was disagreed upon. Whoever wishes to see the details of that should refer to the relevant sources.

3 — Regarding the chronological order of the Surahs' revelation: It is narrated in al-Itqan from ibn al-Durays in Fada’il al-Quran, who said: Muhammad ibn ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Ja‘far al-Razi narrated to us, ‘Amr ibn Harun informed us, ‘Uthman ibn ‘Ata’ al-Khorasani narrated to us from his father from ibn ‘Abbas who said: "When the opening of a Surah was sent down in Mecca, it was recorded in Mecca, then Allah would add to it whatever He willed.

The first to be sent down of the Quran was Iqra’ bi-ismi Rabbik, then Nun, then Ya ayyuha al-Muzzammil, then Ya ayyuha al-Muddaththir, then Tabbat yada Abi Lahab, then Idha al-shamsu kuwwirat, then Sabbih isma Rabbika al-A‘la, then Wa al-layli idha yaghsha, then Wa al-fajr, then Wa al-duha, then Alam nashrah, then Wa al-‘asr, then Wa al-‘adiyat, then Inna a‘taynaka, then Alhakum al-takathur, then Ara’ayta al-ladhi yukadhdhib, then Qul ya ayyuha al-kafirun, then Alam tara kayfa fa‘ala Rabbuk, then Qul a‘udhu bi-Rabbi al-falaq, then Qul a‘udhu bi-Rabbi al-nas, then Qul huwa Allahu ahad, then Wa al-najm, then ‘Abasa, then Inna anzalnahu fi laylat al-qadr, then Wa al-shamsi wa duhaha, then Wa al-sama’i dhat al-buruj, then Wa al-tin, then Li-ilafi Quraysh, then Al-qari‘ah, then La uqsimu bi-yawm al-qiyamah, then Waylun li-kulli humazah, then Wa al-mursalat, then Qaf, then La uqsimu bi-hadha al-balad, then Wa al-sama’i wa al-tariq, then Iqtarabat al-sa‘ah, then Sad, then al-A‘raf, then Qul uhiya, then Ya-Sin, then al-Furqan, then al-Mala’ikah, then Kaf-Ha-Ya-‘Ayn-Sad, then Ta-Ha, then al-Waqi‘ah, then Ta-Sin-Mim al-Shu‘ara’, then Ta-Sin, then al-Qasas, then Bani Isra’il, then Yunus, then Hud, then Yusuf, then al-Hijr, then al-An‘am, then al-Saffat, then Luqman, then Saba’, then al-Zumar, then Ha-Mim al-Mu’min, then Ha-Mim al-Sajdah, then Ha-Mim-‘Ayn-Sin-Qaf, then Ha-Mim al-Zukhruf, then al-Dukhan, then al-Jathiyah, then al-Ahqaf, then al-Dhariyat, then al-Ghashiyah, then al-Kahf, then al-Nahl, then Inna arsalna Nuhan, then Surah Ibrahim, then al-Anbiya’, then al-Mu’minun, then Tanzil al-Sajdah, then al-Tur, then Tabarak al-Mulk, then al-Haqqah, then Sa’ala, then ‘Amma yatasa’alun, then al-Nazi‘at, then Idha al-sama’u infatarat, then Idha al-sama’u inshaqqat, then al-Rum, then al-‘Ankabut, then Waylun lil-mutaffifin. This is what Allah sent down in Mecca.

Then Allah sent down in Medina Surah al-Baqarah, then al-Anfal, then Al ‘Imran, then al-Ahzab, then al-Mumtahanah, then al-Nisa’, then Idha zulzilat, then al-Hadid, then al-Qital, then al-Ra‘d, then al-Rahman, then al-Insan, then al-Talaq, then Lam yakun, then al-Hashr, then Idha ja’a nasru Allah, then al-Nur, then al-Hajj, then al-Munafiqun, then al-Mujadilah, then al-Hujurat, then al-Tahrim, then al-Jumu‘ah, then al-Taghabun, then al-Saff, then al-Fath, then al-Ma’idah, then Bara’ah."

Surah Fatihat al-Kitab fell out of the narration; it is sometimes said that it was sent down twice, once in Mecca and once in Medina.

It is also narrated therein from al-Bayhaqi in Dala’il al-Nubuwwah, that he narrated with his chain from ‘Ikrimah and al-Husayn ibn Abi al-Hasan, who both said: "Allah sent down of the Quran in Mecca Iqra’ bi-ismi Rabbik..." and they recounted the tradition similar to the previous tradition of ‘Ata’ from ibn ‘Abbas, except that al-Fatihah, al-A‘raf, and Kaf-Ha-Ya-‘Ayn-Sad fell out of what was sent down in Mecca.

Also, it mentioned therein Ha-Mim al-Dukhan before Ha-Mim al-Sajdah, then Idha al-sama’u inshaqqat before Idha al-sama’u infatarat, then Waylun lil-mutaffifin before al-Baqarah among what was sent down in Medina, then Al ‘Imran before al-Anfal, then al-Ma’idah before al-Mumtahanah.

Then al-Bayhaqi narrated with his chain from Mujahid from ibn ‘Abbas that he said: "The first that Allah sent down upon His Prophet of the Quran was Iqra’ bi-ismi Rabbik..." the tradition, and it corresponds to the tradition of ‘Ikrimah in its order, and it mentioned the Surahs that fell out of ‘Ikrimah's tradition among what was sent down in Mecca.

In it, from the book al-Nasikh wa al-Mansukh by ibn Hissar: "The Medinan [Surahs] by consensus are twenty Surahs, the disagreed upon are twelve Surahs, and everything else is Meccan by consensus." [End of quote].

What they agreed upon as Medinan are: al-Baqarah, Al ‘Imran, al-Nisa’, al-Ma’idah, al-Anfal, al-Tawbah, al-Nur, al-Ahzab, Surah Muhammad, al-Fath, al-Hujurat, al-Hadid, al-Mujadilah, al-Hashr, al-Mumtahanah, al-Munafiqun, al-Jumu‘ah, al-Talaq, al-Tahrim, and al-Nasr.

What they disagreed upon regarding its Meccan or Medinan status are: Surah al-Ra‘d, al-Rahman, al-Jinn, al-Saff, al-Taghabun, al-Mutaffifin, al-Qadr, al-Bayyinah, al-Zilzal, al-Tawhid, and the Mu‘awwidhatayn (the two Surahs of seeking refuge).

Knowledge of the Meccan and Medinan status of the Surahs, and then the order of their revelation, has a significant impact on research related to the prophetic mission (الدعوة النبوية) and its spiritual, political, and civil course during his time (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the analysis of his noble biography (السيرة). The narrations—as you see—are not fit to stand as a relied-upon proof in establishing any of that, especially since the contradictions among them strip them of credibility.

Thus, the determined path for this purpose is contemplation (التدبر) on the context of the verses and seeking aid from the indications and internal and external signs that are obtained. We shall proceed upon this in this book, and Allah is the One whose help is sought.
